title: Systemic Non-Compliance in Pilot Boarding Arrangements
description:
This theme is characterised by a persistent and widespread failure to rig, maintain, and inspect pilot boarding arrangements in accordance with SOLAS requirements. Across the incident population, deficiencies range from frayed side ropes and improperly placed ladder weights to non-compliant fastening methods, absent securing points, and deteriorated accommodation ladder equipment. What unifies these incidents is not isolated or opportunistic error, but a systemic normalisation of deviance within shipboard safety culture, whereby sub-standard pilot transfer arrangements were repeatedly prepared and used without detection or challenge. The high volume of 'other' category incidents alongside crew injuries underscores that these failures represent a standing operational risk rather than exceptional occurrences, and the breadth of vessel types affected—container ships, bulk carriers, tankers, cruise ships, and general cargo vessels—confirms this is an industry-wide rather than sector-specific problem.

The latent causal architecture of this theme involves the convergence of hardware deterioration, procedural breakdown, and human factors deficiencies. On the hardware side, pilot ladders and accommodation ladders were permitted to degrade through absent or ineffective maintenance regimes, with no mechanism to trigger timely inspection or replacement before operational deployment. Procedurally, pre-arrival checklists either did not exist, were not enforced, or lacked sufficient specificity to capture SOLAS rigging non-conformances, allowing deficient arrangements to pass unchallenged. Human factors analysis points to crew who lacked adequate familiarisation with SOLAS pilot transfer requirements, combined with insufficient officer oversight of a safety-critical evolution. Night-time operating conditions further compounded these weaknesses by reducing the visibility available during rigging and inspection, increasing the probability that physical defects would be overlooked. Collectively, these factors indicate a failure of the vessel's Safety Management System to treat pilot boarding arrangements as a standing regulatory compliance requirement subject to rigorous pre-operation verification.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 23: Pilot Transfer Arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Surveys, Certification, and Port State Control)
preventable_by: Mandatory implementation of a dedicated pre-arrival pilot boarding equipment checklist, aligned explicitly with SOLAS Regulation V/23 and IMO Resolution A.1045(27), requiring a named responsible officer to physically inspect and certify ladder condition, rigging method, fastening compliance, and stanchion geometry before every pilot embarkation, with completed records retained for Port State Control review.

Establishment of a planned maintenance system (PMS) entry specifically for pilot ladders and associated boarding equipment, prescribing defined inspection intervals, condition criteria for withdrawal from service, and mandatory replacement schedules for side ropes, steps, and securing hardware, with defect rectification tracked to closure and verified by the Master prior to the next port arrival.

Delivery of targeted crew familiarisation and competency verification training on SOLAS-compliant pilot transfer arrangements for all officers and ratings involved in rigging operations, including practical demonstrations of correct fastening methods, prohibited configurations, and night-time inspection protocols, with refresher training incorporated into vessel safety management routines and records maintained as part of the SMS audit trail.

representative_cases.description: Container ship's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. Weight of the ladder was resting on a step. The side ropes of the ladder were very frayed in places. The stanchions did not go all the way down to the deck and were too far apart.

Bulk carrier's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. The ladder was shackled to the deck and not backed up. Accommodation ladder had no inboard station and all the gear was in poor condition.

A general cargo vessel had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that it was secured with shackles. The pilot ladder arrangement was rectified upon boarding and the master was advised by the pilot. The vessel was reportedly very cooperative.

A cruise ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. The pilot ladder was secured to the deck head in the shell door opening instead of the deck. After inspection it seems there were no securing points on the deck.

A crude oil tanker had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that the ladder was not rigged as per the current SOLAS regulations. The level of the pilot slant platform was 2.5m above the waterline and there was a chock missing from a step meaning that the step was un-even/not horizontal.

A container ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. Stanchions secured by pins at the base but not rigidly secured at the top such that they had significant give and movement in them. General concerns about diameter of stanchions being too thick, width apart being too great and distance inboard from deck edge also too much.

A bulk carrier had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that it was in poor condition and poor manufacturing standards. The ladder was new however the chocks for the steps were loose meaning that the steps were able to move and one of the spreaders lay flat on the deck when the ladder was laid out on deck, demonstrating how loose it was.

A cruise ship crewmember was climbing down from their bunk when the hook for the ladder broke, causing them to fall to the deck below and fracture a limb. The cause of the accident was attributed to the hook of the ladder broken, which was later repaired. The crew member signed off due to their injury.

A ro-ro cargo vessel had an inadequate pilot ladder arrangement due to the fact that the deck stanchions were not continuous down to the deck to allow the pilot to easily transition from the deck to the ladder side ropes.

Container vessel's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. Ladder was in general condition poor. Three out of four spreaders bars cut less than standard width causing significant rotation away from ships side, when descending last three metres.

title: Systemic Pilot Transfer Arrangement Non-Compliance Across Fleet Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by pervasive and recurring non-compliance with SOLAS pilot transfer arrangement requirements across a broad range of vessel types, including container ships, chemical tankers, oil tankers, and ro-ro vessels. Deficiencies span improper rigging methods, degraded or counterfeit equipment, missing structural components such as stanchions and heaving lines, and failure to cross-reference vessel loading conditions with freeboard height thresholds before deployment. Critically, these are not isolated one-off errors; they represent systemic failures in onboard Safety Management System enforcement, planned maintenance culture, and crew training. The normalisation of non-compliant practices, compounded by underreporting of near-miss events, has allowed these deficiencies to persist across global fleets despite well-established regulatory frameworks and repeated identification through Port State Control detentions and marine safety investigations.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including worn side ropes, missing rubber steps, twisted spreaders, improperly seized rope joints, and the infiltration of counterfeit safety equipment into supply chains — create structural hazards that are frequently undetected due to inadequate inspection regimes and poor equipment status communication. Human factors including insufficient crew familiarity with SOLAS Regulation V/23 technical requirements, absence of verified pre-rigging checklists, lack of supervisory oversight during ladder deployment, and reactive rather than proactive safety cultures further compound the risk. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations with reduced visibility, consistently elevate the consequence severity of existing deficiencies by impairing the ability of both crew and pilots to visually identify non-compliant or structurally compromised arrangements during the most safety-critical phase of the boarding or disembarkation operation. The convergence of these latent hardware, human, and environmental factors produces conditions in which a fatal fall-from-height or man-overboard event remains a foreseeable and preventable outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 23 – Pilot Transfer Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter I, Regulation 14 – Maintenance of the Ship and Equipment
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate the integration of a standardised, SOLAS V/23-referenced pilot transfer arrangement checklist into pre-arrival procedures, requiring documented sign-off by a responsible officer that confirms ladder condition, rigging configuration, and freeboard height compliance against current loading condition data before deployment — with results logged in the vessel's SMS records.

Establish a mandatory fleet-wide competency verification and refresher training programme specifically addressing SOLAS Regulation V/23 technical requirements, including correct rigging methods, prohibited attachment hardware, step spacing tolerances, and tripping line positioning, ensuring all officers responsible for pilot transfer arrangements can demonstrate practical compliance knowledge at scheduled intervals.

Require flag States and Port State Control authorities to enforce pre-arrival pilot transfer equipment certification checks, including third-party procurement auditing to detect counterfeit or substandard ladder components, supported by a structured out-of-service tagging and replacement protocol within the vessel's Planned Maintenance System to eliminate the use of degraded or non-compliant equipment.

representative_cases.description: Container ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. The ship is in ballast condition.
A chemical tanker had an inadequate pilot ladder arrangement. There were no stanchions present and the gate and handle were not connected to the deck and were difficult for the pilot to hold onto.
Removable platform at pilot door rigged at heigh less that 5m above sea level, what makes it not compliant.   Master advised to not use the platform in that loading condition.
Construction did not meet current SOLAS standards. Side ropes were two separate lengths seized together underneath the bottom step.
A general cargo vessel's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. A tripping line was rigged between the last two steps.
Ladder secured by shackles. Ladder was not rigged per current SOLAS regulations.
A container vessel had an inadequate pilot ladder because it was supported using a deck tongue and the tripping line was incorrectly rigged.
Construction did not meet current SOLAS standards. Heaving line was not available for immediate use. No stantions on the deck and the deck rail fittings at the entrance are too wide to grip
A ro-ro cargo vessel's pilot ladder was not rigged to SOLAS regulations. The ladder rigged with the step on the holding platform. Master and crew informed and promised it would be changed.
Container ship's ladder was not rigged per current SOLAS regulations. Choke shackles used, vessel in poor condition.

title: Grounding Due to Inadequate Planning and Situational Awareness
description:
This theme is characterised by vessels grounding on submerged hazards, wrecks, rocks, or berth structures as a result of deficient passage planning, inadequate navigational watchkeeping, and a failure to integrate available tidal, charting, and depth information into real-time decision-making. Incidents span both commercial and recreational vessel types, occurring in coastal waters, narrow channels, and at berths, yet share a common latent cause: procedural failures that allowed navigators or berth operators to proceed with incomplete, outdated, or unverified safety-critical information. The recurrence of this pattern reflects systemic weaknesses in pre-departure risk assessment, the absence of formalised checks on navigational data currency, and over-reliance on single information sources or past experience rather than verified, up-to-date navigational products. Night-time conditions amplify these deficiencies by removing the visual cues that might otherwise serve as a compensating check on inadequate planning.

Latent hardware contributions are present but secondary; mechanical failures such as conveyor bearing faults extend berth time into critical tidal windows, but their effect is only hazardous because procedural safeguards — real-time depth monitoring, current berth operational limit documentation — are absent. Human factors are pervasive across the cluster: masters and watchkeepers demonstrate reduced situational awareness, delayed recognition of developing grounding scenarios, and insufficient urgency when conditions deteriorate. Environmental conditions, particularly tidal state, low-light periods, and wind-induced set and leeway, consistently act as force multipliers that convert latent procedural and human weaknesses into physical groundings. Critically, adverse weather is rarely the primary cause; rather, environmental conditions expose the inadequacy of planning and watchkeeping that would be acceptable under more forgiving circumstances.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 34-1: Safe Manning, Passage Planning, and Navigational Watch)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships: Safety Management Systems, Risk Assessment, and Procedural Compliance
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (as it relates to receipt of Navigational Warnings and Notices to Mariners containing updated hazard information)
preventable_by: Mandate formal, documented passage plans for all voyages — including recreational and short coastal passages — that explicitly identify charted hazards, submerged wrecks, tidal windows, and minimum under-keel clearance thresholds, with a requirement that plans are reviewed and approved by the master prior to departure and updated when berth or navigational data is found to be outdated.

Require berth operators and port authorities to establish a formalised periodic review cycle — no greater than twelve months — for berth operational limit documentation, including available water depth, tidal datum references, and safe berthing windows, with mandatory communication of any changes to vessel masters prior to arrival and a real-time independent depth-monitoring capability alongside during low water periods.

Implement structured night passage and low-visibility watchkeeping protocols that require at minimum two-person bridge watches, continuous cross-checking of electronic positioning against paper or ECS chart data, and defined positional fix intervals scaled to proximity of hazards, with masters of recreational and small commercial vessels completing competency-assessed night navigation training as a condition of relevant certification renewal.

representative_cases.description: A sailing vessel with three crew on board ran aground on a submerged wreck. As the tide began to fall, the vessel became hard aground. The local lifeboat service attended to assist one crew member to shore, whilst two crew remained on board to monitor the situation and wait for the tide to rise. The vessel refloated on the rising tide and was undamaged by the incident, it continued on passage. The

A trailing suction hopper dredger was alongside at an aggregate berth when the vessel's conveyor suffered a roller bearing failure. The operatives on the jetty advised the ship's crew that this could potentially delay the cargo operations for approximately 2 to 3 hours. The master of the vessel decided to shift the vessel along the berth into deeper water for the low water period. The vessel was p

A sailing yacht with six crew onboard grounded on rocks and requested assistance. A nearby yacht assisted by towing the grounded yacht into safe water and stood by until the yacht confirmed there was no obvious damage or water ingress. Once the crew confirmed there was no damage they continued on passage to a harbour

A general cargo vessel grounded whilst in a narrow channel with a pilot on board. The vessel grounded at high tide and then the tide started to fall. Two tugs attended and when the tide rose again, the vessel floated free of where it had grounded. The vessel was assisted by the two tugs to a berth.

A search and rescue vessel was launching at the designated launching ramp when shortly after it was clear from the carriage (trailer) the vessel's propellers grounded, sustaining minor damage. The crew reported that a number of days of sustained easterly wind had changed the beach condition, causing sediment buildup.

Fishing vessel was leaving a port when they hit the sandbar and placed themselves aground. All crew members are fine, no injuries reported and no damage reported at the moment. They will wait for tide to refloat and will provide further information when afloat.

A service vessel grounded resulting in no damage. The vessel made contact with the sea bed. Speed 11kts. The tide graph on the Time zero plotter it stated there was still 2.1m of tide above chart datum. The Time zero plotter showed a 2m contour depth at chart datum on the route he intended to take. The skipper concluded there was 4m of depth. The vessels draft is 1.6m The vessel slowed as it made

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service carrying out a search near to the shore when it grounded. The tide was flooding, no obvious damage was seen and the vessel returned to station. It was later lifted for a full inspection and no damage was sustained as a result of the grounding. No reported crew injuries.

An inshore ro-pax vessel grounded on rock with four crew on board. The vessel remained on the rock for approximately 10 mins before refloating with significant damage to the vessel keel but no water ingress. No noted damage to vessel propulsion. There were no injuries to crew and no pollution noted.

A guard vessel ran aground while carry out a crew transfer, refloated on the next tide. Vessel got stuck in the mud on the northern approaches and could not proceed further. Tide height was 1.30m at the time and falling. The vessel reported no water ingress, damage or injuries and informed VTS that it required no further assistance. Vessel waited for the tide to rise and refloated.

title: Night-Time Grounding: Navigational Failures in Coastal Waters
description:
This theme is characterised by a persistent pattern of vessel groundings occurring predominantly during night-time operations across a diverse range of vessel types, including fishing vessels, cargo ships, recreational craft, aquaculture support vessels, and SAR assets. The unifying thread across these incidents is not adverse weather — conditions were benign to moderate in the majority of cases — but rather a failure of navigational discipline compounded by reduced visibility. Recurring causal factors include inadequate pre-passage planning, over-reliance on local knowledge or a single navigation system, insufficient use of depth-sounding and electronic navigation aids, and complacency bred by repetitive routes or familiarity with local waters. The irony of a SAR training vessel becoming a grounding casualty exemplifies the broader systemic failure: operational focus or routine familiarity displaces the rigorous navigational vigilance that darkness demands. Tidal miscalculation, pilot-master exchange deficiencies, and poorly defined abort criteria for tide-dependent departures further illustrate the procedural gaps that allow latent risk to materialise into casualties.

From a human factors perspective, fatigue associated with night operations, normalisation of deviance on repetitive routes, and degraded situational awareness in reduced visibility are the dominant contributors. These are amplified by systemic deficiencies in bridge resource management, particularly the absence of a second independent navigational check or effective watchkeeping practices. Environmental conditions, while rarely the primary cause, act as a force multiplier: even modest wave heights and moderate winds degrade a crew's ability to visually detect hazards and maintain precise positional awareness when operating without adequate instrumentation. Hardware and software factors include sensor errors producing unreliable sea state data, over-reliance on GPS without cross-referencing chart depth data, and inadequate use of echo sounders in shoal-prone waters. The consistent pattern of post-grounding flooding escalation in several incidents further highlights deficiencies in damage control preparedness. Collectively, these incidents are largely preventable through stronger procedural controls, improved training standards, and mandatory use of available navigational technology during night-time coastal transits.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 24, 25, 34: navigational equipment, watchkeeping, voyage planning, and safe speed)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (watertight integrity and post-grounding flooding response)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Safe Management and Operation of Ships (SMS passage planning, fatigue management, and bridge resource management procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate formal written passage plans for all coastal and night-time transits, including predefined abort criteria for tide-dependent operations, minimum underkeel clearance thresholds, and waypoint-based hazard alerts — with compliance verified through SMS audits and flag state port state control inspections.

Require continuous use of echo sounders and cross-referenced electronic chart systems (ECDIS or equivalent) during all night-time operations in coastal, confined, or shoal-prone waters, with bridge watchkeeping procedures explicitly prohibiting sole reliance on GPS or local knowledge as the primary navigational reference.

Implement targeted night-operations competency training and fatigue risk management frameworks for small commercial vessel sectors — including fishing, aquaculture, and recreational charter operators — incorporating bridge resource management principles, post-grounding damage assessment drills, and mandatory simulator or scenario-based exercises for night-time coastal navigation.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on a training exercise when a wave lifted the vessel onto rocks. Four crew were on board with no injuries.  Damage to be assessed.
Fishing vessels struck some rocks and started to sink. The crew on board went onto the life raft which resulted in no injuries to them and were recovered by a passing by fishing vessel.
Container ship grounded while under pilotage due to the tide. The vessel sustained a leak in the pipe duct and water ingress in the double bottom water ballast tanks.
A fish carrying vessels, from a fish farm, ran aground
A cargo vessel ran aground, no reported damage.
A sail training vessel grounded when navigating close to the edge of a narrow channel in order to give space to a passing ferry. The seabed was mud and the vessel was undamaged.
Sailing vessel grounded, no further information. Informed by another countries agency.
Sailing vessel's crew was motoring around looking for a location to anchor when it ran aground on soft mud. No damage was reported.
Fishing vessel grounded as a result of misjudging the available depth of water. No damage has been reported.
A general cargo vessel had departed port under pilotage, when the vessel grounded on mud. Prior to departure a calculation for a surge had been made but the surge did not occur.

title: COLREGs Non-Compliance and Close-Quarters Collision Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by repeated failures among recreational and small commercial vessel operators to fulfil their obligations under the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), particularly with respect to maintaining a proper lookout (Rule 5), proceeding at a safe speed (Rule 6), taking early and substantial action to avoid collision (Rule 8), and adhering to give-way responsibilities (Rules 12 and 16). The incidents share a common pattern: a give-way vessel — typically a sailing craft, personal watercraft, or small recreational powerboat — fails to yield, often due to operator inexperience, inattention, or inadequate watchkeeping routines. This forces the stand-on vessel into reactive, uncoordinated manoeuvres that themselves introduce collision risk. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a systemic deficit in recreational operator competency and compliance culture, rather than isolated individual errors.

Latent human factors dominate this theme, including insufficient training, poor situational awareness, and the absence of enforced night watch routines, as evidenced by obscured cockpits, invisible crew, and erratic helm behaviour. Physical hardware deficiencies — notably cockpit canopies obstructing both outward visibility and crew observability — compound these human failures by degrading the effectiveness of any lookout that is attempted. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operation and moderate sea states, consistently compress the time available for threat detection and response, reducing the margin for error to near zero. The near-total absence of inter-vessel VHF communication across these incidents means that intentions are never coordinated, leaving collision avoidance dependent on last-second reactive manoeuvres. Together, these factors create a systemic vulnerability concentrated at the intersection of recreational vessel operations, confined or trafficked waters, and reduced-visibility conditions.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19 and 34: navigational watchkeeping, voyage planning, and use of navigational aids)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for watchkeeping at sea, including lookout requirements and master's standing orders)
SOLAS Chapter VI / STCW Code (A-VIII/2) – Principles of Watchkeeping and Collision Avoidance (officer and operator competency in applying COLREGs)
preventable_by: Mandate structured COLREGs competency assessments and practical collision-avoidance training as a condition of recreational vessel licensing, with specific modules on give-way obligations at night, in confined waters, and in mixed-traffic environments — ensuring operators can correctly identify their role and act decisively before close-quarters situations develop.

Require that all vessels operating in trafficked or confined waters at night maintain a continuous VHF bridge-to-bridge watch on Channel 16, and introduce harbour authority or coastguard-led radio communication protocols for small recreational craft transiting busy channels, reducing reliance on sound signals or shouting as last-resort warnings.

Implement and enforce speed restriction zones in near-quay, channel, and high-traffic recreational areas through physical signage, chart notation, and where feasible, automated vessel monitoring — supported by flag state or port authority guidance that explicitly requires operators to reduce to a safe speed consistent with COLREGs Rule 6 when visibility is reduced or traffic density is elevated.

The sailing vessel crossed port to starboard across channel with no consistent course or speed. Sailing vessel continued changing course and speed mid channel. The Master of the passenger vessel decided to sound five short and rapid blasts. The Master then dec

A sailing yacht was observed approaching from behind, on port tack but no crew visible as a canopy was obscuring the cockpit. The sail training skipper yelled ‘starboard’, a crew member on the yacht looked out and over to the training vessel. The training vessel headed up to bring it on to

The collision between the two vessels was reported near a quay, while they were trying to take evasive action and at approximately  25 knots. Two persons were recovered into ambulance care, one female with head injuries, and one male with swelling to the chest. Both vessels were escorted back to port by marine police and lifeboats and no water ingress was reported

3 PW users where racing each other and approached a small powered leisure craft with the intention of overtaking it. 2 of the PWs may have contacted each other and then collided with the leisure vessel.  The two riders came off their craft into the water. Bothe returned to their craft, but one lost consciousness prior and was then assisted back to the launch bay and was subsequently airlifted to h

The offshore supply vessel had two speedboats cutting across its bow and was in a head-on situation, and the two speedboats approached from just off their port bow. They made no alteration of course and both crossed the bow of the offshore supply vessel and passed green-to-green. The vessel had to stop its engines and narrowly avoided collision with one of the speedboats, which passed 20-30 m ahea

A passenger vessel collided with a moored training yacht, resulting in minor damage to the training yacht. Following a grounding, was successfully floated off . The tow lines were dropped and boat was prepared for the short passage. Shortly after, when proceeding down the Strait, the crew observed a object in front of the boat, too late to alter course. The vessel collided with a small yacht.

A fishing vessel towing gear was in a traffic lane when they altered course to cross the bow of a general cargo vessel using the traffic lane resulting in a near miss collision with a closest point of approach (CPA) of 0.1nm and a time to closest point of approach (TCPA) of 2 minutes. The vessels passed one another at a distance of 0.2nm or approximately 370m. The fishing vessel turned northwards

A recreational speed boat struck a recreational inflatable rowboat in a river, causing one of the crews of the rowboat to be knocked unconscious and sustain cuts and bruises. The speed boat was using a single white light in accordance with local navigation rules whereas the row boat was unlit. The unconscious crew member of the row boat was rescued from the water and taken ashore, where they regai

A chain ferry was travelling across a river on an ebbing tide, the ferry was approaching the bank and was starting to lower the prow. A sailing yacht was proceeding with the ebb tide and did not appear to alter course as it neared the chain ferry. The yacht turned away late, then collided the chain ferry and made contact with the southern most chain and was briefly stuck before freeing itself. No

A rider on an electric foiling board was travelling on open water, a motor vessel was proceeding in the same area. The rider was not aware of the presence of the motor vessel until shortly before it collided with them and went over the top of them. The motor vessel stopped, retrieved the injured rider and their board and then deposited them at a nearby jetty. The motor vessel crew did not contact

title: Passenger Slip, Trip and Fall Injuries Aboard Cruise Vessels
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of passenger slip, trip, and fall injuries occurring predominantly during night-time operations, across a range of sea conditions from benign to moderate-to-rough. Incidents occur in diverse locations including cabins, bathrooms, pool decks, doorway thresholds, food service areas, and open passenger decks, indicating that the hazard is systemic rather than location-specific. A consistent and significant finding across the cluster is the discrepancy between officially reported sea states and measured wave heights, suggesting that actual vessel motion is frequently more severe than formally classified. This under-reporting of conditions likely prevents the timely activation of precautionary passenger advisories and risk controls, embedding a latent systemic failure into the operational response chain. The elevated frequency of night-time incidents further implicates lighting adequacy and passenger disorientation in unfamiliar environments as compounding causal factors, with injury severities ranging from minor head wounds to fractured hips, wrists, and bilateral ankle fractures, as well as at least one spinal injury, confirming that outcomes can be serious and life-altering.

The latent hardware and environmental factors driving this theme include insufficient anti-slip deck and bathroom surfaces, inadequate or poorly designed ambient lighting in passenger spaces, absence or inaccessibility of handrails and grab rails at key transition points such as doorways, pool egress areas, and cabin berths, and passive hazard controls such as warning cones that are demonstrably insufficient under dynamic sea and lighting conditions. Human factors are equally prominent: passengers are frequently unaccustomed to shipboard movement, unfamiliar with cabin layouts in darkness, and insufficiently briefed on fall risks during pre-departure safety orientations. Organisational and procedural failures include the absence of condition-based access restrictions to high-risk areas such as pool decks during elevated sea states, a lack of real-time passenger advisories from bridge teams during deteriorating conditions, and insufficient inspection and maintenance protocols for flooring and grab infrastructure in high-footfall and wet amenity areas. Collectively, these latent deficiencies form a predictable accident pathway that is preventable through targeted investment in passive safety design, accurate environmental monitoring, and proactive passenger safety communication.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction (Regulation 4 and related provisions on passenger vessel design safety, including structural and outfitting standards applicable to passenger accommodation and public spaces)

SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19 and 37 – Emergency instructions, passenger safety familiarisation, and muster drills applicable to passenger orientation and hazard communication)

SOLAS Chapter IV / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Management for the Safe Operation of Ships – requiring operators to identify risks, implement preventive measures, and maintain documented safety management procedures including hazard reporting and corrective action processes
preventable_by: Implement condition-based access restriction protocols for high-risk passenger areas including pool decks, open promenade decks, and wet amenity spaces, using predefined vessel motion thresholds (e.g., wave height exceeding 1.5m or sea state 4 and above) to trigger automatic closure, physical barriers, and real-time passenger advisories broadcast by the bridge team, thereby replacing static passive signage with dynamic operational controls.

Conduct a fleet-wide passive safety infrastructure audit of all passenger accommodation and public spaces, mandating the retrofitting of grab rails at cabin berth egress points, bathroom walls and shower areas, doorway coamings, and pool egress ladders, alongside the installation of motion-sensitive low-level LED lighting in cabins and corridors to provide continuous orientation cues during night-time hours without disrupting rest, in alignment with human factors and inclusive design principles for elderly and mobility-impaired passengers.

Reform the passenger safety orientation programme to include specific, mandatory pre-departure and post-departure briefings on shipboard fall risks, covering cabin night-time hazard awareness, footwear guidance, handrail use, and the significance of vessel motion advisories, supplemented by in-cabin printed safety cards and digital safety channel content; simultaneously, introduce a standardised sea state and wave height cross-validation procedure requiring officers of the watch to reconcile instrument-measured wave data against formally logged sea state classifications and escalate discrepancies to the safety officer, eliminating the persistent under-reporting of actual conditions identified across this incident cluster.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger fell out of bed and landed on their left shoulder. The passenger was taken to the medical centre and was diagnosed with a fractured left clavicle.
A cruise ship passenger fell and resulting in an open wound to the head. The passenger to an ambulance and taken to hospital for a further assessment and treatment.
Cruise ship's passenger lost their balance and fell forward striking their face and wrist on the floor. They sustained a fractured wrist.
Cruise ship's passenger slipped on wet surface and fell, resulting in a dislocated shoulder. No defects found.
Cruise ship's passenger was dancing when another passenger bumped into them resulting in them to fall. The passenger reported a fractured wrist however, no defects were found on the scene.
Passenger ship's guest claimed they lost balance and stumbled against the bed inside the cabin. Vessel was inside sheltered waters, under pilotage - minimal movement.
Cruise ship's passenger was near the pool when they fell and sustained a knee fracture. Yellow cones are placed around the pool as a standard.
An inland passenger vessels passenger fell resulting in a fractured hip. Passenger slipped and fell in the toilet, breaking there hip.
A cruise ships passenger fainted resulting in a fractured ankle.
A cruise ship passenger tripped in the bathroom resulting in a fractured ankle. The cabin lights were switched off but the bathroom lights were on. They were not wearing any footwear at the time.

title: Night Berthing Contact Incidents: Visibility and Coordination Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by a high frequency of low-to-moderate severity contact incidents occurring predominantly during night-time berthing, unberthing, and port manoeuvring operations across a diverse range of vessel types. The overwhelming prevalence of contact events (94 of 99 incidents) underscores a systemic and recurrent operational risk concentrated in the final approach and alongside phases of port operations. Common conditions include degraded night-time visibility reducing accurate spatial judgement of clearances, moderate wind and wave activity compounding vessel controllability, and frequent discrepancies between reported sea state and actual environmental conditions. These discrepancies are themselves a significant concern, suggesting either inadequate real-time environmental assessment or a cultural tendency to underreport adverse conditions, both of which distort risk perception and undermine pre-berthing go/no-go decision-making. The incidents recur because the operational environment — routine port movements treated as low-risk evolutions — systematically discourages structured risk assessment and encourages over-reliance on experience and informal judgement.

Latent human factors are the dominant causal pathway across this cluster. Bridge Resource Management deficiencies, insufficient master-pilot information exchange, and poor inter-vessel and vessel-to-terminal communication are recurring precursors. Hardware and infrastructure factors — including inadequate quay lighting, displaced or uninspected fenders, and fixed structures such as cranes with limited clearance awareness — amplify underlying human vulnerabilities. Environmental conditions, while rarely catastrophic in isolation, consistently act as compounding stressors that narrow the safe operating margin during slow-speed manoeuvres. The absence of structured night berthing protocols, formalised abort-and-return contingency procedures, and systematic use of electronic docking aids leaves crews dependent on unaided visual judgement under conditions that demonstrably degrade it. Targeted systemic intervention — rather than case-by-case remediation — is required to address the latent organisational and procedural gaps that sustain this pattern.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Rules 19, 22, 34: navigational watchkeeping, visibility requirements, voyage planning)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for bridge watchkeeping, pilot-master exchange, and BRM obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (casualty investigation, reporting obligations, and safety management system auditing)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory structured pre-berthing risk assessments for all night-time port movements, incorporating verified real-time environmental data, defined go/no-go criteria based on wind speed, wave height, and visibility thresholds, and explicit abort-and-return contingency procedures; these assessments should be documented and signed off jointly by master and pilot prior to commencement of the manoeuvre.

Standardise and enforce pilot-master information exchange protocols to include berth-specific hazard briefings covering overhead and lateral clearances, known infrastructure features such as crane positions and fender condition, and assignment of dedicated bridge team roles for clearance monitoring during the critical final approach phase, with a formalised challenge culture that empowers any bridge team member to call a halt to the manoeuvre.

Require port authorities and terminal operators to conduct regular night-time lighting audits of berths and approach channels, mandate pre-berthing fender inspection and accountability checks by shore-side personnel with results communicated to the inbound vessel, and promote the adoption of electronic docking aid systems — including laser-based or camera-assisted berthing guidance — for berths with documented histories of contact incidents.

representative_cases.description: Cargo ship was shifting in dock when it made contact with 3 berthed tugs however no damage was reported.
A tanker was berthing when it made contact with an inner fender with the stern of the vessel.  No reported hull damage other then superficial paint damage. No observed damage to fenders or concrete.
A general cargo vessel was going alongside at a berth when the flare of the bow made contact with a shore-side crane.
A container ship under pilotage was preparing to berth when the bridge wing canopy made contact with a shoreside crane.
General cargo hit a jetty during a swing. The vessel sustained a dent on the port quarter 1m below mid deck. No damage has been reported on the jetty.
A general cargo vessel collided with a buoy whilst departing a port, without the pilot on board. This resulted in damage to the buoy which lost its top mark. The vessel did not report any damage.
A platform supply vessel made contact with a pier whilst entering a port. There were no injuries, pollution or damages reported.
A ro-ro vessel made contact with the knuckle of a quayside during manoeuvring. This resulted in a damaged fender on the shore side.
An offshore support vessel was berthing alongside another offshore support vessel in a port when a protruding piece of metal from the other vessel touched a window and broke it.
Cargo vessel was approaching the berth when it made contact with the quay. This resulted in a cracked quay and the vessel's bow quarter has scratches.

title: Manual Handling Injuries Driven by Procedural and Human Failures
description:
This theme encompasses crew injuries arising from manual handling tasks and physically demanding activities in which latent procedural deficiencies — rather than discrete mechanical failures — are the primary enablers of harm. The incidents are characterised by a convergence of inadequate return-to-work protocols, absent or unenforced manual handling risk assessments, and systemic rest hour non-compliance that collectively leave crew members in a state of unrecognised physical vulnerability. Across these events, self-assessment routinely substitutes for formal medical clearance, ergonomic hazards go unaddressed at the task-design level, and time pressure — whether self-imposed through rushing or externally imposed through emergency tasking — overrides whatever informal precautions crew members might otherwise take. The recurring nature of these incidents reflects not individual lapses in isolation, but an organisational tolerance for procedural gaps that renders foreseeable injuries effectively inevitable.

Latent hardware and equipment design deficiencies — such as poorly accessible dipstick locations requiring dangerous reach postures, and below-deck spaces inadequately configured for safe immersion suit donning — create physical conditions in which even routine tasks carry disproportionate injury risk. These are compounded by human factors including cumulative fatigue from chronic rest hour violations, inexperience among first-contract crew members unfamiliar with vessel-specific spatial hazards, and cognitive underestimation of residual physical vulnerability following prior injury. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations, further erode situational awareness and motor precision at precisely the moments when procedural safeguards are most needed. The absence of formal safe work procedures, ergonomic task assessments, and enforced fitness-for-task evaluations means there is no systemic barrier between these converging risk factors and the crew member's body, making injury the predictable outcome of an otherwise manageable set of circumstances.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (equipment accessibility, immersion suit donning procedures, and emergency preparedness drills)
SOLAS Chapter XIII – Verification of Compliance (flag State oversight of rest hour regulations, safe manning, and occupational safety management system enforcement)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code requirements for risk assessment, safe work procedures, and crew health and fitness management)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce a formal return-to-work and fitness-for-task medical clearance protocol requiring independent medical sign-off — not crew self-assessment — before any crew member resumes manual handling duties following a musculoskeletal injury, with the protocol embedded in the vessel's SMS and auditable by the flag State.

Conduct task-level ergonomic and manual handling risk assessments for all routine shipboard physical tasks, including storeroom operations, engine room instrumentation access, and cargo handling, with documented controls such as mechanical aids, buddy systems, or equipment repositioning where awkward postures or heavy loads are identified; review assessments whenever equipment layout changes or an incident occurs.

Establish a robust, actively monitored rest hour compliance system with independent verification by the master and company safety management, including mandatory rest day enforcement and a crew fatigue reporting mechanism, ensuring that cumulative fatigue — particularly for crew nearing contract end or new to the vessel — is identified and mitigated before it contributes to injury during routine or emergency tasks.

representative_cases.description: On board a ro-pax ferry, a crew member was working in a store room when they attempted to move a 20l drum from a shelf. The crew member had previously injured their shoulder but thought it had heeled. Despite using the correct manual lifting technique on the 20l drum, the crewmember pulled their shoulder. This resulted in a torn rotator cuff.

A cruise ship crew member was walking through the restaurant area when they took a miss-step and twisted their right foot resulting in a fracture. The crew member was carrying a light tray of empty sugar bowls and they twisted their foot at an 'abnormal' angle. The crew member had been working for approximately 5 hours before the incident and was only 10 minutes from the end of their shift. They h

A service ship crewmember was leaning over some railings to reach a dip-stick when they injured their ribs. The crewmember said that they were rushing to get the job done. It was not determined if the ribs were bruised or broken.

A search and rescue vessel was responding to an emergency tasking and was underway when a crewmember went down below to don their immersion suit. The weather was calm at the time, but whilst dressing, the crewmember moved in an awkward manner resulting in a dislocated knee. The knee popped back into place shortly after and the crewmember attended hospital where they were given a knee brace and cru

Passenger ship's crew member was assigned to remove the boxes of different kinds of fruit/vegetables from pallets and arrange them. They were doing this for approximately 3 hours. The following day, they stated that there was pain in their shoulder. No defects found, the crew member has been kept more than 72h off work.

A tug crew member was carrying out maintenance on the tow hook on the aft deck. After greasing the hook, the crew member then attempted to move the tow hook from side to side to make sure there was free movement. The crew member strained their lower back resulting in severe pain and time off work.

During routine maintenance of the IRB, a male vessel crew member lost his footing and fell against railings while transferring from the IRB to the vessel. As a result of the impact he was in pain, however, after a few minutes, the pain subsided and he was able to continue with his normal duties until two days later when he had to be replaced onboard. He was diagnosed with a sprained abdomen

A cruise ship crewmember strained their back whilst cleaning. The crewmember was kneeling down and cleaning a drainage area with a towel. Whilst getting down to floor level, they felt a 'twinge' in their lower back. Initially, the level of pain was low, however the crewmember went to their cabin and later in the day, found that the pain had increased significantly. The area where the person was wo

A ro-ro cargo vessel crew member sustained an injury to their finger. Following radio medical advice that the crew member sought medical care in a suitable timeframe the vessel continued on it's passage.
A cruise ship crewmember broke their ankle. Then they had an allergic reaction to anti-inflammatories administered on board and were evacuated ashore for further treatment.

title: Crew Exposure and Fall Hazards During Nighttime Operations
description:
This cluster is characterised by incidents in which crew members are exposed to unmitigated physical hazards — striking submerged objects, falling overboard during manoeuvres, tripping over mooring lines, or being caught in cargo-handling operations — under conditions that materially elevate risk, particularly reduced visibility at night and elevated or confused sea states. The recurring pattern is not isolated human error but a systemic absence of proactive hazard controls: no pre-departure risk assessments, no defined crew positioning protocols during dynamic manoeuvres, inadequate watchkeeping for navigational hazards, and an over-reliance on personal protective equipment such as lifejackets as the primary — and sometimes only — safety barrier. The frequency of crew injury incidents (71 of 79 categorised events) confirms that the human body remains the terminal point of failure in a chain of unaddressed latent risks.

Latent hardware deficiencies, including the absence of forward-looking navigational aids, inadequate grab rails or crew positioning fixtures on RIBs, and uncontrolled mooring line routing at berths, create physical environments in which crew injury is foreseeable and largely preventable. Human factors compound these deficiencies through insufficient pre-operational briefings, gaps in emergency and survival training, and the normalisation of unsafe practices in routine low-consequence operations. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time darkness, wave heights inconsistent with reported sea states, and wind-driven spray — consistently degrade situational awareness and reaction time, yet are rarely integrated into dynamic operational risk assessments. The convergence of these three dimensions across multiple incident types within this cluster indicates a shared organisational failure to apply systematic safety management to routine small-vessel and berth operations where the consequences of a single uncontrolled event can rapidly become life-threatening.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for lifejackets, MOB equipment, drills, and muster procedures)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (watchkeeping standards, navigational equipment, voyage planning, and hazard detection obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Safe Manning, Safety Management Systems, risk assessment, and emergency preparedness obligations
preventable_by: Mandate formal pre-departure operational risk assessments for all small-vessel and night-time passages, explicitly addressing crew positioning during manoeuvres, navigational hazard detection capability, and sea state verification against multiple sources, with a documented go/no-go decision threshold based on vessel-specific exposure limits.

Implement engineering controls to eliminate foreseeable deck hazards, including the installation of adequate grab rails and crew restraint points on RIBs and work vessels, defined and physically segregated mooring line routing at berths to prevent crew transit conflicts, and the fitting of forward-looking sonar or equivalent collision-avoidance aids on vessels operating in object-risk environments at night.

Establish and regularly exercise mandatory crew safety briefings prior to dynamic operations — including helm manoeuvres, cargo transfers, and night departures — covering safe positioning, MOB procedures, lifejacket donning compliance, and vessel re-boarding protocols, with competency verified through recorded drills rather than assumed from certification alone.

representative_cases.description: The vessel hit an unknown object causing it to capsize with crewmembers ended up in the water for twenty minutes. Two of the crewmembers wore lifejackets and were holding onto the vessel and the other crewmember wasn't holding onto the vessel. The persons and equipment were recovered by the lifeboats and three other privately owned vessels. The casualties were transported to a harbour and neither

A passenger rib was on passage, as the helm was turning the rib, it hooked and the crew member fell overboard. The helm initiated the man overboard procedure and recovered the crew member within thirty seconds, the crew member's automatic lifejacket had inflated upon immersion. There were no injuries to the passengers onboard or the crew member.

Tug's crew member was transiting the normal access/egress arrangement at berth when they lost their footing whilst manoeuvring past the mooring line and fell into the water. The crew member was wearing a lifejacket which inflated and they recovered quickly via the quayside ladder. No injuries sustained.

Upon disembarkation from a cargo vessel to a service vessel, the bosun was caught by the luggage net resulting in a minor injury. Once 3 off-signers safely stepped down onto the boat the luggage was lowered. Suitcases were lowered in a net using the ship’s provision crane. While the off-signing Master and Bosun tried to extract the last 2 suitcases(total 4) from the net, the Bosun was caught and d

A sailing vessel crewmember fell overboard when they were trying to bring in the sails. They were not wearing a lifejacket. The other crewmember on board tried to recover them from the water but they were unable to do so. They called for assistance and the person was recovered from the water several hours later and declared deceased.

A rowing boat crew member who was taking part in a race became unconscious and fell into the water. They were recovered by the safety boat and then transferred to the emergency services, who despite their best efforts, were unable to revive the crew member. The crew member was taken to hospital and  pronounced deceased. It is unclear if they were breathing before they entered the water.

Customer fell overboard under sail downwind.  Was briefly entangled in a rope in the water, but once free was recovered with no apparent injury, cold shock or water inhalation.. Was taken ashore and checked over by the ambulance who took them to hospital to check their lungs were ok.  Cleared by a consultant and returned to the vessel the same evening.

A sailing vessel skipper was discovered missing when their friend came by on their own vessel as they were planned to depart together to the next port. The friend raised the alarm and search and rescue assets deployed and discovered the missing skipper on a sand bank. The skipper was deceased. It was unconfirmed how and when the skipper entered the water.

A sail training vessel was alongside, crew had been ashore and were making their way back onboard. A crew member had boarded then stopped and stepped backwards which caused the crew member behind to slip and fall overboard. A crew member on the pontoon was able to grab hold of them and asked crew onboard to get assistance. A horseshoe was deployed to aid flotation and then crew used a helicopter s

A sailing yacht crew member was attempting moor their vessel when they fell into the water. They managed to make it into shore on their own. An ambulance was requested to check the crew member over however there were no health concerns.

title: Non-Compliant Heaving Line Weights in Night Mooring Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated use of non-compliant, improvised, or dangerously overweight heaving line monkey fists during mooring and unmooring operations, with a pronounced prevalence during night-time berthing evolutions. Across the incident cluster, weights ranging from marginally overweight (570g) to catastrophically dangerous (a 3.25-tonne shackle) were deployed in place of approved equipment, exposing linesmen, tug crew, and dock workers to severe blunt-force projectile strike hazards. The persistence of this pattern across vessel types — including tankers, offshore supply vessels, general cargo ships, and hopper dredgers — indicates that this is not an isolated behavioural failure but a systemic deficiency in equipment governance, pre-operational inspection culture, and crew awareness of internationally recognised mooring safety standards. Environmental and operational pressures, including adverse weather, high winds, and time pressure during port arrivals, appear to be normalising the use of heavier-than-approved weights to achieve throwing distance, further embedding non-compliant practices within routine mooring evolutions.

From a latent causation perspective, the incidents share a convergence of hardware non-compliance, human factors, and environmental conditions that together elevate risk to critical levels. On the hardware side, the absence of robust equipment audit regimes allows non-standard or improvised monkey fists — including cement-filled, metal-embedded, and rubber weights — to remain in active service undetected. Human factors include normalisation of unsafe practice, inadequate training on approved heaving line specifications, supervisory complacency, and task familiarity breeding disregard for procedural controls. Night-time conditions consistently amplify consequences by reducing the ability of receiving personnel to visually track incoming line trajectories and take evasive action. The combination of these factors reflects a systemic failure within vessel Safety Management Systems to translate known industry guidance — including that published by OCIMF, IMCA, CHIRP, and the UK P&I Club — into enforceable, verified deck-level compliance, underscoring the need for structural corrective action at both the operator and fleet management level.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety equipment standards and operational readiness)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe operational practices, risk assessment, and bridge/deck resource management during port approaches)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for SMS implementation, equipment control, hazard identification, and crew training)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory pre-berthing heaving line equipment inspections with documented weight verification against the ~500g industry-recommended maximum, using calibrated scales carried aboard all mooring stations, with non-compliant lines quarantined and logged before any line-handling evolution commences.

Develop and enforce fleet-wide targeted mooring safety training modules — informed by OCIMF, IMCA, and CHIRP guidance — that explicitly address heaving line weight regulations, the injury and fatality risk profile of non-compliant weights, and the prohibition on improvised monkey fist substitutes, with competency assessments recorded in crew training records.

Establish a formal mooring equipment governance framework within the vessel SMS that mandates periodic fleet-wide equipment audits by safety officers or port state inspectors, requires procurement controls ensuring only type-approved heaving line assemblies are supplied aboard vessels, and introduces a mandatory near-miss reporting and review cycle for heaving line incidents to enable systemic trend analysis across the fleet.

representative_cases.description: Weighted heaving line was thrown from the aft mooring station onto the general cargo. It was removed by the linesman and taken apart. No damages reported.
Use of dangerously weighted heaving line
General cargo vessel's crew used a weighted heaving line.
A tanker used a dangerously weighted heaving line. The non-compliant heaving line weighed eight hundred and sixty grams.
An offshore supply ship used a dangerously weighted heaving line. Heaving line thrown during berthing.
An LNG tanker used a dangerously weighted heaving line. The weighted heaving line was used by the tanker to a tug. The weighted heaving line was retained, logged and reported.
Dangerously weighted heaving line
Container ship's crew threw down a monkey's fist with protruding metal during sailing from dock.
The crew of a tanker used an unsafely weighted heaving line. The weight was 570g.
Chemical tanker used a weighted heaving line resulting in no damages.

title: Snap-Back and Entrapment Hazards in Deck Line Handling
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries arising from stored-energy release events, line entrapment, and uncontrolled tension during mooring, trawling, and berthing operations. The incidents share a common failure pattern: personnel are positioned within the line-of-fire or snap-back zone of tensioned lines or wires, in the absence of pre-task hazard controls, exclusion zones, or structured communication protocols between vessel crews. The recurrence of these incidents is driven by normalisation of high-risk manual handling practices, an absence of stop-work authority or tension-check procedures when obstructions are detected, and a systemic failure to formalise task preparation — including deck clearance, equipment stowage, and dynamic risk assessment — as mandatory prerequisites before commencing mooring or line-transfer operations. Night-time conditions and adverse sea states appear as consistent aggravating factors across multiple incidents, yet operational tempo is not being reduced to reflect the elevated risk.

At the latent level, hardware deficiencies — including unsecured deck equipment, unguarded fairleads and rollers, and wire management arrangements that lack mechanical energy-absorption features — create the physical conditions for snap-back and entrapment. Human factors including positional error, loss of situational awareness at critical moments, and inadequate inter-vessel coordination compound these hardware deficiencies, particularly when crew turn away from the direction of rope travel or when tanker winch operators continue heaving without deck-level confirmation. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time reduced visibility and wind- and wave-induced vessel movement, elevate both wire tension unpredictability and the cognitive demand on personnel already operating without structured procedural guidance. The cluster as a whole reflects an organisational gap: the absence of Safety Management System procedures that mandate formal pre-task briefings, defined exclusion zones, and cross-vessel communication protocols as non-negotiable controls for all tensioned line operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe working practices and crew safety procedures during deck operations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (operational safety management, including bridge and deck coordination and adverse weather risk assessment)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for SMS procedures, risk assessment, and crew training for high-risk deck tasks)
preventable_by: Mandate a formal pre-task deck preparation checklist for all mooring and line-transfer operations, requiring documented confirmation that the working area is clear of unsecured equipment, snap-back zones are marked and enforced, and all involved crew — including those on adjacent vessels — have confirmed readiness before tension is applied to any line or wire.

Establish and enforce a cross-vessel stop-work communication protocol for mooring and towing operations, requiring an agreed signal or radio call between tug/trawler deck crew and winch operators on the receiving or towing vessel; any crewmember detecting a snag, obstruction, or unsafe condition must have the authority and procedure to immediately halt hauling operations until the hazard is resolved.

Introduce mandatory night-time and adverse-weather operational controls for all tensioned deck work, including reduced haul rates, supplementary task lighting at mooring and trawl wire stations, enhanced PPE requirements (including cut-resistant gloves and face protection near wires), and a requirement that a dedicated safety observer with no other tasking is posted during active line-handling in conditions of reduced visibility or significant vessel movement.

representative_cases.description: A tug vessel was transferring hoses and fenders to a tanker. The tug was alongside and passed the tail end of a fender rope up to the tanker crew which they made fast to their winch. They was still approximately 15 of the fender rope on the tug's deck including the spliced eye. As the tanker crew heaved on the tail/light pennant, a boat hook got caught on the rope spliced eye. The other end of the

A trawler vessel's crew member was struck in the face by a wire and began to feel dizzy, vomited and stated that their legs were feeling numb. The vessel returned to port and the crewmember's family drove them to hospital.

A cargo ship crewmember sustained a crush injury to their fingers during mooring operations. The mooring lines required adjustment due to wind and the crewmember was working on the aft mooring stations, slacking a stern line by hand. Whilst slacking the vessel came off the berth and the line came under tension trapping the crewmembers hand between the line and the roller. There were no other injur

A service vessel crew member caught their arm in a loose line that became taut resulting in superficial injuries. Crew moored/berthed towed barge to moorings, one crewmember attended to loose rope and was caught as the rope became taut.

A passenger vessel was proceeding towards port, the crew were prepping for the arrival with one crew member setting up the aft mooring lines. The crew member injured their hands when they tried to stop one of the lines being lost through the fairlead. Once ashore the crew member attended the local hospital where a sprain injury to the hands was confirmed. The mooring line was retrieved by a local

A general cargo vessel was departing the berth when a spring line became entangled on the lip of a fender, causing it to part as the vessel crew tried to heave it in before realising it was stuck. There were no injuries.

Chemical tanker was shifting between mooring buoys when a crew member sustained an injury to their finger. Whilst mooring alongside on the aft mooring station the crew member suffered a fractured finger by the mooring ropes that jumped from the bollard due to tension. First aid was applied and they were transferred for further medical assistance.

Cruise ships crew member sustained an injury during mooring operation. 2 spring lines were sent to shore bollards as the vessel adjusted its final position. Throughout the process both springs remained slack and worked evenly. When the vessel reached the final position, one of the springs (under tension) parted. Immediate medical response was initiated. The crew member sustained a minor injury.

Passenger ship's headline was made fast on capstan when the rope slackened off. This resulted in the rope jumping off under tension and striking a crew member in the abdomen area. No other injuries/damages were reported.

Incident Summary Date of incident: 10.12.2024 Location: Carradale Fish Farm Activity: Fish farm operations, lifting a tarp rope with a capstan Events: lifting rope overrode the capstan drum, contacting operators hand Injury: Soft tissue – hand (no fracture)  The capstan drum became overloaded during operation causing the rope to ride up and over the top of the drum

title: Entanglement and Struck-By Hazards During Deck Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by crew members sustaining serious injuries as a result of entanglement with, or being struck by, running lines, ropes, chains, and anchor equipment during routine and competitive maritime operations. Across all incidents, the common thread is the physical proximity of personnel to dynamic load-bearing or rapidly moving deck equipment in the absence of enforced exclusion zones, formal safe systems of work, or adequate positional discipline. These incidents recur because the hazard is inherently normalised within small-scale fishing, recreational racing, and commercial vessel operations — tasks such as shooting pots, anchoring, net hauling, and sail manoeuvring are performed repeatedly without formal pre-task risk assessment, fostering a culture where proximity to danger becomes routine until a catastrophic event occurs. The consequences range from finger amputations and fractures to near-fatal drowning, underscoring the severity latent within what crews may perceive as low-risk, familiar activities.

The latent hardware dimension includes the absence of rope management devices such as chutes, guards, lead blocks, and physical barriers that would mechanically prevent lines from contacting crew. Lifejacket design, as evidenced in the anchor entanglement case, can itself introduce snagging hazards when worn in proximity to moving equipment, indicating that PPE specification and operational context must be jointly evaluated. Human factors are central: complacency, inadequate hazard awareness, and the absence of pre-task safety briefings create conditions where positional risk is neither identified nor communicated. The competitive racing environment introduces additional pressure that can override safety-conscious behaviour. Environmental conditions — principally night-time operations with reduced deck visibility and moderate sea states increasing rope tension and vessel motion — consistently act as compounding factors that amplify the consequences of procedural deficiencies. Collectively, these incidents reflect an organisational and cultural failure to implement and enforce formalised safe working practices at the point of task execution.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for emergency equipment, man-overboard procedures, and life-saving appliance suitability)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe manning, lookout, and operational safety duties including deck watchkeeping and situational awareness)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (flag State obligations regarding safety management systems, inspections, and investigation of near-miss and injury events)
preventable_by: Mandate formal written Safe Systems of Work (SSOW) for all high-risk deck operations — including anchoring, pot shooting, net hauling, and sail manoeuvring — that define exclusion zones, crew stand-off distances, and required PPE compatible with the specific equipment in use, with compliance verified during port State and flag State inspections.

Require installation and maintenance of physical rope and line management hardware (rope chutes, fairleads, guards, and running line containment systems) on all vessels where crew regularly operate in proximity to dynamic loads, ensuring that engineering controls are prioritised over reliance on behavioural compliance alone.

Implement mandatory pre-task safety briefings and night operations risk assessments, to include explicit identification of entanglement hazards, confirmation of adequate deck lighting levels, and rehearsal of man-overboard and rapid recovery procedures, supported by vessel operator training programmes aligned with the ISM Code and applicable flag State fishing vessel safety regulations.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel crewmember got caught by the anchor and went overboard. The casualty was wearing a lifejacket and, as the anchor deployed, it caught on the lifejacket and pulled them overboard The casualty was recovered by the crew and then transferred by air to hospital as a precaution. The casualty was discharged after half a day of observation and returned to fishing two days later.

A potting fishing vessel crewmember became entangled in the rope of a lobster pot whilst shooting and was dragged over board. They were recovered from the water with a twisted leg, which was broken. They were initially unresponsive and CPR was performed by the crew on board. The crewmember regained consciousness and was able to breathe on their own. They were extracted by helicopter and taken to t

Two vessels were taking part in a race at the time sailing around a buoy, a rope became looped around one of the vessel's crew member's neck and was pulled into the water. Another crew member managed to get them out safely from the water and they were taken via ambulance to the hospital

A chemical tanker was engaged in a berthing operation when one of the deck crew left hand when was trapped between a  rope and roller, amputating the finger tips. First aid was administered on scene to the crew member and then taken to hospital for further assessment and treatment.

A fishing vessel was proceeding to haul its nets, a crew member was working on port side and should have had the back straps of port door hooked but these were unhooked. As the net was hauled a chain caught around the port quarter and tightened trapping the crew members hand that was placed on the chain. The haul was stopped, first aid and pain relief was administered to the crew member. The vesse

A fishing vessel crewmember injured their hand when working in the hauler area. They grabbed onto a rope that was passing through the hauler and their hand was caught between the rope and the hauler. They were signed off the vessel due to their injuries.

Following entrapment of foot in mooring rope, casualty suffered an Open Fracture with a Major Bleed. A makeshift tourniquet was applied to reduce blood loss. Casualty was extracted by Coastguard Rescue Helicopter with the assistance of RNLI All-Weather Lifeboat.

A fishing vessel crew member sustained an injury while shooting gear. They caught their left hand between the chain and wire. Evacuation by a helicopter was impossible due to weather conditions therefore, the individual was taken to the nearest port to seek further medical assistance.

A passenger vessel was alongside an anchored cruise ship, a crew member on the bow caught the heaving line from the cruise ship and went to secure it to a pre-set mark on the rope. As they were securing the line, they noticed there was not enough slack to secure the line to the correct mark. They pulled on the line at the bollard as it gained tension to get some slack however they caught their han

A fishing vessel crewmember was removing a damaged net from a drum when a piece of net struck their finger. The vessel had come into harbour to repair the net rather than open sea, but the crewmember did not look to see if their was a broken piece of net when they lowered the drum. They have been signed off work for four weeks.

title: Propeller Fouling and Gear Entanglement During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by the loss of propulsion in small commercial fishing, sailing, and survey vessels resulting from propeller or thruster fouling on fishing gear, ropes, nets, and surface debris, predominantly occurring during night-time operations. The incidents cluster around inshore and coastal waters with high concentrations of static and active fishing gear, where the combination of reduced ambient lighting and moderate sea states substantially diminishes the crew's ability to visually detect and avoid surface hazards. The overwhelming dependency on Search and Rescue services and peer-vessel towing across these incidents indicates a systemic failure of onboard self-recovery capability, elevating routine operational hazards into SAR-demanding events. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel types and geographic contexts confirms this is not an isolated equipment failure but a persistent systemic risk rooted in operational norms, environmental conditions, and inadequate preventive controls.

From a latent causation perspective, hardware deficiencies play a significant role, specifically the widespread absence of rope-cutter devices, shaft seals, and propeller protection systems that could mitigate or prevent fouling escalation. Human factors are equally prominent: watchkeeping standards during night passages in known gear-dense areas are demonstrably insufficient, and procedural controls governing net and gear management relative to propeller exposure during hauling operations are frequently absent or unenforced. Solo crewing and fleet size constraints further reduce the capacity for effective visual lookout. Environmentally, night lighting conditions are the primary aggravating factor across nearly all incidents, reducing marker buoy detectability and increasing reaction distances beyond practical avoidance thresholds. A systemic gap also exists in pre-passage coordination between mobile vessel operators and static gear fishers, and in the availability of spatial data on gear deployment areas, both of which, if addressed, could substantially reduce encounter frequency.
