solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, and 34: navigational watchkeeping, lookout standards, and voyage planning obligations in hazard-dense waters)

SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Regulation 26-35: propulsion machinery reliability and maintenance obligations to prevent total loss of propulsion)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (Obligations to maintain distress and safety communication readiness given the high SAR dependency demonstrated across this incident cluster)
preventable_by: Mandate the fitment of rope-cutter devices and shaft-protection systems on all small commercial fishing and survey vessels as a condition of vessel certification, and require documented evidence of their maintenance as part of annual survey inspections, thereby reducing the frequency with which fouling events escalate to total propulsion loss.

Require operators of vessels regularly transiting inshore fishing grounds to conduct structured pre-passage planning that incorporates up-to-date static gear position data, sourced through formalised coordination with local fishing associations or national gear-registration databases, and to record this planning in the vessel's voyage log as a mandatory element of safe watchkeeping.

Introduce sector-specific night watchkeeping competency standards for small commercial fishing and survey vessel crews operating in gear-dense coastal waters, including training in low-light hazard detection techniques, emergency propulsion-loss drills, and onboard self-recovery procedures for entanglement events, reducing systemic over-reliance on SAR intervention for recoverable mechanical incidents.

representative_cases.description: A sailing vessel fouled there propeller resulting in search and rescue towing them to safety.
A fishing vessel sustained a fouled propeller which resulted in gearbox damage and the vessel was unable to proceed. The local lifeboat arranged a tow and brought it back to port.
A survey vessel fouled its propeller on a lobster pot and called for assistance. A lifeboat attended and towed it back to port. There were no injuries, pollution or damages.
Fishing vessel required towage after fouling their propeller. 2 persons on board - no injuries.
Fishing vessel fouled its propeller on their net and were towed by another fishing vessel. There was no reported injuries or damage.
A fishing vessel fouled its propeller while hauling nets. A search and rescue vessel towed the casualty vessel back to harbour.
Fishing vessel became stuck on a potting line and was freed by lifeboat and escorted back to the marina. There was no damage to the vessel or injuries sustained.
A sailing vessel lost propulsion due to propeller wrap losing engine power, vessel anchored using emergency anchor. No injuries and no further damage to vessel.
A recreational sailing vessel fouled its propeller on a lobster pot, a fishing vessel assisted the sailing vessel to safety.
A fishing became entangled in creel lines outside harbour. The vessel was towed back into harbour by a search and rescue vessel.

title: Blackout and Propulsion Loss During Critical Manoeuvring
description:
This theme is characterised by sudden and total loss of electrical power — and consequent loss of propulsion and thruster control — occurring during the most operationally vulnerable phases of a voyage, namely berthing, unberthing, and confined-water manoeuvring. The incidents share a consistent failure pattern: a latent deficiency within the main electrical generation or power management system (including main switchboard failures, PTO unit faults, generator changeover vulnerabilities, and main engine mechanical faults) is not detected during pre-arrival or pre-departure machinery checks, and then manifests catastrophically at the precise moment when full propulsion authority is most critical. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel types — ro-ro cargo, crude oil tanker, ro-pax, cruise vessel, and offshore supply vessel — indicates that this is not an isolated equipment problem but a systemic issue rooted in inadequate pre-manoeuvre readiness verification and insufficient redundancy safeguards within power management architectures.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant contributory factor, encompassing deferred or insufficient maintenance of main engines, generator systems, PTO units, and power management switchgear, all of which exhibit no overt failure signal until placed under operational load during a critical manoeuvre. These hardware vulnerabilities are compounded by procedural gaps: pre-arrival and pre-departure checklists do not consistently mandate the verification of emergency power switchover readiness, automatic blackout recovery systems, or the integrity of generator changeover sequences under load. Human factors, while comparatively limited in frequency, manifest in the premature decision to re-attempt berthing or continue manoeuvring without confirmed root cause identification following a blackout — a 'return-to-service' gap that materially elevates the risk of a second, potentially more consequential, failure. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations, consistently amplify the consequences of these events by reducing situational awareness, constraining navigational options, and increasing crew cognitive load at the moment of failure.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Parts D and E — machinery and electrical plant reliability, redundancy, and emergency power requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (relevant to fire incidents arising from electrical faults and black smoke events associated with engine failures)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34 — safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including adequacy of pre-passage/pre-manoeuvre planning and contingency measures)
preventable_by: Mandate a structured Pre-Manoeuvre Electrical Readiness Protocol requiring engineering officers to verify, prior to entering any constrained or pilotage water, that all standby generators are on auto-start and tested under load, emergency switchover systems are fully functional, power management system (PMS) settings are confirmed for manoeuvring mode, and PTO changeover sequences have been completed and verified — with completion recorded in the engine room log and bridge notified before manoeuvring commences.

Establish formal 'Return-to-Service' criteria following any in-port or underway blackout event, requiring that a documented root cause investigation is completed, the causative fault is confirmed as rectified, and an independent engineering sign-off is obtained before a subsequent manoeuvring attempt is authorised — explicitly prohibiting re-attempt on the basis of power restoration alone without root cause identification.

Require all vessels to conduct regular blackout recovery drills specifically simulating loss of power during a berthing or manoeuvring scenario, incorporating testing of automatic blackout prevention systems, emergency generator start sequences, and bridge-engine room coordination procedures, with drill outcomes reviewed against manufacturer specifications for PMS interlock settings and generator changeover safeguards to identify and rectify latent configuration deficiencies.

representative_cases.description: A ro-ro cargo vessel was swinging to starboard prior to berthing. Approximately half way through the swing the vessel experienced a blackout with loss of engine and thrusters. The anchor was lowered to the waterline as a precaution but not used. Within a minute the engine and thrusters were restarted and the vessel manoeuvred into the harbour, to assess the situation. It was decided the vessel was

Crude oil tanker had a pilot on board as it was about to berth. The vessels alarm started to sound on the bridge followed by a black smoke emitting from the funnel. Pilot was informed that the vessel suffered a failure in the main engine which was shortly followed by a full blackout. 4 tugs assisted in order to position the vessel. The vessel was monitored throughout and power to the winches was r

The vessel suffered a loss of electrical power and propulsion whilst changing over from the shaft to diesel generators. This was as result of a failure of PTO which then tripped out generators. No assistance was required for the vessel. Ro-pax vessel whilst on passage they changing over from shaft to diesel generators they lost propulsion and electrical power

A cruise lost electrical power, full power restored, no damage or injuries. Vessel suffered a blackout and emergency generator started, Broadcast to all guest and crew to update them on the situation and for the reduced lightning, also contacted FOC and DPA. Full power restored and restarting propulsion and thrusters.

As offshore supply vessel was manoeuvring to depart the vessel experienced a blackout effecting the main engine, one bow and one stern thruster leading to the vessel grounding, vessel came free under its own steam. No damage reported from the vessel and engine fault resolved.

The vessel was in transit and the generator shut down on the alarm and could not be restarted. The backup system of the alternator system failed to engage to provide power, they sent a DSC informing the local coastguard station who contacted the vessel company DPA who arranged for a towing vessel to be sent in the interim, a tug was tasked to attend, and provide initial tow.

A windfarm support vessel experienced propulsion failure. They called for assistance and a fishing vessel proceeded and took them under tow to port. The problem was caused by a control fuse linking the electrical supply from the generators to the distribution board which blew.

An offshore support vessel was on passage when a malfunction in one of the 4 auxiliary engines caused a piston/cam-shaft to be ejected out of one of the crank case doors along with fuel oil. This engine was in use as the sole power generator at the time which meant there was a blackout resulting in the loss of power for the main propulsion thrusters. The emergency generator started as necessary an

A cruise ship was approaching its berth when it lost PODs power for approximately two minutes. The port anchor was dropped to slow the vessel's spee. Both PODs were restored and the turn was completed. There was no damage to the vessel.
A container ship had two engine blackouts whilst in a lock.

title: Fishing Vessel Propulsion Failures Due to Deferred Maintenance
description:
This theme is characterised by recurrent loss of propulsion events aboard small commercial and charter fishing vessels, consistently resulting in reliance on external tow assistance from SAR services, lifeboats, or nearby vessels. The incidents share a common operational profile: night-time underway operations, absence of redundant propulsion, no injuries or structural damage, and a root cause traceable to deferred or inadequate engine maintenance. Specific failure modes identified across the cluster include propeller fouling, piston failure, fan belt failure, exhaust manifold compromise, fuel system faults, and general mechanical wear — all of which are detectable and addressable through structured planned maintenance systems (PMS). The disproportionate representation of fishing vessels in machinery failure statistics reflects systemic issues within the sector, including aging fleets, high vessel utilisation rates, limited engineering oversight, and a reactive rather than preventive maintenance culture driven by economic pressures and resource constraints.

Latent hardware factors include aging and worn machinery components that have exceeded service intervals without replacement or inspection, absence of redundant propulsion systems, and inadequate spare parts inventories onboard. Human factors are equally significant: crew engineering competency gaps reduce the likelihood of at-sea fault diagnosis or repair, pre-departure checklists are frequently incomplete or absent, and go/no-go decision-making fails to account for the compounded risk of operating in adverse weather or night conditions with mechanically marginal vessels. Environmental conditions — including night operations, elevated wind speeds, rough sea states, and proximity to shipping lanes or port approaches — do not cause these failures but substantially elevate the risk profile once propulsion is lost, transforming manageable breakdowns into potential life-safety emergencies. The consistent absence of injury across the cluster reflects adequate distress communication and peer-vessel coordination, but this outcome cannot be relied upon as a systemic safeguard.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E – Additional Requirements for Machinery, Boilers and Electrical Installations)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness and distress response)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, operational limits, and safe manning requirements)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce documented Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS) for all commercial fishing vessels, with flag state or port state inspection of maintenance logs as part of periodic survey requirements, with particular focus on propulsion-critical components such as drive belts, fuel systems, exhaust manifolds, and lubrication systems.

Require completion of a standardised pre-departure engine and propulsion checklist before each voyage, with minimum mandatory spare parts inventories (including belts, filters, and fuel system components) carried onboard, and make compliance a condition of vessel operating licences.

Introduce mandatory basic engineering competency training for fishing vessel crew, enabling at-sea diagnosis and minor repairs to common propulsion failure modes, alongside targeted fleet inspection campaigns in regions or vessel categories with recurring machinery failure incident rates.

representative_cases.description: Fishing vessel had issues with its propeller so the crew decided to stop the engine in order to prevent damage. They requested a tow and no further damages have been reported.
Fishing vessel's engine failed resulting in a tow. There was no reported damage or injuries.
Fishing vessel suffered an engine failure. The crew requested assistance to get back to shore. No further damages/injuries reported.
Fishing vessel machinery failure. Vessel was towed to port.
A fishing vessel experienced an engine failure which resulted in a tow to harbour from another vessel. There was no imminent danger throughout and no damage or injuries reported.
A fishing vessel had fuel problems resulting in being towed back in to harbour by another fishing vessel, search and rescue vessel launched to assist if needed.
Fishing vessel was returning to port when it lost its propulsion power. The crew anchored the vessel safely and they requested a tow.
A fishing vessel suffered an engine failure and required towage. They suspect exhaust manifold on main engine, lost oil out of engine into header tank - engine shut itself.
Charter fishing broke down and requested a tow. No injuries/further damages reported.
Beam trawler's engine failed resulting in a tow from another fishing vessel. There was no reported damage or injuries and there was no imminent damage.

title: Propulsion Failure in Adverse Conditions, Single-Engine Vessels
description:
This theme is characterised by the catastrophic convergence of mechanical propulsion failure and hazardous operating conditions aboard recreational and small commercial vessels. The majority of incidents (53 machinery-related) involve single-engine vessels that lose all motive power, leaving them beam-on to wind and sea, adrift in shipping lanes, or subject to progressive flooding risk. These incidents recur because operators routinely accept the latent vulnerability of single-engine configurations without adequate compensatory measures — no redundant propulsion, no functioning sailing backup, and no credible contingency plan. Compounding factors such as night-time departure, solo operation, and unseaworthy vessel condition transform what might be a manageable mechanical deficiency into a life-threatening emergency requiring external search and rescue intervention. The decision to operate in deteriorating weather or at night without verified propulsion reliability is a consistent antecedent across this cluster.

The latent hardware failures at the root of these incidents — undetected fuel contamination, deferred maintenance, worn or neglected engine components, and overall vessel disrepair — reflect a systemic failure of pre-departure safety culture rather than isolated mechanical misfortune. Human factors including overconfidence, normalisation of deferred maintenance, and inadequate voyage planning are pervasive, with operators frequently departing without verifying that primary or backup systems are serviceable. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time sea states of Sea State 3–4, moderate winds, cold temperatures, and swell, do not cause these incidents but act as force multipliers that compress the time available for self-rescue and dramatically increase the severity of crew harm, as evidenced by cases of hypothermia and beam-on rolling risk. The consistent absence of adequate anchoring capability, working sailing rigs, or secondary propulsion means that once the engine fails, the vessel and crew are entirely dependent on external SAR resources, creating preventable risk to both survivors and rescuers.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (machinery maintenance and propulsion reliability requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (distress signalling, lifejackets, and survival equipment for loss-of-propulsion emergencies)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, weather assessment, and safe manning obligations including contingency preparedness)
preventable_by: Mandate documented pre-departure engine checks and periodic maintenance records for all registered recreational vessels operating beyond sheltered waters, with enforceable minimum standards for fuel system integrity, cooling systems, and drive components, enforced through targeted compliance audits at departure points.

Require single-engine vessels operating at night or in offshore or exposed waters to carry a functional secondary means of propulsion (such as an auxiliary outboard, a serviceable sailing rig, or a verified anchor system appropriate to local depths), and include verification of these systems as part of any mandatory safety briefing or vessel registration renewal process.

Implement mandatory passage planning and weather check requirements for night-time recreational departures, including a formal go/no-go decision framework that accounts for forecast sea state, wind strength, crew numbers, vessel propulsion configuration, and available SAR coverage, supported by accessible public guidance and enforced through marina operator and harbour authority briefing obligations.

representative_cases.description: A motor vessel suffered an engine failure and requested towage. A search and rescue vessel was tasked to tow the vessel back as there was concern was that the vessel was sitting beam on and rolling around. The two crew onboard were wearing lifejackets.

A recreational motor vessel experienced engine failure whilst fishing. The single crew member on board called for help and a lifeboat was dispatched to tow them back to port. There were no injuries, pollution or damage.

A sailing vessel with an auxiliary engine experienced propulsion failure. The crew could not raise the sail due to the fact that the vessel was in poor condition. Another vessel evacuated the two crew on board and brought them ashore. The crew of that vessel attempted to set up a tow but due to the condition of the casualty vessel, it was not successful. The vessel was abandoned and left to drift.

Recreational motor-cruiser experienced engine failure and was drifting in a shipping lane. The vessel crew issued a distress signal. Once assistance arrived they realised one of the crewmembers was in need of medical attention as they had cold hands, warm face and were feeling seasick. The crewmember was fully alert the entire time and shivering. They brought the crewmember ashore for further trea

A recreational motor vessel experienced engine failure possibly through debris in the fuel. The vessel was unable to anchor as it required the engine to bring the anchor in but the vessel was in deep water and able to drift. A search and rescue (SAR) was tasked to the stricken vessel. One SAR crew member went onboard to assist the lone skipper and set up a tow, the vessel was then towed to a harbo

A fishing vessel had an engine failure, another fishing vessel started to tow the casualty vessel but was struggling to make any headway. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to tow the fishing vessel back to shore.

A fishing vessel with two crew onboard experienced an engine failure, the vessel was drifting towards the a breakwater entrance until the crew anchored. As there were no vessels in the area to assist and location of the fishing vessel, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel towed the fishing vessel to shore. No reported crew injuries or visual vessel damage.

A commercial sight seeing motor boat experienced propulsion failure whilst out on an excursion. They dropped anchor and called for help using the VHF. Two search and rescue vessels launched. One extracted the passengers and brought them ashore whilst the other one set up a tow and brought the vessel back to harbour. There were no injuries, pollutions or damages.

A recreational fishing vessel experienced an engine failure at a harbour entrance. The two crew onboard attempted to restart the engine but were unsuccessful. The vessel was anchored and the crew waited for a search and rescue (SAR) vessel to tow the stricken vessel into harbour. No reported injuries or vessel damage.

A sailing yacht was on passage when it had an engine failure and later becalmed due to the light winds. A PANPAN was broadcasted however due to heavy traffic in the area, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to tow the yacht to shore. No reported damage.

title: Flooding and Structural Failures in Small Vessel Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by uncontrolled water ingress events aboard small fishing, recreational, and hire vessels, frequently occurring during night-time operations and in sea conditions insufficient to explain the severity of outcome. The dominant pattern is one of latent structural or mechanical vulnerability — failed hull fittings, compromised shaft seals, deteriorated through-hull components, or unchecked bilge systems — that goes undetected until flooding is advanced and self-recovery is no longer possible. Night operations consistently emerge as a risk amplifier, delaying crew detection of ingress and slowing emergency response. Cascade failure sequences are recurrent: flooding triggers secondary system loss (propulsion, steering), which compounds the emergency and generates dependency on external SAR intervention. The clustering of incidents across vessel types — from commercial trawlers to racing yachts and hire motorboats — indicates a systemic, sector-wide deficit in pre-voyage inspection rigour, damage control preparedness, and onboard flood detection capability, rather than isolated operator error.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the primary enabling condition across this cluster. Ageing or poorly maintained hull integrity components — sea cocks, stern gear seals, through-hull fittings — represent chronic vulnerabilities that pre-departure visual inspections alone are unlikely to identify without structured, documented regimes. Onboard pumping capacity is repeatedly found to be inadequate relative to actual flooding risk, requiring external pump deployment from lifeboats or helicopters to prevent sinking. Human factors compound these hardware deficiencies: solo operation, reduced crew watchkeeping capacity during night passages, and in competitive contexts, a cultural tendency to continue despite early warning signs, all reduce the probability of timely detection and effective response. Environmental conditions, while rarely the primary cause, consistently serve as secondary risk amplifiers — darkness, tidal variation, and even moderate wave action increase hull stress on already compromised structures. The unknown ingress source in several incidents further underscores inadequate routine hull surveillance as a latent systemic failure with direct causal weight.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (watertight integrity, damage control, bilge pumping arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (EPIRB carriage, liferaft deployment, distress signalling readiness)

SOLAS Chapter XII: Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers (extended by convention to fishing and small vessel contexts via analogous flag state regulations governing structural surveys and hull integrity)
preventable_by: Mandate installation and operational testing of automated bilge alarm systems with audible and visual alerts on all small commercial and hire vessels, with flag state or port state inspection verification that alarms are functional and bilge pump capacity is formally assessed against vessel flooding vulnerability as part of pre-season certification.

Require documented pre-departure hull integrity checklists covering sea cocks, stern gear seals, through-hull fittings, and bilge system operability, with structured periodic underwater or dry-dock inspection intervals proportional to vessel age and operating environment, enforced through the relevant Small Commercial Vessel or Fishing Vessel Code survey regime.

Introduce mandatory crew training and onboard emergency drill requirements specifically covering flooding identification, damage control response, and escalation procedures for night operations, with particular emphasis on solo or short-handed vessel operators and hire fleet crew, including minimum competency standards for bilge pump operation and emergency plug and patch equipment carriage.

representative_cases.description: A motor boat had engine failure and was taken under tow by another vessel which subsequently had engine failure. Both were then towed by a SAR craft
A standby safety vessel suffered water ingress from unknown source, came alongside safely to effect repairs.

A stern trawler began taking on water and issued a mayday call via VHF (Very High Frequency) radio. Two commercial vessels and two local lifeboat services attended as well as a rescue helicopter that lowered a winch operator with a pump onto the trawler. The lifeboats also provided pumps to keep the trawler afloat. The lifeboat pumps were able to stabilize the pumping operation and prevent further
A hire pleasure vessel started to take on water following engine failure. A passenger vessel assisted the hire boat and towed to safety.
A fishing vessel with three crew onboard sank. The crew set off their EPIRB and were recovered safe and well by another vessel.
A recreational sailing vessel started to take on water, vessel used pumps to help slow down the intake water. Flooding controlled but no engine so search and rescue towed vessel to safety.
A yacht was taking part in a race when it began to take on water. Both persons onboard issued a mayday and abandoned to a liferaft and were successfully rescued by lifeboat. The casualty vessel sank.
A sailing vessel started to take on water, search and rescue towed vessel to safety, no injuries. TAKING ON WATER AND PUMPS UNABLE TO COPE ESTUARY OF RIVER.

A recreational vessel began to take on water. The two crewmembers issued a MAYDAY on Channel 16 and several vessels in the area responded. The two crewmembers were recovered from the water and the vessel drifted before sinking.
A catamaran had water ingress which resulted in steering failure. Pumps managed to get the water ingress under control, the rudder was repaired and the vessel was towed by search and rescue vessels.

title: Electrical Hardware Failures Escalating to Fire Through Maintenance Gaps
description:
This theme is characterised by small but operationally significant fire events initiated by latent electrical hardware defects — including overheating transformers, degraded plugs, faulty pre-heaters, defective valve spindles, and failed alternators — across a diverse range of vessel types and operational contexts. In every case, the immediate ignition event was preceded by an undetected component-level fault that had been allowed to develop within a preventive maintenance regime insufficiently rigorous to identify degraded or substandard equipment before failure. The high proportion of procedural and hardware contributing factors confirms that these incidents are not random or unforeseeable; they represent a systemic pattern in which maintenance programmes are not effectively closing the gap between scheduled sign-off and the actual physical condition of electrical and mechanical components returned to service. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel classes suggests an industry-wide normalisation of risk around electrical maintenance standards.

Latent hardware vulnerabilities — components operating beyond their thermal rating, with degraded insulation, or in environmentally challenging enclosures — form the foundational failure mode in this cluster. These defects are inherently difficult to detect without component-level inspection protocols that go beyond visual checks or operational testing alone. Human factors, including reduced crew vigilance during night-time operations and the implicit trust placed in maintenance records over physical verification, further reduce the likelihood of pre-failure identification. Environmental conditions such as condensation in cold cabinets or elevated ambient temperatures in machinery spaces accelerate degradation while simultaneously reducing inspection frequency. Critically, the absence of overtemperature protection devices across multiple incidents indicates a systemic gap in engineering safeguards: where prevention fails, no secondary barrier exists to arrest escalation before smoke, alarm activation, or open fire. Effective mitigation requires both improved component-level inspection standards and the mandatory fitting of thermal protection on susceptible electrical circuits.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part D – Electrical Installations)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (as applicable to operational readiness and watchkeeping standards supporting hazard detection)
preventable_by: Implement component-level electrical inspection protocols within Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS) that require physical verification of insulation integrity, thermal ratings, and component condition — not solely operational testing or visual sign-off — for all electrical heating elements, transformers, alternators, and plug/socket assemblies at defined intervals appropriate to their operating environment.

Mandate the installation and periodic testing of overtemperature protection devices (thermal fuses, thermal cutouts, or temperature-monitored circuit breakers) on electrical circuits associated with heating equipment, transformers, and high-load components, ensuring that a secondary engineered safeguard arrests fault escalation before ignition can occur.

Require flag state and classification society audits to include targeted review of electrical maintenance records against the physical condition of high-risk components, with particular scrutiny of equipment operating in thermally or environmentally challenging enclosures (e.g., cold rooms, machinery spaces), to close the gap between documented maintenance compliance and actual equipment serviceability.

representative_cases.description: A landing vessel experienced a small fire after an electrical transformer within a cold cabinet overheated. The electrical transformer released smoke and triggered the fire alarm. The crew mustered, isolated the power to the space and removed the defective transformer to the open air. There were no other damages other than the melted component and no injures or pollution.

A cruise ship sustained a small fire from a short circuited plug in a cabin, resulting in no damage. Bridge call the cabin and crew members advise a burnt plug with smoke and sparks, however the burning and sparks now stopped and smoke clear apart from smell. At 1132hrs teams report no fire, however appears to have been short circuit resulting in flame, sparks, smoke and burning of plug.

The crew of a cruise ship were testing a diesel generator following some maintenance when the smoke detector in the space was activated. The duty engineer who was in the engine room at the time manually activated the ultra-fog alarm and stopped the generator. There was a small fire in the region of the starting air pipe which the engine crew managed to extinguished using local hand held fire fight

Cargo ship sustained a minor fire while alongside. The pre heater of a water pump was faulty and some wires overheated and the coating of the wires and the protective jacket of the pump caught fire.  It set off the alarms and was dealt with by crew on board and rectified. There were no injuries sustained.

A sailing yacht required assistance after a burnt out alternator started a fire, the fire was contained below deck and proceeded under sail to a safe haven. A nearby sailing yacht issued a MAYDAY and followed the casualty yacht. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene, confirmed the fire was extinguished and towed the vessel to a mooring. No reported crew injuries.

Passenger ferry was alongside when the crew noticed smoke in the battery room. Fore brigade attended controlled the situation. The following day the air was checked and the temperature had dropped. No damages reported.

A sail training vessel had a fire on its shaft after sparking occurred on the brake pads causing the brake mechanism to catch fire. The fire did not spread and was extinguished by the automatic fire suppression system. The fire was caused by the mechanical failure of the shaft brake causing it not to release. There was damage to the shaft break mechanism.

A research survey vessel was alongside in port when the fire alarm sounded on board. The crew mustered and began fire fighting emergency response procedures as well as calling shore side emergency services. All crew and others who were not directly involved in the fire fighting efforts, evacuated the vessel onto the quayside. The vessel's fire fighting team located the fire inside an electrical ca

The vessel started its main engines alongside the quay from the offshore mooring buoy. The starboard central engine jacket water heater caught fire after overheating, and surrounding paint and cables ignited producing smoke and flame. The chief engineer raised the alarm by telephoning the bridge, the bridge sounded the General Alarm mustering the crew and advising them to suit up the firefighters

The crew of a fisheries patrol vessel noticed smoke and a burning smell in an accommodation alleyway. After carrying out electrical isolations based on which breaker had tripped, the crew discovered that the source of a fire was the junction box for one of the cabin heaters. By isolating the heater, the smoke stopped and the crew opened the deckhead panels and ventilated the area. There was damage

title: Propeller Fouling from Inadequate Situational Awareness and Planning
description:
This theme encompasses incidents where vessels suffered propeller fouling or entanglement due to a convergence of inadequate pre-operational planning, insufficient situational awareness, and degraded environmental conditions. The incidents span charter yachts fouling mooring infrastructure, fishing vessels entangling their own or adjacent gear, and vessels encountering derelict seabed debris — yet all share a common latent architecture: the hazard was foreseeable, the conditions that activated it were predictable, and the procedural or physical safeguards that could have interrupted the causal chain were absent or ineffective. Night-time operations appear as a consistent aggravating factor, systematically reducing the crew's ability to visually detect submerged lines, gear proximity, or debris before contact. Tidal constraints, wind, and sea state further eroded the margins available for corrective action, converting manageable risks into propulsion emergencies requiring SAR intervention.

The hardware dimension of this theme is characterised by the near-universal absence of mechanical mitigations such as rope cutters or propeller protection devices, meaning that once contact with a line or net occurred, escalation to full fouling was essentially inevitable. Human factors are prominent in the form of single-handed operation, inadequate lookout arrangements, and failures to complete pre-departure or pre-approach checks — including securing trawl gear, surveying mooring field layouts, and accounting for tidal effects on navigable clearance. At the procedural level, there is a consistent absence of formal protocols governing navigation through mooring fields at night, departure clearance confirmation from adjacent vessels, and gear management relative to the propeller during fishing operations. Together, these latent conditions — hardware deficiency, human capacity limitations, and procedural gaps — repeatedly align under adverse environmental circumstances to produce outcomes that are both foreseeable and preventable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 31, and 34: navigational watchkeeping, distress situations, and voyage/passage planning)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 4: port State control and inspection of operational readiness)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19: emergency training and drills, including response to propulsion loss and SAR coordination)
preventable_by: Mandate formal pre-approach and pre-departure checklists for operations in or adjacent to mooring fields, requiring documented confirmation of mooring infrastructure layout, tidal clearance assessment, and securing of all deployed gear before manoeuvring — with particular procedural rigour enforced for night-time operations.

Require the fitting of rope cutters or shaft protection devices on all commercial fishing vessels and charter vessels as a baseline engineering control, reducing the probability that incidental line contact escalates to full propeller fouling and loss of propulsion.

Establish minimum safe manning and watchkeeping standards for night-time fishing and berthing operations that prohibit single-handed vessel management during high-risk evolutions such as gear retrieval, mooring field transit, or confined berth departure, ensuring a dedicated lookout is maintained whenever the vessel is manoeuvring in proximity to potential fouling hazards.

representative_cases.description: A charter sailing yacht with four crew onboard was preparing to moor when the yacht passed over another mooring and fouled the propeller. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist, the SAR crew cut the mooring as they were unable to free the line from the propeller and towed the yacht to another mooring. A diver was organised for the following to remove the line. No crew injuries or re

A fishing vessel was departing its berth when its trawl door became entangled in the mooring ropes of another fishing vessel still alongside. The vessel collided with the moored fishing vessel and when attempting to break free caused the moored vessel to strike the quay wall. The crew of the fishing vessel that was underway, boarded the moored fishing vessel and freed the trawl door from the moori

A stern trawler fouled its propeller on its gear and was unable to free the propeller without help. Search and rescue assets were launched to help the single occupant of the vessel by towing it back to port where the propeller was freed.

A fishing vessel was recovering static fishing gear from the seabed when the propeller became fouled by an old net, rope or similar debris. The fishing vessel broadcast an alert and the local lifeboat launched and assisted the vessel back to port with a tow. All people on board were fit and well with lifejackets and there were no injuries, pollution or damages to the vessel.

A sailing vessel experienced propulsion failure after it fouled its propeller in the shipping lane at the entrance to a port. They transmitted an urgency call for assistance and another nearby vessel rendered assistance, setting up a tow and taking them into the harbour to a safe mooring. The vessel could not sail or use their engine.

A hospital/ambulance vessel (patient transport) grounded after the jet units became clogged by seaweed causing propulsion failure. The skipper reported that the seaweed got into the jets due to not keeping an effective enough look out to sight the seaweed and take evasive action.

A fishing vessel experienced propulsion failure after the propeller became fouled in the rope of its fishing gear. The vessel could not be freed by towage alone and a search and rescue vessel was dispatched with the appropriate equipment to free the fouled propeller. The search and rescue vessel towed the fishing vessel back to port. There were no injuries, damages or pollution.

A search and rescue hovercraft was returning from a tasking when it contacted with a lobster pot which resulted in the port side of the skirting. This caused significant damage that meant that the vessel had to go out of service to complete repairs. The commander of the vessel also injured their thumb requiring first aid treatment.

A passenger vessel experienced propulsion failure after a lobster pot line fouled one of the propellers. It pulled out the propeller shaft from the starboard propulsion meaning that the vessel was only able to manoeuvre on one engine. The crew decided to request a tow back to port from another vessel as a safety precaution.

A single hand fishing catamaran became entangled in ropes from lobster and crab pots close to the shore and fouled the propeller. The vessel dropped an anchor to try to prevent it moving onto the rocks and called for help. The local lifeboat service attended and set up a tow to bring it back to port.

title: Steering System Failures Due to Latent Mechanical Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by the failure of primary steering systems — spanning hydraulic components, control circuits, and mechanical linkages — across a range of vessel types and operational contexts, with failures consistently occurring during high-demand phases of navigation such as berthing, channel transit, and manoeuvring under pilotage. The recurrence of these incidents points to a systemic pattern in which latent hardware degradation, including seal deterioration, hose failure, and hydraulic system faults, develops over time without detection, only manifesting catastrophically at the moment of greatest navigational risk. While emergency steering systems and contingency procedures are generally available and, in most cases, successfully employed, their activation represents a reactive response to a failure that should have been identified and rectified through proactive maintenance and rigorous pre-arrival functional testing. The persistence of this pattern across vessel classes and operators indicates that maintenance regimes and inspection protocols for steering systems are frequently inadequate, deferred, or insufficiently documented.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant causative factor in this theme, with steering component degradation going undetected due to gaps in preventive maintenance programmes and insufficient adherence to SOLAS-mandated steering gear test requirements prior to arrival in port. Human factors compound the risk: bridge teams and operators demonstrate variable competency in executing rapid switchover to emergency steering modes, and pre-arrival checks are inconsistently applied, reducing the opportunity to detect faults before they become critical. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time operations, moderate winds, and confined waterways — consistently amplify the consequences of steering failures by compressing crew reaction time, reducing situational awareness, and limiting sea room for vessel recovery. The convergence of undetected hardware deterioration, procedural non-compliance, and adverse operating conditions creates the conditions for escalation from a manageable equipment fault to a serious marine incident involving channel exceedance, collision risk, or grounding.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1 (Part E) — Steering Gear: Construction, Testing, and Operational Requirements including pre-departure and pre-arrival steering gear tests
SOLAS Chapter V — Safety of Navigation: Bridge procedures, navigational watchkeeping, and pilot-bridge information exchange during critical manoeuvres
SOLAS Chapter IX — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships: ISM Code obligations for planned maintenance systems, defect reporting, and emergency preparedness
preventable_by: Mandate and verify full functional testing of all steering modes — including emergency and non-follow-up systems — at every pre-arrival check in compliance with SOLAS Regulation II-1/29, with completed test records signed by the Officer of the Watch and retained for inspection by port State control and flag State auditors.

Implement a structured, condition-based planned maintenance programme for all hydraulic steering system components — including hoses, seals, actuators, and fittings — with defined inspection intervals, replacement life limits, and mandatory defect escalation procedures under the ISM Code to prevent latent degradation from reaching operational failure.

Conduct regular, scenario-based emergency steering drills that require bridge teams and pilots to practise rapid switchover to emergency and non-follow-up steering control under simulated time pressure, with performance outcomes recorded and used to inform refresher training, ensuring effective crew response when primary steering failure occurs during critical phases of navigation.

representative_cases.description: A survey vessel had a hydraulic oil leak from the steering system. The leakage was contained on board in the machinery space. The vessel was able to make it to port using their emergency steering system.

Cargo vessel was approaching a dock when it was noted that a steering control failure had occurred. Navigation control was shifted back to the center conning position for the final approach to the berth. The pilot tug leaned back and increased from half to full - used to arrest the forward motion. The gain for the steering control did not occur until it was switched into emergency push button oper

A bulk carrier under pilotage experienced steering failure and left the channel, possibly grounding. The vessel was able to be freed from the area with the assistance of tugs that were already made fast. It left the port and went to anchorage for investigations.

It was reported that a hire cruiser had a steering failure, a passing cruiser encountered the hire cruiser broadside across a river. The vessel had a steering failure to both the upper and lower helm positions. Using the bow thruster and the engine the crew managed to reach the bank, disembark and secure the vessel to the bank. No reported injuries to crew.

A replenishment tanker was manoeuvring with a pilot on board when a technical malfunction caused steering failure. The crew switched over to the non follow up steering control. There was no damage or pollution.

A cargo vessel was preparing to depart a berth, the planned manoeuvre was to conduct a swing and proceed downriver. There was a strong tidal stream with the flooding tide. The navigation was passed to the pilot, after releasing the spring line, the engine was stopped, the rudder was hard to starboard position and the current continued to turn the vessel. The pilot gave the command full ahead, soon

A domestic ro-pax vessel was departing from port when one of the rudders became stuck hard to one side due to mechanical issues. The vessel was able to maintain its heading until the tugs arrive to assist the vessel back to port so that the malfunction could be rectified. There were no damages, pollution or injuries.

A foot passenger vessel was departing from the berth when it experience failure of the primary steering control. The crew switched over to the secondary steering, which also didn't work so they proceeded to switch over to the third steering option of the emergency tiller. They managed to manoeuvre back to the berth but on attempting to berth stern first control was lost and the tide pulled the ves

A cruise ship was departing port with two tugs in attendance. It was reported that the steering was non-responsive and a slow rate of turn to port, corrective action was made using the thrusters. The cruise ship developed a slow starboard swing, the speed was reduced and the forward tug was asked to push to maintain a safe position in the channel. The emergency steering procedures were initiated,

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was returning to station, as the vessel was proceeding to the carriage there was minimal output from the steering inputs. The helm applied power resulting in the engines going hard over, the vessel veered away and the stern came into contact with the side of the carriage. The propellers struck the rubber carriage bed, causing damage to the propellors. The vessel wa

title: Unsafe Heaving Line Modifications Endangering Pilot Transfer Personnel
description:
This theme is characterised by the deliberate or uninformed modification of heaving line monkey's fists with unauthorised metal weights — including shackles, nuts embedded in resin, and solid metal objects — creating dangerous projectiles during pilot embarkation and mooring operations. The dominant pattern across incidents is a normalisation of improvised practice, where crew members add excessive weight to improve throwing distance or accuracy without adequate appreciation of the serious injury risk posed to pilot launch personnel and shore workers. The preponderance of mooring-category incidents confirms this is a systemic operational risk concentrated in the specific task of heaving line deployment. Incidents recur because shipboard Safety Management Systems (SMS) either lack explicit, enforceable procedures governing heaving line weight limits and approved equipment, or those procedures are not effectively communicated, supervised, or enforced at the deckhand level, allowing the unsafe practice to persist undetected across multiple voyages and vessel types.

Latent hardware failures are evident in the absence of certified, compliant monkey's fists available on board, forcing or enabling crew to improvise with metal substitutes; this supply-chain gap is a systemic SMS deficiency. Human factors dominate the causal chain: insufficient training on the injury potential of weighted projectiles, weak supervisory oversight during routine berthing operations, and a cultural tolerance for workarounds that prioritise operational convenience over procedural compliance. Environmental conditions — specifically night-time operations and elevated sea states — are consistent amplifying factors across the cluster, reducing the likelihood that unsafe modifications will be visually detected during pre-operational checks and increasing the kinetic hazard to personnel on a moving pilot launch. The combination of latent procedural gaps, inadequate pre-operational inspection regimes, and environmental degradation of oversight creates a high-probability pathway to a serious or fatal injury that is only currently being intercepted by the vigilance of external personnel rather than shipboard controls.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 23: Pilot Transfer Arrangements, mandating safe equipment standards for pilot embarkation and disembarkation)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: requiring SMS to identify hazards, establish procedures, and ensure crew competency for all shipboard operations)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (governing the maintenance, inspection, and operational readiness of equipment used in safety-critical operations)
preventable_by: Mandate a formal pre-arrival heaving line inspection protocol embedded within the SMS, requiring a named officer to physically inspect and weigh all heaving lines before pilot embarkation or mooring operations, with results recorded in the deck logbook; any heaving line exceeding an approved maximum weight threshold (e.g., 0.5 kg) must be withdrawn from service immediately and replaced with a certified compliant alternative held in dedicated deck stores.

Issue and maintain a dedicated stock of certified, approved heaving line weights (e.g., soft-material monkey's fists or bean-bag weights meeting recognised weight standards) as part of routine deck equipment requisition, and include explicit SMS procedures and company circulars prohibiting the use of any metal object — including shackles, nuts, bolts, or resin-coated assemblies — as heaving line weights, with non-compliance treated as a serious safety violation subject to disciplinary action.

Integrate targeted crew training on the biomechanical injury risk of weighted heaving line projectiles into onboard safety familiarisation programmes and toolbox talks, using photographic evidence from actual incidents to illustrate consequences; supplement this with periodic unannounced checks by Masters and Chief Mates during night-time and adverse-weather berthing operations to reinforce supervisory accountability and deter the normalisation of unsafe workarounds.

representative_cases.description: During discharge operations, approx. 8mt of steel rebar was lost into the river. Discharge was carried out by shoreside crane using pre-slung lifting strops. Failure of lifting strop during lift.

The crew of a vehicles carrier sent down a heaving line with an unsafe weight on it. It had a compliant monkey's fist but there was a steel shackle attached as an additional weight. The shackle was confiscated by the crew of the pilot boat.

A chemical tanker used an unsafely weighted heaving line when embarking a pilot. (to pick up pilot luggage). The weight used was a stainless steel shackle that weighed 0,885kg and was removed by the crew of the pilot launch.

A container ship used a dangerously weighted heaving line. The monkeys fist was cut open to find two large metal nuts with some sort of putty. The monkeys fist had also been dipped in resin creating a hard surface although some of this had chipped off. Approximate weight was two hundred and ninety grams.

A container vessel used a dangerously weighted heaving line while berthing. The weight on the end of the line reportedly contained 'solid metal' and weighed 740g. The shore workers cut the weight off and provided a 518g beanbag weight instead. The chief mate of the vessel agreed that the weight was heavy compared to the bean bag and the vessel would check the other heaving lines in use.

The crew of a general cargo vessel threw an unsafely weighted heaving line. It was 430 grams in weight with a metal core and rubber outside. The shore workers removed the weight and supplied a suitable replacement.

The crew of a container ship used a dangerously weighted heaving line which struck the windscreen of a vehicle parked on the quay, cracking the windscreen. There were no injuries. The crew of the vessel accepted the seriousness of the incident and checked the other heaving lines on board for similar dangerous weights.

A container ship crew used an unsafely weighted heaving line that consisted of a monkey's fist that had metal scrap inside. The weight was confiscated and replaced by a sandbag and the crew on board were instructed by the dock workers on the correct weight to use.

A container ship used a dangerously weighted heaving line. Two weighted heaving lines thrown from the container ship. One monkeys fist contained nuts and bolts weighing approximately four hundred grams and the other contained a metal chain and weighing approximately one kilogram.

A container ship used a dangerously weighted heaving line. Heaving line thrown from aft during berthing, travelling at speed considering the little effort made by crewman, upon inspection its clear the item has been dipped in a resin and also contains multiple nuts and is dangerous. Item was removed from line and bean bag replaced onto line, chief mate came to view item and agreed solid compared w

title: Night Watchkeeping Failures in Mixed-Traffic Waterways
description:
This theme is characterised by close-quarters situations and contacts occurring predominantly at night between vessels of significantly disparate size, manoeuvrability, and operational profile — most commonly large commercial vessels and small recreational or fishing craft. Across 49 incidents spanning navigation failures and direct contacts, a consistent latent pattern emerges: smaller vessel operators demonstrating insufficient knowledge of or compliance with COLREGS give-way and stand-on obligations, particularly Rules 5, 7, 8, 9, 15, 16, and 18, while commercial vessel watchkeepers fail to maintain adequate lookout for low-profile, low-radar-cross-section targets. The recurring convergence of night-time conditions, asymmetric vessel manoeuvrability, absent inter-vessel VHF communication, and inadequate AIS or radar monitoring creates a systemic collision risk that is disproportionately concentrated in port approaches, traffic separation schemes, confined channels, and anchorage areas where recreational and commercial traffic routinely share navigational space.

Environmental conditions — while occasionally elevated by moderate wind and swell — are largely benign across this incident cluster, confirming that human factors and procedural deficiencies are the dominant causal pathway. Latent organisational factors include the absence of mandated watch alarm systems on small fishing vessels, cultural normalisation of single-handed night operation, and insufficient pre-departure passage planning among recreational operators. On the commercial side, bridge resource management failures and overly reactive rather than proactive watchkeeping postures are evident, particularly in the context of detecting non-standard hazards such as capsized dinghies or anchored vessels without adequate lighting. The systemic underreporting of near-miss events in this category further masks the true frequency and severity of this risk pattern, inhibiting corrective intervention by flag states, port authorities, and vessel traffic services.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, 24, 28, and 33 relating to navigational equipment, bridge watchkeeping, VTS compliance, and distress obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security (as it intersects with VTS reporting obligations and vessel identification via AIS)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (regarding mandatory VHF watchkeeping and DSC distress alerting relevant to disabled and non-communicating vessels)
preventable_by: Mandatory COLREGS competency reassessment and targeted night navigation training for recreational and small commercial vessel operators, delivered through national maritime authorities and port-level outreach programmes, with particular emphasis on Rules 5, 9, 16, and 18 and the practical implications of large vessel restricted manoeuvrability and stopping distances.

Implementation of proactive Vessel Traffic Service (VTS) protocols in high-risk corridors — including port approaches, TSS boundaries, and known anchorage areas — that include automated AIS-based alerts for developing close-quarters situations between commercial and recreational vessels, supplemented by mandatory VHF Channel 16 monitoring requirements enforced as a condition of entry for all vessel classes.

Requirement for commercial vessel operators to adopt enhanced low-profile target detection procedures within bridge watchkeeping standing orders, including mandatory radar watch intervals, the use of AIS Class B target filtering, and the fitting of fatigue detection or watch alarm systems on small fishing and charter vessels operating during night hours in shared waterways.

representative_cases.description: Pleasure craft was engaged in fishing when a tug proceeded towards the vessel and collided with it. No injuries and/or damages reported from both vessels,
A sailing yacht had a near miss with a tanker. The sailing yacht impeded the tankers passage, the ships whistle was used but no response from the yacht forcing the tanker to take avoiding action.
A sailing yacht had a close quarters situation with a tanker. The pilot on board the tanker reported that the yacht cut across the bow of the tanker. The approximate distance was two cables.
An angling charter vessel was at anchor when a yacht came down the starboard side and across the anchor rope and missed the beam by ten feet. There was no damage or injuries.
A containership on passage and a sailing yacht had a close quarters situation whilst the containership was constrained to a deep water route.
One sailing vessel was on a mooring when a second sailing vessel collided with them and left the scene. The vessel on a mooring reported only superficial damage. No injuries reported.
A fishing vessel had a near miss COLREG infringement as the skipper fell asleep at the helm.
Chemical tanker was impeded by a fishing vessel who passed approximately 6 cables ahead. VTS contacted the fishing vessel to keep well clear. No damages/injuries sustained.
Two fishing vessels collided with each other minor damage, no injuries. No reports of injuries or water ingress. Vessels made way to port.
A sailing yacht had a near miss, close quarters, with a tanker. The sailing yacht impeded the tankers passage as it was outbound from a port.

title: Night-Time Lock Transit Contacts in Confined Waterways
description:
This theme is characterised by vessel-to-infrastructure contact events occurring predominantly during night-time transits through lock systems and confined port waterways. The overwhelming preponderance of contact incidents (44 of 45 events) over other navigation-type incidents underscores the systemic nature of this problem. Common to nearly all incidents is the combination of restricted navigational geometry, reduced visibility in darkness, and insufficient procedural safeguards governing approach speed, lateral positioning, and crew deployment during high-risk confined transits. Vessel types range from ro-pax ferries and general cargo vessels to tugs and dredgers, indicating the risk is not vessel-class-specific but is instead rooted in the operational and procedural environment of lock navigation itself. Familiarity with fixed routes compounds the risk through complacency, reducing the situational vigilance necessary for precision manoeuvring in spaces with minimal margin for error. Hydrodynamic effects — including propeller wash in confined chambers and tidal or current forces during lock approach — represent underappreciated dynamic contributors that are rarely formalised in pre-entry risk assessments.

From a latent causation perspective, hardware deficiencies such as inadequate lock lighting, absent or poorly positioned guidance fenders, and ageing timber or coping stone infrastructure amplify the consequences of minor positional errors. Human factors are predominant: spatial misjudgement under reduced visibility, inadequate bridge team coordination, overreliance on pilotage without standardised supplementary protocols, and failure to account for wind and tidal effects on vessel track during entry. Procedurally, the evidence points consistently to the absence of formalised night lock-entry checklists, undefined environmental operating thresholds, and insufficient crew briefing on hydrodynamic interaction risks within confined chambers. Collectively, these latent conditions create an environment in which a single minor navigational misjudgement — routine in open water — results in contact with infrastructure that has little capacity to absorb the energy of even a slow-speed deviation from centreline.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 14, 15, and 34: Manning, Bridge Watchkeeping, and Voyage/Passage Planning)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: SMS procedures for high-risk manoeuvres and confined waterway operations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 4: Port State Control and inspection of operational procedures and documentation)
preventable_by: Mandate formalised Night Lock-Entry Procedures within vessel Safety Management Systems, to include defined environmental operating thresholds (wind speed, tidal state, visibility), minimum crew deployment requirements, explicit approach speed limits, and stage-by-stage positioning checkpoints referenced to fixed navigational aids or lock lighting infrastructure.

Require pre-entry hydrodynamic risk assessments for all confined waterway transits, specifically addressing propeller wash effects, tidal and current forces at lock approach, and vessel beam-to-lock width clearance margins, with outcomes recorded in the passage plan and communicated to all bridge team members and tug operators prior to entry.

Establish port and lock authority infrastructure standards mandating calibrated approach lighting, clearly marked centreline guidance systems, and regular structural inspection regimes for fender, coping stone, and lock gate components, ensuring that contact hazards are mitigated at the point of risk and that minor contacts do not result in disproportionate infrastructure damage or latent structural hazards.

representative_cases.description: A general cargo vessel was transiting through a lock when it made contact with the edge of the lock. This resulted in a dent and scraping on the hull of the vessel and a crack to the coping stones.
A ro-pax vessel made contact with an outer lock gate on arrival into port. No reported damage.
A general cargo vessel made contact with a lock side wall whilst manoeuvring. The vessel was coming into the lock on the flood.
Service operational vessel whilst it had pass through the lock at a dock ,it contacted east outer floodgate causing some damage to wooden fender in the lock and forwards rubber fender on the vessel.
Tug was running up in the lock when it lifted a coping stone , when it had dropped back into place, but it was still protruding slightly and would need resealing.
A hopper dredger made contact with a lock gate and sustained bends to safety rails and ladders on its port side.
Whilst making fast bow to bow with the cargo vessel, the tug made contact with the ships port bow. This caused the tug to lose position and manoeuvre away from the ship resulting in mast damage.
A general cargo vessel under pilotage made contact with a lock wall during a lock entry manoeuvre. The vessel sustained superficial damage the lock wall and a fender were also damaged.
A general cargo vessel was entering a lock when the vessel made contact with the inner burleigh wheel, resulting in in vessel starboard side rails bending.

A general cargo vessel was entering a lock when it landed on the roller fender in the lock. The vessel sustained damage to the handrails on the starboard side, no injuries to crew and no damage reported to the dock infrastructure.

title: Galley Thermal Hazards: Procedural Gaps and PPE Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of thermal burn injuries sustained by galley crew during routine cooking and cleaning tasks, arising not from exceptional circumstances but from the normalisation of unsafe practices within an inadequately governed working environment. The incidents share a common latent cause: the absence of defined, enforced safe systems of work for high-risk galley operations — including oven handling, steam equipment use, and hot liquid transport. Without task-specific procedures, crew members default to ad hoc methods, routinely underestimating thermal hazards associated with accumulated condensate in steam ovens, simultaneous removal of multiple hot dishes, and the transport of uncontained boiling liquids. The frequency of these incidents across vessel types and operational contexts indicates a systemic failure at the safety management system level, rather than isolated individual error. The co-occurrence of fire incidents within the same cluster further suggests that galley thermal risk management is broadly deficient across affected fleets.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including steamer ovens prone to water accumulation, the absence of spill-resistant or lidded transport containers, and inadequate provision of appropriate PPE such as heat-resistant and waterproof protective footwear — compound the procedural failures by eliminating the last line of defence against injury. Human factors, including reduced situational awareness during night-time operations, failure to anticipate secondary hazards such as condensate pooling or co-displaced cookware, and likely insufficient task-specific training, consistently transform routine galley tasks into injury events. Environmental conditions, particularly vessel motion in moderate sea states, introduce additional dynamic risk to an already inadequately controlled environment by displacing hot liquids within enclosed equipment. Collectively, these findings indicate that thermal injury risk in the galley is foreseeable, preventable, and currently insufficiently mitigated by existing onboard safety arrangements.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (Regulation 10 – fire safety measures including galley equipment and cooking appliance fire risk control)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19 – emergency training and drills, inclusive of onboard occupational safety training obligations)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring operators to establish safe working procedures, risk assessments, and PPE provision for all routine operational tasks)
preventable_by: Develop and implement mandatory task-specific safe systems of work for all high-risk galley operations — including staged steam oven door-opening procedures to allow pressure and condensate equalisation, defined protocols for simultaneous multi-dish oven handling, and controlled hot liquid transport procedures requiring temperature dilution, lidded containers, and designated filling points proximate to cleaning areas — with compliance verified through scheduled internal audits.

Mandate the provision and documented use of appropriate PPE in all galley environments, specifically heat-resistant and fluid-impermeable protective footwear as a minimum standard for all personnel engaged in cooking or galley cleaning tasks, supported by a formal PPE risk assessment embedded within the vessel's SMS and subject to periodic review by the Designated Person Ashore.

Introduce a structured galley-specific safety induction and recurring competency training programme covering the identification and management of thermal hazards, including recognition of condensate accumulation in steam ovens, safe hot liquid handling, and emergency first aid response for scalds and burns; training records should be maintained onboard and reviewed as part of flag state and port state control inspections.

representative_cases.description: A dredging vessels crew member split how fat on foot resulting in burns to the foot. On removing an oven tray from the oven inadvertently also took the roasting dish out at the same time spilling hot fat onto there foot.

IP was working in the main galley on Deck 7. When IP opened the steamer oven door, water inside spilled onto IP's right foot causing hot water burn to the top of IP’s foot and the top of right ankle causing redness and blistering.

A ro-pax vessel was alongside for a refit, a crew member was filling a mop bucket from the hot water geyser in the galley. Whilst transporting the bucket to the area they were cleaning, the crew member accidentally struck the mop bucket against either their leg or a door. This resulted in boiling water to spill onto the back of the crew member's leg, ankle and foot. The area quickly blistered, a c

A ro-pax vessel crew member was working in the galley when they sustained a scald injury to their wrist. They were removing a tray from the steam oven using oven gloves. When removing the tray, hot water from the top of the tray spilled onto their left arm. The crew member took first aid actions, running their injured arm under cold water for 20 minutes however the burn was not getting better. The

A cruise ship suffered a small galley fire on the hot plate due to oils and fats. The hot plate was in operations when a fire ignited caused by fats and oils on the hot plate. The crewmember nearby placed a stainless steel pan over the fire to smother it whilst another crewmember collected a fire blanket. The fire blanket extinguished the fire. No fire alarms were activated. The crew let the hot p

A sailing yacht grounded from a possible engine failure which resulted in a crew member sustaining a scald injury to their hand and face whilst trying to remove the radiator cap. Search and rescue assets were tasked to the scene, where a shore rescue team assisted the three crew members ashore. The injured crew member was passed into the care of the ambulance service.

A cruise ship crewmember sustained a scald injury from a steam vent whilst working in the galley. The crewmember was stirring a pot in the galley when they accidentally operated the foot operated valve for a steam vent outlet whilst their leg was positioned in front of the steam vent pipe. The crewmember sustained a scald injury to their ankle from the steam and were signed off work for 5 days in

A bulk carrier crewmember was scalded by hot water. They were working with one other crewmember inside a boiler room fire box when hot water spilled on them from above, filling their work boot and causing scalds to their foot. They were given first aid on board and the crew called for an ambulance as the vessel was alongside. The ambulance had a one and a half hour wait time, so a stevedore took t

A ro-pax vessel crewmember was heating food in a galley microwave when the lid came loose and the hot contents spilled over their arm. The burn was treated with first aid by running the crewmember's arm under cold water for twenty minutes. Then a burns dressing was applied. The burn was 30 centimetres in length. They continued their shift however they later attended hospital where they were advise

Sailing vessel suffered an engine failure resulting in a tow request. The casualty on board sustained a burn injury due to getting sprayed by boiling water on the face and arm. They were taken for further medical assistance.

title: Propulsion and Machinery Failures Across Diverse Vessel Types
description:
This theme is characterised by a persistent pattern of propulsion and machinery failures spanning commercial, passenger, support, recreational, and specialist vessel categories, with the overwhelming majority of incidents (43 of 45) classified as machinery-related. The incidents share a common underlying thread: failures that are mechanical or maintenance-related in origin rather than environmentally driven. Across virtually all cases, sea state and weather conditions were either benign or only marginally elevated, confirming that adverse environment was not a primary causal factor. Instead, the incidents point consistently to deferred maintenance, inadequate pre-departure checks, poor compliance with Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS), aging or fatigued components, and insufficient engineering redundancy. The recurrence of this pattern across such a diverse fleet profile indicates a systemic, industry-wide deficiency rather than isolated operator failure, with ISM Code non-compliance a common latent enabler.

Night operations emerge as a near-universal contextual amplifier across this cluster, increasing response complexity, limiting situational awareness, and elevating the consequences of what might otherwise be manageable mechanical faults. Human factors — including normalisation of deferred maintenance, insufficient engineering crew competency, and failure to act on early warning signs — represent the dominant latent causal layer. Hardware vulnerabilities such as single-point propulsion dependencies, unmonitored rotating shaft components, degraded exhaust systems, and aging electrical infrastructure further compound risk. Recreational operators display the weakest pre-departure discipline, while commercial and specialist operators show systemic gaps in PMS execution and condition-based monitoring. The breadth of vessel types affected underscores that neither vessel age, operational sector, nor regulatory regime is providing adequate protective barriers against this failure pattern, and that a structured, proactive maintenance culture supported by mandatory audit mechanisms is essential to reduce recurrence.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Parts C, D, and E covering machinery installation, steering gear, and electrical requirements)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – mandatory PMS, maintenance records, crew competency, and emergency preparedness obligations)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (relevant to engine room fire risk arising from machinery defects, overheating components, and electrical faults)
preventable_by: Mandatory enforcement and third-party audit of Planned Maintenance System (PMS) compliance for all propulsion-critical components, including rotating shafts, exhaust valves, cooling systems, and electrical distribution boards, with documented evidence required prior to voyage departure and available to Port State Control inspectors.

Introduction of standardised pre-departure checklists for all vessel categories — including recreational and tender vessels — that require a verified propulsion and electrical systems check by a qualified engineer or responsible officer, with checklists retained as part of the vessel's official safety management records and subject to random inspection.
