Implementation of condition-based and predictive maintenance programmes for high-cycle propulsion components on commercial, offshore, and passenger vessels, incorporating vibration analysis, thermographic inspection, and alarm trend monitoring to detect early degradation signatures before in-service failure occurs, particularly for vessels operating in night or reduced-visibility conditions where recovery options are most constrained.

representative_cases.description: Port tender vessel reported port engine issues. No further damage reported.
Port tender had an engine issue and was not available for berthing. No further defects reported.
Tug was tasked to assist another vessel when the main engine alarmed and de-clutched. The engine has been restored and the tug proceeded to assist the vessel without causing any damages.
The vessel suffered a malfunction of the main engine exhaust valve unit no.1, unsure if any damage was sustained or tow was required.
A recreational fishing vessel lost propulsion due to flat battery resulting in being towed back to mooring.
A wind farm transfer vessel lost propulsion resulting in being towed back to harbour. HAVE GOT ONE ENGINE WITH A BIT OF STEERING BUT THEY ARE CONCERNED THAT THEY WILL LOSE THAT, REQUIRING A TOW BACK.
The cargo vessel's main engine failed and the crew requested three tugs. The vessel was in ballast mode when the incident occurred. No further damages were reported.
A general cargo vessel had an engine failure, the crew were unable to affect repairs. The vessel was waiting for a tug to be arranged to tow the vessel to a port.
Pilot boat suffered an electrical failure. The vessel still had propulsion but limited steerage. No further defects were reported.
Ro-Ro cargo's portside engine failed, however they were able to make their way back to port without any assistance.

title: Undisclosed Incidents Pending MAIB Investigation Reports
description:
This theme is characterised entirely by the absence of substantive incident data, with all records deferring to pending Marine Accident Investigation Branch (MAIB) reports that were not available at the time of analysis. The 44 incidents span a range of severity classifications — from Marine Incident through to Very Serious — yet share a common analytical deficiency: no narrative, causal chain, vessel activity, or outcome data has been disclosed. Environmental metadata indicates a consistent pattern of night-time occurrences across varying sea states, from calm to moderately adverse conditions (wave heights ranging from 0.12m to 4.52m, wind speeds from 6.9 to 52.5 kph), suggesting these incidents are not confined to a single operational context. Multiple records also exhibit data quality inconsistencies between reported sea state descriptors and measured wave heights, indicating systemic issues in environmental data recording that may compromise future analysis even when full reports become available.

From a safety investigation standpoint, the recurrence of this pattern reflects a structural gap in near-real-time incident intelligence: incidents of significant severity are being logged without actionable detail, preventing timely identification of latent causal factors including equipment failure, human error, navigational hazard management, or procedural non-compliance. The predominance of night-time conditions across this cluster warrants particular attention, as reduced visibility is a well-established human factors risk amplifier relevant to watchkeeping, collision avoidance, and emergency response. Until MAIB reports are published and integrated into this dataset, the underlying causal taxonomy — whether rooted in hardware deficiencies, organisational failings, or environmental mismanagement — remains entirely indeterminate. Systematic re-analysis upon report publication is essential to extract preventive value from these records.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 19: Navigational equipment; Regulation 22: Bridge visibility; Regulation 34: Safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 6: Casualty investigation and mandatory reporting obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (Emergency alerting, distress communication, and coordination with rescue authorities during night-time and adverse weather incidents)
preventable_by: Mandate immediate preliminary incident reporting: Require vessel operators and flag states to submit a structured preliminary incident report within 24 hours of any serious or very serious marine incident, capturing vessel type, incident category, causal hypothesis, and environmental conditions, independent of and prior to the completion of the formal MAIB investigation. This would eliminate data voids and enable timely safety signal detection.

Implement automated environmental data validation at point of entry: Introduce real-time cross-validation logic in incident reporting systems to flag and reject inconsistencies between reported sea state descriptors and measured wave height or wind speed values. This would address the recurring data quality discrepancies identified across multiple records and improve the reliability of environmental correlation analysis.

Establish a mandatory night-operations risk review protocol: Given the consistent night-time context across all incidents in this cluster, require vessels operating between sunset and sunrise to conduct and log a formal bridge risk assessment covering watchkeeping arrangements, navigational aid operability, communication readiness, and fatigue management. Compliance should be verifiable by Port State Control and flag state inspectors under SOLAS Chapter V obligations.

title: Passenger Threshold Trip Hazards in Circulation Areas
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of passenger falls caused by door thresholds, concealed steps, and level-change transitions within vessel circulation routes and embarkation areas. The incidents share a common latent cause: physical deck-level hazards that are insufficiently differentiated, inadequately controlled, or actively obscured, resulting in predictable and preventable injuries ranging from shoulder trauma to hip and metatarsal fractures. These incidents recur because the hazard is treated as a residual and accepted feature of vessel architecture rather than as an engineerable risk. Over-reliance on passive warning signage, the absence of systematic hazard inspections, and the failure to assess how installed materials such as AstroTurf interact with underlying deck geometry all reflect an organisational tolerance for known hazards that does not meet the standard of active risk reduction expected in high-footfall passenger environments.

The latent hardware factors are central to this theme: thresholds lacking high-contrast edge markings, tactile indicators, or adequate surface differentiation create trip hazards that are visually indistinguishable from the surrounding deck, particularly under reduced night-time lighting. Human factors compound the risk profile significantly — passengers unfamiliar with vessel architecture, those with mobility impairments, and individuals wearing inappropriate footwear such as flip flops are disproportionately vulnerable, while assisting crew members may lack specific training in guiding mobility aid users across physical obstacles. Environmental conditions, principally reduced lighting and vessel motion in even slight sea states, interact with these hardware deficiencies to narrow the safety margin further. Collectively, the evidence indicates a systemic gap in how threshold-level trip risks are identified during design review, assessed during routine safety inspections, and mitigated through engineering controls rather than administrative warnings alone.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (structural design, safe means of access, and watertight integrity of openings including thresholds)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe embarkation and movement of passengers in tender and evacuation routes)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection (doorway and threshold design requirements in passenger accommodation and circulation spaces)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory threshold and level-change hazard register for all passenger circulation routes, requiring each identified threshold to be assessed against minimum engineering standards including high-visibility contrasting edge markings, non-slip tactile strips, and adequate fixed lighting, with remediation actions assigned, tracked, and closed out prior to passenger operations.

Establish a formal pre-installation and periodic maintenance review process for all deck coverings and surface materials in passenger areas, requiring explicit confirmation that no installed material conceals or reduces the detectability of a level change, step, or threshold; any covering found to obscure a hazard must be removed or modified before the area is reopened to passengers.

Develop and deliver structured crew competency training specific to the assisted navigation of passengers with mobility limitations — including walker and wheelchair users — across threshold obstacles, supplemented by a vessel-specific policy restricting the use of open-toe or unsupported footwear in embarkation zones and tender walkways, with crew empowered to advise or redirect non-compliant passengers before boarding.

A cruise ship passenger was walking over a pontoon on the port side of the vessel towards the tenders when they tripped. As they fell they put out their arms and sustained a fracture. The passenger was wearing flip flops and the area was carpeted with a piece of AstroTurf which obscured a step. The AstroTurf was removed after the incident to make the step more visible to people.

A cruise ships passenger caught there foot in a threshold resulting in them falling and fracturing there hip. Passengers foot got caught on the threshold of the door between of the two ADA ramps, and suddenly fell. No defect or deficiencies were noted in the area of the accident. Location was well lit by artificial electrical light. Safety signs 'Watch your step' are posted on both sides of that d

A cruise ship passenger was walking from the open deck to go to an inside venue. As the passenger approached the door they lifted their to place it over the door threshold, with the assistance of a crew member. The walker hit against the door threshold, causing the passenger to fall forward and hit their head against the walker bars. The passenger was escorted to the ship's medical centre for trea

A cruise ship passenger was walking towards his cabin door which lead to the balcony. They lost their balance on the ram leading to the sliding door and fell on their right side. The injured passenger sustained fracture of the base of the fifth metatarsal of the right foot as well as a fracture of the proximal phalanx of the fifth digit of the right foot. The guest remained on board for the remain

A cruise ship crew member was heading toward the dishwash area, as they passed through a door they slipped on the threshold. As a result they fell forwards and hit their left hand on the deck. The crew member went to the medical centre where a fractured finger was diagnosed. The crew member was medically disembarked and repatriated. An inspection of the area was carried out with no defects or haza

A cruise ship passenger was walking towards a seating area in a bar, whilst walking they did not notice a step in front of them. Their right foot caught the step, they then fell forwards to the floor. Other passengers assisted the injured passenger. They were treated for a fracture to the right elbow and disembarked at the next port. The area was inspected and no defects found, there is an led lig

Cruise ship's passenger was approaching the door when they tripped on the door ramp and fell landing face down on their left shoulder. The casualty sustained a shoulder dislocation, soft tissue injury to upper lip and a chip to a tooth. No defects found in the area.

A cruise ship was alongside in port when a passenger tripped on the threshold of a fire screen door and fell to the deck. The passenger sustained a fractured radius in their right arm. They were wearing flip flops with a thick sole at the time of the accident.

A cruise ship passenger tripped on a tripod stand that was obscured by a curtain. They fell to the floor. Medical staff attended and took them to the medical centre for evaluation and treatment. The passenger sustained a sprain.

title: Night-Time Man Overboard: Tethering and Recovery Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by man overboard (MOB) events occurring predominantly during night-time sailing and racing operations under moderate to benign environmental conditions. The dominant feature across incidents is not adverse weather but rather a persistent failure to enforce consistent use of personal safety equipment — particularly harness and tether systems — combined with inadequate night watch protocols. The disproportionate fatality rate in this cluster underscores that MOB outcomes at night are driven primarily by delayed detection, compromised visibility, and the absence of automated alerting devices rather than the severity of sea state. Racing culture emerges as a critical systemic enabler, normalising non-compliance with tethering requirements through competitive pressure and the prioritisation of performance over personal safety. The single most defining latent cause is the inconsistent and unenforced application of tethering protocols across crew members, evidenced starkly by the contrast between a tethered skipper surviving and an untethered crewmember dying in the same incident.

From a human factors perspective, complacency in calm or moderate conditions, diffusion of safety responsibility in racing crews, and the absence of a mandatory pre-departure safety briefing culture collectively erode compliance with established protocols. Environmental conditions — darkness, slight to moderate sea states, and wind-induced deck movement — act as amplifying factors rather than primary causes, significantly reducing the window for successful detection and recovery once a person enters the water. Hardware deficiencies include the absence or non-activation of personal locator beacons (PLBs), lack of automatic inflating lifejackets with crotch straps, and inadequate jackstay rigging on deck. Procedurally, alert validation failures and over-reliance on passive or single-channel communication systems further compromise emergency response. Taken together, these latent conditions form a predictable and largely preventable causal pathway that continues to result in fatalities across offshore and inshore sailing operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Watchkeeping and Lookout Requirements)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (Distress Alerting and PLB Requirements)
preventable_by: Mandate compulsory pre-departure safety briefings for all crew on racing and passage yachts that include explicit confirmation of harness fitment, tether attachment to jackstays, and PLB activation procedures, with compliance logged by the skipper and verifiable by race organisers or port authorities.

Require all crew members to wear automatically activated personal locator beacons (PLBs) and AIS-equipped MOB devices during night passages and racing events, and establish minimum equipment standards — including fitted safety harnesses with crotch straps and continuous jackstay coverage fore and aft — as a condition of race entry or departure clearance.

Implement structured night watch protocols with a minimum two-person watch system, defined MOB drill requirements at the commencement of each night passage, and a formal MOB alert verification procedure requiring crew-count confirmation before any distress signal is escalated, reducing both undetected real MOB events and false alarm activations that desensitise responders.

representative_cases.description: Sailing yacht crewmember presumed deceased after falling overboard during a race.
MOB from yacht. Unknown vessel recovered the casualty and returned it to the yacht. No injuries sustained.
The vessel was found to be drifting with no persons onboard with the engine running and signs of recent occupancy. Crew member that was missing from vessel when located and was confirmed as deceased.
MOB from a yacht, MOB recovered onto a RIB and is safe and well. No further information provided.

A sailing yacht was taking part in a race when the two crew were thrown overboard. The skipper was tethered and was able to recover to the yacht. The other crewmember was recovered by a nearby fishing vessel and taken by helicopter to hospital where he was declared deceased. The skipper and yacht were towed to port by rescue services.
Man overboard from a yacht. The casualty was pulled aboard and no injuries were reported.
Initially reported as a man-overboard by NFCA on the basis of interrupted phone call. No evidence to support person went in the water.
Sailing vessel crewmember fell overboard during a race. They were recovered by the vessel, which continued on its passage.  Time in water: 3 minutes
A sailing vessel crew member went overboard resulting in missing presumed fatal, another crew member injured.

A recreational sailing vessel had a person over board, recovered with no injuries, no further assistance needed. Both persons were safe and well on board after recovering one from the water and the AIS MOB was switched off. WE HAD A MOB BUT THEYRE BACK ON BOARD.

title: Undetected Hull and Machinery Deficiencies Causing Flooding
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding incidents arising from latent structural and mechanical deficiencies — including failed through-hull fittings, degraded hoses, deteriorated pump casings, and compromised hull integrity — that were not identified prior to the flooding event. The incidents span recreational vessels, commercial fishing vessels, and SAR platforms, indicating the issue is not confined to any single sector. A consistent pattern emerges whereby the underlying physical vulnerability had been developing over time through corrosion, material fatigue, rot, or wear, yet no pre-departure or routine inspection regime was in place or sufficiently rigorous to detect it. Once flooding commenced, inadequate onboard bilge pumping capacity, the absence of damage control materials, and — in several cases — the vessel being operated at night or the crew being asleep, allowed water ingress to progress to a critical or life-threatening stage before effective intervention was possible.

Latent hardware deficiencies represent the foundational cause across this cluster, but their consequences were consistently amplified by compounding human and environmental factors. Absent or inadequate maintenance and pre-departure seaworthiness checks reflect systemic failures in safety culture and regulatory compliance, particularly among recreational vessel operators. Human factors — including limited crew knowledge of damage control procedures, delayed detection due to night-time operations, and sole operators being asleep — removed opportunities for early intervention at each stage of the flooding sequence. Environmental conditions, including rough seas and confused swell, introduced additional mechanical stress and complicated emergency response. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types and operational contexts demonstrates that without structured inspection regimes, minimum onboard damage control capability, and crew competency in emergency flooding response, latent hardware vulnerabilities will continue to manifest as serious or potentially fatal flooding events.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (watertight integrity, bilge pumping arrangements, and machinery maintenance requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (by analogy, applied to damage control readiness and onboard emergency equipment standards)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (safe manning, operational readiness, and the master's responsibility for vessel seaworthiness before departure)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure seaworthiness checklists for all vessel classes — including inspection of through-hull fittings, seacocks, hoses, bilge alarms, and pump operational status — with records retained onboard and subject to periodic audit by flag state or port state control authorities.

Require all vessels to carry a minimum standard damage control kit (including soft patches, expanding plugs, hydraulic cement, and seacock closure tools) and ensure crew are trained and exercised in its use, with competency in identifying and isolating flooding sources forming part of mandatory safety familiarisation.

Implement enforceable planned maintenance system (PMS) requirements — scaled to vessel type and age — that include periodic inspection and replacement schedules for high-risk hull penetrations, pump casings, and associated fittings, with particular attention to wooden-hulled vessels and ageing mechanical components susceptible to corrosion and fatigue.

representative_cases.description: A recreational motor vessel began to flood and the two crewmembers on board called for assistance. A search and rescue vessel located the flooding vessel and disembarked the two crewmembers. The crew of the search and rescue vessel attempted to pump out the water but it was still coming in and they could not keep up with the ingress. They decided that they could do no more and left the vessel to s

Fishing vessel experienced flooding in the engine room as the result of the casing on a service pump breaking. The service pump in questioned pumped salt water to the deck wash pipe and could also be used to generate suction on the bilge system.

A wooden recreational motor vessel sprung a leak whilst it was moored alongside and began to progressively flood before foundering. The single crewmember who was inside the cabin sleeping at the time, was initially trapped but managed to make their way out to the wheelhouse roof and was rescued. They suffered the effects of hypothermia due to the night time air and the cold water but they were not

A sailing yacht had started to take on water, the lone skipper had noticed the ingress when they got up in the morning. A vessel in vicinity attended but was unable to assist, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the yacht with their salvage pump. The ingress was under control and the yacht towed to a mooring as the skipper was fatigued.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was being stood down from service when the bilge alarm sounded. There was water ingress in the engine room. Emergency salvage pumps were used to support the bilge pump in clearing the water ingress while the vessel made it's way back to the station. No injuries to any persons reported.

A fishing vessel started flooding following a crew mistakenly opening a seacock in port. Member of the crew accidentally opened a seacock which instigated the flooding incident. They had no engineering officer aboard.

The crew of a service vessel were carrying out ballasting when one of the crewmembers noted that the tanks were not overflowing when expected. The crew then found water ingress into the engine room. This resulted in partial power failure and the emergency generator automatically started. The main engine continued to operate. The crew then found that the water was coming from a manhole cover that d

A converted trawler was on passage when it began to take on water and the vessel's pumps could not keep up with the ingress. The two crew on board issued a mayday call and abandoned to their tender before being rescued by the local lifeboat service. The vessel partially sank, drifted and later broke up ashore. There were no injuries. There was pollution in the form of debris from the vessel.

A recreational motor vessel with four crew onboard starting taking on water. The vessel still had a working engine and headed to a slipway, the crew started bailing the water out. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel attended to assist with pumping out the water and then escorted the motor vessel to shore.

Lifeboat were tasked to a RHIB taking on water with 7 POB. One lifeboat recovered all persons on board, while another lifeboat towed the RHIB back. Ambulances assessed all casualties, who were confirmed to be in good health.

title: Small Vessel Coordination Failures in Confined Navigational Channels
description:
This theme is characterised by close-quarters situations and contact incidents arising from the uncoordinated movements of small port-support, pilot, and guard vessels in confined, high-traffic waterways, particularly during night-time operations. The incidents share a consistent pattern: a small vessel initiates or alters a manoeuvre without adequately assessing the speed, proximity, or intentions of nearby commercial traffic, creating rapidly developing emergencies in environments where reaction time and manoeuvring margins are already severely constrained. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a systemic procedural gap — the absence of formalised departure and movement coordination requirements for small vessels operating in or adjacent to commercial shipping channels — leaving collision avoidance entirely dependent on the reactive skill of individual mariners rather than structured, predictable protocols. Night-time conditions consistently amplify the consequences of these gaps by reducing visual acquisition range and degrading mutual situational awareness between parties.

From a human factors perspective, failures of situational awareness, lookout, and rule-compliance by small vessel operators are the proximate drivers across this cluster, with operators repeatedly misjudging the speed and proximity of larger vessels or failing to communicate intentions before manoeuvring. Environmental conditions — specifically night-time visibility limitations and moderate wind affecting the handling of smaller vessels — compound these errors by narrowing the window for detection and corrective action. Hardware deficiencies, while comparatively low in this cluster, are not absent; the limited radar and AIS fit typical of small port-support vessels reduces their detectability and tracking capability relative to commercial traffic. The overarching latent cause, however, is organisational: port authorities and VTS operators have not universally mandated real-time communication and movement reporting requirements for small vessels, nor have they established defined separation or coordination protocols at channel entry and departure points, allowing individual operator error to escalate unchecked to near-miss and contact outcomes.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, and 33: navigational watchkeeping, bridge equipment, and distress/coordination obligations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Regulation 12: Vessel Traffic Services (VTS) obligations and coordination requirements in port approaches and confined waters
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for lookout, situational awareness, and officer responsibility during navigation in confined and high-traffic environments)
preventable_by: Port authorities should mandate a formal small vessel movement notification protocol requiring all guard, pilot, and port-support vessels to report intended departures and course alterations to VTS via VHF prior to entering or crossing the commercial shipping channel, with VTS empowered to issue clearances and actively intervene when conflicts are identified.

VTS operators in ports where these incident types are prevalent should implement real-time AIS and radar tracking overlays specifically configured to generate automatic proximity alerts when small vessels approach defined safety buffers around inbound or outbound commercial traffic, enabling early intervention before close-quarters situations develop.

Port operators should require all small vessel operators working in or adjacent to commercial shipping channels to complete mandated night navigation and confined-waters situational awareness training, including scenario-based exercises that simulate the speed differentials, detection limitations, and manoeuvring constraints encountered when operating near large commercial vessels during reduced visibility conditions.

The dredging vessel had to take evasive action to avoid collision with the container shi

The second fishing vessel ignored the instruction and manoeuvred from the berth, cutting ahead of the outbound fishing vessel. The outbound vessel and second fishing vessel took avoiding action to prevent a collision.

Vessel Surge caused by a container ship passing at a reportedly high speed close to a tanker, which was moored. Vessel movement caused the shore aft loading arm to sustain damage and about 30ltrs of jet fuel cargo leaked in the drip tray. No oil lost overboard/or in sea.

The ferry approached the stern of the outward general cargo vessel at 21 knots. Unable to reduce speed sufficiently, the ferry maneuvered into the centre of the channel resulting in the inward chemical tanker leaving the channel and proceeding over the top. No damage or injuries to any crew members

As the support vessel was passing between two vessel, a crew member called to the bridge to advise thrusters were on the cargo vessel, it was called though to stop thrusters due to a another vessel getting close the support vessel counteracted but made contact with the cargo v

At the point of impact the front of the yacht appeared to be turning slightly away from the Floating Bridge. A line was passed to the yacht by another vessel and freed.

title: Deck Machinery Entrapment and Struck-By Crew Injuries
description:
This theme is characterised by a high concentration of hand, finger, and upper limb injuries sustained during deck operations aboard fishing vessels, yachts, and offshore support vessels, arising from direct interaction with powered machinery, moving lines, heavy gear, and fixed structural hazards. The incidents recur predominantly during night-time operations in moderate to rough sea conditions, where vessel motion, reduced visibility, and crew fatigue collectively erode situational awareness and increase the likelihood of misjudged proximity to rotating, tensioned, or swinging equipment. Contributing injury mechanisms include entrapment in winches, capstans, and haulers; crush injuries from doors, anchor chain, and mooring ropes under tension; struck-by events from swinging pots and fishing weights; and entanglement in nets and lines. The severity of outcomes — including traumatic amputations, degloving injuries, and at least one fatality — underscores that these are not isolated lapses but manifestations of systemic deficiencies in safety culture, operational risk management, and equipment design across maritime sectors where informal working practices are normalised.

The latent causes driving this theme span hardware, human, and environmental dimensions in a deeply interrelated manner. On the hardware side, recurring deficiencies include inadequate or absent machine guarding on powered deck equipment, the absence of engineering controls such as dead-man switches and physical barriers at nip points, and the non-use or unavailability of appropriate PPE including cut-resistant gloves and helmets. Human factors are equally significant: gaps in task-specific training for high-hazard machinery operations, absence of enforced safe working distance protocols, jewellery prohibition non-compliance, and a pervasive reactive rather than preventive safety culture — particularly in small-scale commercial and recreational fishing fleets. Environmental conditions, including night operations, sea states of 4 and above, and high winds, consistently amplify these latent weaknesses by degrading physical stability and cognitive performance without triggering operational pause decisions. The frequent requirement for helicopter evacuation and search and rescue intervention further indicates that medical contingency planning and remote operational risk assessment are insufficiently integrated into voyage and task planning across this vessel cohort.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Machinery guarding and structural safety of deck equipment installations
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements — Emergency response preparedness including medical evacuation planning
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation — Safe Manning, watchkeeping, and operational risk assessment in adverse environmental conditions
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce comprehensive physical guarding on all powered deck machinery including winches, capstans, haulers, and net drums, with regular inspection regimes and prohibition on operation when guards are removed or bypassed, supported by flag state and port state control enforcement during vessel inspections.

Implement weather-threshold operational pause protocols that require cessation or modification of high-risk deck tasks — including line handling, pot deployment, and net hauling — when wind speed, wave height, or sea state exceed defined limits during night operations, with these thresholds formally embedded in vessel safety management systems and pre-departure risk assessments.

Establish mandatory task-specific safety training for all crew operating or working in proximity to powered deck equipment, including competency verification on machinery hazard awareness, PPE selection and use, and emergency first response for traumatic injuries, with particular focus on fishing vessel and small commercial vessel operators where informal working practices and safety culture deficiencies are most prevalent.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel crew member entrapped their arm in an arm machine resulting in a traumatic below the elbow amputation. The crew member was evacuated by helicopter and transferred to hospital.

Dive support vessel crewmember sustained hand and finger injuries and was airlifted to shore. The injury details state that the casualty had a broken hand and finger and the decision to airlift was due to the vessel's location.
A fishing vessel crew member caught their hand in the door resulting in the amputation of a finger, as a result, they were taken off and taken to the hospital for further treatment for their injury.
A fishing vessels crew member hit head on a net drum resulting in a superficial head wound.
A yacht crewmember got their hand caught in an electric winch and died as a result of their injuries.
A recreational fishing vessel crew member was hit in the head with a fishing weight.
Crew member on a yacht caught their middle finger in the capstan and the top is hanging off. The casualty managed to bandage it and return back to port for further medical assistance.
A fishing vessel crewmember severed the tops of three of their fingers. They were rescued by helicopter and taken to hospital.
A fishing vessels crew member was hit with a crabbing pot resulting in a injury to the ribs and medivac'd from the vessel.
Fishing vessels crew member got struck by a lobster pot resulting in a cut to the forehead and a possible concussion.

title: Crew Injury During Training Drills in Adverse Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries occurring during training evolutions — principally man-overboard drills, crew pick-up exercises, and berthing manoeuvres — where the inherent hazards of the training activity are compounded by adverse environmental conditions and insufficiently robust procedural controls. A consistent pattern emerges across incidents: the routine and repetitive nature of drill execution appears to suppress the same risk controls that would be mandated during actual operations, producing a normalisation of risk in which unsafe working postures, premature crew movement, and exposure to objectively severe sea states are tolerated without formal safety barriers. In several cases, a discrepancy exists between the assessed and actual sea state, suggesting that pre-exercise risk assessments are either cursory or fail to incorporate real-time environmental data, permitting training to proceed under conditions that exceed the safe parameters implied by the drill design.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including the absence of anti-slip surfaces on sponsons, inadequate handholds on inshore rescue boats, and the inadvertent positioning of hazardous impact surfaces such as conning consoles within fall zones — create physical environments that are poorly engineered for dynamic crew movement in adverse conditions. Human factors, including misjudgement of vessel motion, impaired spatial orientation in night-time conditions, and a culturally embedded pressure to complete training evolutions without interruption, further erode the margins of safety. Environmental stressors, particularly the combination of reduced night-time visibility and elevated sea states, systematically degrade crew situational awareness and physical stability yet are frequently underweighted in pre-exercise risk assessments. Collectively, these latent conditions reveal a systemic gap in training safety governance: the absence of explicit competency gates, enforced environmental thresholds, and mandatory physical restraint requirements that would constrain training activity to conditions commensurate with crew capability and vessel design.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19: Emergency Training and Drills, including requirements for the safe conduct of drills and the condition of life-saving equipment)

SOLAS Chapter V — Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34: Safe Navigation and Avoidance of Dangerous Situations, applicable to risk assessment obligations prior to operations in adverse environmental conditions)

SOLAS Chapter I — General Provisions (Regulation 19: Surveys, and broader obligations on flag state oversight of operational safety standards and the adequacy of safety management systems governing drill conduct)
preventable_by: Establish mandatory environmental threshold criteria for training drill authorisation, specifying maximum permissible wave height, wind speed, and visibility levels for each drill type, with a requirement for real-time verification of actual sea state against those thresholds immediately prior to exercise commencement — not reliance on forecast or reported conditions alone.

Require that any training evolution involving crew movement at or near the vessel's side, across sponsons, or during berthing in adverse conditions includes a documented pre-exercise safety briefing that explicitly identifies physical restraint requirements, mandatory handhold usage, designated safe working positions, and a defined 'all fast' or 'position confirmed' protocol before crew movement is permitted.

Introduce a structured training progression framework for high-risk drills, incorporating competency gates that restrict crews from undertaking combined environmental stressors — such as night operations in elevated sea states — until demonstrated proficiency has been assessed in controlled conditions, with periodic review of drill SOPs to mandate anti-slip surface standards and ergonomic hazard elimination on vessels used for training evolutions.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue vessel was carrying out a MOB drill with a tennis ball when one of the crew members fell over board. The crewmember was kneeling inside the search and rescue boat, leaning over the side when they overbalanced and fell into the water. They remained hanging onto the grab lines on the side of the boat and were able to get back on board the boat. They were wearing an inflatable lif

Lifeguards were practicing crew pick ups. On the approach to the casualty, crew came across the IRB from starboard side to port, as instructed by the helm. Crew slipped from the sponson and fell into the water, resulting in a person over board. Helm welfare checked both crew and “casualty”, who we were being using for training. Due to conditions and following procedure in standard operating proced

Workboat crewmember attempted to disembark the boat as it was coming alongside a pontoon, and before it had been properly secured. As she pushed off from the boat it moved away from the pontoon and the crewmember lost balance and fell backwards onto the conning console. She was attend3ed by paramedics 15 minutes later and walked off the boat with assistance 90 minutes after the accident. She was t

On board an inshore rescue boat a passenger who had been taking part in a person over board drill fell over board. Reportedly they were not holding onto the designated hand hold and the sponson was wet. They slipped into the water and were reboarded the craft with the assistance of the crew. They were not injured by the incident and were wearing a wet suit.

A cargo vessel crewmember was taking part in what was described as a 'physically demanding' training activity when they felt light headed and fainted. The crewmember was given first aid and taken to the ship's hospital where they were administered oxygen and monitored for half an hour. They were then stood down and rested before returning to work after 24 hours. It was reported that the crewmember

Whilst opposite the station the opportunity was taken to demonstrate the boats capabilities and conduct a general performance check including manoeuvres at speed (circa 20 knots). These are a regular manoeuvre during training to emulate condition

A search and rescue vessel was out on an exercise when the vessel slammed into a wave. This caused a crew member to lose their balance and collide with part of the vessel superstructure, resulting in two broken ribs. There was no damage to the vessel and no other injuries.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was conducting training when the helm proceeded to do a tight turn and a crew member went overboard. The helm immediately turned to avoid striking the crew and recovered them back onboard. No injury/damages reported.

A search and rescue vessel was taking part in a training exercise when the crewmember on the helm fell overboard whilst performing a turn. The remaining crewmember on board stopped the engine and assisted the crewmember back on board.

A tanker was heaving its anchor when an unexpected ground swell occurred. A wave hit the vessel resulting in a crew member falling against an anchor windlass and fracturing their arm. First aid was provided and tele medical assistance was requested where it was advised that the crew member be evacuated. title: Manual Tool and Catch Handling Injuries from Procedural Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by preventable lacerations, puncture wounds, and soft tissue injuries sustained during routine manual tasks involving knives and hazardous catch species aboard both fishing vessels and commercial ships. The incidents share a common thread of procedural non-compliance or absence, whether through unsafe cutting techniques directed toward the body, failure to wear mandated PPE such as cut-resistant gloves, or the handling of venomous species without species-specific safe handling protocols. The recurrence of these injuries strongly suggests systemic latent failures in safety culture, procedural enforcement, and hazard identification rather than isolated lapses in individual judgement. Normalised deviance — where the routine disregard of protective measures becomes culturally embedded and tacitly accepted — appears to be a primary driver across multiple operational contexts, from the fishing deck to the ship's galley.

Environmental conditions consistently compound these procedural shortcomings. Night-time operations conducted in significant wave heights and elevated wind speeds introduce vessel motion and reduced situational awareness, transforming otherwise manageable manual tasks into high-risk activities. The discrepancy identified between reported sea states and measured wave heights in several incidents raises additional concerns regarding accurate environmental risk assessment prior to task commencement. Human factors including fatigue, habitual bypass of safety requirements, and inadequate supervisory oversight further erode the already fragile procedural barriers. Hardware considerations, notably the absence of cut-resistant PPE, inadequate task lighting, and the lack of species-appropriate handling equipment, remove the last line of defence against injury. Collectively, these factors indicate that the barriers protecting crew from harm during manual handling tasks are insufficiently layered, poorly enforced, and rarely verified.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures, emergency response, and onboard medical care obligations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe working conditions, environmental risk assessment, and situational awareness obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (safety management systems, procedural compliance, hazard identification, and normalised deviance prevention)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce mandatory task-specific safe working procedures for all knife use and hazardous catch handling operations, including a formal requirement to cut away from the body, use cut-resistant gloves and forearm protection, and apply species-specific handling protocols for venomous catch such as dogfish; compliance must be verified through regular drills and documented supervisory checks.

Introduce environmental go/no-go decision criteria for high-risk manual tasks conducted on deck or in the galley, requiring skippers and duty officers to formally assess wind speed, wave height, vessel motion, and lighting conditions before authorising cutting or hazardous handling operations at night or in adverse weather, with task postponement or additional control measures mandated when thresholds are exceeded.

Establish a structured PPE compliance assurance programme under the vessel's Safety Management System, including scheduled audits of glove and protective equipment availability and use in both deck and galley environments, mandatory reporting of PPE non-compliance as a near-miss event, and targeted safety culture interventions to address normalised deviance where protective equipment use has been routinely bypassed.

A crew transfer vessel crewmember was working in the engine room on a fire flap when their finger was cut whilst removing the metal cover. A replenishment vessel crew member was cutting cheese in galley with a knife, the knife slipped and the blade caught the crew member's wrist resulting in a minor laceration. The crew member was treated by the onboard medic and was able to resume duties as normal.

A cruise ship crewmember was using a meat slicing machine when the block of meat shifted out of position. Without stopping the machine, the crewmember tried to reposition the block of meat and caught their finger on the blade causing an injury to their finger. The crewmember was wearing plastic food handling gloves. They were off work for four days as a result of the injury. They lost some of the

title: Propulsion Control Failures During Critical Maneuvering Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by the sudden or undetected failure of propulsion or engine control systems at the most operationally demanding phases of a voyage — typically during final approach to berth, compass calibration in confined waters, or departure from a fuel berth. Across all representative incidents, the common thread is not simply that machinery failed, but that the failure occurred without warning, without adequate pre-maneuver verification, and without effective contingency protocols to arrest the escalation from equipment fault to structural contact. The cluster spans vessel types from passenger ferries and cement carriers to fishing vessels and crew transfer vessels, indicating that the underlying systemic deficiencies — inadequate predictive maintenance, absent or unfit alarm systems, and underdeveloped emergency response procedures — are not sector-specific but reflect broader industry-wide gaps in managing propulsion reliability during high-risk maneuvering evolutions.

Latent hardware deficiencies, including faulty actuators, degraded clutch and telegraph systems, and unmonitored control-system faults, form the foundational failure layer in each incident; these faults existed prior to the maneuver but were not surfaced by maintenance regimes or condition-monitoring systems. Human factors then compounded the mechanical failures: masters and crew were denied reaction time by the absence of alarms, emergency stop procedures were either not executed or failed to function, and communication breakdowns between pilots and masters prevented coordinated corrective action. Environmental conditions — night-time operations reducing situational awareness, moderate-to-fresh winds degrading vessel controllability, and sea states imposing drift forces — consistently narrowed the margins available for recovery once propulsion control was lost. The interaction of these three layers, latent hardware vulnerability, inadequate human procedural response, and adverse environmental conditions, explains both why these incidents recur and why they so frequently result in contact damage even when the initial propulsion anomaly might have been manageable under better-prepared circumstances.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Parts C, D and E covering machinery reliability, control systems, alarms, and emergency arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulations 14 and 34 covering crew sufficiency, safe manning, and voyage/maneuver planning including contingency planning)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code requirements for planned maintenance, emergency preparedness, and master-pilot communication procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate pre-berthing and pre-maneuvering propulsion system readiness checks as a formal, signed-off bridge procedure, including verification of telegraph response, engine-to-propeller correspondence, and emergency stop operability, with any discrepancy constituting a hard stop on commencing the approach until the fault is resolved or a documented contingency plan is in place.

Require installation, regular testing, and maintenance of independent engine control and telegraph mismatch alarm systems that provide immediate audible and visual alerts on the bridge whenever propeller response deviates from telegraph or throttle commands, ensuring crews receive actionable warning during the maneuver rather than discovering the fault through vessel behavior.

Implement mandatory contingency planning as a documented component of every port entry or berthing plan, requiring the master — and pilot where one is embarked — to brief the full bridge team on single-engine or total propulsion loss scenarios before entering the final approach phase, including pre-assigned crew stations, tug availability confirmation, anchor deployment readiness, and a clear pilot-master communication protocol to be followed in the event of a propulsion emergency.

The vessel then drifted onto the nearby tanker jetty causing damage to the jetty and minor damage to the vessel. The cause of the contact was attributed to the propulsion failure. The propulsion failure was reportedly cause by a faulty actuator related to the clutch and telegraph for

A fishing vessel left a fuel berth in port and proceeded to a refit berth, as the vessel came towards the berth the skipper went to go astern, however, the vessel stayed going ahead.  The skipper attempted again to engage astern and applied throttle however the vessel continued on its course making contact with the quay. The vessel sustained damage to the bow.

A crew transfer vessel was returning to port from an offshore windfarm when it experienced propulsion failure and contacted the breakwater at a speed of approximately five knots. This resulted in minor damage to the fendering on the bow of the vessel and a crew member and a passenger sustained bruising to their knees. The port jet unit of the vessel experienced control failure which lead to the st

A cement carrier was departing from port on river passage with a pilot on board when there was a momentary propulsion failure. The bridge team observed that the revolutions suddenly dropped and the controls for the main engine switched to 'engine control'. Approximately two seconds later, the main engine control was ready for 'service on bridge' and the master took back control of the main engine

A general cargo vessel had departed a lock and was preparing to berth. The pilot reported that there was a control failure of the port main engine whilst the propeller pitch was set ahead. During this time the vessel had begun to swing to port and was unable to stop due to the engine failure. This resulted in the vessel striking the quay side. Both vessels sustained minor damage but no pollution reported. An inspection of the vessel was carried out to determine why the hydraulic drive belt slip

Before departure, the vessel had carried out some maintenance on the telegraph and before unberthing, the bridge team tested the telegraph in the presence of the pilot and no issues were detected. However, once the stern lines had been let go, the pilot operated the telegraph to move ahead, but no wash or ahead motion was

Cruise ship was berthing when the crew noticed that the speed should be reduced. The crew member moved from the bridge centre console to the bridge starboard wing console and tried to take control however, it was not working. They assumed they had lost control of the engines. The vessel increased speed before it was noticed that astern propulsion was being applied. The vessel made contact with a f

title: Crew Injury from Falls During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries resulting from falls, trips, and loss of balance occurring predominantly during night-time operations across a range of vessel types, from sailing yachts to offshore supply ships and replenishment tankers. The incidents share a consistent pattern: a crewmember is moving around or working on deck or below in reduced visibility conditions, often in adverse sea states, without adequate procedural controls governing safe movement or task execution. The consequences range from dislocated hips and broken limbs to head and neck trauma, and in several cases the injury itself generated secondary operational emergencies — most notably where a minimal crew complement was rendered unable to complete basic vessel handling after a single casualty. These incidents recur because the combination of night conditions, vessel motion, and structural or task-specific hazards is predictable yet insufficiently controlled at the procedural and organisational level.

The latent conditions underpinning this theme operate across all three principal factor domains. Environmentally, reduced night-time visibility degrades hazard perception, while moderate wind and sea states introduce vessel motion that directly destabilises crew footing and working posture. Hardware factors include fixed structural hazards such as canopy frames and open bilges positioned in proximity to crew movement and working areas, unguarded deck obstructions, and fittings that require excessive force to release — all of which amplify injury severity when a fall occurs. Human factors are consistently rooted in the absence of pre-task risk assessment, failure to adopt stable three-point contact postures or use available safety equipment such as jackstays and handholds, and inadequate situational awareness in low-light conditions. At the organisational level, the defining latent failure is the absence of robust procedural controls — including safe movement protocols for night passages, minimum safe manning risk assessments, and task-level hazard identification for routine maintenance — that would interrupt the chain of causation before injury occurs.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe movement, personal protective equipment, and crew safety procedures on deck)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Structure, Subdivision and Stability (structural arrangements, machinery spaces, and safeguarding of open bilges and fall hazards)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (passage planning, watchkeeping arrangements, and minimum safe manning considerations for short-handed operations)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory pre-departure and pre-task risk assessments for night passages and engine room maintenance that explicitly identify fall hazards, minimum safe crew manning requirements, deck obstructions, and the proximity of unguarded fall zones such as open bilges, with mitigations documented and briefed to all crew prior to commencement.

Establish and enforce a formal safe movement protocol for adverse conditions and night operations, requiring crew to use jackstays, tethers, and designated handholds when moving on deck, and mandating stable three-point contact postures and braced working positions when applying force to fittings or working near open machinery spaces.

Conduct routine vessel safety audits to identify and remediate fixed structural hazards in crew movement areas — including canopy frames, unguarded bilge openings, and deck obstructions — through physical guarding, high-visibility marking, improved task lighting in engine rooms and on deck, and updated vessel-specific risk registers that reflect the operational profile of short-handed or night passages.

representative_cases.description: Sailing yacht was in passage when a crew member suffered a fall onboard which resulted in them ramming against the canopy with their head, also additionally a jarred neck injury was also sustained to them and they were met at a port where a coastguard lifeboat met them and they were then taken further  via ambulance to a local hospital for further treatment

A sailing vessel crewmember broke their leg after tripping on deck. The other crew member was not able to bring the vessel alongside on their own so they called for assistance. An crew member of a replenishment tanker was working in the engine room on a generator fuel housing using a spanner to remove the fitting. The fitting was tight and required the crewmember to exert some force. Suddenly, the fitting came loose and due to the direction the crewmember was pulling, they fell into the bilge, resulting in a broken rib and bruises to the back. The IP fell due to the forc

Before they landed, they hit some part of their body on the tugger rope which may have reduced the impact of the fall. Yacht crewmember fell and dislocated their hip while in a marina. CRT were tasked to assist the ambulance service and the casualty was extracted from the vessel and passed to the care of the ambulance service.

A motor cruiser was alongside when a crew member fell and injured their back and possible fractured ribs. A shore rescue team and ambulance were tasked to the scene where the crew member was given pain relief before being evacuated and transported for hospital for an assessment and further treatment.

During the sealing, sea spray came over the bow causing a crew member to slip and hit their leg against the forecastle steps. Fi
