The crew of the vessel were able to recover it successfully and found that the damaged sponson had begun to deflate. They repaired the sponson with a temporary repair kit and
Normal training recovery onto slipway, propellor made contact.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was recovering stern first into the floating boathouse, the starboard propeller struck the cradle. The cradle had not been fully lowered which resulted in a starboard propeller being entirely damaged and in need of replacement.

title: Mooring Line Failure and Snap-Back Injury Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated failure of mooring lines and associated deck fittings under dynamic loading conditions, resulting in either direct snap-back injuries to crew or high-potential near-miss events. The incidents span a range of vessel types — including tankers, ro-ro ferries, bulk carriers, tugs, and cruise ships — and operational contexts such as berthing, unberthing, lock transits, and cargo operations, indicating that the root causes are systemic rather than vessel- or operation-specific. A consistent pattern emerges: mooring lines part under loads that exceed their working capacity, driven by combinations of environmental forces (wind, tide, swell, and confined waterway surge), inadequate mooring configurations, and latent equipment degradation. Night-time conditions are a near-universal contextual factor across this cluster, significantly impairing the crew's ability to visually detect early indicators of line distress such as fraying, excessive elongation, or vibration. The persistence of this pattern across the incident database indicates a failure of systemic safety controls rather than isolated human error.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including ageing mooring lines with inadequate inspection and retirement policies, corroded or fatigue-compromised bollards, and fittings not rated for dynamic shock loads — are recurring contributors that often remain undetected until catastrophic failure. Human factors compound these equipment vulnerabilities: crew members are repeatedly found within or adjacent to snap-back danger zones, exclusion zone protocols are inconsistently enforced, and bridge-to-deck communication during high-risk mooring evolutions is frequently inadequate. Environmental conditions, particularly night operations in moderate wind or tidal streams, reduce situational awareness and delay the recognition of developing hazards. At the organisational level, the incidents collectively point to deficiencies in pre-operational mooring assessments, the absence of dynamic re-evaluation of mooring arrangements as conditions evolve during a vessel's time at berth, and insufficient fleet-wide standardisation of mooring safety culture. The high proportion of near-miss outcomes in this cluster should not be interpreted as evidence of acceptable risk management; rather, they represent a latent injury risk of the highest severity that has not yet fully materialised.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (including structural integrity of deck fittings and mooring equipment)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety, protective equipment, and procedures during deck operations)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (bridge team management, situational awareness, and operational decision-making including night operations and environmental go/no-go criteria)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory snap-back exclusion zone enforcement protocols for all mooring operations, supported by clearly marked deck zones, night-rated lighting at mooring stations, and pre-evolution briefings that explicitly assign crew positioning responsibilities and prohibit entry into danger zones while lines are under tension.

Establish a fleet-wide mooring equipment inspection and retirement programme with documented criteria for line condition assessment (including retirement thresholds for age, load history, and visual defects), mandatory bollard and fitting load-rating verification, and a post-incident inspection protocol requiring formal damage assessment after any line parting or dynamic load event.

Introduce dynamic mooring risk assessments as a standing operational requirement for all berthing, unberthing, lock transit, and cargo operations, incorporating real-time environmental data (wind speed, tidal stream, sea state) against mooring configuration capacity, with defined go/no-go criteria for night mooring evolutions in adverse conditions and a structured bridge-deck communication protocol throughout the evolution.

representative_cases.description: A vehicle crewmember was struck by a parted mooring line during mooring operations and suffered an open fracture to the leg.
A ro-ro vessel was in a lock when it parted one of its stern lines. Reportedly, there was no indication that the line was about to part however there were no injuries or further damage sustained.
A tug was assisting another vessel when the two lines parted resulting in loss of bollard. The tug continued to assist the vessel and no other damages were reported.
Bulk carrier's stern line parted due to high winds and tide. There was no other damage reported and the line was replaced.
The vessel parted a stern line whilst running down in the lock for departure. Unknown if there was any damage
The vessel parted a line whilst berthed. Unknown if there was any damage
A fishing vessel was hauling pots when a short length of rope came back on board in a whipping motion and hit one of the crewmembers, causing a minor injury. There were no damages.
It was reported that a tanker's spring line got caught whilst the vessel was unberthing and recoiled towards the vessel.
A cable laying vessel was making fast when the tightening stern line and bollard sheered. There were no injuries.
Chemical tanker's spring line parted resulting in no injuries.

title: Capsize Risk in Surf and Breaking Wave Environments
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated capsize of small vessels — including tenders, RHIBs, and recreational craft — in surf zones, breaking wave environments, and areas of localised wave amplification. A persistent pattern across these incidents is the mismatch between reported sea state classifications and actual wave conditions encountered, suggesting systemic underassessment of environmental risk at the point of decision-making. Compounding this, several incidents occurred at night, where degraded visibility fundamentally impaired crew ability to read wave sets, time manoeuvres, and identify hazardous localised features such as spits and shoals. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a latent procedural failure: formal dynamic risk assessment frameworks — including defined wave height or sea state thresholds for specific vessel types and crew competency levels — are either absent, not consistently applied, or suppressed by operational urgency, particularly in SAR contexts where the imperative to assist a casualty generates urgency bias that compresses or bypasses risk tolerances.

Human factors play a central role across this theme. Helm inexperience or insufficient surf-specific training results in mistimed manoeuvres and failure to execute wave avoidance techniques effectively, particularly under time pressure and in low visibility. The suitability of vessel types — notably small tenders and inflatable RHIBs — for surf zone or breaking sea operations is frequently not assessed prior to launch, representing a hardware-selection gap with direct safety consequences. Environmental conditions, including wave height exceeding sea state classifications due to localised amplification, wind-driven sea generation, and night-time lighting deficits, consistently exceed the assumed safe operating envelope. The combination of under-specified equipment, undertrained crews, inadequate environmental assessment, and urgency-driven decision-making creates a latent failure pathway that is activated whenever vessels are committed to breaking wave environments without structured go/no-go criteria.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34: Safe Navigation and Avoidance of Dangerous Situations; Regulation 14: Ships' manning)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (survival craft suitability and deployment procedures in adverse conditions)
SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (flag state oversight of operational safety standards and voyage risk assessment obligations)
preventable_by: Develop and mandate formal go/no-go surf threshold criteria for all small vessel and tender operations, specifying maximum wave height, sea state, visibility, and crew certification requirements by vessel type, with explicit prohibition on waiver under SAR urgency unless a documented dynamic risk assessment is completed and authorised by a shore-based duty controller.

Require surf and breaking wave competency training as a mandatory, periodically assessed qualification for all crew operating RHIBs, tenders, or small craft in coastal or nearshore environments, with training conducted at progressively challenging conditions and explicitly including night operations to ensure proficiency under realistic degraded-visibility scenarios.

Implement real-time environmental data integration into operational planning tools, cross-referencing reported sea state classifications against available wave buoy, forecast model, and radar data to alert operators to discrepancies between classified and actual conditions — particularly in areas prone to localised wave amplification near spits, shoals, and surf zones — and trigger mandatory reassessment before vessel launch.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue vessel was launched to assist an injured person on a beach. Due to the surf, the crew launched their tender with two crew on board. However it capsized in the surf. The two crewmembers were ejected into the water but made their way to the beach unharmed.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was conducting a training exercise in surf.  The helm tried to turn and run from a wave but got caught beam on and capsized. The crew righted the vessel and were able to self-recover to shore. No reported injuries, no reported damage.

An inflatable RHIB capsized when approaching a spit due to breaking waves causing the vessel to broach. There were three people on board, two of whom were thrown into the water. The two people in the water were recovered by the local lifeboat service. The crewmember who managed to remain on the upturned hull remained with the vessel and was assisted back to port by the local lifeboat service. Ther

An unmanned survey vessel capsized after being struck by a wave. The USV's movements were monitored by emergency tracking device and the coastguard were notified. A support vessel was deployed and recovered the vessel and towed them to harbour. The USV sustained superficial damage to non-structural components.

Four ribs came towards a passenger vessel at high speed crossing the river intermittently, the Master of the passenger vessel slowed and prepared to stop when one of the ribs crossed directly in front of them. The rib owner panicked then lost control and the vessel capsized. The single occupant was under the water for a few seconds then resurfaced unharmed, the passenger vessel was then subjected

A recreational motor vessel was anchored with two crew onboard, as they were preparing to leave a wave capsized the vessel with the two crew entering the water, both crew were wearing lifejackets. A passing vessel recovered the two crew who had been in the water for about ten minutes and were found to be very cold. Both crew were transferred to a search and rescue (SAR) vessel that had been tasked

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to provide safety cover and possible recovery for a casualty from a rock shelf using the daughter craft. The daughter craft was launched with two crew onboard and after a brief between the crew and a coastguard team ashore, the craft approached the shelf in front of the coastguard team who were on the cliff above. The craft broached on a following wave a

Search and rescue (SAR) assets were tasked to conduct a search for a missing person. One of the SAR vessels launched the daughter craft to conduct a search in shallow water, after approximately two hours of searching the vessel was capsized by a larger set of waves. Both crew were recovered from the water with one crew member going to hospital for an assessment and later discharged. The daughter c

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on a routine training session near to shore before returning to base for a crew change.  The crew had set off and just before the incident they were parallel running within the surf zone.  A wave developed in front of the SAR vessel and the helm turned to run away/ parallel to it.  As the wave broke it was matching the speed of the vessel when the helm decided

A RIB (rigid inflatable boat) capsized in surf and the two crew members were rescued. The boat was secured and recovered later on by the owner. The two crewmembers sustained minor injuries and spent between five to ten minutes in the water before they were rescued.

title: Propulsion Failure Due to Latent Machinery Maintenance Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by complete or partial loss of propulsion arising from undetected mechanical or electrical degradation within vessel power plants, cooling systems, and associated drivetrain components. Across the incident set, a consistent pattern emerges whereby developing hardware faults — including solenoid wear, cooling system failures, oil leaks, and electrical system degradation — were not identified prior to departure or during routine operations, ultimately manifesting as total propulsion loss at sea or in navigationally sensitive areas. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel types ranging from small fishing vessels to general cargo ships and motor pleasure craft indicates a systemic failure of maintenance culture and regulatory oversight rather than isolated random mechanical events. The consequences were largely mitigated by favourable environmental conditions, proximity to assistance, and effective crew responses; however, the operational outcomes were largely fortuitous rather than the product of robust risk management.

Latent hardware degradation is the primary causal driver across this theme, with procedural deficiencies serving as the enabling condition that allows such degradation to progress undetected to the point of operational failure. The absence of documented planned maintenance systems, inadequate pre-departure machinery checks, and the lack of predictive component lifecycle management collectively prevent early identification of developing faults. Human factors, while less prominent in isolation, are embedded in the procedural failures — specifically in the normalisation of deferred maintenance and insufficient watchkeeping vigilance for early warning indicators such as abnormal temperatures, pressures, or fluid levels. Environmental conditions, including night-time operations, moderate sea states, port approaches, and isolated waters, did not cause these incidents but materially increased their consequence severity by reducing the margin for safe recovery once propulsion was lost. Addressing this theme requires structural improvement in maintenance governance, particularly for smaller commercial vessel classes where planned maintenance system requirements and enforcement remain inconsistent.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E — Additional requirements for machinery, boiler and electrical installations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — planned maintenance, deficiency reporting, and safety management systems)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, watchkeeping, and emergency preparedness in navigational contexts)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce the implementation of a documented Planned Maintenance System (PMS) for all commercial vessels, including smaller fishing and pleasure craft, requiring structured inspection intervals, component lifecycle records, and pre-departure machinery checks certified by the vessel master — with flag state or port state control verification during inspections.

Introduce mandatory predictive maintenance requirements for critical propulsion components identified as high-failure-risk (including solenoids, cooling water pumps, and fuel system actuators), specifying maximum service intervals and replacement schedules based on manufacturer guidance and operational hours, with non-compliance treated as a deficiency preventing departure.

Require all commercial vessel operators to incorporate propulsion system readiness verification into voyage planning procedures, including underway monitoring protocols with defined response thresholds for early warning indicators (abnormal temperatures, oil pressure, coolant levels), and ensure crew are trained to recognise and act on these indicators before loss of propulsion occurs.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel experienced power failure and was towed back to port. The vessel was at anchor so was not in immediate danger. Another fishing vessel stood by until the lifeboat arrived and towed the vessel back to a safe haven. There were no injuries, damages or pollution reported.

A general cargo vessel experienced propulsion failure during departure from a port with a pilot on board. The pilot requested permission to anchor in the channel. The port also arranged a tug to stand by until the vessel regained propulsion. The issue was caused by a failure on a solenoid. The vessel crew heaved the anchor and proceeded outbound without further issue.

A potter fishing vessel suffered propulsion failure and called for assistance. Another fishing vessel provided assistance in the form of a tow back to harbour. There were no injuries, pollution or damages.

A motor pleasure craft experienced an engine malfunction leading to propulsion failure. The vessel was able to anchor but due to the incident happening in the approaches to a port, the local life boat service was dispatched to tow them back to prevent the vessel from becoming a hazard to navigation for approaching vessels. There were no injuries, damages or pollution, however the engine did leak o

A stern trawler experienced propulsion failure after the cooling water system for the main engine malfunctioned. There were no other vessels in the area to render assistance, so the fishing vessel issued a very high frequency (VHF) radio alert and the local lifeboat service responded and towed the vessel back to port.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on exercise when suffered a propulsion failure. The vessel was unable to restart the engine, they were towed back by a another SAR vessel. No further damages or injuries reported.

A side trawler fishing vessel experienced propulsion failure whilst approaching port. The vessel issued a VHF call for assistance after asking other fishing vessels in the area for a tow, but none were able to provide one as they did not have suitable equipment. The vessel was given some oil by another fishing vessel but were unable to start their engine because it was in a 'safe mode'. The coastg

A cargo vessel was under pilotage on passage to a port, the pilot asked for the speed to increase but nothing happened. Attempts were made to resolve the problem but it didn't work so an anchor was deployed. Once crew were able to get the engine working (slow ahead only), the cargo vessel was then escorted by two tugs to a nearby dock. The cargo vessel had no further incidents, crew did not know t

A Very Large Container Ship (VLCS) experienced propulsion failure whilst outbound in a narrow channel from a port. The vessel was under pilotage at the time and had 2 pilots on board. The vessel deployed the anchor and the tugs assisted to hold it in place until slack water, when the vessel could be turned in the channel and towed back to the berth. The main engine could not be restarted and the v

A chemical tanker experienced propulsion failure whilst departing from a berth with the pilot on board. The vessel was without power for approximately 2 to 3 minutes before recovering and proceeding to sea without further incident.

title: Navigational Watchkeeping Failures During High-Risk Transits
description: This theme is characterised by a consistent breakdown in fundamental navigational watchkeeping discipline during high-risk transits, particularly in confined or coastal waters, at night, and during channel approaches. Across vessel types ranging from commercial ro-pax ferries to small fishing vessels, incidents recur because multiple procedural safeguards fail simultaneously: passage plans are absent or disregarded, position plotting is not conducted at appropriate intervals, and the Officer of the Watch or crew become distracted by tasks unrelated to the immediate navigational situation. The resulting loss of situational awareness leaves no effective barrier between the vessel and the hazard. The pattern is especially pronounced during night-time operations, where reduced visual acuity demands greater reliance on procedural and technological controls — precisely those controls that are found to be absent or degraded in each incident examined.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (particularly Regulations 19, 24, 25, and 34, covering navigational equipment, watchkeeping, voyage planning, and safe navigation)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships, requiring operators to establish and maintain safe watchkeeping and bridge procedures
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (in context of distress alerting and SAR activation following contact incidents in coastal waters)
preventable_by: Mandatory enforcement of passage planning requirements for all transits in confined or coastal waters, including pre-departure risk assessments that identify known hazards, specify minimum safe distances, and define position-fixing intervals — with Masters required to formally sign off plans prior to departure and bridge teams trained to treat deviation from the plan as an immediate reportable event.

Prohibit the disabling or muting of navigational alarm systems (including chart plotter proximity and cross-track error alarms) during any transit, and introduce operator-level audits and vessel inspection regimes that verify alarm systems are active and correctly configured, particularly on smaller commercial and fishing vessels where oversight is historically limited.

Implement mandatory bridge watchkeeping standards that explicitly prohibit the Officer of the Watch from conducting non-navigational administrative tasks during channel approaches, coastal transits, or night passages, supported by company SMS procedures, regular bridge resource management training, and structured watchkeeping checklists that require documented position fixes at defined intervals throughout the transit.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel was navigating into port via channel when the bow struck a north cardinal mark. There was a minor dent to the bow visor of the vessel and paint scratches above the waterline. There was no pollution or injures and the vessel was able to continue to port. The vessel reported that the passage plan was not being followed and that positions were not being plotted at suitable intervals.

Fishing vessel crashed into a set of rocks, they made attempts to reverse away from the rocks however  the two crew members onboard were located on top of rocks and rescued by lifeboat and it was confirmed that they didn't require any medical assistance. The fishing vessel had been smashed too much after the lifeboat checked the area it was unsalvageable and subsequently sank

The vessel was on passage when it hit a rock and was taking on water at the entrance to a cove. The lifeboat assisted with pumping out the water and towed the vessel to a local port, all of the crew onboard were wearing lifejackets and no injuries were sustained to any of them

SAR tasked to recover 2 persons overboard from an unnamed pleasure craft, after it struck a navigational buoy. Persons recovered to shore by a fishing vessel , and casualty vessel towed to back to a marina by an unnamed vessel.

A ro-pax vessel was scheduled to depart at low water to then transit to a repair yard. The crew discussed the depth and decided to proceed with caution. The vessel passed a pontoon outbound, after the course was altered to starboard the aft propulsion unit became unresponsive, slowed and then shut down. The unit appeared to have fouled in silt that had built up inside the channel, the vessel used

The vessel was motoring towards the next planned anchorage at 7.5knots in 12 metres of water when they suspected that they touched the sea floor with the keel and the speed was quickly reduced to 4.5knots. The damage sustained was some scratches on the keel and no injuries to many persons onboard .The reason for the accident was they believed there was an uncharted rock or incorrect depth measurem

A tug was in passage along the edge of a buoyed channel when the vessel started to slow down suddenly, it was decided to bring the vessel to a stop. After an investigation, the crew checked the position and manoeuvred further into the channel. The propulsion, echo sounder and bow thruster were checked and were all working correctly. It was one and a half hours after low water, the tide was on the

A sailing vessel struck a post resulting in damage to the hull. The vessel started to take on water, the nine crew onboard were wearing lifejackets, six crew transferred to a tender and the remaining crew attempted to bring the water ingress under control. The crew and vessel were assisted by a shore authority.

A sight-seeing passenger vessel reportedly struck a rock at speed which resulted in water ingress. The vessel was grounded on the beach to carry out damage inspection and repairs. There were no injuries.

A sail training vessel grounded on a submerged wreck. The crew were navigating the vessel near the coast as it was a land mark however the crew underestimated the effect of the tide and the vessel drifted onto a submerged wreck. The crew felt the contact however the vessel did not become stuck and was able to make its way to port. There were no injuries, damage or pollution.

title: Confined Waterway Manoeuvring Failures Under Adverse Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring failure mode in which vessels conducting confined waterway manoeuvres — lock entries, berth approaches, and post-lock transits — encounter environmental conditions that exceed the assumptions underpinning their pre-manoeuvre risk assessments, with no effective procedural mechanism to trigger reassessment or escalate safeguards. The unifying pattern across these incidents is not simply that conditions were adverse, but that the planning process was structurally unable to adapt when the environment diverged from forecast parameters. Decisions on tug deployment, speed management, and berthing sequencing were made against static assumptions, and when wind, tide, or squall conditions exceeded those assumptions, crews and pilots were left improvising with diminishing control options, inadequate infrastructure support, and competing hazard priorities in confined spaces at night. These incidents recur because the latent procedural vulnerability — the absence of dynamic go/no-go triggers and pre-defined contingency decision rules — is rarely identified until a contact occurs.

Latent hardware and infrastructure deficiencies compound the procedural vulnerability significantly. Inadequate fendering, absent guide structures in locks, tight beam clearances, and the co-location of freight and passenger infrastructure with narrow separation margins all reduce the tolerance for manoeuvring error to near zero, meaning that even modest deviations from the intended track result in contact. Human factors, particularly the over-reliance on forecast data rather than real-time environmental observation, confirmation bias in pre-manoeuvre assessments, and the cognitive burden of improvised decision-making under time pressure, consistently erode the quality of in-extremis responses. Night-time conditions further degrade visual situational awareness of drift rates and proximity to structures. The environmental contribution — predominantly wind strength and tidal influence — is not in itself unexpected in these operational settings; rather, it is the systemic failure to treat environmental variability as a primary planning parameter, rather than a residual risk, that allows these incidents to persist across vessel types and port locations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 34-1: Safe Manning, Voyage Planning, and Master's Discretion)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: hazard identification, contingency planning, and dynamic risk assessment obligations)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Regulation 29: Steering gear and manoeuvring redundancy requirements)
preventable_by: Mandate real-time environmental trigger thresholds in port and pilotage manoeuvring plans: pre-departure and pre-entry risk assessments must specify explicit wind speed, gust, and tidal current limits at which tug assistance becomes mandatory, with a formal documented reassessment required whenever observed conditions exceed forecast parameters by a defined margin (e.g., 20%) before or during any confined waterway manoeuvre.

Require ports and terminal operators to conduct periodic infrastructure adequacy reviews for confined manoeuvring areas, specifically assessing lock guide structures, fendering systems, and berth layout clearances against the beam and freeboard envelope of vessels approved to use those facilities under adverse condition scenarios, with deficiencies remediated or vessel-specific operational restrictions imposed until resolved.

Implement pre-manoeuvre briefing protocols for pilots and masters that explicitly identify competing infrastructure hazards (e.g., co-located freight and passenger berth structures) and pre-define a priority decision hierarchy and abort criteria, ensuring that conflicted real-time choices — such as stern protection versus bow alignment — are resolved in planning rather than improvised under time and environmental pressure during the final approach.
