The stern quarter struck the passenger vessel first and then the yacht despite the master using the bow thruster to try to avoid the situation. The ro-

A tug was assisting a bulk carrier during departure manoeuvres in a lock. The bulk carrier moved ahead and pinned the tug between it and the lock gate. This resulted in a dent to the transom of the tug. There were no injuries or pollution.

A barge was departing with a tug as lead tow and a service vessel at the stern, Due to the lack of speed on the barge at the time, the tug altered it's heading causing the barge to move starboard. The service vessel manoeuvred to prevent the barge grounding resulting in the service vessel striking a lateral marker. The marker had fallen over, the service vessel returned later and the crew attempte

A tug was assisting a crude oil tanker with departure manoeuvring when it made contact with the shell plating of the port quarter of the tanker. This allegedly resulted in denting to the shell plating of the tanker. There was no damage reported to the tug. The crude oil tanker was able to depart and continue on passage without further issue. There was no pollution or injuries.

A tug vessel was preparing to move a barge and had connected with bow bridles to the barge’s stern. Both vessels were close to a plinth/bridge support as there was a new bridge being constructed in the vicinity. The barge was close by a plinth and from her position at the stern of the barge, the tug could not see the plinth. Once the barge was ready she lifted her spud legs and indicated to the tu

A tug was assisting a cargo vessel with berthing operations. The tug penetrated the hull of the cargo vessel causing a hole in the ship's side into one of the fuel tanks which caused a small amount of diesel oil to spill into the water. The tug was pushing the vessel against the wind which required more power which reportedly lead to the hole. The action of the tugs engines and wash caused the oil

A research vessel was slipping from the hoist into the water and engaged the engines in and out of astern gently while observing the hoist straps and the bow of the vessel, which was still within the hoist. As the bow of the vessel became clear of the hoist, more power was engaged to the engines in astern, but the ebb flow of the tide had already taken the stern downstream and there was too much f

A crude oil tanker was in bound to port with a pilot on board when the vessel was required to slow in order to make the tugs fast. After the tugs had been made fast, the wind pushed the tanker to the eastern side of the channel and the port quarter of the vessel left the channel between two buoys. The tug on the aft part of the vessel, at the port quater, also left the channel and partially ground

title: Capsize Risk Exacerbated by Inadequate Preparation and Safety Equipment
description:
This theme encompasses incidents in which small vessels — including sailing yachts, dinghies, row boats, and punts — capsized under foreseeable conditions, with outcomes ranging from minor injury to fatality. The unifying characteristic is not the capsize event itself, which in many cases was precipitated by identifiable precursors such as overloading, accumulated water, or adverse sea states, but rather the systemic failure to mitigate the consequences of capsize before departure. Across these incidents, mariners consistently embarked without wearing personal flotation devices, without conducting pre-departure vessel assessments, without float plans or shore-based monitoring, and in several cases without accounting for the specific vulnerabilities of individuals aboard, such as non-swimmers. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a deeply embedded cultural normalisation of risk among operators of small, non-commercial vessels, where the perceived brevity or familiarity of a passage suppresses recognition of the genuine hazards involved, particularly at night or in deteriorating conditions.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including vessels lacking self-draining or self-righting design features, inadequate watertight integrity, and the chronic absence of carried or worn personal flotation devices — created conditions in which a survivable capsize became life-threatening. Human factors were equally pervasive: solo operation removed the possibility of immediate crew assistance; non-swimmers were permitted aboard capsize-prone craft without mitigation; pre-departure inspections were omitted; and capsize recovery procedures were either unpractised or entirely absent. Environmental conditions, including night-time darkness, wind speeds exceeding 35 knots, wave heights up to 2.86 metres, and discrepancies between reported and actual sea states, compounded the difficulty of both self-rescue and external rescue response. In combination, these latent failures transformed individually manageable risk factors into cascading, sometimes fatal, sequences of events that shore-side monitoring, proper equipment, and basic procedural compliance could have interrupted at multiple points.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34: Safe Manning, Passage Planning, and Voyage Planning)
preventable_by: Mandate the wearing of properly fitted personal flotation devices (PFDs/lifejackets) by all persons aboard small open vessels at all times underway, with particular enforcement for night operations, adverse weather conditions, and any voyage involving non-swimmers or vulnerable individuals — supported by pre-departure checklists that include confirmation of PFD use.

Require operators of small vessels to complete and lodge a float plan with a responsible shore-based contact before departure, specifying expected route, return time, and emergency contact details, so that a failure to return triggers a timely and structured search and rescue response rather than a delayed notification.

Introduce and promote mandatory pre-departure vessel readiness inspections for small craft, encompassing assessment of passenger loading against safe carrying capacity, removal of accumulated water, verification of vessel buoyancy and stability, and confirmation that all persons aboard have been assessed for swimming ability — with operators of hire or club vessels required to formally exclude non-swimmers from capsize-prone craft unless appropriate flotation is worn.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht capsized with the one person onboard falling into the water. They were unable to self-recover. A lifeboat was tasked and rescued the casualty and returned them to shore. The lifeboat returned to the vessel which had taken on a lot of water but was taken under tow and returned to shore. The casualty did not need any medical treatment.

A dinghy had 5 people on board when it capsized. One of the individuals had limited to no ability to swim which resulted in them going missing. After a few days they were found floating in the water and confirmed deceased. The dinghy may have been overloaded which caused it to capsize.

A skipper of a small row boat drowned after the row boat capsized shortly after they stepped onto it. They became trapped under the boat and when they got free they attempted to swim to safety but lost their life. They were recovered from the water and CPR was commenced but they didn't regain consciousness. The row boat was unstable due to excess water inside from rainfall.

A person fell overboard from a capsized punt. They spent approximately 30 minutes in the water and was recovered by a workboat and passed into the care of the ambulance services. The vessel sunk in shallow water but is recoverable and the casualty was able to walk to the ambulance.

An 8 person rowing boat with young persons on board capsized. The occupants were rescued and the local search and rescue service secured the vessel. There were no major injuries and the crew were treated by the local ambulance service at the boat house of the rowing club.

A dinghy with crew onboard, capsized in a lake and resulted in both entering the water. Multiple search and rescue assets were tasked, including a helicopter. The helicopter was stood down while on route due to assets on the scene recovering the crew. Both crew were uninjured and were taken care of by ambulance staff.

A sailing catamaran capsized with fourteen crew onboard. A gust caused the catamaran to heel and the jib sheet was not released, failure to spill wind caused a greater heel and with wind under the hull the vessel inverted. The catamaran was briefly grounded before losing rigging, the vessel was partially submerged and then started to drift. A search and rescue vessel was tasked to recover the crew

A person fell overboard from a dinghy when it tipped as two other occupants willingly entered the water. The other person fell into the water. They managed to self recover to the shore where they were met by emergency services and checked over. They were cold and distressed but otherwise unhurt. They were wearing a life jacket.

A member of the public reported that a small vessel with one person onboard had capsized near rocks. The person was able to hold onto the upturned hull as they were not wearing a lifejacket. Search and rescue (SAR) assets were tasked to the scene to recover and provide medical care to the person, as they had been in the water for approximately thirty minutes they were passed into the care of the a

A dinghy with four persons onboard capsized. All persons were rescued from the water by the crew of a yacht with the assistance of another vessel. The casualties were examined and taken ashore by lifeboat and passed into the care of the ambulance service who took the casualties to hospital. It is suspected that alcohol may have been involved.   Time in water: 15 mins.

title: Falls from Ladders: Procedural and Supervisory Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by crew members sustaining serious injuries — and in one case entering the water — as a result of falls from fixed or portable ladders under conditions that were insufficiently controlled. The incidents span a range of vessel types and tasks, yet share a consistent set of enabling failures: crew members working alone without a buddy or standby person, absence of permit-to-work or working-at-height procedures, and inadequate pre-task risk assessment that fails to account for environmental conditions such as reduced night-time visibility, wind, and the inherent hazard of steep ladder designs. In several cases the outcome was severe or potentially fatal, yet depended heavily on chance factors — a neighbouring vessel, a bystander's discovery — rather than planned safeguards. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types and operators points not to isolated lapses but to a systemic normalisation of risk, where ladder work is treated as a routine activity rather than a controlled task requiring formal hazard management.

Latent hardware deficiencies compound the procedural failures: ladders that are not secured against movement, absence of anti-slip features, inadequate or absent handrails on steep access ladders, and insufficient fixed lighting on ladder routes all reduce the margin for error available to the crew member. Human factors — including fatigue, possible medical episodes, incorrect body posture (facing away from the ladder), and diminished situational awareness at night — represent the active failure mechanisms, but these are only consequential because the procedural and physical safeguards that should absorb such variability are absent. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations and wind-induced instability, consistently appear as compounding factors that elevate residual risk to unacceptable levels. Collectively, these findings indicate that the root cause is a latent failure in safety culture and management system rigour, where the hierarchy of controls for work at height is either not established or not enforced at the operational level.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (man-overboard preparedness, rescue and recovery procedures)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Structure, Subdivision and Stability (design and structural integrity of access ladders, hatches, and void space entries)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for risk assessment, safe work procedures, and permit-to-work systems)
preventable_by: Mandate a vessel-specific permit-to-work and risk assessment procedure for all work-at-height tasks involving ladders, explicitly requiring documented controls for night-time working, lone working prohibition, buddy or standby person assignment, and environmental condition thresholds (wind speed, lighting) beyond which the task must be deferred.

Conduct a fleet-wide hardware audit of all fixed and portable ladders to verify compliance with applicable construction standards, including: secure fixing arrangements, adequate handrail provision, anti-slip treads, and sufficient fixed or portable lighting on all ladder routes — with deficiencies rectified and recorded in the planned maintenance system.

Deliver mandatory, competency-assessed ladder safety training for all crew that covers correct three-point contact technique, pre-use ladder inspection, recognition of personal risk factors (fatigue, medical history), and the authority and obligation to refuse unsafe tasks — supported by periodic drills and verifiable records maintained under the vessel's SMS.

representative_cases.description: A cargo vessel crew member was on a ladder carrying out a repair, they were on the steps facing away from the ladder when they slipped and fell resulting in a fractured leg. The crew member was passed into the care of the ambulance service and taken to hospital.

A cargo vessel crew member fell overboard, recovered quickly with no injuries. Crew member slipped from a deck ladder on the outboard side whilst inspecting the contents of the cargo hold on there own. Falling backwards and continuing over the ship’s railing they entered the water. The vessel next to them had slack stern lines that crew member grabbed hold off to assist in recovery while other cre

Cargo vessel's crew member was found unconscious at the bottom of a ladder with apparent facial injuries. There were no obvious trip or slip hazards other than the usual steep ladder. The individual could not remember what had happened. The crew member may have sustained a head injury.

Fishing vessel crewman fell from a ladder while climbing to retrieve a rope while berthing, landing initially on the rail, then tipping head first on to the deck. The casualty sustained serious head injuries and was transported to hospital for treatment, recovering from sedation the next day.

A replenishment tanker crew member was descending through a hatch into a void compartment within the bow visor. The top area of the hatch had a small vertical ladder that was able to move in a fore and aft direction and was secured by two bolts but was not fixed. Whilst descending this small ladder, the crew member lost their grip and fell, sustaining a broken ankle.

Passenger ship's volunteers were working when they decided to take a lunch break. The volunteer descended the vertical fixed ladder and partway down the ladder they fell and broke their lower leg. This resulted in further medical assistance. The ladder is in good condition and all permits to work were correctly completed.

A ro-pax vessel was alongside, two crew member's had completed maintenance tasks and were proceeding to climb down a ladder. The first crew member descended without incident, as the second crew member descended their foot slipped off the ladder and they fell approximately 6 feet on to the deck below, landing on the right foot. First aid was administered and the crew member taken to hospital where

Fishing vessel's crew member was loading the catch from the trip into the fish market when a lift of boxes toppled while they were going down the ladder into the fish room. It was reported that the individual received minor injuries. No further damage was reported.

The cargo ship was tied alongside the pier when a crew member slipped. The crew member was trying to access the engine room prior to leaving berth when they slipped. This resulted in a fall from the ladder into the engine room. The ambulance was contacted  and the crew member was transferred for further checks. The crewmember reported a sore/twisted knee and lower back pain.

A cruise ship crewmember was arranging upper bunkbed sheeting when they missed a step on the ladder and fell to the floor resulting in a overextension of the right ankle which kept the crewman off work for over 72 hours.

title: Engine Room Fires: Latent Defects and Unmanned Vessel Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by fires originating in machinery spaces, driven by latent mechanical or electrical deficiencies — including fuel line degradation, lubrication failures, electrical faults, and inadequate heat shielding — that remain undetected until ignition conditions are reached. A recurring and critical systemic failure is the unmanned status of vessels at the time of ignition, which eliminates the possibility of early human detection and intervention, allowing fires to escalate unchecked. Where crew are present, night operations, moderate to rough sea states, and reduced visibility compound the difficulty of early detection and effective firefighting response. The pattern across these incidents demonstrates that neither routine inspection regimes nor onboard emergency preparedness are sufficient to address the underlying vulnerability: a latent defect that reaches ignition threshold in the absence of adequate monitoring, detection, or suppression infrastructure.

Latent hardware deficiencies represent the primary precondition across this cluster, with fuel and oil systems, electrical installations, and high-temperature engine components collectively forming the most common ignition pathways. These defects are rarely self-evident during standard operational checks, and the absence of structured pre-departure engine room shutdown inspections — particularly before vessels are left unmanned — allows hazardous conditions to persist unrecognised. Human factors manifest principally as procedural gaps: the absence of ventilation and gas detection protocols prior to opening engine compartments, insufficient pre-departure machinery space inspections, and inadequate pollution prevention discipline during emergency response. Environmental conditions, including night operations and elevated sea states, act as compounding factors that increase mechanical stress on propulsion systems and degrade crew situational awareness. The cascading consequences observed — loss of propulsion, water ingress, vessel sinking, mooring failures causing secondary damage, and crew injury — confirm that single-point failures in engine room fire prevention and detection architecture carry the potential for catastrophic, multi-system outcomes.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery Installations
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure and pre-unmanning engine room inspection checklists that explicitly require verification of fuel line integrity, electrical system condition, bilge levels, and heat source clearances before any vessel is left unmanned alongside, with records retained as part of the vessel's safety management system.

Require the installation and maintenance of fixed fire detection and automatic suppression systems in all machinery spaces, supplemented by remote monitoring and alarm notification capability to a responsible person ashore or duty officer, ensuring that fires in unmanned or lightly crewed vessels can be detected and responded to at the earliest possible stage.

Introduce mandatory pre-access engine compartment procedures requiring ventilation of the space and, where practicable, hydrocarbon gas detection testing before hatches or doors are opened, alongside regular crew drills specific to machinery space fire scenarios including escalating casualty management, pollution prevention, and emergency mooring contingencies.

representative_cases.description: A tug vessel sustained an engine fire while alongside and unmanned. The fire self extinguished and the damage found by the crew upon re-boarding. The vessel has been taken to a tug barge for an internal investigation to ascertain the cause.

A pilot vessel with twin engines had a fire on one engine. The fire was successfully extinguished by the 3 crew on board and the vessel was still able to manoeuvre, and went back to port under its own power. Once alongside a precautionary inspection by shore side fire services and surveyors was carried out.

A crew transfer vessel experienced propulsion failure following a fire and subsequent water ingress. The crew on board controlled the fire and extinguished it using the vessel's fire suppression system but were unable to regain propulsion. They also noticed shortly after the fire that there was water ingress. The local lifeboat service launched a boat to assist them who towed them back to the bert

A motor vessel caught fire when a crewmember opened an engine hatch. The three crew evacuated ashore. The vessel continued to burn, breaking free from its moorings, causing minor damage to two other moored boats before sinking. There was damage to the quay where the vessel was berthed. Two crewmembers had a leg injuries and another had foot burns. No one was severely injured or taken to hospital.

A sailing yacht crew member suffered superficial burns after a fire incident occurred on board. The fire came between the floorboards, the crew member evacuated into an inflatable tender before it was fully inflated. The yacht subsequently sunk. The crew member was able to get away from the foundered vessel and once ashore taken to hospital for medical treatment.

A private motor yacht suffered an explosion which resulted in total loss of vessel. There were 2 people on board and 2 dogs. One of the members on board was using a solvent on the engine of the boat which then ignited and went up in flames. The 2 individuals escaped the boat however, the dogs were trapped inside. There was approximately 200 litres of fuel on board that has been spilled. One casual

A HSC Vessel sustained a fire in one the engine rooms, assisted back with SAR using other engine. VESSEL SUSTAINED A FIRE IN THE IN ONE OF THE ENGINE COMPARTMENTS WHICH HAS BEEN SUPPRESSED AND SEALED THE VOID WITH WATER. NO TUGS  ASSISTANCE VESSEL IS UNDERWAY ON ONE ENGINE.

A pleasure motor vessel had a fire on its engine. The single crewmember on board was able to extinguish the fire but was unable to make their way back to port. Another vessel came across them and towed them back to port.

A motor cruiser suffered an engine fire and called a mayday. CRT and lifeboat assets were tasked. The vessel was towed to harbour where it was met by CRT and FRS who confirmed that the fire had been extinguished. No persons were injured.

Unnamed vessel needed to refuel because they were starting run low on fuel , when another person that went on to deliver this fuel via a canoe when it had transferred the fuel it was over excessive quantities of fuel when one of the crew members in the crew began to smoke which then set the fuel alight and it ended up being thrown into the sea. No injuries or any damage to the vessel itself or any

title: Berthing Failures Under Environmental and Procedural Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by contact and allision incidents occurring during berthing and unberthing manoeuvres, where the convergence of adverse environmental conditions — principally wind, current, and reduced night-time visibility — with inadequate procedural controls produces loss of vessel control at the most critical phase of port operations. The incidents are unified not by a single catastrophic failure but by a systemic absence of adaptive risk management: weather-responsive berthing procedures are either absent or not enforced, fendering provisions are undersized or misaligned for the operational envelope, and bridge teams frequently lack structured protocols governing approach speed, angle, and abort criteria under degraded conditions. The recurrence of these incidents reflects an industry-wide tendency to treat berthing as a routine evolution rather than a high-risk manoeuvre requiring dynamic pre-manoeuvre risk assessment.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including inadequate fender spacing, structurally exposed components such as paddle boxes, and poorly configured berth layouts that constrain manoeuvring room — amplify the consequences of human misjudgements that might otherwise be recoverable. Human factors, including degraded depth perception at night, reactive decision-making under time pressure, and normalisation of marginal environmental conditions, reduce the helm's capacity to detect and correct positional errors before contact occurs. Environmental conditions, while not individually extreme, consistently exceed the implicit design assumptions of standard berthing procedures. The core latent cause across all incidents is the absence of a formalised, vessel-specific weather threshold and night-operations berthing protocol that triggers enhanced resource deployment, modified approach parameters, or deferral of the manoeuvre — leaving crews with no structured recovery option once conditions deteriorate beyond their unaided control.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 34-1: Safe Manning, Voyage Planning, and Operational Precautions)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: SMS procedures for high-risk manoeuvres and environmental risk assessment)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 4: Port State Control and flag state oversight of operational safety procedures)
preventable_by: Develop and enforce vessel-specific dynamic berthing risk assessment protocols that define quantified environmental thresholds (wind speed, sea state, visibility) triggering mandatory modified approach procedures, additional tug assistance, or manoeuvre deferral — with master and pilot authority to abort formalised in the Safety Management System.

Conduct scheduled fendering audits at each operating berth to verify that fender type, spacing, and coverage are matched to the vessel's hull geometry, maximum approach angle, and the range of environmental conditions under which night operations are conducted, with deficiencies remediated before continued night-time berthing is permitted.

Implement structured Bridge Resource Management (BRM) protocols specifically tailored to berthing manoeuvres under night-time and adverse weather conditions, including mandatory pre-manoeuvre briefings covering abort criteria, designated lookout positions, communication responsibilities, and contingency actions — with compliance verified through periodic drills and port entry audits.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was returning to berth after being stood down. As the vessel was coming alongside a pontoon the vessel was caught by a large gust of wind and blown off the pontoon. To prevent crushing a small boat, the helm controlled the situation and drift and allided with a larger steel vessel. The crew had fendering out but this did not prevent damage to the guardrail wire and

A cargo vessel made contact with shore side object while berthing, minor damage to hull reported. Vessel made contact with the wharf between fenders. Subsequently the vessel received deformations and removal of paintwork of the hull above the waterline, and a fender on the wharf was damaged at the top of the securing bracket. Their was no reported breach to the vessels hull or pollution in the wat

A passenger vessel was berthing when they landed against the pier with enough force to cause damage to the vessel's paddle box. It was pushed inwards causing it to come into contact with the port side paddle. The vessel was taken out of service for the day and tugs were arranged to move it to a layby berth for repairs to be undertaken.

A container vessel made contact with a locks wall, resulting in minor damages. DURING THE EBB APPROACH (STERN FIRST) INTO LOCK THE SHIP’S BOW WAS MOVED TOO FAST TO THE PORT BECAUSE OF NORTHERLY WIND AND CURRENT THE VESSEL TOUCH THE WALL OF THE LOCK ENTRANCE WHILE MOVING ASTERN.

A general cargo vessel under pilotage was unberthing, a jack-up barge was berthed adjacent to the cargo vessel. As the cargo vessel manoeuvred to starboard, the cargo vessel allided with a nearby ro-ro ramp and the jack-up barge. The cargo vessel was able to manoeuvre clear and returned to a nearby berth. The cargo vessel was holed above the waterline and damaged the aft mast. Damage to the jack-u

General cargo vessel was under pilotage while entering stern first, attempting to berth the vessel made contact with dock bullnose which  as a result had caused a 3 ft vertical dent on its starboard quarter and also  had two tugs assisting.

A general cargo vessel was coming alongside when the port side in the bow quarter made contact with the pier whilst being pushed by a tug. Minor damage was sustained to the wing tank and there were no injuries or pollution.

A ro-ro cargo vessel was berthing when its stern struck the bow of a berthed offshore support vessel. The ro-ro vessel sustained a puncture hole above the waterline and the offshore support vessel sustained damage to some deck equipment. The offshore support vessel sustained damage to its deck crane and bow stanchions as well as secondary hull damage to the port side where it came into contact wit

A tanker with a pilot onboard was backing into a lock from the dock system. The stern tug was asked to stay hard east as the the tanker was entering the lock (the wind was south easterly). Once in the lock the stern was set to the west and even with engines set ahead and port helm ordered the stern made contact with the lock gate. The tanker's crew gave no indication that the vessel was closing on

A ro/ro cargo vessel was approaching its berth when it made contact with quay resulting in damage to the aft port quarter.  The vessel managed to berth alongside safely and the vessel was given a three month condition of class. No pollution or injuries were reported.

title: Machinery Failure During Critical Port Manoeuvring Phases
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated failure of propulsion and auxiliary manoeuvring systems — including main engines, steering gear, bow thrusters, and engine control mechanisms — during the highest-risk phases of vessel operation: port approach, berthing, departure, and pilotage. These incidents cluster around a common operational window where manoeuvring margins are smallest, traffic density is highest, and the consequences of equipment failure are most severe. The recurring nature of these events across vessel types — tankers, container ships, passenger vessels, ro-pax, tugs, and tall ships — indicates that this is not an isolated or vessel-specific problem but a systemic industry pattern rooted in inadequate pre-arrival readiness protocols, deferred preventive maintenance, and insufficient contingency planning for single-point propulsion failures. Night-time conditions consistently feature as a compounding risk factor, reducing crew reaction time and situational awareness precisely when machinery reliability is most critical.

At the latent level, these incidents reflect entrenched hardware deficiencies including aging thruster units, unresolved engine faults carried forward from prior failure events, degraded control linkages, and fuel system vulnerabilities — all of which suggest systemic gaps in planned maintenance system (PMS) adherence and pre-voyage inspection regimes. Human factors are equally significant: bridge teams and engineering departments frequently adopt a reactive rather than proactive posture, allowing known defects to persist and deploying tugs only after failure rather than as a precautionary measure. The ISM Code framework, while providing a structural basis for risk assessment and pre-arrival checks, appears inconsistently applied across operators. Environmental conditions — tidal streams, moderate winds, darkness — do not cause these incidents but reliably transform mechanical failures into high-potential near-misses or contact events. The repeated safe outcomes attributable to tug assistance and pilot intervention mask the underlying systemic vulnerability and may contribute to normalisation of risk at the organisational level.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (steering gear testing, propulsion redundancy, engine control systems)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — maintenance management, pre-departure risk assessment, contingency planning)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, pilotage risk assessment, tug deployment protocols)
preventable_by: Mandate and verify completion of pre-arrival and pre-departure machinery readiness checklists — including functional testing of main engines, steering gear, and bow thrusters — as a hard gate prior to entering confined or port waters, with non-compliance reportable to port state control under ISM audit frameworks.

Require that any vessel with a known or recently experienced propulsion or steering defect undergoes confirmed rectification and sea-trial verification before resuming port approach operations; where defects cannot be resolved, pre-arranged tug escorts must be designated as a mandatory contingency measure rather than a reactive response.

Implement mandatory cross-fleet trend analysis of approach-phase machinery failures within company safety management systems, requiring operators to report and review recurring equipment failure patterns across their fleet and submit corrective maintenance action plans to flag state administrations, supporting early identification of systemic PMS deficiencies before a catastrophic event occurs.

representative_cases.description: A chemical/products tanker had bow thruster failure on approach to the berth. The approach was aborted and the vessel proceeded to an anchorage position. The vessel was later berthed using tug boats.
A container ship suffered main engine failure after a pilot boarded, an escort tug was arranged. The main engine failed a second time as the vessel was in a port area with two tugs attached.
A container ship was departing port under pilotage and with two tugs assisting when it experienced main engine failure. The vessel with assistance from the tugs returned to a berth in port.
A small passenger vessel lost engine while approaching berth and was assisted in getting safely alongside due to the wind conditions. There were no injuries or damage to the vessel.
Cargo vessel was underway when it collided with an anchored tanker. This occurred due to a steering gear failure. No damage reported other than scratching of the paint on the cargo vessel.
A cargo vessel collided with a jetty whilst berthing, extensive damage to jetty and vessel lost its portside anchor. Vessel bow thruster malfunctioned during berthing resulting in the contact.
Hopper dredger was making a strong tidal approach to port when the engine stalled. This was reported as a near miss.  No damages or pollution reported.
A general cargo vessel had an engine failure before going to anchor. Two tugs assisted the vessel into port.
A chemical tanker was berthing when it experienced bow thruster failure. The pilot decided to manoeuvre the vessel away out to sea again and berth at a later time.
EERV made contact/grounded with the fairway resulting in damage to the thruster. Upon investigation by the ship the thruster has been noticed as missing from the bottom of the vessel

title: Solo Night Sailing: Watchkeeping Failures and Navigational Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by navigation and contact incidents arising from single-handed vessel operation during night-time passages in coastal and rock-hazard waters. The recurring pattern across these incidents is the structural absence of redundancy in watchkeeping: a lone skipper operating at night carries the full cognitive burden of navigation, vessel handling, hazard detection, and safety monitoring simultaneously, with no crew member available to cross-check decisions, maintain a lookout, or respond to an emergency. When any brief lapse in attention occurs — whether due to fatigue, distraction, or incapacitation — there is no human or automated failsafe to intervene. The most severe manifestation of this vulnerability is the unwitnessed man overboard event, where the vessel continues unmanned to grounding with no mechanism to detect or report the skipper's absence. These incidents recur because solo night passages remain lawful and common, yet the systemic safeguards necessary to manage their inherent risks are absent, voluntary, or unenforced.

Latent hardware and software deficiencies play a significant enabling role: the absence of automated watchkeeping alerts, personal locator beacons, deadman alarm systems, and integrated vessel monitoring means that human error or incapacitation produces no automatic safety response. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time darkness, moderate winds, and wave heights sufficient to complicate course-keeping — consistently amplify the consequences of reduced situational awareness, compressing the time available between a positional error and a grounding event. Human factors, including cognitive overload from single-handed operation, fatigue associated with night passages, and possible over-reliance on electronic navigation aids without adequate positional cross-checking, underpin nearly all incidents in this cluster. Procedural failures, such as the absence of pre-passage plans appropriate to solo night conditions, failure to wear personal safety equipment, and lack of float plans filed with shore contacts, represent systemic gaps in risk management culture that leave both skipper and vessel without a meaningful safety net.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, and 33: navigational watch arrangements, bridge watchkeeping, and search and rescue obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (distress alerting, EPIRB and PLB carriage and activation requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (personal survival equipment, man overboard prevention and recovery equipment)
preventable_by: Mandate the use of calibrated deadman alarm or automated watchkeeping alert systems on vessels undertaking solo passages, requiring the skipper to acknowledge a periodic prompt; failure to respond triggers an automatic DSC distress alert to the coastguard with vessel position.

Require skippers of solo night passages to file a formal float plan with a designated shore contact or Maritime Rescue Co-ordination Centre, including planned route, waypoints, and expected arrival time, with a defined overdue escalation protocol that initiates SAR tasking if contact is not established.

Introduce mandatory carriage and active use of personal safety equipment — including a PLB registered to the vessel, a lifeline and harness clipped to a jackstay, and an AIS-enabled personal MOB device — for all solo passages conducted between nautical twilight and sunrise, with compliance verified as part of pre-departure vessel safety checks.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht with a lone skipper grounded on rocks whilst sailing. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist but were unable to help due to the water depth, the skipper remained onboard until the SAR vessel could return at a later time. When the SAR vessel returned, the yacht was towed to deeper water, anchored and the skipper transferred to the SAR vessel. The yacht had broken it's

A sailing yacht was discovered aground with its sails still up and the skipper was not found on board. The search and rescue services mounted an investigation but there was no sign of the skipper and the search and rescue was called off.

A single handed yacht grounded on rocks and called a mayday. Search and rescue assets were launched, including a helicopter. However, the helicopter was terminated prior to launch due to the fact the crew member was able to step onto the rocks if needed. The crew member was transferred to a search and rescue (SAR) vessel and the yacht left with anchor down.

An angling charter vessel was proceeding along the coast when the skipper took evasive action to avoid a semi submerged buoy for a crab/lobster pot. The sudden course changed caused concern for one of the passengers who came into the wheel house to ask the skipper some questions. The skipper was distracted and the vessel struck the bottom. The vessel was travelling at 15kts at the time and the ski

A historical fishing vessel ran aground on a mud bank at the entrance to a river whilst under sail. The vessel was on a run, once they reached the return point, the engine was turned on. The helm started to head back upwind and towards the river entrance and then tacked. The helm was paying close attention to the depth sounder, the sails, and the tiller as well as watching navigation but inadverte

A small charter angling vessel grounded on rocks due to drifting while angling. Skipper was helping anglers and lost positional awareness.  The vessel returned to harbour with water ingress and list, salvage pump was able to pump out water. No pollution or injuries reported. The crack in the hull was in a sealed compartment forward of the engine room.

The vessel went hard aground on the sand on the coast of an island,  A few hours later, it was refloated and proceeded to a different port to check for damage. No damage, water ingress, or defects were confirmed. The charterer confirmed that the chief officer was the officer of the watch, he had fallen asleep, there was no additional lookout, and the BNWAS was not switched on.

A fishing vessel crew member had taken the vessel out of gear, displayed the not under command (NUC) lights and left the wheelhouse. Another fishing vessel on passage collided with the fishing vessel's port derrick resulting in damage to the derrick, hydraulic pipes and deck. Water ran through the holes in the deck and into the engine room. Through communication between the two vessel it was found

A sailing yacht grounded on a sandbank, the lone skipper issued a PANPAN, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was deployed to assist the yacht. The SAR vessel towed the yacht into deeper water, the skipper then carried out an inspection as the keel may have been damaged during the grounding, no visible damage seen and no injury to the skipper. The yacht then continued its passage under engine.

A sailing yacht hit rocks resulting in the lone sailor abandoning the vessel. The skipper was able to climb onto the rocks uninjured and made their way to shore into the care of a coastguard team. Search and rescue (SAR) assets were stood down whilst on route. The vessel broke up with the only thing left being the mast.

title: Crew Injuries During Stairway Transit in Adverse Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries sustained during routine stairway descent, where commonplace shipboard movement becomes a significant injury risk under the compounding influence of vessel motion, reduced night-time visibility, and behavioural lapses. Across incidents, the immediate causes vary — missed steps, slips, caught fingers, and loss of balance — but a consistent underlying pattern emerges: crew members are transiting stairways without adequate precaution, frequently in the absence of enforced safe movement protocols such as three-point contact or controlled descent speed. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types, from passenger ships and ro-pax ferries to cargo vessels and dredgers, indicates that stairway transit injury is not an isolated or vessel-specific problem but a systemic safety failure rooted in the normalisation of risk during routine crew movement.

Latent hardware deficiencies play a material role in several incidents, including inadequate anti-slip surfaces, insufficient or poorly positioned handrails, and exposed structural hazards such as proud bulkhead panel seams adjacent to crew transit routes. Environmental conditions — particularly significant wave heights, Beaufort Force 5–6 winds, and night-time lighting limitations — substantially elevate the baseline risk, yet in several cases the recorded sea state data was inconsistent with measured wave heights, suggesting a failure in environmental situational awareness that may have led crews and officers to underestimate prevailing conditions. Human factors, including distraction, excessive descent speed, improper grip posture, and a failure to brace against vessel motion, further compound these vulnerabilities. The convergence of inadequate hardware safeguards, unreliable environmental reporting, and an organisational culture that does not effectively enforce safe movement procedures creates conditions in which injury during a routine transit between decks becomes a foreseeable and preventable outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision and Stability (safe design of internal passageways, stairways, and structural hazard mitigation)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures and emergency response to personnel injury)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – safety management systems, procedural compliance, and risk assessment for routine operations)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce a mandatory Safe Movement Policy requiring three-point contact on all stairways during vessel movement, with compliance embedded in the Safety Management System, reinforced through regular drills, toolbox talks, and documented supervision by officers — with particular emphasis on night-time operations and adverse weather conditions.

Conduct structured hardware audits of all crew transit stairways, including inspection and remediation of anti-slip surface condition, handrail adequacy and positioning, and identification of structural snag hazards such as exposed panel seams or fittings; ensure corrective actions are tracked to completion and findings are incorporated into pre-voyage safety checks.

Introduce rigorous environmental situational awareness procedures requiring bridge officers to cross-validate logged sea state conditions against measured wave height and wind speed data, with mandatory dissemination of accurate weather and vessel motion advisories to all crew before and during periods of adverse conditions, prompting crew to apply heightened caution during movement throughout the vessel.

representative_cases.description: Passenger ship's crew member suffered a knee injury while descending the stairs. This did not result in a fall however, they managed to twist their leg and sprain their knee.  As a consequence of the accident, the crew member had to be medically disembarked.

Hopper dredger's crew member was walking down the stairs from the bridge to the mess room when they slipped and fell down the stairs. This fall resulted in bruised ribs and in an ankle luxation. The casualty was evacuated with the ship's crane on a stretcher and placed onto a lifeboat. The individual was taken for further medical assistance.

A ro-pax crew member caught their finger whilst descending a staircase resulting in a fracture and subsequent sign off from the vessel. There were no witnesses to the incident but investigations carried out by the vessel's safety officer concluded that the manner of descent contributed to the injury. The crew member had their fingers splayed whilst the descended at speed and reportedly caught them

A cruise ship crew member missed a step whilst descending a staircase and fractured their ankle. The crewmember reported that they were distracted whilst walking down the staircase. The area was inspected and no defects were found.

A general cargo vessel crewmember was descending a staircase in the engine room when they fell down. They hit their head on the way down sustaining superficial injuries such as bruises and cuts to the head, shoulder and chest. They were taken ashore for a check up at the hospital.

A cruise ships crew member slipped from ladder resulting in closed fracture to the leg. Descending the safety ladder from the upper bunk in there cabin facing forward, without holding the railings , when stepping down to the second last step, foot slipped off the edge of the step, striking there left ankle, and causing them to slip and slid down on to the floor.

An offshore support vessel crewmember was walking down a staircase with items in both of their hands when they fell. They were unable to arrest their fall and fell fourteen steps to the bottom. They sustained a sprained shoulder which may have also been fractured as well as surface wounds on their arm and hand.

A cruise ship crew member was going down a staircase when they missed the last step which resulted in a twisted knee. The crew member said that they were grabbing the handrail but in a hurry which was a result of the accident. No defects or deficiencies found.

Passenger ship's crew member toppled down 6 steps while trying to go to the crew area. Crew member had a cramp in their calf and while lifting their foot with one hand, back of the shoe gt caught at the edge of the stairs and they stumbled down. This resulted in dislocation of the left elbow.

A cruise ship crew member was carrying a box with both hands whilst descending a stair case when as they stepped off the last step they twisted their right ankle. The crew member's supervisor called for medical help and the injured crew member was taken to the medical center on board. The crew member was declared unfit for duty. The area of the accident was inspected and found to have no defects a

title: Lifting and Deck Operations: Inadequate Controls and Communication
description: This theme is characterised by a recurring failure to implement and enforce formalised safe work systems during high-risk deck operations involving lifting equipment, mooring machinery, and cargo handling. The incidents share a common pattern: personnel are exposed to hazardous energy — whether from suspended loads, tensioned lines, or moving crane equipment — without the protection of structured authorisation protocols, exclusion zones, or verified two-way communication. Operations proceed, or are allowed to continue, on the basis of informal verbal exchanges or individual judgement rather than binding procedural controls. This systemic reliance on informal coordination as the primary safety barrier means that a single lapse in attention, a misunderstood signal, or an unexpected equipment failure is sufficient to place crew members directly in harm's way with no secondary defence.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance of mechanical and mooring equipment)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe operational procedures and crew safety during deck operations)
SOLAS Chapter VI: Carriage of Cargoes (safe handling and stowage procedures, including cargo gear operation and personnel safety during cargo operations)
preventable_by: Mandate formalised Permit-to-Work and Lock-Out/Tag-Out protocols for all lifting, crane, and mooring operations, requiring explicit written authorisation before work commences and a verified two-way all-clear confirmation before any suspended load is moved or machinery is restarted, with no operation permitted to resume on the basis of informal verbal communication alone.

Require documented pre-operation equipment inspections and formal lift plans for all non-routine lifting operations, including independent verification of load ratings, rigging integrity, and hook and attachment condition, with mandatory establishment and enforcement of physical exclusion zones that prohibit personnel from entering drop, recoil, or crush areas during any phase of the lift or line-handling operation.

Implement mandatory contingency stop-work procedures for critical equipment failures during active operations — such as winch power loss during mooring — that automatically halt the operation and prohibit defaulting to unsafe manual handling, supported by regular drills so that crew responses to mid-operation failures are trained, rehearsed, and not left to individual improvisation under pressure.

representative_cases.description: Container ship's crew member was supervising cargo operations when they noticed that the right-hand twist lock for securing containers was not in proper position. The crew member informed that they were going to fix the twist lock and contacted the gantry driver to stop loading. While they were fixing the twist lock, the gantry operator resumed loading resulting in an open fracture injury. The cre

A general cargo vessel crew member was tying up the forward spring by hand as the winch suffered a power issue, the crew member possibly fractured their wrist during the mooring operation. The crew member was treated in hospital.

The fishing vessel was undertaking a transfer of a new generator from some contractors that attended, during which they needed to lift the generator and transfer it to the engine room. During the transfer/lifting the hook and attachment of the chain block failed and the chain recoiled, striking a contractor. The contractor was taken via ambulance to hospital where their injuries were confirmed to

A fishing vessel crew were transferring a rope from a lifeline winch to the crane roller when the lifeline winch sped up causing tension on the crane. This resulted in the head to break off and hitting a crew member. Search and rescue medevac'd the crew member to hospital.

Contractors had been working on a compressor for the air lubrication system on a cruise ship. They had suspended the compressor above the deck in order to alter it’s mountings. Later that day, whilst the vessel was manoeuvring out of port, one of the contractors was laying beside and below the suspended unit when it shifted and the corner fell onto their chest. They were medivaced by helicopter. T

Sailing vessel's crew member was part of a group hoisting the mizzen sail. The sail was 1/3 way up and was depowered. The drum of the winch on the mast came off during the manoeuvre and hit the deck. The drum hit the casualties arm which resulted in further medical assistance.

An offshore support vessel crewmember was struck on the head by the hook of a crane during a lifting operation. There was a medic on board at the time who assessed the injured crewmember and suggested the vessel return to port in order to get further medical treatment. They had a level 1 concussion and were conscious and alert with some slight dizziness. The coastguard monitored the vessel's progr

A tug crewmember sustained a comminuted fracture (multiple fractures in wrist and arm similar to shattering) when their arm was struck by a winch handle connected to the towing winch arrangement. The crewmember was medically evacuated with assistance from the local lifeboat service and shore ambulance service.

A fishing vessel crew member was injured from a falling block resulting in superficial wounds to the head. Disconnecting a shackle off the rope on the power block for holding up the trawl door, doing so the crew member sat on a winch control lever causing it to heave, it jammed in the top pulley and the rope broke and the hook dropped falling onto the crew members head, they where not wearing a ha

A contractor was working onboard a ro-pax vessel to carry out work on life saving equipment. The contractor sought the support of the vessel's crew after encountering a problem. One of the crewmembers agreed to work on a part of the equipment on the lathe in the engine room workshop. However, when the crewmember was preparing and putting on their PPE, the contractor started to operate the lathe wi

title: SAR Vessel Contact with Hazards in Shallow Inshore Waters
description:
This theme is characterised by SAR vessels making contact with submerged rocks, harbour walls, or other fixed hazards during inshore and shoreline search operations, predominantly at night and in moderate to elevated sea states. The incidents share a common operational context: vessels operating at close proximity to the shoreline under time pressure, reduced visibility, and dynamic wave conditions that collectively degrade navigational situational awareness. A recurring pattern is the compression of safety margins driven by mission urgency — whether responding to a missing person, a person on rocks, or even a lower-priority tasking such as locating an animal — which leads crews to accept navigational risks that would otherwise be considered imprudent. The absence of defined abort criteria, minimum safe-distance protocols, and real-time depth monitoring procedures before entering shallow water creates the conditions in which individual judgement errors become consequential. Task fixation, once visual or perceived contact with the search object is established, further suppresses the application of standard navigational risk management, producing a consistent failure mode across otherwise dissimilar incidents.

Latent causal factors span all three domains. From a human factors perspective, the dominant influences are task-induced time pressure, task fixation, and the normalisation of accepting reduced safety margins in SAR contexts — a cultural disposition that prioritises mission completion over vessel and crew safety in circumstances where that trade-off is not always warranted. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness, wave-induced vertical displacement, tidal variability in effective water depth, and wind-driven set and drift, systematically erode the crew's ability to accurately assess under-keel clearance and proximity to hazards. Hardware and procedural gaps compound these vulnerabilities: echo sounders and depth-monitoring equipment are either absent, not active, or not integrated into the decision-making process during shallow-water approaches, and there are no standardised shallow-water approach protocols mandating defined minimum depths, abort triggers, or crew briefing requirements before entering hazardous inshore areas. Together, these latent conditions create a system that is structurally predisposed to contact events whenever SAR operations bring vessels into proximity with the shoreline under suboptimal conditions.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 24, and 34: navigational equipment requirements, safe speed, and voyage planning obligations applicable to SAR vessel operations)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (operational readiness and safety procedures for rescue craft and associated crew protocols)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Safety Management Systems: requirement for operators to identify risks, establish procedures, and implement safeguards for hazardous operations including inshore SAR)
preventable_by: Mandate the use of active echo sounding and real-time depth monitoring as a standing operational requirement whenever SAR vessels conduct inshore or shallow-water approaches, with defined minimum under-keel clearance thresholds that must trigger an immediate abort and withdrawal regardless of mission status.

Develop and implement formal shallow-water approach protocols within the SAR operator's Safety Management System, specifying minimum safe standoff distances from the shoreline by sea state and visibility category, mandatory crew briefings before entering hazardous inshore areas, and pre-defined abort criteria that are communicated and agreed before any approach commences.

Embed structured risk-benefit decision-making training into SAR crew competency programmes, explicitly addressing the hazards of task fixation and mission-priority distortion, and establishing clear organisational guidance that low-priority taskings — including searches for animals or property — do not justify accepting elevated navigational risk to vessel or crew.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist some casualties when the vessel made contact with either the harbour wall or the outlaying rock below the water. The crew was aware of the tide and had done their calculation prior to their departure. The only damage reported was chip in gel coat approximately 6 centimetres long.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was launched to assist in locating a dog. The SAR vessel conducted a shoreline search and the crew thought they could see the dog on the rocks. The helm trimmed the engines up and slowly crept into the shallows. As they proceeded the helm felt a slight knock on one of the engines – but had no loss of power or steerage. Once back in deep water, the engine was lifted

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service carrying out a shoreline search about 60-70 from the shore and travelling at approximately 5 knots. A wave passed under the vessel, when it was in the trough of the wave, the hull made contact with rocks. No damage seen to the vessel and the vessel continued with it's search and returned to station. Incident phoned in, waiting for ARF.

Whilst completing a night time shoreline search searching for a missing person believed to be on the shoreline/in and around rock fishtails within the area the ILB struck its propeller on a submerged rock whilst entering one of the bays off of the beach.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service to assist a person on rocks, The SAR vessel was approaching into shallow water with one engine lifted out of the water, when it made contact with an unknown object. No damage was observed at the time and was later inspected when the vessel was back at the station. No injuries to crew.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service searching for a person in the water close to cliffs. Whilst manoeuvring near the casualty, the vessel struck a submerged object (likely rock), the SAR vessel was able to return to station. On inspection both propellers had sustained damage and were replaced. No reported injuries to crew.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was recovering onto the trailer and made contact with a rock at the recovery site.  The vessel berths stern to on the trailer and the engines are lifted but contact does happen with this recovery.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on a training exercise practicing anchoring and veering in shallow waters. Whilst the vessel was slowing veering astern the propeller stuck the seabed. On return to the station, it was noticed that the propeller had sustained minor damage. No injuries to crew or other damage to vessel.

A search and rescue (SAR) had been tasked to a scene where two crew members were placed ashore to support with the casualty care efforts, the SAR vessel returned to safe water. A decision was made to recover the casualties from the shoreline and take them to the lifeboat station. The SAR vessel was placed bow first on the rocky shoreline, with the casualties stepping into the vessel and the crew t

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service to a casualty who had fallen onto rocks. Whilst heading to a concrete outfall to disembark crew, the helm stopped the vessel after sighting a submerged rock. As the vessel was reversing the port propeller struck a rock that hadn’t been sighted on the approach. The propeller had sustained damaged but the vessel still had manoeuvrability.

title: Pending MAIB Investigation Incidents Lacking Substantive Data
description:
This cluster is characterised not by a shared causal pattern among incidents, but by a common data deficiency: all 23 incidents are represented solely by placeholder references to pending MAIB investigation reports, with no substantive narrative, causal analysis, vessel activity details, or outcome information available. The incidents span a range of severity classifications from Less Serious to Very Serious, and occurred predominantly under night-time conditions across varying sea states, from calm (Sea State 0) to rough (Sea State 5, 2.5–4 m). Without access to the underlying investigation findings, it is not possible to determine whether these incidents share genuine latent causes or represent a diverse cross-section of marine occurrences. The recurrence of this data gap across 23 cases highlights a systemic issue in the completeness of incident records held prior to the publication of formal investigation reports.

In the absence of substantive incident details, no meaningful determination can be made regarding the role of latent hardware or software failures, human factors, or environmental conditions in contributing to these events. The consistent notation of night-time conditions across the cluster warrants cautious consideration of visibility and lighting as potential contextual factors, and the presence of several incidents occurring in rough sea conditions (Sea State 5) may indicate that environmental factors warrant examination once full reports are available. The primary concern this cluster raises is an analytical and governance one: the absence of interim safety information in pending investigation records limits the ability of safety managers, port authorities, and fleet operators to act on early signals. It is strongly recommended that classification and thematic analysis of all 23 incidents be revisited and revised upon publication of the respective MAIB investigation reports.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 31 & 32: Danger messages and casualty reporting obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 6: Casualty investigation)
SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Regulation 21: Casualty reporting and investigation requirements)
preventable_by: Establish a mandatory interim safety data requirement whereby flag states and administrations must record minimum substantive incident details (vessel type, activity, preliminary cause category, and immediate outcome) in official databases at the time of initial notification, independent of and prior to the completion of a full MAIB or equivalent investigation report.

Implement a structured incident triage protocol within marine casualty reporting systems that prevents the submission of placeholder-only records for serious and very serious incidents, requiring at minimum a preliminary factual summary and an initial contributing factor hypothesis to be completed by the reporting officer within 72 hours of the occurrence.

Develop and mandate the use of a standardised interim investigation disclosure framework, under which investigating authorities such as the MAIB publish preliminary safety findings or safety bulletins within a defined period following a serious or very serious incident, ensuring that actionable safety intelligence is disseminated to the maritime community without awaiting the completion of the full investigation report.

title: Night-Time Slip, Trip and Fall Incidents Aboard Vessels
description:
This theme is characterised by a persistent pattern of slip, trip, and fall (STF) injuries occurring predominantly during night-time operations across a diverse range of vessel types, including ro-pax, ro-ro, inland passenger, offshore supply, replenishment tankers, and service vessels. The incidents share a convergence of compounding risk factors: reduced ambient lighting, wet or contaminated deck surfaces, unsecured equipment in pedestrian pathways, and routine task familiarity that breeds complacency. The injuries sustained range from sprains and bruising to fractures and head injuries, confirming that STF events carry meaningful harm potential even in benign sea states. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel types and operational contexts indicates that these are not isolated human error events but rather symptoms of systemic weaknesses in deck housekeeping culture, pre-task hazard identification, and safety management enforcement during low-visibility operations.

The latent hardware and environmental conditions contributing to this theme include inadequate or poorly positioned task lighting on stairways, egress points, and working decks; absence of non-slip surfaces, handrails, or physical guarding at known hazard locations such as open hatches and stairwells; and transient deck hazards introduced by maintenance activities such as washing or crane operations that are not promptly identified or resolved. Human factors include fatigue during night watches, familiarity bias in routine movement, PPE non-compliance particularly regarding appropriate footwear, failure to apply three-point contact discipline on ladders and stairways, and distraction such as mobile phone use during descent. Organisational and safety culture gaps are evidenced by insufficient housekeeping inspection regimes, inadequate shift-to-shift hazard communication, and inconsistent enforcement of procedural controls. Collectively, these latent conditions create an environment where STF incidents are highly foreseeable and, critically, highly preventable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (covers structural safety including safe means of access, lighting standards, and non-slip surfaces on stairways and passageways)

SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requires safe and unobstructed access to muster stations and emergency equipment, directly relevant to clear-deck discipline and egress point safety)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships – ISM Code (mandates that Safety Management Systems include hazard identification, risk assessment, and procedural controls for routine operational activities including housekeeping and night operations)
preventable_by: Implement a structured Night Operations Deck Safety Protocol requiring pre-departure and pre-task lighting adequacy checks, mandatory clear-deck inspections at all egress points and stairwells, and formal sign-off on hazard-free status before commencing night-time maintenance or vessel preparation activities.

Enforce vessel-wide PPE compliance standards mandating appropriate non-slip footwear for all crew on working decks, stairways, and accommodation ladders at all times, supported by regular toolbox talks, visible signage, and accountability measures integrated into the Safety Management System under the ISM Code.

Conduct engineering control audits of all high-risk STF zones — including internal stairways, engine room egress points, hatch surrounds, and vehicle decks — to ensure installation and maintenance of adequate task lighting, anti-slip treads, physical barriers at open hatches, and secure equipment stowage fixtures, with findings tracked through a formal corrective action register.

representative_cases.description: Rope secured on step, notified and educational material given.
Inland passenger vessel's crew member was preparing the boat when they slipped and fell into a hatch. Sustained an unknown head injury.
A ro-pax vessel crew member slipped whilst descending a staircase and sprained their back. The crewmember's boots were wet from deck washing which may have contributed to them slipping.
A service vessels crew member tripped on hook resulting in a sprained ankle. Engineer came out of the aft engine room door and tripped on a boat hook that was on the deck.
A replenishment tanker crew member stepped over an obstruction, tripped and fell to the deck. They sustained bruising to their wrist and were put on light duties for seven days.
A replenishment vessel crew member was returning to their cabin when they tripped and fell sustaining a shoulder fracture. They were taken for further medical assistance.
Dredger's crew member tripped on the sill and lost their balance resulting in a fall. The crew member sustained a fractured knee.
A ro-ro vessel crewmember fell down the stairs and had bruising to their left side below their rib cage and on their left hand.
A general cargo crewmember fell coming down the stairs. The casualty fell from around two meters height and sustained a head injury.
Passenger ships crew member fell from bunk ladder resulting in closed fractures to the hand. Crew members foot slipped from bunk ladder and fell, impacting there hand that was holding a mobile phone.

title: Boom Strike and Dynamic Manoeuvre Injuries in Night Sailing
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries — predominantly head trauma from uncontrolled boom strikes — occurring during dynamic sailing manoeuvres, most frequently at night and in active wind and sea conditions. The incidents cluster around a convergence of predictable risk factors: single-handed or under-supervised operation, night-time conditions that degrade situational awareness, and the sudden, forceful movement of the boom during gybes, tacks, or unplanned sail transitions. The pattern is compounded by the absence of boom preventers or restraining devices, lack of mandatory head PPE culture in recreational and competitive sailing environments, and insufficient pre-manoeuvre communication protocols. Racing contexts further introduce risk-tolerance pressures that may lead crew to execute high-load manoeuvres without adequate preparation or coordination. These incidents recur because the hazard — a swinging boom in dynamic conditions — is well-understood yet systematically under-mitigated, particularly in the recreational and sail training sectors where procedural discipline is inconsistent and PPE compliance is largely voluntary.

Latent hardware deficiencies, including the absence or improper rigging of boom preventers, vangs, and restraining tackles, mean that an involuntary gybe or sail transition translates directly into uncontrolled kinetic energy with no mechanical safeguard to arrest boom movement. Human factors — including inattention, positional unawareness, fatigue during night watches, and inadequate training in adverse-condition sail handling — place crew members in the arc of the boom at the moment of highest risk. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness, moderate-to-fresh wind, and irregular sea states, act as latent amplifiers that reduce the window for corrective action and impair early detection of developing gybes or sail changes. The escalation of several incidents to SAR involvement demonstrates that even injuries classified as less serious can rapidly overwhelm the remaining crew capacity of a short-handed vessel, transforming a manageable casualty into a rescue operation. Systemic recurrence prevention must therefore address all three layers: procedural controls, hardware safeguards, and a cultural shift toward mandatory PPE use in sailing operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for personal protective and safety equipment, including provisions relevant to crew protection during operations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe watchkeeping, bridge and deck procedures, and the master's obligation to ensure safe operational practices including in adverse conditions)

SOLAS Chapter IV / ISM Code (International Safety Management) – Safe Manning and Operational Procedures (procedural safety systems, risk assessment obligations, and crew training requirements applicable to vessel operators)
preventable_by: Mandate the use of certified head protection (sailing helmets) and personal flotation devices as minimum PPE during all night-sailing operations, gybing manoeuvres, and single-handed or short-handed passages, with compliance enforced through vessel safety management procedures and pre-race or pre-passage safety briefings.

Require the fitting and use of a boom preventer or vang restraint system as a standing operational procedure before any downwind sailing or anticipated gybe, and introduce structured pre-manoeuvre communication drills — including a standardised verbal 'ready to gybe' protocol — to ensure all crew are positionally clear of the boom arc before execution.

Introduce night-specific risk management policies for recreational, racing, and sail training vessels, including mandatory reduced-manoeuvre protocols in low-visibility or high-load conditions, competency-based qualification requirements for night watch supervision, and post-incident reporting obligations for single-handed vessels to close the systemic gap in safety data caused by unwitnessed incidents.

They were taken to the local hospital by ambulance once they were landed a

A recreational sailing vessels crew member sustained head injury from boom, resulting in search and rescue towing to safety.
A recreational yacht's crew member got their hand trapped in a winch and their head hit by a boom. They were taken for further medical assistance.

A sailing yacht was in passage when a crew member was then hit by a sailing boom on their head. After receiving coastguard doctor advice regarding their injuries and it was found they had pain in their neck & shoulder.
