A yacht with three crew members on board was taking part in the race when they involuntarily gybed and the boom struck one of the crewmembers. They sustained a head injury and went over board. Another yacht that was taking part in the race recovered the crewmember from the water and then a life boat that was in the area carrying out training took the crewmember ashore. The crewmember had a minor h

A yacht crew member who was taking part in a race at the time, was hit on their head by a rope. The vessel was also able to continue with passage whilst the crew members then managed to administer first aid until it had arrived into a port . The crew member had sustained swelling and bleeding however they were fully conscious and the bleeding also stopped by the time they were nearing the port. A

The vessel capsized resulting in a crew member getting hit by a boom causing a bad gash to their leg and also pain in the left side of their rib injury and was assisted by local CRT. The crew member was collected from the water, taken to the jetty, and taken via ambulance to a hospital

A male crew member was knocked out during a tack,  he was hit on the head by the boom resulting in him becoming unconscious for 30 seconds. The crew member became awoken and was sitting. No open wounds were found, and he was hit on the forehead. He didn't respond at all and there was no sign of deformation on his head. He was taken via ambulance to a hospital for further care.

title: Cargo Hold Fires in High-Risk Bulk Scrap Cargoes
description:
This theme is characterised by fires igniting in bulk cargo holds, predominantly involving scrap metal but also high-moisture organic cargoes such as grain, where the common latent cause is the acceptance of inadequately screened or assessed cargo containing embedded ignition sources, reactive materials, or conditions conducive to spontaneous self-heating. These incidents recur because the cargo acceptance and pre-loading inspection regime consistently fails to identify and exclude hazardous contaminants — including residual fuels, pressurised gas cylinders, reactive metals, and moisture-laden organic matter — before loading commences. Compounding this is the absence of robust continuous monitoring within cargo holds during and after loading, meaning that smouldering or self-heating goes undetected until it has advanced to a stage requiring external emergency service intervention, vessel detention, and in several cases extensive unloading operations to access the seat of the fire. Night-time operations further reduce the likelihood of early visual detection, and the large volumes of firewater required in suppression efforts demonstrate a systemic mismatch between installed fire-fighting capability and the actual risk profile of the cargo being carried.

Latent hardware deficiencies are evident in the inadequacy or absence of fixed gas detection and temperature monitoring systems within cargo holds, as well as the demonstrated ineffectiveness of installed CO2 and foam suppression systems against deeply seated fires in dense bulk cargoes. These hardware gaps are compounded by human factors including insufficient vigilance during cargo watch routines, reduced crew alertness during night-time operations, and a normalisation of risk associated with routine bulk cargo handling that may lead officers to underestimate the hazards of mixed scrap or high-moisture grain consignments. Environmentally, night-time conditions consistently delayed initial detection and complicated coordinated response. At the systemic level, the recurring nature of these incidents across multiple vessel types and cargo categories points to regulatory and procedural gaps in cargo acceptance standards, the absence of mandatory pre-loading cargo condition certification for high-risk bulk cargoes, and insufficient shipboard emergency preparedness plans tailored to the specific fire-fighting challenges posed by dense, inaccessible bulk cargo stowage configurations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter VI: Carriage of Cargoes and Oil Fuels (including safe loading, cargo documentation, and pre-loading assessment requirements)
SOLAS Chapter VII: Carriage of Dangerous Goods (classification, packaging, and stowage of hazardous materials in bulk)
preventable_by: Mandate enforceable pre-loading cargo screening and acceptance protocols for scrap metal and high-risk bulk cargoes, requiring documented certification that consignments are free from pressurised containers, reactive metals, excessive oil contamination, and other identified hazards, with physical inspection by a competent surveyor prior to loading authorisation.

Require installation and continuous operation of calibrated temperature and combustible gas detection systems within cargo holds during all bulk cargo loading operations and subsequent storage alongside, with defined threshold alarm levels and a documented response procedure that triggers immediate hold closure, atmosphere management, and escalation to shore-based emergency services.

Develop and drill cargo-specific shipboard fire emergency plans for high-risk bulk cargoes that address the limitations of fixed suppression systems against deeply seated fires, pre-identify crane access and cargo removal sequencing for emergency use, and specify boundary cooling protocols and firewater demand calculations consistent with the maximum credible fire scenario for each cargo type carried.

Fire fighters attended to extinguish the fire and the shore side cranes unloaded the material on top to assist in accessing the burning material. CO2 was dumped into the hold, the hold was later opened. The fire within hold was out and of limited extent, the damaged cargo was offloaded and cooling was continued.

The crew of the vessel were evacuated ashore and

Once the crew members started to cool down the steam became more intense and a fire alarm was sent. The cargo has not been declared as hazardous as for this reason they decided to return to the port in order to discharge the overheated cargo in trailer. Th

This water had a pH value of 5 (acidic) and it was noticed that the paint was peeling off. It was instructed to not ventilate the holds and to monitor all parameters of the cargo. The crew later on noticed smoke coming out of the cargo surface however, no fire was detecte

A bulk carrier with scrap metal on board experienced a scrap fire whilst alongside in port. The vessel was loading scrap at the time. The shore side emergency services attended and monitored the situation. There were no injures, damage or pollution reported.

A general cargo vessel was alongside during loading operations when there was a small isolated cargo fire in the hold. The local fire brigade attended to assist the vessels crew. Fire quickly extinguished and no reported damage to the vessel or injuries. The fire is believed to be caused by battery mixed with the cargo beyond the ships and management control.

title: Failures in Electrical Safety and Energy Isolation Controls
description:
This theme is characterised by a systemic breakdown in the management of hazardous energy aboard vessels, encompassing live electrical work without mandatory PPE, arc flash events during unverified circuit breaker resets, and the defeat or disregard of lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures on isolated equipment. Across all incidents, the immediate physical harm — electric shock, arc flash injury, barotrauma — was directly enabled by the absence of, or non-compliance with, established safe-work procedures. The recurring nature of these incidents points strongly to a normalisation of deviance within the safety culture of affected vessels: crews routinely underestimate the residual risk of energised systems, bypass procedural controls under operational or time pressure, and lack sufficient oversight mechanisms to detect and correct these deviations before harm occurs. The vessel safety management systems nominally contain the required procedures, yet enforcement, verification, and crew accountability appear consistently inadequate.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including absent arc flash interlocks, insufficient physical deterrents on LOTO controls, and machinery lacking startup-prevention interlocks — create preconditions that amplify the consequences of human error. Human factors, notably complacency, inadequate inter-crew communication, and failure to conduct or act on task-level risk assessments, are present across every incident and represent the dominant causal pathway. Environmental conditions, including night-time operations reducing situational awareness and moderate sea states introducing vessel motion near energised equipment, act as compounding stressors that increase the probability of inadvertent contact or misjudgement. Taken together, these incidents do not reflect isolated individual lapses but rather a systemic failure in the layered defences that should prevent energised-system hazards from reaching crew members, demanding both procedural reinforcement and hardware-level safeguards.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (electrical safety requirements for shipboard systems)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety and protective equipment obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — SMS procedural compliance, risk assessment, and safety culture obligations)
preventable_by: Mandate and audit task-specific risk assessments and permit-to-work documentation for all live electrical work and energy isolation tasks, with independent verification by a responsible officer before work commences, and enforce a zero-tolerance policy for LOTO defeat or bypass through regular unannounced SMS compliance audits.

Require all crew working on or near energised electrical systems to wear arc-rated PPE (including rubber insulating gloves and matting) as a non-negotiable minimum standard, and install arc flash hazard labels on all relevant panels; supplement with hardware controls including multi-lock LOTO hasps, startup-prevention interlocks on incinerators and similar equipment, and arc flash barriers on switchgear.

Implement a structured, competency-verified electrical safety training programme for all engineering personnel, covering arc flash awareness, correct LOTO application and release procedures, and the specific prohibition on resetting tripped breakers without fault investigation; reinforce training with regular drills and include near-miss reporting to capture normalised unsafe practices before they result in injury.

representative_cases.description: A cargo vessel crewmember was attempting to turn on a switch inside an electrical panel when they touched a live part of the panel and received an electric shock. They were uninjured and returned to work directly after. For the maintenance that was being carried out it was a requirement for the system to be live. The crewmember was not wearing rubber gloves or using a rubber mat.

Cargo ship's engineers were battling a circuit breaker as it had tripped and could not be reset. Once there was an alarm, the engineers immediately went into the engine control room to take a glance at the issue. The crew member who had previously used the incinerator loaded materials in and after switching it on they saw the temperature rise. As the temperature was increasing, there was a loud bang within the incinerator chamber meaning, the bang echoed around the room. The comp

A crewmember of a container vessel fractured their fingers whilst working in the engine room. They were working on the air conditioning plant and got their fingers trapped between the belt and the pully of the drive mechanism for the fans. They were disembarked from the vessel due to their injuries.

Passenger ferry was alongside and shut down on our maintenance berth at the time due to the fact that the vessel was not due to be in service on that particular day and the engineering department had used this time to complete some planned maintenance tasks prior to the vessel going back into service on the next day. During the maintenance itself on the engine room fixed fire fighting system (FM20

Passenger ship's contractor was removing a valve from a generator while the ship was in dry dock. The contractor had not checked to ensure the system had completely de-pressurised which resulted in a lower jaw injury. The individual has 3 breakages.

Dredger's crew member sustained an electrical shock from the fuse carrier while conducting repairs. Dredger's electrical circuit that controlled the hydraulics had failed. The crew member has not reported any serious injuries.

The vessel's main engine was shut down at the request of an external surveyor. After the shutdown the engine reset unexpectedly and for unknown reasons, causing a discharge of compressed air to hit the crewmember performing the shutdown. The discharge of air knocked off the crewmember's glasses and ear defenders and injured their ear. The crewmember was taken to hospital where they were treated fo

The second engineer turned to retrieve an adjustable spanner to use to try and stop the movement, however, while he was doing this the ETO used his thumb to try and push it down to stop the pulsing. According to the ETO the valve stem rose quickly and trapped the ETO’s right thumb between the top of the valve

A service vessel experienced an electrical blackout during replacement of light fittings in the vicinity of one of the vessel's high voltage switchboards. The crew member who was working on replacing the light fittings leant heavily against a door to the transformer room that was fitted with a sensor. The sensor activated causing safety circuit systems to activate which opened the distribution tra

title: Night Operations Slip, Trip and Fall Crew Injuries
description:
This theme is characterised by a high-frequency pattern of crew injuries resulting from slips, trips, falls, and struck-by events occurring predominantly during night-time operations across a broad range of vessel types, including fishing vessels, dredgers, tankers, workboats, and recreational craft. The common thread across all incidents is the convergence of reduced ambient lighting with inherently high-risk activities such as mooring, vessel transfers, working at height, and deck maintenance. Adverse sea states, vessel motion, and wet or cluttered deck surfaces frequently compound the risk, yet a significant proportion of incidents occur even in benign environmental conditions, confirming that lighting deficiency, inadequate hazard controls, and systemic gaps in pre-task risk assessment are the primary latent causes rather than weather alone. The disproportionate representation of fishing vessel incidents reflects the sector's elevated baseline risk and historically lower safety culture maturity relative to larger commercial shipping operations.

From a latent causation perspective, these incidents reflect deeply embedded organisational and hardware deficiencies that persist across vessel classes. Fixed structural hazards such as unguarded holds, steel frames, and poorly marked mooring lines represent unmitigated hardware risks that are manageable in daylight but become serious injury precursors under night conditions. Human factors including fatigue associated with night-shift patterns, reduced depth perception, diminished situational awareness during post-task transitions, and the distraction of instructional or supervisory roles further elevate individual vulnerability. Critically, PPE non-compliance, the absence of robust working-at-height and working-at-night standard operating procedures, and inadequate pre-task risk assessment culture are consistent systemic contributors. The recurrence of these incidents across multiple vessel types and operational contexts indicates that corrective action must be addressed at the organisational and regulatory level, not treated as isolated individual failures.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (crew access, structural safeguards, and deck infrastructure standards)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures, PPE requirements, and emergency response capability)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (operational risk assessment, safe manning, and environmental condition monitoring obligations)
preventable_by: Mandate vessel-wide night lighting audits against a defined minimum illuminance standard (e.g., IACS or flag state guidelines) for all working decks, stairways, companionways, transfer points, and machinery spaces, with deficiencies rectified prior to night operations being authorised and documented in the vessel's safety management system.

Implement mandatory pre-task risk assessments specifically tailored to night operations, requiring explicit identification and mitigation of fixed structural hazards (unguarded holds, protruding fittings, mooring lines), PPE compliance verification including non-slip footwear and head or face protection where applicable, and a nominated safety observer for high-risk evolutions such as mooring, vessel transfers, and working at height.

Develop and enforce a formal Night Operations Safety Procedure within each vessel's SMS, encompassing safe movement protocols during heavy weather or vessel motion, prohibition of jumping between vessel and structure interfaces, minimum crew requirements for after-dark deck evolutions, and regular competency-based drills that replicate night-time conditions to embed safe behavioural norms across all crew ranks.

representative_cases.description: Oil tanker's crew member was rigging the bosun chair and showing how to lower the manila rope when they fell on their posterior side of the pelvic region. The individual took 7 days off from work.
A fishing vessel crew member fell down a staircase on board and fractured their knee. The vessel returned to port where a shore side ambulance attended and took the injured crewmember to hospital.
A suction dredger crewmember suffered a fractured leg after slipping and falling onboard while alongside. Ambulance and CRT arrived on scene, stabilised the casualty and extracted them to shore.
Inland tanker's crew member lost their balance and fell from the bridge deck onto the vessels main deck (approximately 2.5 metres)
A work boat crew member struck their ankle on a steel frame whilst working. They were signed off work for 8 days with a sprain.
Fishing vessel crewmember fell down stairs resulting in a fractured wrist. The crew member was landed ashore and taken to hospital for treatment.
A crewmember had a fall on-board a yacht which resulted in a puncture wound to their arm.
Whilst mooring a motor cruiser, one of the crew members slipped and fell into the hold resulting in a minor head injury.
Inland waterways vessel crew member tripped, resulting in sprained ankle. Crew moored/berthed towed barge to moorings, one crewmember tripped when making way back to the vessel and twisted ankle.
An angling vessel crewmember was injured when the vessel hit a wave causing the crewmember to fall to the deck and dislocate their shoulder.

title: Anchoring and Grounding Failures from Latent Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by grounding events arising from a convergence of unresolved mechanical deficiencies, inadequate contingency planning, and insufficient watchkeeping during anchoring operations. The incidents share a common pattern in which a primary means of safe manoeuvring — whether propulsion, steering, or anchoring equipment — is compromised prior to or during the critical phase of operation, and the vessel's procedural framework fails to detect, report, or remediate the deficiency before it is operationally tested. In several cases, damage or mechanical failure was known or knowable in advance, yet no effective incident-to-maintenance feedback loop existed to close the gap between identification and rectification. This systemic procedural weakness is the recurrent latent cause that transforms otherwise manageable defects into grounding events.

Latent hardware failures — including unrepaired anchor damage, propeller fouling, and engine failure — remove the vessel's margin of control at the most critical moments, leaving crews with degraded options in constrained or shallow waters. Human factors compound these hardware deficiencies: decision-making under pressure, inadequate anchor watch procedures, and insufficient pre-departure planning for propulsion loss scenarios all reduce the crew's ability to detect and arrest developing situations in time. Environmental conditions, including moderate winds, wave heights, and night-time operations, consistently act as the exposing agent that transforms a latent deficiency into an active casualty. The evidence indicates that none of these environmental conditions were exceptional; rather, they represent normal operational risk that a properly maintained and procedurally sound vessel should be capable of withstanding. Prevention therefore requires addressing the procedural and hardware maintenance systems that allow deficiencies to persist into operations, rather than treating the environmental conditions themselves as the primary cause.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance and operability of propulsion and steering systems)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (watchkeeping, navigational planning, and use of navigational equipment including anchor drag monitoring)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — defect reporting, corrective maintenance, and contingency planning requirements)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory defect-to-maintenance closure process within the vessel's Safety Management System, requiring that any damage or deficiency identified during an incident — including minor contact events — is formally recorded, assessed for fitness-for-service, and signed off as rectified before the affected equipment is returned to operational use.

Establish and exercise pre-departure contingency plans for loss of primary propulsion in the vicinity of harbour approaches and anchorages, including pre-identified emergency tow arrangements, contacts for immediate assistance, and crew drills for sail-only or anchor-only close-quarters manoeuvring in confined or shallow waters.

Require the use of calibrated anchor drag alarm systems or continuous GPS position monitoring during all overnight or unattended anchorages, supported by documented anchor watch procedures that define explicit response thresholds, escalation criteria, and maximum allowable drag distances before engine start or emergency action is initiated.

representative_cases.description: A sail training vessel with fifteen crew onboard was anchored in a bay. Overnight the anchor dragged resulting in the vessel grounding on a reef and requiring assistance. The vessel was lifted for an inspection and damage to the keel was found. It was found that the anchor was damaged, this was from a previous incident where the vessel left a harbour and made contact with the quay resulting in the

A sail training vessel was under sail when the skipper noticed there was an issue with the gear/propeller. There was no tow available into a local harbour and the vessel attempted to anchor under sail, as the engine was not available due to the issue. Whilst attempting to anchor, the vessel grounded. The tide was rising, so the vessel's crew waited and deployed the anchor. The skipper was able to

A charter yacht with five persons on board lost engine power and grounded on a sandbank. The vessel sustained minor damage and no injuries were reported. A lifeboat and CRT were tasked but the casualty was taken under tow by another vessel. The casualty was moored to the pontoons with the assistance of lifeboat and CRT.

A general cargo vessel was on approach to a port a pilot embarked for the arrival. During pilotage the vessel experienced a black, resulting in reduced manoeuvrability due to a lack of power to the steering gear. The vessels pitch was reduced however the vessel was still moved forwards. An anchor was dropped to try and slow the vessel, but the vessel grounded. The main engine was stopped and the d

Approaching a planned anchorage.  Decided to anchor 80m short of planned position as it appeared there were more mooring buoys ahead than on previous visits.  Plan was to lower 30m of cable. Cable jammed in hawse pipe with 10m out and anchor on seabed.  Gentle astern motion did not cause anchor cable to pay out and while attempting to resolve the issue and recover the anchor to relay we had drifte

A search and rescue vessel was manoeuvring close to the shore to land a crew member on the rocks when it grounded. The skipper of the vessel was attempting to move away from the shore but the swell was hampering the movement of the vessel and the tilt function of the engines was not operating properly. The crew manually lowered the engines and the vessel was able to move away from the rocks, but n

A recreational motor vessel had anchored 100 – 200m off a beach and switched off the engine. The crew realised the conditions were not as calm as expected and decided to return to harbour. One crew member pulled up the anchor and attempted to re-start engine but it wouldn’t start, they did not try to anchor again. Phoned for assistance but the vessel had already grounded by the time a search and r

A fishing vessel fouled its propeller with fishing gear, attempted to anchor and the anchor was lost due to a cable snapping. The vessel grounded on a nearby rocky shore. Search and rescue assets attended and found the crew safe and well. The vessel was re-floated on the next high tide with the assistance of a lifeboat. The vessel was towed into port by the lifeboat.

A small sailing vessel with four crew drifted aground after having engine failure as there was not enough wind to sail. The crew were recovered ashore by a lifeboat. The casualty vessel was later towed into harbour by a private vessel when the vessel re-floated. There were no injuries or reported damage.

The vessel's anchor had started to drag, the master electric switch had burnt out and there was no power to the winch. The anchor was potentially not out enough and as a result the vessel grounded onto rocks. The lifeboat towed them off the rocks and assisted the vessel back into port. No damage or water ingress was reported.

title: Undetected Hull Ingress Pathways and Inadequate Bilge Safeguards
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding incidents arising from latent deficiencies in hull integrity systems — principally failed or deteriorating through-hull fittings, exhaust system components, and seacocks — that go undetected until water ingress has reached a critical level. A consistent pattern across incidents is the absence of any effective first line of defence: bilge pumps are undersized or non-functional, bilge alarms are absent or inoperable, and pre-departure or routine maintenance inspections have failed to identify the degradation of components that are known high-risk ingress pathways. Where vessels are left unattended at berth without watchkeeping arrangements or remote monitoring, the situation is further compounded, with chance observation by members of the public becoming the sole means of detection. The incidents span both underway and alongside contexts, indicating that the underlying procedural and equipment deficiencies are systemic rather than situational.

Latent hardware failure is the proximate cause in the majority of cases within this cluster, but the severity and frequency of these incidents is sustained by compounding human and environmental factors. Inadequate familiarity with a vessel's through-hull configuration, insufficient routine inspection regimes, and an absence of formalised pre-departure watertight integrity checks reflect systemic procedural shortcomings at the owner and operator level. Environmental conditions — particularly night operations and moderate sea states — reduce the crew's ability to detect ingress early, locate its source, or conduct effective damage control. The absence of operable VHF radio communications in several incidents further delayed distress alerting, demonstrating that multiple independent safeguards were either absent or had silently degraded prior to the emergency. Collectively, these incidents illustrate how the convergence of unmonitored hardware deterioration, procedural inattention, and environmental challenge transforms a preventable maintenance failure into a SAR-requiring emergency.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Watertight Integrity (including maintenance of hull openings, through-hull fittings, and bilge pumping arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (applicable by analogy to detection and alarm systems, including bilge alarm and monitoring requirements)
SOLAS Chapter IV: Radiocommunications (carriage and operability requirements for distress alerting equipment, including VHF DSC radio)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce a structured pre-departure watertight integrity checklist for recreational and charter vessels that explicitly requires physical inspection and operation-testing of all through-hull fittings, seacocks, exhaust system connections, anti-siphon valves, and bilge pump functionality before each voyage, with records retained by the skipper or responsible person.

Require the installation and periodic testing of automatic bilge alarms and, where vessels are routinely left unattended at berth, remote monitoring systems capable of alerting the owner or a responsible party to rising bilge water levels in real time, thereby eliminating reliance on chance observation as the primary detection mechanism.

Introduce a mandatory operational readiness requirement for VHF radio carriage and crew competency on all vessels operating beyond sheltered waters at night or in reduced visibility, supported by targeted safety campaigns addressing exhaust system maintenance as a high-probability flooding pathway specific to auxiliary-engined sailing yachts.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht under engine started taking on water, as the bilges started to fill, the engine stopped and smoke was seen but no fire. The water ingress then stopped but both the bilges and engine bay were full, an anchor was deployed as the yacht was drifting towards a lee shore. The yacht requested assistance by mobile phone as there was no radio communication. A harbour vessel attended and tow

A charter fishing vessel was alongside, it was reported by members of the public that the vessel was sinking. A shore worker attended the scene as the vessel was unoccupied. The vessel was secured with additional mooring lines. There was minimal fuel aboard with no fuel seen leaking from the vessel, this was monitored and no boom defence deployed. The vessel was recovered four days later by a comm

A sailing yacht started taking on water, the pump was unable to cope with the ingress and the source of the ingress was unknown. A nearby vessel offered assistance until a search and rescue (SAR) vessel arrived and used their salvage pump. The SAR vessel then towed the sailing yacht to a harbour as the engine kept cutting out.

A small alluminium tender that was being used to conduct surveys in a shallow estuary began taking on water. The vessel was taken to a pontoon and tied up but continued to take on water and eventually became partially submerged. The vessel was towed across to the opposite side of the marina to where it was tied up and was lifted out by a crane and returned to its trailer.   There was no visible da

A pleasure craft began taking on water and called for assistance. A nearby search and rescue vessel was able to attend and provided a pump to control the water ingress. They then towed the vessel to a nearby peer and then back to its yacht club. All the crew were reported fit and well.

A stern trawler was found to be have a foot of water ingress inside the vessel's fish room coming out from the bottom of the vessel which appeared to to be a split. However it was able to make its own way into port utilising their own  hand held emergency pump that had been able to stem the water until the vessel arrived into port safely where it was also assisted by a lifeboat.

A sail training vessel was heading upwind when the bilge alarm went off so the bilges were pumped out. It was then reported that a floor board was floating, upon checking there was significant water in all of the bilges. The strainer was checked and each section pumped out but it started to fill again. The vessel turned around started to head back, a salvage pump set up and a PAN PAN issued. Two s

A fishing vessel reported water ingress in the engine room, the reason at the time was unknown. The vessel proceeded to the nearest harbour whilst the crew attempted to pump out the water. A nearby vessel escorted the fishing vessel until it made it safely to harbour where the water was pumped out by a search and rescue (SAR) vessel.

An offshore powerboat with two crew onboard started taking on water, two nearby vessel assisted along with two search and rescue (SAR) that deployed to the scene. Salvage pumps were placed onboard before the vessel was towed to safety. The water ingress was found to have come from the exhaust system.

Fishing vessel was taking on water which was followed by successful pumping actions. The crew members contacted the shore for assistance however, they were able to resolve the problem with the pumps. They realised that there was a 2 inch pipe where the leak was coming from. Crew then decided to turn the seacock off which stopped the ingression. The lifeboat escorted the vessel back to shore.

title: Unguarded Deck Trip Hazards Causing Crew Injury
description:
This theme is characterised by crew members sustaining injuries after tripping over unguarded or inadequately marked structural deck hazards — including raised hatches, riser beams, deck brackets, anchor chain hawse-pipe covers, and scaffold bars — during routine operational tasks such as lifting, mooring, and equipment recovery. A consistent pattern across these incidents is that the physical hazard was pre-existing and foreseeable, yet remained unmitigated at the time of injury. The recurrence of these incidents is fundamentally attributable to the normalisation of deck-level obstructions as an accepted feature of the working environment, combined with a systemic failure to conduct meaningful pre-task hazard identification that accounts for dynamic operational conditions, particularly reduced visibility during night operations and constrained working spaces created by cargo or equipment placement.

The latent hardware deficiency in each case is the absence of adequate engineering controls — guarding, demarcation, anti-trip covers, or conspicuous marking — on known structural protrusions within active crew movement zones, reflecting gaps in vessel design review and maintenance safety standards. Human factors are equally significant: in the majority of incidents, the crew member's attentional focus was directed toward the primary task — monitoring a suspended load, passing equipment, or navigating purposefully — leaving insufficient cognitive resource for foot placement awareness, a well-documented consequence of task-induced attentional narrowing. Environmental degradation, principally night-time low-light conditions and adverse sea state introducing vessel motion and deck instability, consistently elevated the residual risk beyond levels that daytime procedural controls were designed to manage. The combined effect of unmitigated hardware deficiencies, divided human attention, and degraded environmental conditions, in the absence of task-specific risk assessment, created the conditions for repeated, preventable injury.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structure, Subdivision and Stability (safe design of working decks and structural features)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe operation and recovery of fast rescue boats and associated deck areas)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (safe working practices, operational risk management, and onboard safety culture)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-task hazard identification and toolbox talk for all deck operations conducted in low-light or night conditions, explicitly requiring identification and communication of all deck-level trip hazards within the working zone, with documented sign-off by the responsible officer before operations commence.

Engineering controls requiring all permanent or semi-permanent structural protrusions in active crew working areas — including raised beams, hatches, brackets, and frame members — to be fitted with high-visibility marking, physical guarding or chamfered anti-trip profiles, and reviewed during periodic vessel safety audits as a mandatory maintenance and design compliance check.

Implementation of a load landing and working space assessment protocol for lifting and cargo operations, requiring the responsible officer to verify that the intended load landing position does not restrict crew freedom of movement or force personnel into proximity with unguarded structural hazards, with alternative landing positions designated in advance where spatial constraints are identified.

representative_cases.description: On board a ro-ro passenger vessel that was moored alongside, two members of the ship's crew were tasked with recovering the fast rescue boat. Earlier in the day, the fast rescue boat had been used to carry out an inspection of the ship's side and was moored alongside. After embarking onto the fast rescue boat from the pilot door via a pilot ladder, the crew manoeuvered the fast rescue craft under

A offshore supply vessel had a large crate that contained a coflex hose which had been landed onto the riser deck between one of their riser joints and the telescope point. The riser bays had also raised beams running forward and aft between the samson posts, when one of the crew member was in the process of assist to remove one of the wire slings used to transport the load, they were in the proce

A tug crew member was holding a tag line on the back deck during a routine lifting operation. As the lift came round the crew member was watching the load, stepped back and tripped on a bracket bolted to the deck. The crew member fell and landed on the deck sustaining a wrist injury. The crew member was taken to hospital where a fracture to the left wrist was confirmed.

A Ro-Ro passenger vessels crew member tripped over anchor chain resulting in closed fracture to the hand. A crew member, on forecastle mooring station, when walking, tripped on the edge of the anchor chain hawse-pipe cover guide (Painted Yellow) and lost balance, falling to deck, landing on and striking his left fore arm.

A replenishment vessel crew member was walking from forward to aft on the upper deck through an access tunnel when they tripped over a ankle high horizontal scaffold bar. This scaffold tower had only just been built and the bar was not very clear visually and positioned at a low height. The crew member was treated for cuts and returned to work.

A passenger vessel crewmember was walking on deck when they fell over a fender that had been left there. They tried to grab hold of a door to prevent their fall but landed heavily on their left knee and twisted their right shoulder. They were diagnosed with a dislocated shoulder and a dislocated patella.

A ro-pax vessel crew member tripped over a set of bitts on the deck and fell, sustaining a sprained wrist. The crew member was letting the ropes go from a set of bitts that were mounted on the bulwarks Whilst doing this, they tripped over another set of bitts that were attached to the deck. They were signed off work for a week but returned a few days earlier to partake in light duties. The bitts o

A crew member slipped and fell whilst proceeding to forecastle in preparation for singling up mooring lines and used their arm to grab onto frame of door to prevent a fall to the deck. PPE correctly worn and in good condition. This resulted in a shoulder dislocation.

Ro-Ro cargo vessel's crew member tripped resulting in musculoskeletal back and right foot pain. The crew member was taken for further medical assistance. The reason of this accident could be partly because of inattention of the new crew member as well as a flaw layout of the particular area in the engine room.

Crew member rolled ankle while disembarking the ships gangway resulting in closed fracture. 	IP had transited the gangway to the jetty, and stepped off the end of the accommodation ladder to the quayside. On the quayside was a pothole obscured by moss / plant growth within the pothole. IP stepped onto the edge of the pothole, and when IPs weight transferred to the edge of the pothole, IPs ankle ro

title: Slip Hazards on Vessel Surfaces: Latent Safety Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by recurring slip-and-fall incidents occurring across a range of vessel types and operational contexts — including passenger open decks, crew working spaces, internal staircases, and transitional threshold areas such as door sills and ramps. The common thread is not a single point of failure but rather the convergence of degraded or inadequate surface conditions with environmental stressors and the absence of proactive risk controls. These incidents persist because surface integrity is treated as a static property rather than a dynamic operational variable: anti-slip surfaces degrade through scale accumulation, contamination, or wear; gradient and edge hazards at deck transitions are normalised until injury occurs; and reactive cleaning or maintenance tasks introduce compounding chemical or physical hazards that are not assessed before work commences. The result is a systemic pattern in which foreseeable slip risks are permitted to mature into injury events without meaningful intervention.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including inadequate anti-slip surface treatments, absent or ineffective edge markings, and design features that create abrupt transitions between internal and external deck environments — establish the underlying conditions for injury. These deficiencies are frequently invisible until an incident reveals them, partly because routine inspection and maintenance regimes do not systematically link environmental operating conditions (humidity, sea state, night operations, chemical contamination) to physical asset integrity checks. Human factors compound this: crew and passengers alike underestimate dynamic hazard states, particularly when familiar surfaces become significantly more hazardous due to moisture, oil, or vessel motion. Environmental conditions — moderate sea states, night-time lighting, heat and humidity — act as force multipliers, elevating inherent design risks to injury-causing events. The near-total absence of access controls, dynamic hazard warnings, and structured safe systems of work for reactive maintenance tasks indicates that procedural frameworks have not kept pace with the operational realities of these vessels.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structural integrity, watertight integrity, and design of access and working spaces including stairs, ramps, and deck surfaces
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements — Crew readiness, maintenance of safety equipment, and procedures governing passenger and crew movement in adverse conditions
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships — ISM Code obligations for hazard identification, risk assessment, safe systems of work, and proactive maintenance regimes
preventable_by: Implement a dynamic surface hazard management protocol that mandates condition-based inspections of all high-risk deck surfaces — including ramps, stairs, thresholds, and void space decks — triggered by defined environmental thresholds such as sea state, humidity, precipitation, or night operations, with findings logged and corrective action escalated before personnel access is permitted.

Establish a structured Safe System of Work (SSOW) for all reactive spill remediation and cleaning tasks involving chemical agents, requiring a written task-specific risk assessment that explicitly identifies synergistic hazard combinations (e.g., oil plus degreaser), mandates staged application methods, specifies required PPE and slip-prevention controls (absorbent materials, cordoning), and prohibits unsupervised individual judgement as the primary hazard control.

Introduce a scheduled anti-slip surface integrity programme under the vessel's planned maintenance system (PMS), incorporating quantitative friction testing or tactile inspection criteria for all pedestrian surfaces in humid, wet, or chemically exposed environments, with mandatory remediation — including descaling, re-coating, or replacement — before degradation reaches a threshold that creates unacceptable slip risk, supplemented by visible dynamic hazard signage deployed automatically or by crew during adverse environmental conditions.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was walking towards the open deck, as they exited the door on to the open deck the passenger slipped on the ramp falling to the deck. The passenger was treated for a fracture to the right wrist. The passenger was not medically disembarked. No defects were found at the scene.

The crew of a ro-pax vessel were working in the void space for the clam doors to clean up a hydraulic oil spill when one crew member slipped on the combination of hydraulic oil and degreaser and fell to the deck. They stopped work and were given first aid on board in the form of pain relief. However the injury did not improve and the crewmember disembarked and attended a local hospital where it wa

A ro-ro cargo crew member was descending a stair case when they slipped on a step and slid down the rest of the way to the bottom of the stairs. The crew member's right arm got caught on the hand rail as they were sliding down resulting in a dislocated shoulder. The crew member received medical treatment on board due to medically trained supernumeraries being on board at the time. The crew member

A service vessel crew member slipped on a door seal resulting in a sprained ankle. The injured party was leaving the walk-in fridge and stepped onto the door frame sill, when their right foot slipped. The injured party's right knee twisted and as they put weight onto their left foot, they then sprained their left ankle.

Class V passenger vessel guest slipped and suffered a suspected broken leg. Lifeboat crew gave initial casualty care before transferring to the care of the ambulance service who transferred the casualty to hospital.

Mooring launch crew member slipped on a newly painted surface on the gunwale whilst boarding resulting in a minor injury. The mooring launch was taken out of service and repainted with anti-slip material. The crew member continued to work after the fall.

A cruise ship passenger fell and broke their wrist. They were standing next to an x-ray machine when they slipped and in an effort to break their fall they put out their hand resulting in a broken wrist. The area was inspected and there were no defects found however the floor had been recently polished the night before. The passenger was wearing flat rubber soled shoes.

A cruise passenger slipped on wet tile resulting in a fracture to the face. Passenger exiting the buffet seating area aft going toward the elevators, right foot slipped on the marble tile next to the carpet causing the fall face forward. Medical report: Fracture of the nasal bridge.

A cruise ship passenger was entering the bathroom in their cabin when they stepped on the metal threshold with their foot and slipped. They managed to prevent their fall by grabbing the wash basin table and the bathtub, however their foot bent back causing a fracture. The scene of the accident was inspected and no obvious defects were found. There were no damages or pollution.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in the pool area when they slipped and fell, sustaining a fractured patella. The area was reported to be wet at the time due to rain and there were warning signs in place to alert passengers to the wet surface. The vessel reported that the wet floor and lack of care on the part of the passenger were what caused them to slip.

title: Mechanical Lifting System Failures Under Adverse Operational Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by the failure or loss of control of mechanical lifting and transfer systems — including replenishment winches, lifeboat davits, and helicopter hoists — during operational or maintenance deployments, frequently occurring under degraded environmental conditions such as night-time, elevated sea states, and wind. A recurring pattern across these incidents is the convergence of undetected latent hardware defects with procedural shortfalls: crews apply improvised or temporary fixes rather than executing controlled abort procedures, maintenance regimes fail to identify developing mechanical faults before they manifest operationally, and contingency protocols for compound failure scenarios are either absent or inadequately rehearsed. The consequence is that personnel are routinely exposed to residual hazards — snap-back energy, prolonged immersion, or uncontrolled mechanical loads — that a well-structured emergency response should have mitigated or eliminated entirely.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a primary driver across this cluster, manifesting as brake failures, electrical burnouts, wire overloads, and undetected davit mechanism faults that escape detection through infrequent operational use and insufficiently rigorous maintenance programmes. These mechanical vulnerabilities are compounded by human factors, including inadequate crew knowledge of snap-back danger zones, poor situational awareness of emergency abort criteria, and decision-making under time pressure that prioritises continuity of operation over safety. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time reduced visibility and sea states that exceed formally assessed parameters — act as force multipliers, masking early mechanical warning signs and increasing dynamic loading on already-stressed systems. The systemic latent cause is an organisational tolerance of maintenance and training gaps in life-saving and transfer appliance systems, where the infrequency of failure creates a false assurance of reliability until conditions conspire to expose the underlying deficiencies.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (covering davit systems, lifeboat deployment, LSA maintenance and drills)
SOLAS Chapter VI / VII – Carriage of Cargoes and Dangerous Goods (covering replenishment and transfer operations, winch and hose-handling equipment)
SOLAS Chapter I – General (survey, inspection, and maintenance standards applicable to shipborne mechanical equipment and operational readiness)
preventable_by: Implement enhanced periodic operational testing and condition-based maintenance protocols for all mechanical lifting and transfer systems (winches, davits, hoist equipment), requiring documented pre-operation inspections before each deployment and independent verification of brake, wire, and limit-switch integrity — not solely reliance on scheduled drill cycles.

Develop and mandate vessel- and operation-specific emergency abort procedures for winch, davit, and hoist failures, including clearly defined snap-back exclusion zones, wire-tension management protocols, and coordinated contingency plans for multi-asset operations (e.g. helicopter–vessel coordination for winch failure scenarios), with competency assessed through realistic scenario-based drills.

Introduce mandatory pre-operation environmental threshold assessments that reconcile reported sea state classifications with actual observed wave heights and wind speeds, with defined operational limits that trigger automatic suspension or escalated risk controls when conditions exceed planned parameters — preventing operations from proceeding under formally under-assessed environmental conditions.

representative_cases.description: Replenishment tanker lost control of its winch wire which had tensioned between two ships, when a crew member managed to make a temporary fix, but it had the potential for a snap-back if the wire was to snap.

A service vessel lifeboat davit failed to operate during maintenance. .  As the davit was being deployed, it suddenly stopped at approx. 30 deg from the horizontal deck.  All attempts to continue to deploy or recover the davit were unsuccessful.

A service vessel was carrying out operations with a helicopter winching crewmembers to and from the deck. During one winching operation, the helicopter winch encountered difficulties wherein the person being winched was dropped onto the aft railings causing them to fall onto the transom of the vessel. To prevent the helicopter becoming entangled with the vessel, the crew of the helicopter cut the

A replenishment tanker was carrying out a replenishment activity whilst doing a transfer. This caused the winches controlling the transfer hose to experience electrical failure, The crew restored power by replacing the faulty electrical components which had burned out.

A replenishment tanker was carrying out an operation of the lifeboat davit using the ‘Dead Ship Mode’ (DSM) lowering without electrical power to the system. The first practice was with an unoccupied lifeboat under electrical power. The crew then embarked ready for lowering via the DSM method which is controlled from inside the boat. Once the lifeboat was in position for lowering, pressure was appl
Tanker was in passage when it experienced heavy weather conditions. As a result a lifeboat free fell and was lost.

A standby vessel was launching its fast rescue craft (FRC) as a test. The crew boarded the FRC, the davit deployed the vessel but the spooling winch also started causing the FRC to tilt. One crewmember was tossed into the water and was recovered by the FRC once it was in the water. The crewmember was wearing a lifejacket and sustained no injuries.  A valve within the davit launch system was found

During the recovery of the FRC (fast rescue craft) following a drill, with one crewmember onboard, the wire drum free fall break on the davit  failed and started lowering the boat back to sea level. This was from approximately one metre high. The crewmember exited the FRC at sea level via the embarkation door . The brake was tested several times and intermittently failed to hold the FRC in a suspe
A boat davit collapsed when carrying out a load test on a workboat davit. No pollution occurred.  There was structural damage to the starboard workboat aft davit arm.

Whilst alongside in harbour, routine maintenance was being carried out on a lifeboat.  The boat had been lowered and was being hoisted when it unexpectedly began to slowly lower itself.  The crew attempted to stop the descent manually but this had no effect.  The boat was empty at the time and there were no injuries to crew or damage to the boat, davit structure or fall wires.
