title: Propulsion Failure with Cascading Emergency Anchor Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring sequence in which the loss of main propulsion — attributable to latent mechanical defects in engines or propellers — triggers an emergency anchoring response that subsequently fails to hold the vessel, allowing it to drift into grounding or contact with navigational hazards. The pattern appears consistently across vessel types and operational contexts, indicating that the underlying causes are systemic rather than circumstantial. Vessels are routinely departing with undetected or unaddressed mechanical deficiencies, and crews are defaulting to anchor deployment as an emergency fallback without adequate consideration of anchor type, scope, holding ground suitability, or prevailing environmental forces. The absence of alternative contingency measures — such as readily deployable tow lines or pre-established emergency towing arrangements — means that once the anchor fails, the vessel is entirely dependent on opportunistic third-party assistance or SAR intervention, several of which resulted in vessel loss or serious structural damage.

Latent hardware failures, specifically propulsion system defects that were present but unidentified prior to departure, represent the foundational causal factor across this cluster. These failures are consistent with inadequate or insufficiently rigorous pre-departure and routine maintenance inspection regimes. Human factors compound the problem: crews operating alone or in small numbers at night face significantly degraded situational awareness, impairing accurate assessment of proximity to hazards and reducing the effectiveness of manual interventions. Environmental conditions — moderate winds in the range of 16 to 19 knots and wave heights approaching 1.5 metres — while not extreme, proved sufficient to overcome the holding capacity of improperly deployed anchors, accelerating drift toward coastal hazards. The convergence of these latent mechanical, procedural, and environmental factors, in the absence of robust emergency preparedness frameworks and navigational safety margins, explains both the frequency and the escalating severity observed within this incident cluster.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (machinery maintenance, propulsion system integrity and emergency source of power requirements)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, proximity to hazards, watchkeeping and situational awareness obligations)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness, distress signalling, and survival craft readiness including MAYDAY procedures and SAR coordination)
preventable_by: Mandatory structured pre-departure machinery inspection checklists with documented sign-off by a competent person, specifically requiring verification of propulsion system integrity — including propeller condition, shaft seals, and engine mounts — before each voyage, with records retained and subject to periodic port State or flag State audit.

Development and regular drill-testing of a vessel-specific propulsion loss contingency plan that includes pre-identified emergency anchorage positions with confirmed adequate depth and holding ground, minimum anchor scope calculations for expected wind and sea conditions, and a permanently rigged emergency tow line of sufficient strength readily accessible on deck, reducing dependence on SAR or third-party vessels for tow establishment.

Implementation of mandatory voyage risk assessments for operations in proximity to coastal hazards, requiring skippers to apply defined navigational safety margins from reefs, shoals, and lee shores, and to adjust departure timing or route to avoid night-time transits in areas where mechanical failure would leave insufficient sea room for effective emergency response, with additional requirements for single-handed vessels to carry a functioning EPIRB and AIS transponder at all times.

representative_cases.description: General cargo vessel suffered an engine failure so the crew decided to drop the anchor. The vessel touched the bottom on the port side. However, the vessel was refloated with the assistance from a local craft and was taken under tow to port.

A fishing vessel experienced a propulsion failure due to a defective propeller, an anchor was deployed but didn't hold, the started to drift close to shore, a nearby vessel was able to assist and prevent the vessel from grounding but unable to tow. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked and established a tow. The stricken vessel was towed to a harbour.

A fishing vessel with two crew onboard had a mechanical failure, the crew dropped an anchor but the anchor started to drag. Further attempts were made but the vessel continued to drift towards a rocky reef. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was deployed and towed the stricken vessel to a harbour.

A fishing vessel had an engine failure, the lone skipper put out an anchor whilst they attempted to restart the engine and send a MAYDAY via radio. The anchor started to drag with fishing vessel being blown onto the rocks, capsized and eventually broke up on the rocks. The skipper had abandoned the vessel and taken shelter on the rocks, search and rescue assets were tasked to the scene where the s

A general cargo vessel had an engine failure, an anchor was dropped but the vessel then later grounded due to the falling tide. The vessel was re-floated two days later with the assistance of tugs and then towed alongside. There was no damage or pollution.

Cargo vessel lost propulsion power when approaching the bridge and dropped both emergency anchors. The vessel managed to stop right before the bridge however, due to the wind the vessel was pushed and made contact with the corner of a pier. There were no damages on either the vessel or the pier.

A windfarm crew transfer vessel was manoeuvring in a port area when it experienced control failure and made contact with a pontoon. It then drifted across the harbour. The crew tried to stop the drift by dropping the anchor but it was not effective in preventing the vessel from colliding with a moored fishing vessel. The fishing vessel sustained dents and scrapes to the wheelhouse, the crew transf

The vessel was returning to port after harvesting salmon from a fish farm when the engine stopped unexpectedly. The vessel crew were unable to restart the engine and dropped the anchor. The anchor didn't hold and the vessel crew continued to try to restart the engine. The vessel was one nautical mile away from shore when the engine restarted. A lifeboat arrived on scene and escorted the vessel bac

Sailing vessel abandoned due to taken on water after dragging anchor resulting in running aground. Small electric engine not powerful enough to get out of the situation. 2 POB abandoned ship and swam 2 miles back to shore, vessel then drifted and ran around due to onshore winds, sails still in sail bags.

An offshore windfarm crew transfer vessel experienced propulsion failure shortly after departing from the berth and began to drift up river on the current. The crew noticed that the vessel was drifting onto a nearby ferry terminal and deployed an anchor however the anchor did not hold the vessel. Another work boat came and took the stricken vessel under emergency tow to a safe berth for further in

title: Manual Handling Musculoskeletal Injuries in Night Maritime Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of musculoskeletal and physical injuries sustained by crew members during routine manual handling tasks aboard a diverse range of vessel types, including ro-pax, cruise, ro-ro, and service vessels. The incidents consistently involve lifting, pulling, twisting, or carrying loads without mechanical assistance, frequently in night-time conditions and in the presence of vessel motion. What unites these cases is not the novelty of the hazard but its very ordinariness: injuries occur during stores handling, mooring operations, galley duties, and equipment movement — tasks so routine that their inherent risk is routinely underestimated. The systemic recurrence of these incidents across vessel types and operators points firmly to sector-wide deficiencies in manual handling training, risk assessment culture, and the enforcement of mechanical aid usage policies, rather than isolated instances of individual error. The frequency and distribution of these events strongly suggest chronic underreporting, masking the true scale of the problem within the short-sea and deep-sea sectors alike.

Latent hardware and organisational failures play a central role in this theme. The persistent absence or non-utilisation of mechanical handling aids — trolleys, hoists, and conveyors — at key operational locations such as mooring stations, galleys, and stores access points reflects an engineering and procurement gap within vessel Safety Management Systems. Human factors including fatigue (particularly during night watches), time pressure during port turnarounds, and ingrained habits of manual effort over mechanical assistance compound the physical exposure. Environmental conditions, notably vessel motion from sea states that are frequently under-reported relative to actual wave heights, introduce dynamic instability that significantly amplifies biomechanical injury risk during otherwise manageable tasks. The convergence of degraded lighting, moving platforms, awkward postures, and insufficient training in situational risk assessment creates a predictable injury pathway that current SMS frameworks are failing to interrupt. Regulatory obligations under the ISM Code and ILO Maritime Labour Convention require meaningful corrective action at the level of safety culture, task-level risk assessment, and operational scheduling.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code): Safety Management System obligations for hazard identification, risk assessment, and safe working procedures
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (safe access, workspace design, and equipment provision obligations)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew readiness, physical fitness, and watch-keeping welfare as preconditions for safe operational performance)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce the use of mechanical handling aids (trolleys, hoists, conveyors) at all designated manual handling locations across vessel types, supported by formal SMS procedures that prohibit solo manual lifting above defined weight thresholds without a documented risk assessment, and audit compliance during Port State Control and internal ISM inspections.

Implement a structured, vessel-type-specific manual handling training programme — delivered at onboarding and at regular intervals — that incorporates dynamic sea-state scenarios, night-time condition simulations, and task-specific ergonomic techniques for mooring, galley, and stores operations, with demonstrated competency assessment rather than passive e-learning completion.

Introduce a mandatory pre-task dynamic risk assessment protocol for all manual handling operations conducted during night watches or in sea states above Sea State 2, requiring crew to formally consider load weight, vessel motion, environmental lighting, fatigue level, and availability of mechanical aids before commencing the task, with findings logged within the vessel's SMS safety observation system to enable fleet-wide trend analysis and intervention.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel crew member was lifting a box of stores when they collapsed. When they regained consciousness it was discovered that they had dislocated their elbow.
The passenger ship was berthing once a crew member hurt their back due to pulling up the slack in the mooring line by hand.
A service vessel crewmember was moving a hose and when pressing it onto the manifold and twisting it, the crewmember experienced back pain.
A cruise ship crewmember suffered a sprained back while lifting a crate of cutlery. The crewmember was unable to continue their duties and was later medically disembarked.
Cruise ship's crew member was in the process to lift the upper bunk when they felt pain in their knee. Crew member disembarked as they required further medical assistance.
A cruise ship crewmember was moving a baggage trolley when they were unable to stop it and it struck their ankle resulting in a strained foot. The crewmember was signed off work for 7 day.
A replenishment tanker crew member was going down a ladder when they banged their head on a sprinkler and sustained a cut to their ear.
A crewmember on a ro-ro ferry strained their back whilst heaving in a line during departure from its mooring resulting in them being signed off work.
Passenger Ro-Ro's crew member tripped while carrying a dish resulting in open wound to the finger.
A crew transfer vessel windfarm technician injured their back lifting a personal bag awkwardly. The crewmember was met by an ambulance ashore.

title: Anchor System Failures: Maintenance, Procedure, and Situational Awareness
description:
This theme is characterised by a convergence of deficient maintenance regimes, inadequate procedural controls, and insufficient pre-operational risk assessment across anchoring and mooring operations. The incidents share a common latent vulnerability: anchor gear — including shackles, chains, and stowage arrangements — is not subject to sufficiently rigorous inspection cycles to identify material fatigue, corrosion, or wear before failure occurs in service. Compounding this, procedural gaps in pre-anchoring risk assessment mean that critical hazards such as subsea infrastructure are not consistently treated as explicit verification requirements. The willingness of operators to accept cost-driven risk decisions, such as electing not to recover lost anchors, suggests a broader organisational culture in which maintenance and safety expenditure is subordinated to short-term financial considerations, creating conditions in which latent hardware deficiencies go unaddressed until they manifest as operational failures.

Latent hardware degradation — particularly material fatigue and corrosion in shackles and chain links — is the proximate physical cause across multiple incidents, but the enabling conditions are primarily human and organisational in nature. Environmental factors, notably night-time operations, rough sea states, and dynamic loading from wind and swell, consistently reduce the effectiveness of visual verification and increase the dynamic stresses placed upon already-degraded equipment. Human factors including failure to follow or apply pre-arrival checklists, inadequate chart consultation, and insufficient bridge team situational awareness during anchoring manoeuvres further erode the barriers that should prevent these failures from escalating. The pattern indicates that no single control is failing in isolation; rather, a systemic absence of defence-in-depth across maintenance, procedure, training, and environmental risk management creates the conditions in which anchor system failures recur across vessel types and operating contexts.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structure, Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (watertight integrity and hull damage prevention)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (chart carriage, voyage planning, and situational awareness obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — maintenance systems, risk assessment, and safety management obligations)
preventable_by: Mandatory periodic inspection and load-testing of anchor gear components — including shackles, swivels, and chain links — at defined intervals within the vessel's planned maintenance system, with findings recorded and reviewed by a competent authority prior to operational use, and explicit prohibition on continued use of components exhibiting corrosion, wear, or deformation beyond class-defined tolerances.

Implementation of a structured pre-anchoring risk assessment checklist that requires explicit verification of subsea infrastructure using up-to-date charts and relevant navigational publications, confirmation of anchor gear condition and readiness, and documented supervisory sign-off before anchoring in any location, with enhanced scrutiny required for night-time or adverse weather operations.

Development and enforcement of post-arrival anchor stowage verification procedures requiring bridge or deck officer confirmation — supported by physical inspection where practicable — that the anchor is correctly housed and secured before the vessel proceeds to berth, with bilge alarm response protocols updated to include anchor position as an immediate investigative step when forward void space alarms activate.

representative_cases.description: General cargo vessel broke their anchor chain and lost one anchor. The anchor shackle snapped during anchor lifting and the owners decided that the cost to recover the anchor exceeded the value of the anchor.

A ro-pax vessel experienced water ingress into two forward void spaces. The vessel had just come alongside at its arrival port when the crew were alerted to bilge alarms in two of the forward void spaces. It is believed that the anchor was not housed correctly and caused holes in the hull resulting in water ingress. There was no pollution or injuries.

A ro-ro cargo vessel allegedly caused damage to an underwater cable with their anchor. The vessel was at anchor in a designated anchorage. They were contacted the next day by the company that manages the fibre optic cable in the area. The cable reportedly was damaged and the vessel had been anchored in that area at the time of the damage.

A sail training vessel was at anchor close to the coast when it began to drag anchor and grounded approximately 10m from the shore. The crew broadcast a mayday and the local lifeboat service dispatched a vessel to assist. Before the lifeboat arrived, the sail training vessel refloated and the crew were able to get it back underway. The lifeboat escorted the sail training vessel back to a nearby ma

A cruise ship was at anchor, the vessel was swinging on its anchor, with one main engine and two bow thrusters on line to alleviate the swing. One of the links in the anchor chain broke resulting in the loss of the port anchor and approximately one cable of chain. The vessel maintained position as the main engine and two bow thrusters were still online at the time of incident. There was no damage

The incident occurred while the vessel was lying to its port anchor in 35 to 40 knots of wind. One of the links in the anchor chain broke resulting in the loss of the anchor and three cables of chain. The anchor and chain were recovered at a later date.

SAR vessel stuck submerged unknown object resulting in damaging the propeller blades. Anchor fouled on submerged object or wreckage. During manoeuvres by lifeboat to free anchor, the starboard propeller struck part of the object below the surface. Propeller blades damaged beyond repair.  Anchor subsequently freed from object and lifeboat continued training exercise.

A general cargo vessel dragged anchor in adverse weather conditions and was close to grounding. The crew attempted to hold position using the engine but continued to drag closer to shore. Lifeboats and a rescue helicopter were initially tasked but were stood down once the vessel recovered under its own power to safer water. There was no reported damage or injuries.

A ro-ro passenger vessel accidentally released their anchor on approach to harbour. Approximately two cables ran out and the anchor was initially dragged along the sea bed. It transpired that the automatic release mechanism failed at speed and the entire port anchor cable was let go.

A cruise ship was at anchor in high winds when it started to drag the anchor. The crew started the engine and heaved up the anchor. They recovered the anchor cable, but the anchor itself was missing. The reason for this was thought to be a failed D shackle.

title: Unattended Heat Sources and Poor Housekeeping Fire Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by fires arising from the convergence of inadequate procedural controls, poor housekeeping practices, and insufficient supervision of heat-generating equipment in high-risk spaces. Across all incidents, a common thread emerges: combustible materials — whether lint accumulation in a tumble dryer, a rag left on an exhaust surface, or a malfunctioning vacuum cleaner — were permitted to reach ignition threshold in the absence of effective procedural barriers or active supervision. These incidents recur because the underlying organisational controls — pre-departure inspection checklists, post-maintenance clearance checks, and appliance supervision policies — were either absent, inadequately enforced, or had been eroded through normalisation of minor housekeeping lapses. The busy operational context, including port arrival phases and routine maintenance activities, further diverted crew attention at precisely the moments when vigilance was most required.

Latent hardware deficiencies, including degraded electrical wiring, poorly maintained tumble dryer lint traps, and vacuum cleaners with undetected mechanical faults, created the underlying ignition potential that procedural failures then failed to intercept. Human factors, notably complacency, task saturation during high-tempo operations, and an absence of a culture reinforcing hot-surface and housekeeping discipline, allowed these hardware vulnerabilities to remain unaddressed. Environmental conditions such as reduced night-time visibility during engine room walkarounds and the inherently flammable atmosphere of engine spaces amplified the consequences of each lapse. Collectively, these factors indicate a systemic organisational weakness in the management of ignition risk associated with routine equipment and maintenance activities, where no single robust barrier existed to prevent a foreseeable minor oversight from escalating into a fire event.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Emergency Preparedness and Mustering)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce mandatory pre-departure and post-maintenance hot-surface clearance checklists requiring a designated crew member to physically verify that all exhaust surfaces, engine room areas, and heat-generating equipment are clear of combustible materials before departure or following any maintenance activity, with completion recorded in the vessel's safety management system.

Establish a formal portable appliance and laundry equipment management programme that includes scheduled lint trap cleaning, periodic electrical safety inspections, and a strict prohibition on operating unattended heat-producing appliances — particularly tumble dryers and vacuum cleaners — in high-risk spaces, with supervisory requirements documented in the SMS and verified during internal audits.

Introduce structured engine room and high-risk space safety walkarounds at defined operational milestones (pre-departure, watch handover, post-maintenance) using a standardised checklist that explicitly addresses housekeeping standards, combustible material clearance, and appliance status, supported by crew training that reinforces the fire risk associated with normalised housekeeping lapses and the critical importance of these checks during busy or high-tempo phases.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax ferry experienced a fire in the laundry whilst alongside in port. The vessel was just about to lower the forward ramp to discharge passengers and vehicles when the fire alarm activated. The vessels crew carried out a muster, disembarking the passengers and preparing to fight the fire. The fire was extinguished by the crew and the fixed sprinkler system in the laundry. After the fire was c

A crew transfer vessel was outbound to a work site when the crew noticed on the engine room camera that there was a fire. A crewmember went to investigate and found that a rag had been left on top of an exhaust which caught alight due to the hot surface. The crewmember extinguished it with another wet rag to smother the flames and water to cool the area. There was no damage and the vessel proceede

A service vessel was on passage when a fire alarm activated. Two crew members made their way to the engine room where they could see and smell smoke. As they entered the engine room they found an unattended vacuum cleaner emitting flames and smoke. The vacuum was unplugged and flames quickly extinguished with a fire extinguisher. No damage to vessel.

An crew transfer vessel experienced a fire on one of its engines. The vessel crew noticed on the engine room CCTV (closed circuit television) that the engine appeared to be smoking or steaming. Then the fire alarm sounded and flames were sighted in the exhaust area of the engine. The crew shut down the engine and closed down the space, then released the fixed fire fighting system. They returned to

Waste disposal operators on board a passenger ship discovered a small fire within the waste silo. The vessels incinerator had been shutdown the morning prior and consequently meant that no damage had been caused to the silo - as far as could be seen on a visual inspection. The crew discharged a water and CO2 extinguishers as well as the silo's water mist system.

A trawler was alongside in port when a fire started on a tumble dryer. This produced a lot of smoke and the five crew disembarked from the vessel onto the quayside and the shore side fire service attended. One crew member was given first aid for smoke inhalation by the fire fighters who also removed the tumble dryer from the vessel. There was no serious damage, pollution or injuries.

Smoke was noticed in the galley on a cruise ship. The crew members proceeded to investigate when they noticed a fire in the oven. The crew extinguished the fire with two CO2 extinguishers. The fire occurred as a result of an electrical short circuit. No injuries were reported and/or significant damage.

Small fire onboard a cruise ship in the valet area resulting in minor damage to a drying machine. Confirmed white smoke coming from the dryer in the Valet area. Shortly after First Stage response fully mustered. Ventilation and power confirmed off.  Discovered a small Fire from the dryer machine drum that was extinguished, by First Stage Response, using the closest dry Powder fire Extinguisher.

A crewmember noticed smoke coming from the deckhead and raised the alarm by using the closest push button fire point. The emergency alarm was sounded and all crew mustered. They shut down all ventilation and activated of the fire pump. A crewmember checked the door and entered the cabin with a portable water extinguisher. On entering, they could see smoke billowing out of the deck head and flames

The vessel's smoke head was activated in the area of the galley and fridge freezers and adjacent heads indicated rising levels. A crewmember went to investigate, smoke was reported in the area by a member of the catering crew and the location of the fire was established being in a rapid thawing unit. Crewmembers started using CO2 fire extinguishers to try to put out the fire. Due to the rising smo

title: Solo Passage Failures: Redundancy, Competency, and Preparedness
description: This theme is characterised by the convergence of single-handed operation, critical system failures, and inadequate pre-departure risk management across a range of vessel types and passage profiles. In each incident, the absence of crew redundancy transforms an otherwise manageable technical or navigational problem — engine failure, tiller breakage, adverse conditions — into a situation requiring SAR intervention. The recurring pattern reveals a systemic failure at the pre-departure stage: skippers undertaking solo passages do not consistently demonstrate, nor are they consistently required to demonstrate, that their competency, vessel equipment, and emergency protocols are commensurate with the demands of the intended passage. When the primary system fails, there is no fallback — human, technical, or procedural — to prevent escalation, and SAR becomes the sole safeguard in the risk chain.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness, distress signalling, and survival equipment requirements for vessels on offshore passages)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (mandatory carriage and operability of distress and safety communications equipment, including redundant systems for vessels operating beyond coastal waters)

SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (voyage planning obligations, assessment of vessel and crew fitness for the intended passage, and the duty to carry appropriate navigational equipment including AIS)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure competency and passage fitness assessments for solo offshore passages: harbour authorities, marina operators, and coastguard agencies should implement a structured gatekeeping function requiring lone skippers to demonstrate that their certified competency level, vessel condition, and onboard redundancy (emergency steering, backup propulsion, and communications) are proportionate to the planned passage, with particular scrutiny applied to night passages and exposed coastal routes.

Compulsory float plan registration and check-in protocols for single-handed voyages: all solo passages beyond defined coastal limits should require submission of a float plan to the relevant Maritime Rescue Co-ordination Centre (MRCC) or designated shore contact, including expected waypoints, check-in schedules, and escalation thresholds — with the absence of a scheduled check-in triggering a graduated SAR alert to reduce the overdue detection window from days to hours.

Regulatory requirement for minimum onboard redundancy standards on solo-crewed vessels: vessels undertaking single-handed passages should be required to carry and maintain in a ready-to-deploy state at least one redundant steering solution (emergency tiller, steering oar, or autopilot backup), one independent means of propulsion (outboard engine in deployable condition or auxiliary sail configuration), and a minimum of two independent distress communication devices (including a registered EPIRB and handheld VHF), with compliance verified as part of any applicable vessel safety examination scheme.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht reported an engine failure but had power. Radio communication between the lone skipper and the emergency services was broken so a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The yacht was towed back to a marina and safely berthed.

A sailing yacht was on passage under engine when the yacht had an engine failure. The lone skipper continue under mainsail but was not an experienced sailor and struggled with the conditions. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was deployed to assist the yacht to ensure the skippers safety. The SAR vessel took the yacht under tow to a harbour. No injury to the skipper, no damage to vessel reported.

A sailing yacht broke its tiller, the lone skipper had no steering and was unable to get the outboard engine prepared before the yacht grounded at the entrance to a harbour. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist, the water was too shallow to tow the yacht clear so the skipper was transferred to the SAR vessel and taken ashore. As the yacht was not anchored, there was concern the ya

A singe handed sailing vessel was taking part in a challenge and was reported as overdue. The vessel had no AIS and no satellite. The vessel was overdue by ten days and then the boat was found empty by a passing fishing vessel. The RNLI towed the sailing vessel to harbour and the sailing skipper is presumed deceased.

A lone skipper onboard a fishing vessel requested assistance by radio, another vessel starting towing the fishing vessel. During the tow the fishing vessel had a shift in weight on-board, possibly crates of fish, the tow was released and an attempt to recover the skipper. The fishing vessel sank, an emergency call was placed with search and rescue (SAR) assets tasked to the scene.

The lifeboat was putting a crew member ashore to aid 3 swimmers who had been washed onto rocks, a larger wave picked up the lifeboat and then as the water receded it grounded onto what where once submerged rocks causing damage to the hull.

A solo sailor was sailing and making way to a harbour when they injured their hand whilst tacking. As they were unable to enter the harbour safely on their own the skipper requested assistance. A decision was made to task a search and rescue (SAR) vessel to assist as necessary. To SAR went onboard to assess the skippers injury and set up a tow. The yacht was towed to a mooring in the harbour, the
A sailing vessel tiller broke and the vessel lost steering. It then subsequently ran aground. The vessel was being operated single-handedly

A single handed potter skipper was taking their vessel from one port to another but were reported overdue after being out for more than ten hours. They were rescued by search and rescue services. They had become disorientated as it became dark and were succumbing to the effects of the cold, however they were not severely injured.

A pleasure sailing yacht was anchored next to a breakwater on a lee shore. The wind was building throughout the day and there was concern for the crew member, a search and rescue vessel was tasked to investigate and remove the crew member. They were unable to do so due to the proximity to the surf zone, the anchor eventually failed and the yacht drifted onto the breakwater and started to break up.

title: Navigational Misjudgement During High-Risk Confined Transits
description:
This theme is characterised by navigational errors occurring during high-risk, spatially constrained manoeuvres — principally bridge transits, platform approaches, and pilotage in restricted waters — where officers or pilots misjudge clearances, issue incorrect helm orders, or become distracted from their primary watchkeeping duties. A recurring pattern across incidents is the absence of structured procedural frameworks specific to these manoeuvres: no formal bridge transit protocols, no platform approach checklists, and no enforced helm order verification procedures. This procedural vacuum means that individual human error — whether a momentary lapse in situational awareness, a miscommunicated helm order, or distraction by an administrative task — is not intercepted by any systemic safeguard, allowing minor misjudgements to escalate into contacts, groundings, or crew injuries. The frequency of these incidents suggests an organisational tolerance for ad hoc navigation practices during phases that objectively demand the highest level of procedural discipline.

Latent hardware and environmental conditions consistently reduce the margin available to detect and recover from human error. Night-time operations appear as a recurring environmental factor, degrading visual situational awareness precisely when accurate perception of clearances and vessel positioning is most critical. Moderate wind and sea state further reduce manoeuvrability and helm responsiveness, compressing the time available for corrective action. Hardware deficiencies — including the absence of active ECDIS guard zones, proximity alarms, and adequate bow fendering — mean that vessels lack automated early-warning capability and physical resilience during close-quarters work. The combination of degraded environmental conditions, absent automation safeguards, and procedural gaps creates a predictable latent failure pathway: the officer or pilot is the sole, unsupported barrier against contact or grounding, and when that barrier fails, no secondary defence exists to prevent the incident or mitigate its consequences.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (particularly Regulations 15 and 34 concerning bridge procedures, passage planning, and safe watchkeeping practices)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for watchkeeping at sea and in port, including officer distraction and bridge resource management obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (International Safety Management Code – obligation to establish safe working procedures, identify hazards, and implement corrective safeguards)
preventable_by: Mandate the development and use of formal High-Risk Manoeuvre Protocols for all bridge transits, platform approaches, and pilotage operations, requiring a pre-manoeuvre briefing, defined roles for all bridge team members, a helm order repeat-back and verification procedure, and prohibition of non-navigational tasks for the duration of the manoeuvre phase.

Require active use of ECDIS guard zones and proximity alarm systems during all confined-water and close-quarters approaches, with standing orders that automated alerts must be acknowledged and acted upon before the manoeuvre commences, providing a hardware-based secondary barrier independent of officer vigilance.

Implement a vessel-wide movement warning system — analogous to an aviation seat-belt sign — requiring the officer of the watch to broadcast an all-crew alert prior to any high-risk manoeuvre so that crew members in unsecured working positions can take appropriate precautions, directly addressing the latent risk of crew injury from contact events during confined-water transits.

representative_cases.description: A utility vessel made contact with a concrete support of a bridge resulting in minor damage and one minor injury. There was a dent in the bow roller, no damage to the ships hull. Due to the impact, an AB working in the galley lost his balance and fell in the entrance to the accommodation below deck and injured his head.

A general cargo vessel grounded on shingle and was stuck for ten minutes before returning to port. The grounding was reportedly due to an incorrect helm order from the pilot which meant the vessel failed to turn as intended.

The vessel's second mate had just taken over the watch. The was no current work available to do so the vessel was on normal standby and was required to carry out cargo operations at a local platform. Later during the watch, the second mate started towards the platform at a slow speed. At approximately 4.5 knots, the 2nd mate then started to carry out work on the bridge computer and stopped maintai

Cargo vessel's crew was informed that the bridge was fully open when they proceeded and adjusted the speed as necessary. The vessel made light contact with the cabin structure of the bridge resulting in minor paint damage. The berthing was proceeded and no other damages were reported.

A passenger ship's ramp was raised whilst passengers were disembarking resulting in passengers having to jump onto the slipway. The crew held the ramp in position until the passengers cleared the area and then offered support and first aid to the passengers. One passenger sustained a minor injury. The incident occurred to a reported 'communication break down' between the bridge and the deck becaus

An offshore supply vessel was shifting from one berth to another. The master was manoeuvring the vessel past a Ro/Ro passenger ferry when the officer supporting the master provided the wrong distance. The master applied power on the port engine and the distance was not checked from the starboard quarter to the passenger ferry. This resulted in the supply vessel making contact with the bow of the f

A general cargo vessel was turning inside a dock area when the bow of the vessel struck the quay. The vessel was under pilotage at the time. There were no structural damages to the quay and slight damage to the bow and forward railings of the vessel. The incident reportedly happened due to 'pilot error'. The vessel reported that they should have been critical of the pilot to prevent the accident (

An inland waterways passenger vessel made contact with a bridge, resulting in minor damage to the wheelhouse and also to a fitting on the bridge. There was no structural damage to the bridge and no material damage to the vessel. There were no injuries or pollution. The vessel was able to move away from the bridge under its own power and proceed to a berth.

The vessel made contact with a bridge buttress, resulting in damage to the bow above the water line which consisted of 60cm of splits and 260cm in length with a spread of 25cm. The vessel successfully managed to anchor, and no injuries to any persons onboard were confirmed. Damage to the bridge buttress wooden fendering and concrete top face.

An offshore windfarm crew supply vessel was drifting after deploying some teams to offshore turbines. The master was reportedly distracted by paperwork and didn't notice that the vessel was on a collision course with a turbine. The vessel hit the turbine at 5kts. A crewmember was exiting the toilet at the time of the impact causing them to fall and injure their ribs (fracture) The vessel returned

title: Mobility Aid Misuse and Passenger Vulnerability Risks Aboard
description:
This theme is characterised by a convergence of improvised passenger behaviour, unregulated mobility aid operation, and the absence of structured vulnerability assessment processes. Incidents in this cluster recur because vessels lack systematic mechanisms to identify mobility-impaired or at-risk passengers and to translate that identification into targeted supervision, physical safeguards, or behavioural guidance. Companions and passengers themselves routinely improvise solutions to mobility challenges — pushing walkers as wheelchairs, operating scooters in restricted areas, or performing routine movements without seeking assistance — in ways that are foreseeable yet unaddressed by any formal shipboard procedure or environmental control. The shipboard setting amplifies these risks through sloped decks, abrupt flooring transitions, open deck edges, and vessel movement, converting low-consequence shore-side behaviours into serious or fatal injury events.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including wheeled mobility aids without effective braking, insufficient physical barriers at open deck edges, and abrupt mixed-surface flooring transitions — create an environment in which a single lapse of control or judgement produces disproportionate consequences. Human factors, including underestimation of personal risk, unfamiliarity with vessel dynamics, and the instinctive desire of companions to assist without formal guidance, sustain a pattern of unsafe improvised behaviour. Environmental conditions such as night-time operation, deck slope, and vessel movement while manoeuvring compound these vulnerabilities. Critically, the absence of passenger-facing safety information, access controls to safety-critical spaces, and crew training in mobility aid risk management constitutes a systemic latent failure within the Safety Management System that permits these foreseeable incidents to occur repeatedly across diverse vessel types.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural deck design, flooring transitions, and physical barriers)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings, vulnerability identification, and evacuation provisions for mobility-impaired persons)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – Safety Management System obligations including risk assessment, procedures, and passenger safety controls)
preventable_by: Implement a formal passenger vulnerability identification process at embarkation, requiring crew to flag and record mobility-impaired or at-risk passengers, assign dedicated safety contacts, and provide targeted briefings on the safe use of mobility aids within the shipboard environment, including restrictions on areas such as the wheelhouse and guidance on slope and flooring hazards.

Establish and enforce access control policies for safety-critical and high-risk areas — including wheelhouses, open deck edges, and areas with abrupt flooring transitions — through physical barriers, signage, and coded procedural requirements that explicitly address powered and non-powered passenger mobility aids, including mandatory parking and restraint provisions when aids are left unattended.

Conduct a vessel-specific risk assessment of all passenger-accessible areas to identify and remediate environmental hazards relevant to mobility aid use, including installing transition strips or bevelled edges at mixed-surface flooring junctions, fitting guard chains or barriers of adequate height and strength at open deck edges, and displaying multilingual safety guidance on mobility aid use at lift lobbies, alleyways, and boarding points.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was with their spouse and was sitting on their mobility aid walker in the alleyway/lobby area near the elevators. Their spouse attempted to push the walker away from the elevator whilst the injured passenger was still sitting on it. The deck there had a slight slope and the walker was fitted with wheels, which lead to the walker moving faster than expected before getting ca

An inland passenger vessels crew member was struck by a passenger mobility scooter, resulting in superficial injuries. Passenger lost control of there mobility scooter and accelerated across the wheelhouse, pinning the skipper against the guard chain on the open port side, bruising both of the skipper’s shins and causing a small cut on the left wrist.

A passenger on board a paddle steamer was leaning down to tie their shoelace when they fell over and fractured their femur. The vessel was on its way to a berth and the local search and rescue vessel crew attended to evacuate the person ashore. It was reported that the passenger had balance issues more generally

A cruise ship passenger was walking in an alleyway towards the passenger lift when they reportedly tripped over their own feet resulting in a fall. When they fell, they extended their hands which impacted the floor resulting in a broken wrist on one side. The area was inspected and no defects were found that could've contributed to the fall. The passenger reported that they were unsteady on their

A ro-pax crew member was tasked to take on an assistance duty when they were at the bottom of stairs and were tasked with moving an empty passenger’s wheelchair from bottom of stairs to the top. From the vehicle deck to cabin up two flights of stairs, up one deck when the injured crew member and one other person went to pick up the chair, the hand hold the other crew member was using came free. As

A cruise ship passenger was entering their cabin when they fell onto the floor hitting their head and hip. They sustained fracture injuries. They were using a mobility aid at the time as they had poor balance. The area was inspected and nothing out of the ordinary was found.

A cruise ship passenger dislocated their knee whilst attempting to remove their footwear. They were in their cabin and balanced on one leg with the other leg resting across it. This put more pressure on the right knee than it could take, causing it to dislocate. The passenger reported that they had a knee replacement previously which could have lead to the knee becoming weaker. The area was inspec

A cruise ship passenger fell inside a tender whilst descending a set of steps. They fell onto their right side and sustained a fractured rib. It was reported that the incident occurred because the tender moved suddenly and that the passenger also had limited mobility.

The passenger was moving around their cabin. They lost their balance and fell, dislocating their artificial hip joint. No one witnessed the event. Their spouse found them less than 5 minutes later. The passenger had a history of mobility issues and had limited feeling in their legs.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in the restaurant area on board when they fell and fractured their shoulder. They used crutches to aid them in walking and one of their crutches hit their foot causing them to overbalance and fall to the floor. They were diagnosed with a fractured shoulder.

title: Propeller Shaft and Stern Gear Integrity Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by progressive, latent mechanical failures within propeller shaft assemblies and associated stern gear components — encompassing stern tube seals, shaft couplings, gland packing, retention hardware, and lip seals — that go undetected until they manifest as acute casualties during normal operations. Across all incidents, a common thread is the absence of a rigorous, structured planned maintenance regime capable of identifying deteriorating conditions in sub-surface or enclosed stern gear before they reach a critical threshold. Contributing to recurrence is the tendency for post-refit sea trials and pre-departure engineering checks to be either insufficiently thorough or narrowly scoped, thereby allowing compromised components to enter service unverified. The clustering of incidents across vessel types — from commercial motor vessels and high-speed craft to sailing yachts and SAR vessels — indicates that this is a systemic deficiency not confined to any single sector, but reflective of an industry-wide underestimation of the risk associated with stern gear degradation.

Latent hardware deficiencies represent the dominant causal pathway in this theme, with fatigue, corrosion, wear, and improper fitting during refit collectively creating the conditions for catastrophic failure under operational loads. Human factors compound the hardware vulnerabilities: initial misdiagnosis of symptoms — interpreting vibration as propeller fouling or a bang as a closing watertight door — delays corrective action and extends the window during which uncontrolled water ingress or further mechanical separation can occur. Environmental and operational conditions, including night-time departures that reduce early visual detection of abnormal vessel behaviour, elevated sea states increasing hydrodynamic pressure on sealing arrangements, and high-speed operations amplifying shaft loads, act as triggering or aggravating factors that accelerate the transition from latent defect to active casualty. Collectively, these incidents demonstrate that the convergence of deferred maintenance, inadequate post-maintenance verification, and symptom misinterpretation creates a predictable and preventable failure pathway.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (particularly Part E, machinery and shaft installations, and watertight integrity requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (applicable where engine room flooding intersects with machinery space safety and damage control readiness)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requirements for planned maintenance systems, defect reporting, and safety management procedures covering critical machinery)
preventable_by: Implement a structured, documented Planned Maintenance System (PMS) specifically targeting stern gear components — including stern tube seals, shaft couplings, gland packing, retention hardware, and shaft alignment — with defined inspection intervals, condition-based criteria for renewal, and mandatory recording of findings, ensuring that progressive deterioration such as corrosion, fatigue, or wear is identified before it reaches a critical threshold.

Mandate a formal post-refit sea trial protocol that includes a dedicated stern gear verification stage: confirming water feed pipe security, seal integrity under load, shaft alignment, and the absence of abnormal vibration or temperature at defined propulsion RPM thresholds, with results signed off by a competent engineer and retained as part of the vessel's safety management documentation before any commercial voyage is undertaken.

Develop and promulgate vessel-specific symptom recognition guidance for crew and skippers covering early indicators of stern gear failure — including shaft vibration, asymmetric vessel behaviour, audible mechanical noise, smoke from the stern tube, and unexpected water ingress in the engine room — together with clear escalation and damage control procedures, so that initial misdiagnosis does not delay the identification of a developing mechanical casualty and the activation of appropriate emergency responses.

representative_cases.description: A commercial motor vessel was on passage after an annual refit when the skipper felt a vibration from the propeller shaft. Initially suspecting propeller fouling, reducing the power seemed to temporarily solve the issue. However, the vibration returned, and smoke was seen coming from the stern tube area via a camera. An investigation found that the tide seal had seized onto the shaft and ripped ou

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel started its passage when the crew noticed that the vessel was pulling to one side and there appeared to be no drive from the starboard propeller. An investigation was conducted when it was noticed that the starboard propeller and the shaft aft were missing. There had been no indication of contact with an object or seabed, no water ingress. No further damages/injuri

A sailing yacht was on passage under engine, the crew noticed a noise from the engine bay. Upon stopping the engine they noticed a propeller shaft misalignment, the crew chose to anchor to carry out an inspection. There was minor water ingress, resulting from a damaged lip seal. The skipper informed a shore authority and contacted a local fishing vessel to request a tow to a nearby harbour. The re

A high speed crew transfer vessel experienced water ingress into the engine room through the propeller shaft area. The crew on board were able to manage the water ingress without outside assistance and made their way back to port for further investigations and repairs.

A high speed passenger vessel was on passage when there was a bang. This bang was initially thought to be a watertight door slamming shut. There was no change to the vessels speed or movement. However, when berthing at the next port and the starboard engine was put astern a louder bang occurred and the starboard engine was not giving astern power. The vessel managed to berth and once alongside che

A sailing vessel experienced water ingress from a suspected hull defect that resulted in the vessel becoming disabled. The single crew member called a MAYDAY and the local search and rescue services attended and put a pump on board and took the crew member from the vessel ashore. The vessel was then towed back to port. It was discovered that the vessel was leaking through the propeller shaft. It w

A sail training vessel experienced propulsion failure due to the failure of its drive shaft coupling. The vessel was close to land and there was not sufficient wind for it to sail. The crew called for assistance. Another vessel set up a tow and brought the vessel into port.

A search and rescue vessel was on passage when it began taking on water through a faulty shaft seal. They called for assistance and their progress was monitored until they made it to port. The vessel was then taken out of the water and it was estimated it would take a week to complete the repairs.

Recreational craft had a leak in the engine's propeller and water was coming in. The people on board mentioned that when they switch on their engine they are unable to handle the ingress of water which resulted in necessary towage. They continued taking on water faster than they could bail out. 2 pumps were used while heading back towards port with the help of a tow. No injuries or other damages r

A charter sailing yacht with six crew onboard radioed a MAYDAY message after the vessel started taking on water. Upon an inspection it was found that the propeller shaft had detached from the engine and had slid backwards through the stern gland resulting in an engine and steering failure. A nearby vessel responded to the distress and helped to stop the ingress with a wooden bung and took the vess

title: Gangway and Vessel Access Point Safety Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring breakdown in procedural compliance and physical safeguarding at the critical interface between vessel and shore — the gangway and disembarkation point. Incidents span crew injuries, passenger falls, and structural contact damage, yet share a common latent architecture: pre-departure or access procedures that are either absent, insufficiently enforced, or not adapted to prevailing environmental conditions. Whether it is a gangway failing to be stowed before departure, a visitor boarding while encumbered, a passenger slipping at an inadequately controlled pier gap, or a crew member stumbling during an informal transfer between vessels, each incident reflects a systemic tolerance for informal, uncontrolled access arrangements at what is statistically one of the highest-risk locations aboard a vessel. The persistence of these incidents suggests that gangway safety is treated as a routine, low-priority task rather than a structured, auditable safety-critical evolution.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including gangway surfaces lacking adequate anti-slip treatment, absent or undersized fendering, and insufficient gap-mitigation measures at disembarkation points — create the physical conditions for injury. These are compounded by human factors including divided attention (crew carrying items, pilots focused on navigation rather than deck equipment status), insufficient crew presence at access points during boarding and disembarkation, and normalisation of informal vessel-to-vessel transfers without procedural oversight. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time reduced visibility and wind- or wave-induced vessel movement, consistently elevate residual risk beyond what informal controls can manage. The convergence of degraded hardware, absent procedural enforcement, and challenging environmental conditions in multiple incidents within this cluster confirms a systemic gap: maritime operators are not consistently applying a risk-based, conditions-sensitive approach to managing vessel access, and shore-side and vessel-side responsibilities at the gangway interface remain poorly delineated.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (safe means of access and structural integrity of access equipment)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe embarkation and disembarkation procedures, including passenger safety during transfers)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (SMS procedural compliance, pre-departure checklists, and crew coordination protocols)
preventable_by: Mandate a formal, signed pre-departure checklist that explicitly includes confirmation of gangway full retraction and stowage, verified by both an officer of the watch on deck and acknowledged on the bridge before engine order is given — this checklist must remain operative regardless of pilot embarkation status, with the pilot briefed on outstanding deck equipment checks as part of the master-pilot exchange.

Implement and enforce a hands-free boarding and disembarkation standard for all persons accessing the vessel via gangway, requiring that tools, bags, and equipment be transferred separately via a designated hoist, trolley, or buddy-carry system; gangway surfaces must be inspected before each use, treated with certified anti-slip coating, and re-assessed when wet or during night-time operations, with findings logged in the deck logbook.

Deploy a dedicated crew member at every gangway or disembarkation point during all passenger or visitor transfers, empowered to halt movement when vessel motion, gap width, surface conditions, or lighting are assessed as hazardous; physical gap-control measures (e.g., bridging plates, adjustable fenders, barrier systems) must be fitted and inspected routinely, and all gangway risk assessments must be reviewed and updated when environmental conditions — including wind, sea state, tidal variation, or time of day — change materially from those prevailing at the time of the last assessment.

representative_cases.description: A general cargo vessel with a pilot onboard was departing its berth when the vessel's gangway made contact with a shore crane. Damage that was sustained to vessel is was cosmetic damage to the gangway

A surveyor was boarding a vessel from a shoreside gangway when they over balanced and fell from the gangway onto the deck of the vessel, sustaining a minor injury to their leg. Reportedly they were carrying items which prevented them from holding onto the gangway properly, which was also slippery at the time.

Inland passenger vessel was disembarking passengers when they slipped on the edge of the pier and fell. This resulted in the passenger to fall onto their knees and one of their legs went between the pier and the boat. The vessel fendering was holding the vessel off the pier as normal. The passenger sustained an arm fracture and was taken for further medical assistance.

Cruise ship's passenger stepped off the side of the gangway ramp and reached out to grab the barrier stanchion. The passenger list their balance and fell down, landing on their hip. This resulted in a closed hip fracture. The passenger was taken for further medical advice and at this time they were in a stable condition.

Inland waterway vessel's crew member was stepping across to a sister ship post berthing when they caught their boot resulting in a stumble. All of the casualties weight went on to their ankle resulting in further medical assistance.

A fishing vessel crew member had gone ashore for the evening. On their return as they walked up the gangway, they slipped due to it being wet and fell into the harbour. The crew member was recovered from the water and taken to hospital to be treated for fractured ribs.

A workboat crewmember was returning to the vessel when they fell overboard. They stepped down from the ladder onto the vessel but then fell back through the gap into the water. They were reportedly under the influence of alcohol.

An inland waterways workboat crewmember was embarking the vessel when they lost their balance either because they slipped on the gunwale or because they tripped on the rope securing the console cover. The crewmember was alone at the time and couldn't recall the exact cause of their fall. They fell into the vessel and sustained a fractured patella from falling onto the deck. They were wearing all t

An inland waterways passenger was walking down the gangway, which was wet with rainwater, when they slipped. The group of people they were with, helped them to their feet and took them to hospital, where they received surgery for a complex fracture. The accident was reported by the passenger to the company that operates the vessel more than two weeks after the incident. Due to the passenger being

While pulling out the gangway net from the lockers and preparing for use on the vessel gangway, the IP tripped in the netting and fell to the deck onto there left hand, causing considerable pain to the wrist saw the medic straight away where after an inspection the medic recommended that they go to for an X-Ray before the vessel sails. IP visited clinic and was declared as 'not fit for work' with

title: Contact Incidents from Fendering and Geometric Mismatch Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by vessel contact incidents arising from the compounding interaction of inadequate or absent fendering, geometric incompatibilities between vessel and quay or adjacent vessel infrastructure, and dynamic environmental conditions. Across the representative incidents, a consistent pattern emerges whereby a known or foreseeable physical hazard — insufficient fendering provision, freeboard differential, or hull-to-structure height mismatch — was not adequately mitigated prior to manoeuvring. The incidents recur because latent procedural gaps allow operations to proceed despite identified deficiencies, often under the assumption that reduced speed or favourable weather provides sufficient compensation. This assumption consistently proves inadequate when environmental perturbations, even minor ones, introduce unpredictable relative motion or positional error that existing safeguards cannot absorb. The systemic failure lies not in any single decision but in the absence of robust pre-manoeuvre risk assessment protocols that formally account for vessel condition, berth configuration, and environmental state as combined variables.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including worn, absent, or geometrically incompatible fender systems — create the physical preconditions for contact, but it is the interaction with human and environmental factors that converts latent risk into actual damage. Night-time operations consistently degrade the bridge team's and linesmen's ability to perceive vessel proximity, relative motion, and structural alignment, reducing the effectiveness of visual monitoring as a safeguard. Human factors, including misjudgement of positioning, over-reliance on shore-side observation, and the absence of dynamic risk reassessment when conditions deviate from the planned scenario, eliminate the final barriers to contact. The absence of tactile or audible feedback during some events further illustrates a systemic situational awareness gap, whereby damage can occur without immediate on-board detection. Collectively, these factors indicate that procedural frameworks governing berthing and alongside operations in variable conditions do not adequately mandate real-time verification of fender adequacy, geometric compatibility, or environmental thresholds as binding go/no-go criteria.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (structural integrity obligations following hull contact and shell plating damage)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (bridge team management, situational awareness, and safe manoeuvring practices during berthing and alongside operations)

SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (SMS requirements for risk assessment, procedural adequacy, and identification of hazards prior to manoeuvring operations)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory pre-manoeuvre fendering and geometric compatibility assessment, formally documented within the vessel's SMS, that requires bridge officers and pilots to verify fender adequacy, vessel freeboard condition, and hull-to-structure height alignment before any berthing, berth-shift, or alongside operation proceeds — with explicit go/no-go criteria that cannot be overridden without recorded senior officer authorisation and defined compensatory measures.

Establish and enforce quantified environmental threshold limits (sea state, wave height, swell period) for high-risk operations including night-time berthing in lightship condition, pilot vessel transfers, and SAR or small vessel alongside manoeuvres with larger vessels, supported by dynamic risk assessment requirements that mandate reassessment if conditions change between planning and execution phases.

Introduce structured night-time berthing protocols that require active, designated hull clearance monitoring by a bridge team member independent of the conning officer, supplemented by the use of portable lighting, laser rangefinders, or camera-assisted proximity monitoring systems at the vessel quarter and bow, reducing sole dependence on shore-based linesmen for contact detection and ensuring real-time situational awareness of vessel position relative to quay edges, bollards, and adjacent vessel fendering.

representative_cases.description: The vessel was shifting from berth after discharge was complete to lay by on a ro-ro berth. The vessel was in lightship conditions with drafts of 4.8m forward and 5 meters aft. Weather conditions were good for a lightship maneuver, while maneuvering alongside the port quarter went over the quay and there was very little momentum as consideration was being given to the lack of fendering. However, t

While the pilot vessel was servicing a motor hopper, the pilot launch fender caught the deck edge in the light swell resulting in metal on metal contact. No damage was reported to the dredger however, the pilot vessel sustained paint damage and indentation of hull place. No injuries.

SAR vessel made contact with off shore supply vessels fenders resulting in damage to the rails of the SAR vessel. Manoeuvred the vessel port side too the supply vessel, unfortunately as the vessels came together the supply vessel lifted on a wave, SAR vessel was in the trough and when coming up the port handrails got stuck under the ships fendering, ripping off most of the Port stern rails of SAR

A ro-pax vessel was berthing at a terminal when it made contact with a fender, the fender rotated which punctured the vessel's shell plating. The damage was 45 meters or so from the bow, 5m approx. above the waterline, dimensions of the puncture being 50mm x 120mm approx. The fender sustained minor damage. The vessel was permitted to sail after completing temporary repairs that were approved by cl

A landing vessel was alongside the quay berthing with a pilot on board when it made contact with the quay. A fender was close to the mid-ships area causing the vessel to rotate under the wind conditions and the port quarter to impact the quay. The concrete jetty sustained minor damage, the vessel was undamaged and there were no injuries or pollution.

A product tanker was being berthed when its railings made contact with the quay. There was no damage to the railings. The contact was caused by the fact the vessel was laden combined with the height of tide meaning the railings were below the fenders. The vessel was re-positioned with the assistance of tugs.

A service vessel made contact with dock following loss of fender, resulting in minor paint damage. Vessel jolted, crew investigated and confirmed vessel in contact with dock due to missing fender. New fender put in place and all line secured.

General cargo was approaching a jetty to make an ebb approach. Whilst the vessel was backing into the lock park, some of the structure rubbed against the timber fendering causing it to slinter off more and damage the vessel on deck. This damage is damage on top of existing damage. No injuries and/or other damages reported.
A crew transfer vessel was alongside in a port when the fenders between it and the quay popped. This caused the hull of the vessel to come contact with the quay causing damage above the waterline.

As the passenger vessel was departing, a fender got caught on the shell door of the cruise ship, causing damage to the aft deck railings of the passenger vessel. Unable to manoeuvre, the passenger vessel came to a stop and a cleat on the bridge caused superficial paint damage to the cruise ship.

title: Cross-Jurisdictional Reporting Gaps Obscuring Safety Intelligence
description:
This theme is characterised not by a discrete physical marine safety event, but by a systemic failure in incident data governance — specifically, the loss of actionable safety information when investigation responsibility is transferred to another state authority. Across all incidents in this cluster, the originating state's reporting database retains only jurisdictional metadata, with no substantive detail on vessel type, incident category, causal factors, personnel involvement, or outcomes. Compounding this, multiple records are confirmed duplicates of existing reports, indicating weaknesses in input validation and reporting workflow controls. The result is a corpus of serious and very serious incidents — some occurring in operationally significant environmental conditions including night-time operations, high winds, and significant wave heights — that are entirely invisible to national-level safety trend analysis. The recurrence of this pattern across a substantial number of records suggests the problem is structural rather than incidental.

From a latent causation perspective, the primary drivers are organisational and systemic rather than hardware or human error in an operational sense. The absence of a standardised inter-jurisdictional data-sharing framework means that when investigation authority is delegated, the originating state has no formalised mechanism to retrieve or integrate the receiving authority's findings. Data quality issues are further evident in multiple records where wave height values are inconsistent with recorded sea state classifications, suggesting either sensor faults, manual data entry errors, or auto-populated metadata that has not been validated. Human factors manifest as procedural non-compliance in report submission, including the repeated logging of duplicate entries, pointing to insufficient user training and absent system-level deduplication safeguards. While environmental conditions — including night operations and moderate-to-rough sea states — are present across several records and may have been operationally relevant, they cannot be assessed as contributing factors without the underlying incident narratives. The cumulative effect is a material degradation of the national safety intelligence picture, with serious and very serious incidents effectively disappearing from the evidence base used to inform risk management and policy.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Regulation 21: Casualty investigation obligations and flag state reporting duties)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: Safety management system documentation, reporting, and cross-authority communication requirements)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 31-32: Reporting of dangers and casualty reporting obligations to flag and port state authorities)
preventable_by: Establish a mandatory inter-jurisdictional incident data-sharing protocol requiring investigating state authorities to transmit full investigation findings, causal classifications, and outcome data to the originating state's safety database within a defined timeframe, ensuring no serious or very serious incident is recorded without substantive narrative detail.

Implement automated deduplication and data completeness validation controls within the incident reporting system, including mandatory field requirements for incident category, vessel type, and causal factors before a report can be submitted, and system-level flagging or rejection of entries sharing identical incident reference numbers.

Introduce a periodic data quality audit process whereby all records classified as 'investigated by another state' or flagged as duplicates are reviewed by a designated data governance officer, cross-referenced with the responsible authority's findings, and either enriched with complete investigation data or formally archived — with audit outcomes reported annually to support national safety trend analysis.
