A consistent pattern emerges in which adverse or deteriorating environmental conditions, particularly elevated sea states, strong winds, and swell, impose dynamic loads that exceed the capacity of ground tackle systems already compromised by latent deficiencies. Critically, night-time operations feature prominently across incidents, reducing crew situational awareness, limiting visual inspection of equipment condition, and delaying detection of drag or equipment failure. The recurrence of these incidents across mooring, contact, navigation, and machinery categories indicates that anchoring casualties are not isolated equipment failures but systemic events driven by the convergence of environmental stress, equipment vulnerability, and procedural gaps in pre-anchorage planning and anchor watch vigilance.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including metal fatigue, corrosion, worn chain links, degraded shackles, and under-maintained winches — represent a foundational vulnerability that goes undetected in the absence of structured inspection regimes incorporating non-destructive testing. Human factors compound this risk: anchor watch standards are frequently inadequate during anchored status, pre-anchoring risk assessments do not adequately account for weather deterioration or equipment condition, and machinery readiness for emergency manoeuvring is not reliably confirmed prior to anchoring. Environmental conditions, while variable, are routinely underestimated — particularly when single weather parameters such as wind speed are used without accounting for residual swell, sea state, or vessel windage characteristics. The breadth of vessel types affected, from ro-pax ferries and cruise ships to barges and charter vessels, underscores that this is a fleet-wide systemic risk requiring coordinated intervention at the levels of equipment maintenance standards, operational procedures, and watchkeeping culture.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance of anchoring and mooring equipment, machinery readiness)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (anchor watch obligations, navigational vigilance, and bridge watchkeeping during anchored status)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – SMS requirements for maintenance programmes, risk assessment, and procedural compliance)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory periodic non-destructive testing (NDT) of anchor chains, shackles, bow stoppers, and anchor shanks as part of structured planned maintenance systems (PMS), with flag state or classification society oversight to ensure inspection records are current and deficiencies are actioned before vessels proceed to anchor in exposed or deteriorating conditions.

Establish formal pre-anchorage risk assessment procedures within vessel SMS requiring masters to evaluate combined environmental parameters (wind speed, sea state, swell height, and forecast deterioration), confirm adequate chain scope for prevailing conditions, verify machinery readiness for emergency manoeuvring, and designate qualified anchor watch personnel with defined drag-detection response thresholds and inter-vessel communication protocols.

Introduce enhanced anchor watch standards including mandatory use of electronic position-monitoring tools (GPS anchor alarm systems or equivalent) with clearly defined drag alert thresholds, supplemented by improved deck lighting standards for night anchor-handling operations and regular bridge watchkeeper competency assessments specifically covering anchored-vessel risk management and emergency response sequencing.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel lost it's starboard anchor and chain whilst in a bay waiting to enter dry dock. Harbour authorities were notified and a salvage team arranged to recover the anchor and chain.
A buoy laying vessel lost its port anchor. The place that the anchor was lost was marked so it could be retrieved later.
A cargo vessel dragged anchor, grounded afetr which it had difficulty starting its engines. Subsequently got underway and berthed alongside for damage assessement and repair prior to dry dock.
A bulk carrier drifted anchor passing within 1 cable of a container vessel. near miss
A cruise ship parted its anchor cable during heavy weather and started to drift. They recovered the remaining chain and then dropped the other anchor in a different position.
A tall ship dragged its anchor and was blown on to harbour breakwater. The two crew were successfully evacuated. The vessel was declared a total loss and broken up in situ .
A service vessel loss of starboard anchor and cable.
A cruise ship crew was weighing anchor and while bringing home starboard anchor, the shank parted. The lower portion of the shank and flukes were lost. There were no injuries to personnel.
Oil tanker collided with bulk carrier while at anchor resulting in damage to the bow.
A container vessel broke an anchor winch whilst heaving up at the anchorage.

title: Night-Time Small Craft Capsize: Risk Assessment Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by the capsize of small recreational and competitive sailing and motor vessels during night-time operations, where a combination of reduced visibility, underestimated or misreported environmental conditions, and inadequate pre-departure risk assessment created foreseeable but unrealised hazards. A recurring and particularly concerning pattern is the significant discrepancy between reported sea state classifications and measured wave heights, suggesting systemic failures in real-time weather verification and go/no-go decision-making. Across these incidents, survivability was consistently determined by PPE compliance and proximity to SAR assets rather than effective prevention, indicating that safety culture in this sector remains reactive rather than proactive. The overrepresentation of night operations in this cluster reflects a widely underappreciated risk multiplier: darkness not only impairs crew situational awareness but substantially degrades early detection and recovery response by both the crew and potential rescuers.

Latent contributory factors across this theme span all three causal domains. From a human factors perspective, overconfidence in benign-appearing conditions, normalisation of non-compliance with safety protocols, and poor pre-departure judgement — particularly in competitive and solo sailing contexts — are persistent antecedents. Environmental conditions, including localised wind gusts, confused sea states, and rapidly changing weather, were frequently underestimated or inaccurately recorded, pointing to inadequate access to or use of high-resolution meteorological data. Hardware factors, while less frequently cited, include vessel design limitations — particularly low-freeboard racing hulls and inherently tender multihulls — that were operated at or beyond their stability thresholds in conditions that were manageable only under ideal circumstances. Organisationally, the reliance on SAR services and nearby vessels as the primary recovery mechanism, rather than robust preventive safety arrangements, indicates a systemic gap in both regulatory oversight and industry safety culture for small craft night operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (covering PFD carriage, use requirements, and survival craft standards for small vessels)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (covering meteorological services, voyage planning, and the duty of masters to assess prevailing conditions before and during passage)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (covering distress alerting, DSC and AIS carriage requirements to ensure timely SAR activation following capsize events)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure go/no-go checklists for recreational and competitive small craft operators conducting night operations, requiring documented verification of real-time forecast wind speed, wave height, and sea state against vessel-specific stability and crew competency thresholds, enforced at club, marina, and race-organiser level.

Introduction and enforcement of sport- and activity-specific night sailing safety frameworks requiring, as a minimum condition of participation: mandatory PFD wear, AIS or DSC device carriage, dedicated safety boat provision scaled to the number of participants and prevailing conditions, and a defined maximum wind and sea state envelope beyond which night activity is suspended.

A structured campaign to address sea state reporting accuracy for small craft incidents, including training for operators and event organisers in the use of real-time wave buoy and forecast data, combined with post-incident data auditing to identify and remediate systematic discrepancies between reported and measured conditions that currently mask the true severity of small craft operating environments.

representative_cases.description: Sailing vessel capsized with one person on board. The casualty was recovered from water with no sustained injuries. The vessel was towed back to safety.
A recreational motor vessel was capsized and swamped by a wave. It sank and the four crew on board were rescued and taken ashore. They were not injured.
Sailing yacht capsized with two crew on board. The crew were recovered by nearby vessels and the sailing yacht was towed back to shore. No injuries were reported.
RIB capsized with 4 people on board. The RIB was recovered by a local barge. Everyone safe and well, no damages reported.   All wearing lifejackets.  In water: 2 minutes.
A trimaran capsized with all three crew recovered safely to a search and rescue vessel. The vessel was left to drift as it could not be righted and towed, it was later found and towed to harbour.

Members of the public called the emergency services to report three sailing dinghies capsized and in difficulty. Search and rescue (SAR) assets were deployed to assist the sailors, three sailors were recovered by the sailing club safety boat and the other two recovered by the SAR vessel. The three dinghies were recovered and brought ashore. The five sailors were assessed by paramedics and then dis
A sailing vessel capsized. The vessel and all occupants were recovered to safety by a lifeboat. There were no reported injuries.
A speedboat capsized. No persons on board were injured and the services of search and rescue were not required.
A racing speedboat capsized whilst participating in a race. The crew were recovered, uninjured, and the vessel was towed to safety.
A sailing vessel capsized while racing with six crew onboard going into the water. All crew were recovered from the water and taken ashore. The vessel later sank.

title: Recreational Craft Capsize: Night Operations and Safety Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by the capsize of small recreational craft — including fishing vessels, motor boats, kayaks, and sailing dinghies — operating under conditions of elevated risk, most notably at night and in the absence of foundational safety practices. The unifying thread across these incidents is not the capsize event itself, but the convergence of latent safety failures that transformed each capsize into a life-threatening or fatal emergency. These failures include critically insufficient lifejacket provision and non-compliance with wearing requirements, absence of pre-departure safety checks or voyage plans, probable alcohol impairment, solo operation without alerting mechanisms, and no carriage of personal locating devices such as PLBs or EPIRBs. The recurring nature of these incidents reflects a deeply embedded normalisation of risk among recreational water users, particularly on inland and sheltered waters where hazard perception is systematically lower than conditions warrant. The absence of a safety culture — rather than any single equipment failure — is the defining latent cause of this cluster.

Hardware deficiencies are a consistent and critical contributor: insufficient numbers of lifejackets, absence of kill-cords, and no personal alerting or locating devices directly reduced survivability and extended rescue response times in every case where outcomes were serious or fatal. Human factors compound these deficiencies at every stage — from the pre-departure decision to proceed at night without adequate equipment, to probable alcohol-induced impairment of judgment, to the failure to don available lifejackets before the capsize event. Environmental conditions, including night-time darkness, wind, sea state, wave height, and cold water, did not individually cause these incidents but acted as force multipliers that reduced the margin for survival once a capsize occurred. The fatal and near-fatal outcomes in this cluster are strongly attributable to the interaction between absent or defective safety hardware, poor individual risk judgement, and environmental conditions that demanded a higher — not lower — standard of preparation.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for lifejackets, lifebuoys, EPIRBs, and survival equipment carriage and readiness)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, watchkeeping, and the obligation to proceed with due regard to safety including pre-departure risk assessment)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (carriage and operability of distress alerting equipment including EPIRBs and DSC devices enabling rapid SAR response)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure safety checks and condition-based go/no-go decision frameworks for recreational craft operators, incorporating lifejacket count and fit-to-persons-on-board verification, alcohol prohibition enforcement, and a requirement to file a float plan or notify a shore contact — enforced through targeted harbour authority and coastal patrol programmes with particular focus on night-time departures.

Statutory requirement for all persons aboard small recreational craft to wear a lifejacket or PFD at all times when underway, with supplementary carriage requirements mandating at minimum one personal locator beacon (PLB) or AIS MOB device per vessel, and one per person on craft operating at night or in open water — supported by low-cost equipment access schemes and awareness campaigns targeting inland and reservoir user communities.

Structured public safety education and competency programmes specifically designed for recreational kayakers, dinghy sailors, and small motorboat operators, covering cold-water shock and capsize recovery, the compounding hazards of night operations, alcohol impairment on water, and the correct use of PLBs and EPIRBs — delivered through national sailing and watersports governing bodies and integrated into any voluntary or mandatory small craft licensing or registration frameworks.

representative_cases.description: A small recreational craft used for fishing capsized on a reservoir. The two crew entered the water, only one was wearing a lifejacket. They were in the water for around 3-4 minutes and then manged to climb onto the capsized vessel c and awaited assistance. The crew were safely recovered and brought ashore.

A recreational motor vessel with 4 occupants capsized. They were rescued by another recreational craft and the local lifeboat service also attended and escorted the other craft back to shore. The 4 occupants were given first aid at the lifeboat station and were allowed to leave after they were found not to be seriously injured. It was reported that the occupants of the boat had possibly consumed a
Two kayakers both capsized and entered the water. One kayaker self recovered while the other kayaker was not found and presumed deceased after an extensive rescue operation.

A sailing dinghy was found capsized with the helm reported missing. Search and rescue assets were tasked to the scene to carry out a search operation that was suspended overnight and then continued the following morning. The dinghy was found washed ashore but the helm was still missing presumed dead.

A dinghy capsized while the crew member was fishing. They were  sat on top of the upturned vessel. A passing by vessel noticed an individual waving and provided a lifejacket. The individual was helped and brought safely ashore.
Two persons were onboard a kayak when it capsized. One casualty was recovered but the other was declared deceased once found.

A dinghy crew member fell overboard from the vessel itself, who then managed to self recover themselves in the end with the police and lifeboats had been tasked to assist. One of the lifeboats also helped with dinghy recovery had a medical check to an there were no medical issues.

Man overboard from a dinghy clinging onto a buoy required assistance. The casualty is not a good swimmer and was not wearing a lifejacket. A passing yacht assisted the casualty who was taken ashore for further medical assistance.

A two person sit on kayak capsized on a white water section of the river with one paddler on board. The casualty became trapped under the kayak, the paddle and got their legs tangled in the webbing and leg leash leaving their head under the water. The victim was declared deceased by the doctor on the scene. They were wearing a personal flotation device at the time.
A kayaker capsized and was later found deceased.

title: Line-Propeller Entanglement During Mooring and Unmooring Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated entanglement of mooring lines, heaving lines, and messenger lines in active or recently active propulsion units during berthing, unberthing, and towing operations. The incidents share a common latent failure: the absence of formally enforced procedural controls that explicitly prohibit propeller operation while lines are being deployed, recovered, or tensioned in proximity to propulsion units. Across all cases, lines entered the water due to a combination of vessel movement, slack accumulation, simultaneous line-handling, or inadequate tension management, and the rotating propeller provided the energy source for a sudden, catastrophic failure event. The recurring nature of these incidents across vessel types — product tankers, chemical tankers, tugs, and SAR vessels — indicates a systemic procedural gap rather than isolated human error, suggesting that existing safety management systems do not adequately codify line-position monitoring as a critical control point during engine-assisted mooring manoeuvres.

Latent hardware contributions include capstan winch designs lacking adequate guarding or emergency stop capability, fairlead and deck fitting configurations that permit line submersion under dynamic load, and the absence of mechanical barriers preventing operative entrapment in powered machinery. Human factors are significant throughout: failure to assign explicit responsibility for line-position monitoring, simultaneous rather than sequential line-handling, and inadequate positional awareness of personnel relative to bights, recoil paths, and powered deck equipment. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time operations reducing visual monitoring capability, adverse sea states generating unpredictable vessel movement, and wind-driven dynamic load changes — consistently compound these latent weaknesses, narrowing the window for corrective intervention. Together, these interacting factors create a hazard pathway in which procedural gaps, hardware deficiencies, and environmental degradation align to produce uncontrolled energy release events with serious injury and vessel damage potential.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (propulsion machinery controls and safeguards)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe working practices for crew during deck operations)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (bridge watchkeeping, manoeuvring oversight, and communication during berthing operations)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory 'Dead Slow / Dead Stop' propeller protocol requiring formal confirmation that all propulsion units are stopped and mechanically isolated before any mooring line, heaving line, or messenger line is deployed, recovered, or under active management in the vicinity of the stern or bow thruster — this protocol must be codified in the vessel's SMS with a designated officer responsible for verbal or electronic clearance, mirroring pre-entry confined space procedures.

Introduce a formal Line Position Monitoring role during all mooring and unmooring evolutions, with the assigned crew member having no other concurrent duty, equipped with adequate illumination (including portable floodlights for night operations), and holding authority to immediately call for engine stop if any line is observed slackening toward or entering the water — this role and its authority must be explicitly stated in mooring operation checklists and pre-arrival/departure briefings.

Mandate shore-side and vessel-side capstan winch safety upgrades and operational standards, including: the installation of emergency stop devices operable from a safe distance, clearly marked exclusion zones around all powered mooring equipment, prohibition on single-operative capstan operation without a designated safety observer, and regular joint drills between vessel mooring teams and terminal operatives to rehearse emergency stop and quick-release procedures under simulated adverse conditions.

representative_cases.description: A product tanker was approaching it's berth, a heaving line from the stern was sent ashore and a shore operative started to pool it using a capstan winch. As the vessel moved aft towards the jetty, the heaving line became slack, went into the water and caught on the propeller. The mooring line was pulled by the heaving line into the propeller and this caused the rope to be pulled from the jetty an

On berthing the chemical tanker, the process of kicking ahead with port rudder to bring the stern back alongside, the aft springs reported to have dropped below the water surface. This could have resulted in fouled propellers. The vessel continued to tie up and moored shortly afterwards.

A tug had completed a move for a naval vessel. The tug had been cleared to release the lines from the naval vessel.  The starboard lines were dropped into the water one at a time and recovered by the tug. Both port lines were dropped together, the first line was recovered without incident but whilst the second line was being recovered the messenger was drawn into the tug's port propulsion unit. Th

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel fouled one of its propellers during a rescue operation. The crew of the SAR vessel were trying to set up a tow with a casualty vessel. They had sent a heaving line to a crewmember aboard the casualty vessel. When pulling the heaving line, some slack went into the water and fouled the port propeller of the SAR vessel. The crew shut down the port engine and proceeded

A cargo vessel was unberthing, the stern lines were stuck on a bollard and the shore operative had to drop both lines at once. The crew onboard could not heave up the lines fast enough and a line fouled the propeller. The vessel returned to its berth where the line was removed before the vessel departed.

A mooring boat was assisting a vessel in berthing operations when its propeller became fouled by a floating mooring line. The crew took the propeller out of gear and drifted towards a dolphin where they secured the vessel. They attempted to clear the fouled line, however they were unsuccessful. They had to cut the mooring rope free and they were towed to a berth where the propeller was cleared. Th

A ro-ro cargo vessel's forward spring line entered the water when the end of the rope, on shore, had become tangled. Whilst the shore workers where trying to untangle the rope, the vessel's crew kept feeding the rope not realising that the rope had slackened and was now in the water. This resulted in the spring line entering the water where there was a potential for it to get caught in the bow thr

A workboat, which was moored to a windfarm structure, became unknowingly fouled on the structure as the tide ebbed and the crew were unable to free the vessel. They were eventually freed with the assistance of another workboat. No damage was found to either the structure or the workboat.

The vessel was along side in heavy weather so extra mooring lines were added to secure the vessel. When unbreathing there was an issue with letting go of the stern lines resulting in three mooring lines in the water. The ebb tide and winds pushed the mooring lines under the vessel and two of them made contact with the starboard propeller. One line was cut and the other fouled the propeller. More l

A cement carrier was berthing at a port with a pilot on board when the propeller became fouled by a mooring line. The crew on aft stations had sent a heaving line ashore to the linesmen on the quay, but when they started to pay out the mooring line, they paid out more than the linesmen could pick up ashore, causing a portion of line to enter the water. The wash from the assisting tug pushed the mo

title: Hatch Operation Injuries: Procedural and Design Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries sustained during the routine or inspection-related operation of shipboard hatches, encompassing finger-trap entrapments, falls from height, and overbalancing incidents. The incidents share a common underlying pattern: hatch handling is a repetitive, low-perceived-risk task that has not been subject to meaningful risk assessment or the development of formal safe work procedures. As a result, predictable situational variables — including darkness, vessel motion, lone working, and physical effort — are not adequately controlled. The absence of engineered safety features such as hatch stay rods, ergonomic operating handles, and finger-trap guards means that when crew technique deviates even marginally from ideal, injury is a foreseeable outcome. The frequency of these events reflects a systemic failure to treat routine equipment operation as a hazardous task warranting structured hazard controls.

Latent hardware deficiencies play a significant contributing role across this cluster: hatches that are heavy, corroded, or lacking open-position retention devices place disproportionate physical demand on the operator and remove any tolerance for error in balance or grip. Human factors — including inattention, instinctive self-preservation reactions that divert attention from hatch control, and inadequate procedure awareness — further increase vulnerability, particularly when crew are working alone without a buddy system or supervision. Environmental conditions, specifically night-time low-light environments and moderate sea states introducing vessel motion and ladder instability, compound these deficiencies. The convergence of poorly maintained hardware, absent procedural controls, and adverse environmental conditions in what is perceived as a routine task creates a latent accident pathway that is repeatedly realised across fleet operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structure, Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (relating to safe access, escape routes, and mechanical integrity of hatch mechanisms)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (relating to the operability and maintenance of emergency escape hatches and associated equipment)

SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (relating to risk assessment, safe work procedures, and the identification of hazards in routine shipboard tasks)
preventable_by: Develop and implement documented Safe Work Procedures (SWPs) for all hatch operations, including mandatory risk assessments that specifically identify hazards associated with night-time operation, vessel motion, working at height, and lone working. Procedures must define correct body positioning, required lighting standards, and the prohibition of lone working when operating overhead hatches from ladders.

Conduct a fleet-wide engineering review of all operational and emergency escape hatches to identify and rectify hardware deficiencies, including the retrofit of stay rods or gas-assisted struts to hold hatches open safely, ergonomic operating handles to eliminate finger-trap geometry, and anti-corrosion treatment or replacement of stiff wheel lock mechanisms. Integrate hatch mechanical integrity into planned maintenance schedules with defined inspection intervals.

Introduce structured toolbox talks and pre-task briefings specifically covering hatch operation hazards, supplemented by competency verification to confirm that all crew can demonstrate correct hatch-operating technique. Establish a mandatory buddy system requirement for any hatch operation conducted at height, in low-light conditions, or on emergency escape routes, and record compliance as part of the vessel's safety management system audit trail.

representative_cases.description: The vessel had not long departed a port when a crew member closed their finger in a hatch, the vessel turned around and returned back to port to get the crew member checked by medical staff. They didn't require any further medical treatment and were given the all-clear for the vessel to depart the port again and then continue its passage. The crew member had a minor finger injury.

a crew member was assigned to check the emergency escapes in the machinery spaces, when they came to check the escape hatch in the converter room STBD side. At first, the crew member struggled to unlock the hatch via the wheel, once they did the hatch was also difficult to lift. As they were opening the hatch their footing slipped which caused them to concentrate on preventing myself from falling

A general cargo vessel crew member was opening an engine room emergency escape hatch when they lost their balance and fell, sustaining a broken elbow. The vessel reported that the crewmember was standing in the 'wrong position' to operate the emergency escape hatch which resulted in them overbalancing. Their elbow was broken as a result of their fall. The crew member was disembarked to hospital an

After the completion of the inspection we were proceeding out of the outer framed bay on the top level of the tank. We had to climb over pipework to access the vertical lightning holes to get back into the main part of the tank. I reminded the surveyor about the holes in the deck. As I climbed over the pipework, I went to place my foot on the deck and there was a semi obscured hole (by the pipewor

While conducting sail training manoeuvres, a passenger fell onto the main hatch rails as the yacht's motion altered on a wave. The extent of the injury was likely increased as the main hatch was open meaning the fall was approximately 40cm further and onto a cornered aluminium rail as opposed to a rounded GRP hatch. The casualty sustained two broken ribs and was signed off strenuous activities for

A container ship's crewmember was checking reefer containers on the cross deck when they fell through a hatch onto the upper platform of the cargo hold. The fall was around 4 meters. The crewmember was found by another crewmember who was returning to close the hatch. They were taken to the medical centre and following medical advice they were sent ashore for further treatment

Cruise ship contractor fell from ladder resulting in injury to the right armpit. The contractor fell from the ladder into the bulkhead and then slid down to the deck. On the way down a metal bracket punctured the contractor under the arm.

A cargo vessel had finished unloading and the crew were closing the cargo hatches. A lump of wood fell from the cargo hatch and struck a crewmember on the head. The casualty was wearing hard had but they sustained a head injury when they fell backwards from the impact and their head made heavy contact with the ship's structure. The casualty walked to the ambulance themself and was taken to hospita

A service vessel crew member went to the helicopter equipment locker to pick up hearing protection.  After opening the locker door, the crew member went to step inside to get a headset and placed their foot on a bracket resulting in a sprained ankle. The crew member was sent for an x-ray and CT scan to rule out a fractured ankle.

A buoy laying vessel crewmember was on top of a hatch on the main working deck inspecting the maintenance carried out on the speed crane. When dismounting the hatch, the crewmember used a nearby fender and the catface of the crane as a support. The catface was not secure and gave way when weight was applied to it. This caused the crewmember to fall and land awkwardly on a grappling hook.  The crew

title: Night-Time River Grounding Compounded by Tidal Flooding
description:
This theme is characterised by recreational and hire vessels grounding in shallow riverine or estuarial waters during night-time passages, with the grounding subsequently compounded into a serious flooding or sinking event by rising tidal conditions. The incidents share a consistent causal pathway: inadequate pre-departure voyage planning — particularly the failure to account for tidal states, depth margins, and the navigational demands of night-time river transit — creates the initial conditions for grounding. Once aground, crews demonstrate a further failure to anticipate or respond effectively to the dynamic tidal threat, neither kedging off promptly nor taking protective measures to prevent water ingress over low freeboard points such as the stern. The pattern recurs because these vessels are frequently operated by persons with limited underway passage-making experience, including houseboat operators and hire vessel users who are unaccustomed to applying systematic voyage planning disciplines to what may appear to be straightforward transits.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including insufficient or undersized bilge pumps, unsecured outboard engines, and damage-prone propulsion systems — reduce the vessel's capacity to self-recover once aground, transforming a recoverable grounding into a total loss. Human factors are dominant throughout: the absence of a formal watchkeeping regime, failure to use available depth-sounding equipment, and limited familiarity with local waterway hazards all reflect systemic shortfalls in safety culture and training among the recreational and hire sector. Environmental conditions, particularly reduced visibility at night and the unforgiving mechanics of tidal rise on a vessel displaced on a bank, act as force multipliers on these pre-existing vulnerabilities. The combination of an inexperienced crew, a vessel not optimally configured for passage-making, and an unplanned encounter with a tidal grounding scenario consistently exceeds the crew's capacity to manage the emergency effectively.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 35: safe navigation, voyage planning, and danger messages)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness, survival craft, and rescue procedures)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (watertight integrity, bilge pumping arrangements, and damage survivability)
preventable_by: Mandate formal pre-departure voyage planning for all overnight or inter-port transits, requiring operators to document tidal predictions, minimum depth margins along the intended track, and contingency grounding response procedures — including kedging-off protocols and identification of safe waiting anchorages — before departure.

Require hire and recreational vessel operators navigating tidal rivers at night to maintain a continuous active watchkeeping regime, including regular depth-sounder monitoring with pre-set shallow-water alarms, and to complete a demonstrated competency assessment covering night navigation and tidal hazard management before undertaking such passages.

Establish minimum hardware standards for vessels transiting tidal rivers, including certified bilge pump capacity commensurate with vessel volume, secure engine mounting inspections prior to passage, and mandatory carriage of operational depth-sounding equipment — with enforcement through pre-departure vessel safety checks conducted by marina operators or hire companies.

representative_cases.description: A recreational sailing vessel was on passage in a river when it ran aground on a shoal. The vessel became stuck in the mud of the shoal and slipped down the bank so that the tide was able to come over the stern and flood the vessel, causing it to sink. Local SAR services extracted the two crew, who were unharmed, and marked the sunken vessel with a float until it could be recovered.

A motor cruiser grounded following an incident and became stuck on mud. The vessel was primarily a house boat but it was underway to another port where the crew had a second boat. The engine was damaged in the grounding. There were four people on board the boat as well as a large number of animals (fifteen dogs, eight cats and various other small animals including birds). The boat began to flood w

A hire broads cruiser grounded on a sandbank which resulted in a water ingress. The bilge pumps were applied but were not effective as the ingress could not be stopped. The six crew members onboard were wearing lifejackets but due to some of the crew having medical conditions, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene. The crew members  were transferred to the SAR vessel, taken asho

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was conducting a launching exercise, the SAR vessel came off the carriage and proceeded slowly forwards. Soon after a slight vibration was felt, it was suspected from the vessel briefly grounding on a possible new bank that may have developed. The crew continued with the exercise as the vessel seemed fine, after it was recovered there was damage to the port propell

A recreational vessel grounded and was listing about forty-five degrees, as the tide began to rise the vessel flooded through its vents as it was stuck hard aground. The two crewmembers were still onboard and moved to sit on the side of the vessel. Search and rescue services rescued the two crewmembers. The vessel then sank. There was 700 litres of fuel onboard the vessel and a sheen was on the wa

Support vessel grounded resulting in no known damage but loss of power and towage required to deeper waters. The vessel's propeller hit a small sandbank which cut out the engine. Several attempts were made to restart the engine but these failed. Another support vessel with a shallow draft went to the assistance of the support vessel, passed a tow line and towed the vessel into the deeper waters at

A RIB (rigid inflatable boat) with three people on board grounded on mud. The lifeboat extracted a child from the vessel whilst the two remaining people on board waited to refloat. As the vessel refloated, the vessel started to sink and the two people were rescued by the lifeboat.

A sailing yacht hit a sandbank resulting in the loss of one of the bilge keels, the vessel then capsized with the two crew onboard going into the water. Two search and rescue (SAR) vessels were launched, as they arrived the two crew were recovered from the water by a passing vessel. Both crew members were assessed by the SAR crew, no medical assistance was required. The vessel later sank with the

A search and rescue vessel was searching along the coast for a missing person when they went aground on a submerged rock shelf. The vessel was not significantly damaged and was able to continue the search for another hour before returning to their base, where the crew discovered that the propellers were damaged.

A sailing yacht ran aground and was unable to be towed due to the tidal height. The vessel later refloated on the flood tide and it was discovered the rudder had broken off. A search and rescue vessel was tasked to tow the vessel back to a mooring.

title: Undetected Flammable and Toxic Gas Accumulation Aboard Vessels
description:
This theme encompasses incidents arising from the undetected accumulation of toxic or flammable gases — principally carbon monoxide (CO) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) — within enclosed or semi-enclosed spaces aboard vessels. The incidents share a consistent causal architecture: a latent hardware deficiency, whether a compromised exhaust or combustion system, a leaking LPG tank, or a defective fuel system, allows dangerous gases to migrate or accumulate in occupied or unattended spaces. This hazard is compounded in every case by the absence or failure of automated detection systems, meaning that no independent safeguard exists to interrupt the progression toward a casualty event. The pattern recurs across vessel types — from small pleasure craft to commercial utility and crew transfer vessels — indicating that the underlying safety management failures are systemic rather than isolated. The asymmetry between severity outcomes, ranging from minor injury to vessel loss and animal fatality, reflects the degree to which chance, occupancy, and proximity to an ignition source determine consequences rather than any meaningful variation in underlying risk control.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the initiating condition in each incident, but the absence of procedural safeguards consistently transforms a manageable technical failure into a serious casualty. Crews and owners demonstrated no established pre-operation checks — such as gas sniff tests, bilge ventilation procedures, or exhaust system inspections — that would have intercepted accumulation before exposure or ignition occurred. Human factors, including failure to recognise early CO poisoning symptoms, the diminished alertness associated with night-time operation, and the reduced procedural rigour characteristic of single-handed or leisure vessel operation, further eroded the opportunity for timely intervention. Environmental conditions, notably low wind speeds and enclosed berthing arrangements, reduced natural ventilation effectiveness and accelerated accumulation rates. Collectively, these incidents demonstrate a well-documented failure mode in which the safety system has no depth: when the primary hardware barrier fails, no procedural, detection-based, or human-recognition layer exists to prevent harm.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction — Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate the installation, regular testing, and maintenance of type-approved CO and flammable gas detection systems with audible alarms in all accommodation spaces, galleys, and enclosed below-deck areas, with flag State or classification society verification at survey.

Require vessel operators to incorporate pre-departure and pre-operation atmospheric safety checks — including LPG system integrity inspections, bilge ventilation confirmation, and exhaust system condition assessments — into formal Safety Management System procedures, with completion records retained aboard.

Deliver targeted crew and owner safety training on the recognition of early CO poisoning symptoms, LPG accumulation risks, and the correct emergency response to gas alarm activation, with competency in enclosed-space atmospheric hazards integrated into relevant certification and familiarisation requirements.

representative_cases.description: A utility vessel had returned to port and was alongside. A crew member went to hospital as they had been feeling unwell whilst at sea. Following an assessment and blood tests, the crew member was found to have an CO (carbon monoxide) poisoning.  Following this, the vessel was ordered to stay alongside, steps were taken to vent the vessel and the vessel owners initiated an independent inspection of

A sailing vessel with one person onboard was moored on a river, the casualty went to light a stove but the gas exploded, probably due to the tank leaking. The casualty suffered burns to the face and throat and was taken to hospital. There was no pollution from the vessel and only minor damage.
Inland pleasure vessel sank after an explosion. It was alleged that a kettle had been left on the gas ring while the owner was fishing on the bank. No injuries reported.

Pleasure craft with two people and a dog on board suffered an explosion. One of the members sustained no injuries however, the second member was blown out of the boat onto the pontoons sustaining fractures to their legs. The dog was below deck and is believed to have been trapped there. The vessel sank shortly after the explosion. Another pleasure craft that was alongside the explosive vessel suff

A crew transfer vessel was mid-water, waiting for the weather conditions to improve before completing their transfer, when the carbon monoxide alarm in the crew saloon area activated. The detector showed the maximum reading of 200ppm. The crew opened the doors to allow fresh air into the space and the level dropped to 20ppm. The crew decided to return to shore at slow speed with all the doors and

A fire started on the deck of a heavy load carrier. The source was identified as a pallet of gas cylinders, crew onboard dealt with the fire. The fire service and ambulance responded along with a search and rescue vessel. The fire was extinguished, no injuries to crew and no pollution reported.

The vessel sustained a gas explosion on board caused by a gas bottle and the casualty was taken ashore on another uninvolved vessel. 114 litres of diesel were on board the vessel but no spillage was observed at the location or during the passage and lifting out.  A workboat also attended the scene, no pollution or debris was observed at the location and no subsequent pollution. The crewmember sust

A motor cruiser caught fire following a gas explosion and the fire spread to another vessel. The first vessel sank, and the occupants escaped with injuries but their dog was killed. One other vessel sustained material damage as a significant portion of the accommodation above the water line was burned. Another vessel nearby sustained minor damage.

A stove on a recreational motor vessel malfunctioned, caught fire which escalated quickly with the gas bottles onboard exploding. The fire service was called to contain the fire but the vessel sank. Booms were set up to contain debris and any pollution. One person was onboard but they were able to get off, they were treated for superficial burns.

A pleasure yacht was alongside in a marina when an explosion occurred on board. The two crew on board suffered burns, one of them was severely burned on the face, hands and upper body. The shore side emergency services responded and took both casualties to hospital in an ambulance. The marina employees and crew of a search and rescue craft administered first aid to the burned crew at the scene. Th

title: Passenger Bathroom Slip Hazards in Marine Environments
description:
This theme encompasses a recurring pattern of passenger slip, trip, and fall incidents within cruise ship cabin bathrooms, predominantly occurring in shower areas and at shower exit points. The incidents are characterised by wet floor surfaces, inadequate or absent fixed anti-slip provisions, and unsecured or bypassed bathmats in a confined, dynamic marine environment. A common thread across all incidents is the absence of effective passive safety infrastructure — specifically fixed non-slip floor treatments and appropriately positioned grab rails — combined with an over-reliance on moveable soft furnishings such as towel bathmats as the primary, and in many cases sole, slip-prevention measure. These incidents recur not because of isolated acts of passenger carelessness, but because the vessel's safety management system has failed to recognise unsecured bathmats and unprotected wet floor areas as foreseeable, systemic hazards requiring active mitigation in the marine cabin environment.

Latent hardware deficiencies are central to this theme: bathroom design frequently lacks fixed anti-slip surface treatments, secondary grab rails at shower exit points, and appropriately positioned support structures for passengers with mobility limitations. Human factors compound these deficiencies, as passengers — unfamiliar with vessel motion and without adequate safety briefings or cabin literature — make uninformed decisions such as foregoing available bathmats or showering without adequate support. Environmental conditions, including sea states ranging from slight to moderate and night-time operation, further elevate risk by introducing vessel motion and reducing passenger situational awareness in an already hazardous wet space. The post-incident finding of no defects in multiple cases reveals a systemic failure in hazard identification: the vessel's defect reporting and safety management frameworks do not adequately classify soft furnishing placement, fixed anti-slip provision, or grab rail adequacy as auditable safety-critical items, allowing the latent risk to persist undetected and unaddressed across the fleet.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (structural arrangements and design safety of passenger spaces)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings and muster procedures, including onboard safety information obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requirement for operators to identify hazards, assess risks, and implement effective safety management procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate the installation of fixed, permanent anti-slip surface treatments on all passenger bathroom and shower floor areas, and the fitting of at least one securely mounted grab rail at each shower entry and exit point, with compliance verified through flag state or class surveys and documented in the vessel's maintenance management system.

Establish a formal housekeeping and cabin safety protocol requiring crew to inspect and confirm that bathmats have adequate non-slip backing and are correctly positioned at each cabin turnaround, with findings recorded in a structured checklist that feeds into the vessel's hazard identification and defect reporting framework under the ISM-compliant Safety Management System.

Implement mandatory passenger safety communications — delivered at embarkation briefings, through in-cabin video, and printed cabin literature — that specifically address the risk of slipping in cabin bathrooms during vessel motion, the correct use of provided anti-slip aids, and the importance of reporting bathroom hazards or injuries to the medical centre without delay.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was in their cabin, as they went into the bathroom to wash their hands the passenger slipped on the bathmat that was on the floor. They lost their balance, fell backwards and landed on the shower cubicle, the passenger hit their head on the shower handrail. The passenger sustained an injury to their neck and was disembarked the following day and taken to hospital for furthe

A cruise ship passenger slipped and fell whilst in the shower. The casualty went to bed after the fall. The following morning, the pain in their back became more severe and they visited the medical centre, where a sprain/strain of their upper back was diagnosed. The shower curtain in subject and the area of the accident were inspected and revealed no defects. When the vessel came alongside, five d

A cruise ship passenger was stepping out of the shower but did not step on the towel bathmat provided and instead stepped onto the wet floor. The floor was slippery due to the water and the passenger slipped and fell, stretching out their right leg causing a strain which resulted in the rupture of the Achilles tendon on the right leg. There were no defects found in the cabin and the full lighting

A cruise ship passenger was taking a shower when their foot slipped and they consequently fell and sustained a fracture to their left ankle. The onboard emergency call was activated and the guest was taken to the medical centre for treatment. The area where the accident occurred was examined and the shower base was found in good condition without any obvious defect. The occurrence was reported to

A cruise ship passenger was showering alone, sitting on a chair. They reached out to a grab rail to reposition a chair and fell off as the chair shifted. This resulted in a fractured hip and femur and the passenger was medically evacuated via helicopter.

A cruise ship passenger was exiting their cabin bathroom when they slipped on the floor and fell forward, landing on their left side. They sustained a fractured pelvis. The area was inspected and the floor was dry at the time with no defects noted.

The cruise ship was in open waters, a passenger was going to the toilets. As the passenger stepped into the toilets, they slipped on the wet floor with one leg slipping in front and the other going underneath them. One of the medical team was called for and assisted the passenger. The passenger was medically disembarked and taken to a local hospital and treated for a fractured ankle. The passenger

A cruise ship crew member slipped whilst entering the shower in their bathroom and fell, fracturing their hand. The incident happened in the evening and they went to the medical centre on board the day after. The area was inspected for defects and none were found. The crew member was disembarked from the vessel.

A cruise ship passenger lost balance, slipped and fell over a bath tub landing on their right elbow. The passenger went to the medical centre and was diagnosed with a chip fracture on the head on the right radius. The passenger remained onboard. The area was inspected for defects, none reported.

A cruise ship passenger fell when stepping out of the shower, falling backwards and hitting their back against the floor. The guest didn't immediately go to the medical centre on board but attended later on two days after the injury. The area was inspected and found to be in order without defects. The passenger fractured their shoulder.

title: Night-Time Contact with Submerged and Fixed Hazards
description:
This theme is characterised by vessel contact with submerged objects, fixed infrastructure, and navigational hazards during night-time operations, predominantly in coastal and inshore environments. The incidents span a range of vessel types — including SAR vessels, ferries, passenger vessels, and recreational craft — but share a consistent pattern: darkness as the primary environmental risk amplifier, combined with inadequate hazard awareness, insufficient pre-passage planning, and reduced lookout effectiveness. The recurrence of these incidents across operationally experienced crews underscores that professional competence alone does not mitigate the structural vulnerabilities introduced by night operations. Compounding factors include poorly maintained or unmarked aids to navigation, decommissioned or inadequately charted infrastructure remaining in navigable waters, and the operational urgency characteristic of emergency response missions, which may suppress standard navigational caution. Familiarity with a route or launch area further contributes to complacency, reducing crew vigilance around non-obvious but persistent hazards.

From a latent causation perspective, these incidents reflect the convergence of hardware deficiencies, human factors, and environmental conditions. Hardware and infrastructure failures — including aging power plants, dislodged transom shields, submerged mooring blocks, and poorly maintained buoys — create contact hazards that are difficult to detect without adequate lighting or depth-monitoring equipment. Human factors include over-reliance on experiential knowledge rather than systematic passage planning, insufficient use of available navigational instruments such as echo sounders and chart plotters, and degraded watchkeeping discipline during routine or return transits. Environmental conditions, particularly darkness, interact with these latent failures to eliminate the visual margins that would otherwise allow crews to identify and avoid hazards in time. Collectively, these incidents indicate systemic gaps in night navigation protocols, hazard charting accuracy, and the maintenance standards applied to both vessels and navigational infrastructure.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, 24, and 34: navigational equipment requirements, lookout duties, safe speed, and voyage planning)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (in the context of coordination failures and emergency communication during contact events)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: safety management systems, passage planning procedures, and crew training obligations)
preventable_by: Mandate structured night navigation protocols for all vessel types operating in coastal and inshore waters, requiring documented pre-departure passage plans that include hazard identification, speed restrictions in hazard-prone areas, and confirmation of functional depth-monitoring and chart-plotting equipment prior to each night transit.

Establish a systematic programme for hazard mapping and nautical chart updating in frequently used departure routes and operational areas, including the recording and formal reporting of all submerged obstructions, decommissioned infrastructure, and displaced or damaged aids to navigation to the relevant hydrographic and port authorities.

Implement mandatory crew training in night navigation and loss-of-power emergency response scenarios, with periodic drills that simulate contact risk conditions, reinforcing lookout discipline, speed management under reduced visibility, and the procedural steps required when propulsion or power loss occurs in proximity to fixed or submerged hazards.

representative_cases.description: A ferry experienced power failure and struck a disused mooring dolphin. There was no damage to the vessel but possibly damage to the dolphin.
A search and rescue vessel was on passage when it made contact with a submerged buoy. No damage or injuries occurred.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was underway when the skeg of the vessel struck a rock. The vessel was not stuck but the skeg was damaged.
Passenger vessel hit an unknown object underwater which resulted in loss of propulsion. Towage was required. Following examination - transom shield had become dislodged. No further damages/injuries.
Lifeboat was tasking when it struck something under the water - possibly a rock. The only damage was to the gelcoat on the port side hull. No injuries reported.
A lifeboat damaged its propeller after passing through a narrow passage when the propeller made contact with a submerged rock.
Whilst returning to port the vessel's prop made contact with a submerged object, suspected to be a rock. The vessel returned to station and the propeller changed.  There were no injuries to crew.
A sailing vessel made contact with a submarine barrier. The iron keel was potentially hit however no damage was confirmed and it remained on passage to its final destination.
Chartered yacht sank on its moorings after being struck by a SAR tug. All persons onboard the yacht escaped unhurt.
A sailing vessel became ensnared in an underwater obstruction that fouled the propeller. Divers attended and freed the vessel and it was able to continue on passage without further issues.

title: SAR Vessel Groundings and Contacts During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of groundings, contacts with submerged objects, and capsizes involving Search and Rescue vessels operating in coastal and inshore environments under conditions of reduced visibility, primarily at night. The unifying latent cause across this cluster is the tension between operational urgency — inherent to SAR missions — and the maintenance of adequate navigational vigilance. Crews consistently demonstrate task-focused attention narrowing, where the imperative to locate and rescue casualties displaces routine hazard monitoring, chart awareness, and speed management. This cognitive narrowing is further compounded by single-operator workload in small SAR craft, where navigation and rescue coordination duties compete directly for finite attentional resources. The cluster also reveals a systemic environmental data reliability problem: in multiple incidents, significant discrepancies exist between officially reported sea states and measured wave heights, suggesting that crews and coordinators are making go/no-go and operational decisions based on inaccurate environmental intelligence, systematically underestimating inshore hazard severity, particularly in surf zones.

From a latent causation perspective, the hardware and procedural environment offers insufficient protection against these foreseeable human factor failures. SAR vessels in this cluster lack formalised surf zone entry criteria, mandatory speed restrictions for night inshore operations, and pre-planned safe approach corridors for known hazard areas such as launch points and beach gradients. Environmental conditions — night lighting, wind, breaking surf, and shallow water gradients — consistently amplify the consequences of momentary lapses in navigational attention, yet these compounding factors are not systematically incorporated into mission authorisation frameworks. The absence of injury or significant damage across many of these incidents reflects fortunate outcomes rather than adequate risk control, and the cumulative pattern strongly indicates systemic organisational and procedural deficiencies rather than isolated crew error. The loss of navigation-critical equipment such as DF aerials as secondary consequences of groundings further degrades SAR capability, creating a risk of the rescue vessel itself becoming a casualty.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (particularly Regulations 34 and 35 covering safe navigation and reporting obligations, and the duty to proceed with caution in poorly charted or hazardous inshore waters)

SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (pertaining to the operational readiness and integrity of SAR vessels as primary response assets, and crew training requirements for emergency operations)

SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 4, relating to port State and flag State obligations to investigate marine casualties and address systemic safety deficiencies identified through incident analysis)
preventable_by: Implement formalised condition-based go/no-go criteria and surf zone entry protocols for all SAR vessel operations, incorporating verified real-time environmental data including measured wave height rather than observer-reported sea state categories, with mandatory speed restrictions and minimum safe standoff distances from shorelines during night operations.

Introduce dual-role operational procedures for inshore night SAR tasks that explicitly separate the navigation watch function from rescue coordination duties, either through minimum crew complement requirements or designated crew role assignments, to eliminate single-operator cognitive overload and prevent attention-divided grounding events.

Mandate pre-departure and pre-area hazard assessments for all night SAR deployments, including review of local bathymetric data, charted hazards, known surf zones, and mooring buoy positions near launch points, supported by standardised local knowledge briefings and the active use of depth-sensing equipment throughout inshore search operations.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescues XP boat capsized in surf, no damage and no injuries
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was searching for a person close inshore during a still and clear night when it touched the seabed on two occasions. There was no pollution or damage reported.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was launched to carrying out a search for a missing person. During the search  the vessel made contact with an underwater object resulting in a damaged propeller.
A search and rescue vessel grounded on submerged rocks while on a rescue, damaging the prop and gelcoat of the starboard side of the hull and missing DF aerial.
A search and rescue vessel struck propeller on submerged mooring buoy. Just launched on a lifeboat shout, under power. Whilst proceeding a submerged mooring buoy was ran over and struck a prop.
A search and rescue vessel capsized in surf whilst on patrol when a wave hit the broad side of the vessel. There was no damage or injury.
SAR vessel made contact with an unknown submerged object, minor damage to the hull.
A search and rescue vessel grounded during an emergency tasking, resulting in damage to the propeller. The vessel returned to the marina and the crew fitted a new propeller.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel grounded whilst looking for a casualty along a beach. There was no damage to the vessel and it continued on the rescue operation without further incident.
A SAR (search and rescue) vessel's daughter craft capsized on a beach when being walked to the shore by the crew. No damage or injury.

title: Tug Equipment Failures and Operational Readiness Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of towing equipment failures — principally towline and tow wire partings, winch malfunctions, propeller fouling, and loss of propulsion — occurring predominantly during night operations across a range of sea states, including benign weather conditions. The persistence of these incidents across multiple vessel types and operational contexts strongly indicates that the primary causal driver is systemic equipment maintenance deficiency rather than adverse environmental exposure. The fact that many failures occur in near-calm conditions effectively eliminates weather as a primary factor and instead implicates inadequate planned maintenance systems, insufficient pre-operational equipment inspections, deferred maintenance cultures, and poor lifecycle management of critical towing components. Compounding this is the operational vulnerability created by minimal tug redundancy, where the failure of a single asset renders entire manoeuvres non-executable, exposing escorted vessels and port environments to serious navigational risk.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including fatigue-degraded tow wires, worn winch brakes and clutches, and fouled running gear — represent the dominant failure pathway and suggest that inspection and retirement criteria for towing equipment are either absent, inconsistently applied, or inadequately enforced across fleets. Human factors, including insufficient watchkeeping vigilance, lack of dedicated towline observers, and over-reliance on routine without structured pre-departure checks, amplify the likelihood that early stress indicators go undetected. Night lighting conditions consistently emerge as a contributing environmental factor, reducing crews' ability to visually monitor equipment condition or detect dynamic loading anomalies in real time. Collectively, these incidents reflect a systemic gap in the application of ISM Code principles to towing-specific maintenance and operational readiness standards, with the potential for consequences to escalate significantly in higher sea states, confined waterways, or operations involving laden tankers and other high-consequence cargo vessels.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code): mandatory implementation of planned maintenance systems and pre-operational readiness checks for critical equipment
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction, Subdivision and Machinery: requirements for machinery maintenance, propulsion system integrity, and operational reliability of shipborne mechanical systems
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation: duties related to safe watchkeeping, situational awareness, collision avoidance, and the safe conduct of towing operations in all visibility conditions
preventable_by: Implement mandatory towing equipment lifecycle management protocols that specify maximum service intervals, load-cycle retirement thresholds, and mandatory pre-operational inspection checklists for tow wires, winch brakes, clutches, and associated running gear — with non-compliance treated as a condition-of-class deficiency preventing vessel deployment.

Require all tug operators to conduct structured pre-operational readiness assessments before commencing any towing assignment, including functional testing of winches and braking systems under load, propulsion system checks, and explicit redundancy verification confirming that a minimum spare tug margin exists above the stated operational tug requirement for each planned manoeuvre.

Introduce night towing operational standards that mandate the assignment of a dedicated towline observer, the use of illuminated or reflective towline markers, and enhanced bridge watchkeeping protocols during darkness — supported by periodic fleet-wide night-ops competency training that includes dynamic load awareness and emergency response drills for tow failure scenarios.

representative_cases.description: A tug was escorting another vessel (a crude oil tanker) when it experienced an issue that caused all of the towline from the winch drum to go overboard. There were no injuries or damages reported.
A tug was assisting a container ship when a tow parted. No injuries, no damages and gear was recovered. Both of them continued their passage.
While the anchor handling vessel was servicing another vessel, the main tow wire parted. There were two other tugs involved who maintained everything under control resulting in no damages/injuries.
Tug towline parted when towing a ro-ro cargo vessel with no damage or pollution
A tug was towing a general cargo vessel as part of the departure operations when it fouled its propeller. The departure was aborted due to the fact that two tugs were necessary for the manoeuvre.
A tug experienced engine issues whilst involved in the towage of a vessel. Another tug was requested to assist if needed. The engine issue was resolved and the standby tug stood down.
A general cargo vessel passed between two vessels and severed the tow line.
Tug unsuitable to provide assistance due to deficient winch.
A tug ran over its own towline and had damages including a bent shaft, arriving at the port to undergo repairs.
An aft tug's tow line parted after winch failure while berthing a container ship. Berthing operations were completed successfully using other tugs involved in the operation.

title: Engine Room Fire from Degraded Components and Inspection Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by engine room fires arising from the convergence of degraded or defective hardware — including contaminated thermal lagging, failed fuel system fittings, circlip failures, and oil leaks near high-temperature turbocharger surfaces — and systemic failures in maintenance and pre-departure inspection regimes. Across these incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: a latent mechanical deficiency develops undetected through the maintenance and repair cycle, only to manifest as a fire once the engine is started and heat builds over time. The recurring nature of these events indicates that existing inspection protocols are insufficiently rigorous in targeting the interface between potential hydrocarbon release points and ignition-capable hot surfaces, and that pre-departure readiness checks do not consistently include verification of thermal insulation integrity and fuel system fitting condition.

Latent hardware deficiencies — fatigue-failed circlips, degraded valve plugs, deteriorated lagging saturated with hydrocarbons, and seeping turbocharger seals — form the foundational risk in each case, but their escalation to fire is enabled by human and procedural failures at multiple barriers. Maintenance personnel do not identify or rectify degraded components during repair periods, and pre-departure checks do not capture residual risk before engine start-up. Once underway, the absence of automated oil mist or fuel leak detection systems means the crew's first indication of a developing fault is often the fire itself rather than a precursor warning. Environmental factors, particularly night-time operations reducing visual acuity in the engine room and moderate sea states inducing mechanical vibration on already-stressed fittings, further compress the available window for human detection and intervention, transforming what should be a maintainable defect into a serious fire casualty.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure engine room technical inspections following any repair or maintenance period, with a dedicated checklist item requiring physical verification of the condition, integrity, and contamination status of all thermal lagging and insulation on or adjacent to main engines and turbochargers, signed off by a responsible engineer before engine start-up.

Require the installation and routine testing of automated oil mist detection and hydrocarbon/fuel vapour detection systems in engine rooms, particularly in the vicinity of high-temperature components such as turbochargers and exhaust manifolds, so that developing leaks are alarmed at source before ignition can occur rather than being discovered only after a fire has started.

Introduce mandatory, timed engine room watchkeeping rounds during all machinery run-in and warm-up phases — including those conducted at night or in port — with minimum inspection intervals not exceeding 30 minutes, documented in the engine room log, to ensure that early indicators of fire such as smoke, unusual odour, or surface discolouration are detected and acted upon before a fire becomes established.

representative_cases.description: A trailing suction hopper dredger was alongside after a repair period. Departure was expected for 0800 on the day of the incident, so at 0420 the main engine was started to run it in preparation. At 0630, an engineer noticed smoke rising from the main engine heat shields. The crew mustered and fire fighting measures were implemented. After the fire was extinguished it was confirmed that there had

A crew transfer vessel was underway when a fault alarm sounded on one of the engines followed by a fire alarm. The crew saw flames on the CCTV system. They shut down the fuel supply and ventilation for the engine and alerted the coastguard. They then released the fixed fire fighting system (STAT-X). The vessel made its way back to port with an escort from another vessel. The fire was reportedly ca

A pilot vessel had a fire in the engine room which was extinguished by crew using a dry powder extinguisher.  It was found that the main engine turbo lagging had caught fire after a suspected oil leak.

A ro-pax vessel had departed port when a high exhaust temperature alarm sounded, upon investigating it was found that the fuel return pipeline’s circlip had sheared off and started to leak fuel over the hot engine. The bridge was contacted and requested to slow down and stop the aft main engine. The vessel was able to proceed safely to port using the forward main engine. With assistance from a tug

Hopper dredger suffered a fire as a result of a failed fuel injector and manifold gasket. The flames were approximately 2-3 inches high and the fire was immediately extinguished using the nearest foam portable fire extinguisher. No injuries were sustained on board. The injector and manifold gasket were both replaced.

Whilst a tug was pushing up on a vessel, fire alarms were set off in the engine room. The crew were mustered investigated. A fire was reported on the starboard engine. Emergency shutdown on the starboard engine was initiated immediately. Fuel came into contact with a hot surface and ignited. The fire was started because the lay of a low-pressure fuel hose was rubbing on a corner of the engine cove

A ro-pax vessel was in passage when one of the crew members noticed smoke above the starboard main engine. The crew member raised the alarm and the crew mustered. Whilst a two person fire fighting team wearing breathing apparatus was preparing, another crew member took immediate action using a CO2 fire extinguisher. They noticed the flames were on the lagging of the exhaust and this initial action

A ro-ro passenger vessel had a fire in the lagging over the starboard main engine while making approach to harbour. The fire was extinguished by a portable fire extinguisher within four minutes of being reported. The cause of the fire has been identified as a joint on the lube oil sensor pipework which had worked lose and sprayed oil onto the hot engine.

A pilot vessel had a fire in the engine room after a lube oil pipe failed and sprayed oil onto a hot surface. The vessel crew initiated fire fighting activities and activated the fixed fire fighting system and supressed the fire. The vessel then made its way back to berth under tow from another pilot vessel, where they were met by the shore side fire service who checked over the vessel. There were

An inland waterways passenger vessel was underway approaching the berth when there was a fuel pressure alarm on the port engine. After investigating, the crew discovered that fuel was leaking from the main engine and there was white smoke coming out of the engine room ventilation followed by the fire alarm being activated. The crew carried out emergency actions to shut down the engine and fuel sup

title: Bilge System Failure and Inadequate Crew Familiarisation
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding emergencies in which vessels' primary dewatering systems fail at the critical moment they are needed, transforming manageable water ingress into life-safety emergencies requiring external rescue intervention. Across the representative incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: the bilge pump — whether rendered inoperable by mechanical failure, electrical power loss, or lack of maintenance — constitutes a single point of failure with no redundant backup. Compounding this, crew are frequently unfamiliar with vessel-specific systems, particularly in bareboat charter contexts, delaying identification of the ingress source and the isolation of contributing valves or fittings. The recurring outcome — vessels requiring SAR assistance or towing to safety — indicates that these incidents are not isolated equipment failures but systemic deficiencies in vessel readiness and crew preparedness.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant enabling condition in this theme, manifesting as unmaintained bilge pumps, fractured pump handles, failed electrical systems, and the absence of independently powered or manual backup dewatering capacity.
