Human factors intersect critically with these hardware failures: inadequate pre-departure checks allow non-functional equipment to go undetected, while the absence of structured handover processes in charter arrangements leaves crew without the system knowledge necessary to respond effectively under pressure. Environmental conditions — particularly adverse sea states and night-time operations — consistently amplify the consequences by accelerating water ingress beyond manageable levels and reducing the crew's situational awareness and response effectiveness. Together, these latent conditions align to convert foreseeable, preventable equipment states into active flooding emergencies.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (bilge pumping arrangements and watertight integrity requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency equipment readiness and crew familiarity with safety systems)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (pre-departure safety checks, voyage planning, and fitness of vessel for intended service)
preventable_by: Mandate pre-departure safety checklists that explicitly verify the operational status of all bilge pumps, including test activation, and require documented confirmation that at least one independently powered or manual backup bilge pump is aboard and functional before the vessel is permitted to depart.

Require that all bareboat charter operators provide a documented, vessel-specific familiarisation briefing to charterers prior to departure, covering the location, function, and operation of all critical safety systems including sea cocks, bilge pumps, and fire suppression valves, with sign-off retained as a condition of charter.

Introduce mandatory carriage requirements for small recreational and charter vessels to carry a secondary bilge pump with independent power (battery-isolated electric or manual hand pump with a rated spare handle), ensuring dewatering capability is maintained in the event of primary pump failure or total electrical power loss.

representative_cases.description: A workboat began to experience water ingress. The crewmember on board tried to identify the source but could not. They tried to operate the hand bilge pump but the handle snapped. They proceeded to take the vessel to a slipway where they turned off the engine and went back down to the engine room to observe the water intake. They turned off the main seacock valve but the water was still coming in.

A motor cruiser with two crew on board suffered electrical power failure and subsequently experienced water ingress that the bilge pump on board could not keep up with. A search and rescue vessel was dispatched that provided a pump to get the water ingress under control and then towed the vessel to a harbour. There were no injuries or pollution.

A recreational motor vessel started taking on water and experienced engine failure. The pump was not functioning and the two crew were bailing the water but unable to control the level of ingress.  The vessel was towed to safety by a search and rescue (SAR) vessel.

A recreational motor vessel started taking on water resulting in power failure. The water ingress could not be identified, crew onboard stated bailing out the water as the pump was not working. Two search and rescue (SAR) vessels were tasked to assist. Five crew were recovered to one of the SAR vessels with the remaining two crew staying aboard to continue bailing. The vessel was towed back to sho

A fishing vessel experienced engine failure and started taking on water, the vessel's bilge pumps were managing to pump out the water. The vessel was anchored with five crew and twelve passengers onboard. A search and rescue vessel attended to assist if required. The vessel required towing to a harbour. No pollution was reported.

A fishing vessel had water ingress through the stern gland and the bilge pumps could not be operated effectively due to the skipper being new to the vessel. The crewmember panicked distracting the skipper from trying to work out how to control the pumps through a valve chest and the vessel was beginning to fill with water. A SAR (search and rescue) helicopter and lifeboat attended on scene and the

Sailing yacht with five people on board called MAYDAY after the vessel started filling with water. Search and rescue assets were tasked to the vessel but it was soon discovered that the cause of the water ingress was the bilge pump which was pumping water into the vessel rather than out.

A vessel was taking on water. A lifeboat and a police launch were on scene and they requested the vessel's crewmembers to stop their engines which appeared to help with stopping more water ingress. The bilge pumps were successful onboard which ensured the water ingress was completely stopped . The vessel was towed by a rescue vessel into a marina.

A sailing yacht suffered engine failure and started taking on water. The two crew anchored the vessel and started to bail by hand as the bilge pump was not working. The water ingress was located to a hairline crack approximately 4 metres on the starboard side by the starboard keel. The skipper phoned (as the vessel did not have a VHF) for assistance but did not want a search and rescue (SAR) vesse

A recreational motor vessel suffered water ingress, the bilge pump onboard was not working. The crew onboard started bailing the water out but were unable to manage the ingress. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The water ingress was coming in through the prop shaft, the SAR vessel towed the motor vessel to a marina where the vessel was lifted out. Both crew onboard were weari

title: Exclusion Zone Failures During Cargo Handling Operations
description: Latent hardware deficiencies — including the absence of tag lines for suspended load control, missing banksmen equipment, and unsecured loads on forklifts — removed the last physical safeguards against injury once human and environmental factors aligned adversely. Human factors, particularly complacency toward habitual tasks, inadequate situational awareness (compounded in several cases by carrying loads that obstructed sightlines), and operational pressure to sustain cargo schedules, consistently overrode risk perception. Environmental conditions — night-time lighting, moderate sea states inducing vessel motion, and wind speeds sufficient to cause pallet swing — acted as force multipliers on already degraded procedural controls. The convergence of these latent conditions means that injury was structurally embedded in the way these operations were being conducted, and only systemic intervention at the procedural and supervisory level will interrupt the pattern.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Construction: Fire protection, detection and extinction (as applicable to safe working space management and egress route integrity)
SOLAS Chapter III — Life-saving appliances and arrangements (crew safety during embarkation/disembarkation equipment operations including accommodation ladders)
SOLAS Chapter VI — Carriage of cargoes and oil fuels (safe handling, loading, and securing of cargo including forklift and crane operations)
preventable_by: Mandate and physically enforce load exclusion zones during all lifting operations by deploying a designated banksman with authority to halt operations, using physical barriers (cones, barriers, or rope lines) to demarcate drop/swing zones, and requiring pre-lift toolbox talks that explicitly assign crew positions away from the hazard area before any load is raised.

Require tag line use as a non-negotiable standard for all suspended pallet and cargo lifts conducted in conditions where wind, vessel motion, or load geometry creates lateral movement risk, with tag line deployment verified by the officer in charge prior to lift commencement and recorded on the cargo operation checklist.

Implement a formal traffic management plan for all forklift and vehicle operations in shared pedestrian spaces — including ramps, weather decks, and provisions areas — specifying segregated access routes, mandatory banksman deployment for reversing or inclined-surface operations, and a housekeeping standard that prohibits placement of pallets, trestles, or equipment within designated egress corridors, enforced through pre-operation area inspections.

representative_cases.description: During cargo loading, 2 crew members were injured by a pallet that swung in the wind. The first crew member to be injured was crushed between the swinging pallet and a pallet that had already been loading. The second crew member was injured because the swinging pallet fell onto his foot. Both went to hospital as a precautionary measure. Neither sustained serious injuries.

Cruise ship's crew member was working inside the deck port side frozen meat room. While coming out carrying one meat bag on their left shoulder they did not notice a group of stacked steel pallets and accidently hit their left leg against the corner of a pallet sustaining a left leg abrasion. They requested medical care and were declared unfit for duty.

A ro-ro cargo vessel crew member was reversing a forklift down the fixed internal ramp between weather deck and main deck transferring a trestle. They noticed a crew member walking up the ramp. The crew member in the forklift adjusted the steering to give more clearance and applied the brakes to reduce speed further. At this point the fork lift sheared to the to the left then toppled over. The tre
Container ship's crew member suffered a hip and leg fracture after being crushed in between containers. The three containers were locked together and swung trapping the crew member.

A container ship crewmember was injured while trying to stow the ship's accommodation ladder. While standing on the quay, a shore-side forklift was assisting in lifting the bottom of the accommodation ladder clear of an obstruction when it became snagged. The crewmember was trying to see what was snagging the ladder when it broke free and hit them in the jaw. They were taken to hospital and underw

A crew transfer vessel was carrying out a lifting operation whilst alongside. A crew member was struck in the head by either the load or the strop bearing the load. They sustained a concussion and were advised to take 4 to 6 days off work.

A ro-pax vessel crew was loading stores from a lorry that was parked on board in the vehicle decks. The stores were in wheeled trollies that the crew used to move the stores to and from the lorry. The lorry was fitted with a rear lifting platform and whilst loading the empty trollies back onto the lorry, the driver pulled one of the trollies causing another trolley to roll off the lifting platform
A gangway dropped on crewmember's head while being craned ashore using ship's gear. They were taken to hospital for an operation.

A lifeboat crewmember was stowing a warp and chain from deck into stowage below deck through a spurling pipe. The rope fouled with a shackle coming through. As the chain was being guided down, the shackle fell from an upright position on to casualty's head.

A stevedore was undertaking discharge operations in the vessel's hold where one pallet of MDF (Medium Density Fibreboard) had been lifted by a forklift for rigging the sling and the MDF ended up sliding down the forklift forks. The MDF fell down and hit them on their leg.

title: Dynamic Sea Conditions: RIB Occupant Injury from Wave Impact
description:
This theme is characterised by occupant injuries occurring aboard rigid inflatable boats (RIBs) and pilot vessels operating in dynamic sea conditions, where the combination of vessel speed, sea state, and passenger behaviour converges to produce foreseeable spinal and musculoskeletal trauma. The unifying latent cause across these incidents is a systemic failure in operational risk management: the absence of formalised, enforced protocols governing speed and course management relative to sea state, passenger seating allocation in exposed forward positions, and pre-departure safety briefings covering bracing posture and wave impact awareness. These incidents recur because the hazard — wave-induced vertical acceleration applied to an unprepared or biomechanically vulnerable occupant — is well understood yet insufficiently mitigated at the procedural and supervisory level. Operators persistently underestimate the injury potential of moderate sea states, particularly when night-time conditions remove the visual cues that would otherwise allow occupants and helmsmen to anticipate wave strikes.

Latent hardware factors, including suspension seats not adjusted for occupant weight and RIB bow geometries susceptible to stuffing in following seas, reduce the vessel's intrinsic capacity to absorb or deflect wave energy before it reaches occupants. Human factors are dominant, encompassing helmsman speed judgement in following seas, failure by crew to intervene when passengers adopt unsafe postures, and inadequate situational awareness among passengers unfamiliar with vessel motion hazards. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time operations in sea states of Slight to Rough (0.5–4.0 m) — systematically degrade both the helmsman's ability to anticipate wave sets and the passenger's capacity to brace in advance of impact. The interaction of these factors creates a high-consequence injury environment that is disproportionate to the perceived severity of the conditions, making robust procedural controls and active crew oversight the critical preventive layers.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safety briefings, passenger protection, and carriage requirements for survival and protective equipment)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe speed, voyage planning, and navigational watchkeeping obligations relative to prevailing sea conditions)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for documented operational procedures, risk assessment, and crew training)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce a structured pre-departure passenger safety briefing for all RIB and small craft operations, covering optimal seating position, mandatory use of handholds, correct bracing posture during wave impacts, and prohibition of activities such as note-taking or use of handheld equipment while underway in open or exposed waters.

Implement and enforce a documented speed and course management protocol specific to following sea and night-time conditions, requiring coxswains to reduce speed to a level at which the vessel cannot overtake wave crests and to conduct a dynamic risk assessment before and during any passage where sea state or visibility deteriorates, with clear authority to abort or divert.

Introduce a mandatory pre-departure equipment check for all suspension-seated vessels requiring coxswains to verify and record correct suspension seat weight adjustment for each occupant prior to departure, and establish a seating allocation policy that prohibits passengers — particularly those unfamiliar with vessel motion — from occupying exposed forward seats in sea states above Calm (0–0.1 m) unless adequately briefed and physically secured.

representative_cases.description: A passenger rib was involved in an incident where, making passage in following seas, the bow of the vessel stuffed into a wave in front of it, causing a large amount of water to break over the boat.  The force of that water was sufficient to cause an upper arm injury to a passenger, seated in the front port seat of the vessel.  Immediately after the incident the second crew member, the co-pilot qu

A recreational vessels passenger injured their back due to the vessel hitting a wave. Came down off a wave and the passenger complained that they had hurt their back . It was concluded that this was most likely caused by the fact that the passenger was bent over taking notes and not prepared to anticipate the waves.

A pilot boat was outbound to a vessel in waves between 2.5 to 2.5 metres in height. When passing over a wave, the boat moved downwards rapidly causing a pilot who was sitting in a suspension seat to jolt their back. The vessel was reportedly travelling at excessive speed for the weather conditions and the suspension seat was not adjusted for the weight of the seated person. The pilot was signed of

A passenger on a RIB (rigid inflatable boat) ride sustained a crushed coccyx and L1 vertebrae. The vessel was travelling over a wave and crashed down on the other side of it causing the passengers to be lifted from their seats. The view of the sea conditions from the area where the injured passenger was sitting was restricted which may have contributed to the injury because they could not anticipa

A passenger onboard a commercial RIB (rigid inflatable boat) that had left the port were not given any advice in which section of the rib would be the best place to sit. So, they decided to sit in the front row seats. When the vessel left port, it encountered large waves at high speeds so they decided that they should move to the second row of seats instead. After hitting more large waves they had

Small high speed cruise vessel was hit by a large wave which caused the vessel to land heavily behind the wave. This resulted in 2 passenger injuries whilst they were seated in the cabin. One passenger sustained a back injury and the other suffered with a bruised ribs after impacting the table. The vessel owners are looking at installing suspension seats to mitigate this kind of incident.

A RIB was on a sight seeing trip with six passengers and the helm.  The passengers had received a safety briefing before departure, donned weatherproof clothing and lifejackets and commenced the trip. Whilst on passage the helm stopped twice to provide information to the passengers on holding the bar in front and using their legs to brace and to check passenger wellbeing. The RIB was travelling ap

A passenger on board a RIB (rigid inflatable boat) sustained a dislocated shoulder and was evacuated by search and rescue services to a shoreside hospital. The RIB was taking part in a training day and a student was at the helm with an instructor behind them. The nose of the vessel dropped into the trough of a wave causing spray and the vessel to decelerate rapidly. The injured crewmember was thro

Two passengers in front seats of the RIB sustained injuries after the RIB caught a wave from a passing container vessel. The RIB ride was a sight seeing tour with a usual speed 20-22 knots however, it was reduced when noticed that the three waves were approaching. The casualties were not looking ahead but were distracted by the sightseeing tour which resulted in two falls. Both casualties were tak

A highspeed-RIB passenger had a back injury when the craft left the top of a wave and came back down to the surface. The impact with the water caused the passenger to be thrown from their position and struck a seat on the craft.

title: Cargo Securing Failure Under Adverse Weather Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by the systematic failure of cargo securing arrangements when vessels encounter adverse sea states, manifesting across ro-ro, general cargo, and offshore supply vessel types. A consistent pattern emerges in which responsibility for securing is either delegated entirely to third parties such as hauliers, or assessed against inadequate benchmarks that fail to account for the dynamic forces generated by rolling in moderate-to-rough sea conditions. The recurring nature of these incidents points to a structural weakness in pre-departure risk assessment protocols: securing arrangements are routinely accepted without independent officer verification against the vessel's Cargo Securing Manual, and the unique stability characteristics of high-centre-of-gravity or irregular loads — road cranes, tracked stone crushers, concrete blocks, and deck timber — are not adequately factored into lashing calculations. Night-time operations compound the risk by delaying detection of cargo shift, narrowing the window for corrective intervention before secondary damage or loss occurs.

The latent conditions underpinning these incidents span all three contributing domains. Hardware deficiencies — undersized, worn, or improperly rigged lashing equipment, failed twist locks, and inadequate dunnage — represent a persistent vulnerability that is not identified before departure because procedural safeguards are either absent or not enforced. Human factors include over-reliance on the expertise of external parties, insufficient master and officer familiarity with the Cargo Securing Manual requirements for specialist loads, and failure to proactively reduce speed or alter course when deteriorating weather is encountered. The environmental trigger — whether Sea State 5 rolling, a succession of moderate rolls, or confused swell generating unexpected deck loading — consistently serves as the activating condition for these latent failures. Taken together, the evidence indicates that these incidents are not primarily caused by unforeseeable weather events but by a systemic failure to establish securing arrangements robust enough to withstand the sea conditions that are reasonably foreseeable on the intended passage.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter VI – Carriage of Cargoes and Oil Fuels (Part A: General provisions on the carriage of cargoes, including cargo securing and the requirement for a Cargo Securing Manual)
SOLAS Chapter VII – Carriage of Dangerous Goods (relevant where cargo type intersects with hazardous material stowage and securing obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XII – Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers (applicable where deck cargo and structural loading considerations arise in the context of cargo shift and vessel stability)
preventable_by: Mandatory independent verification by a certificated ship's officer of all cargo securing arrangements against the vessel's approved Cargo Securing Manual prior to departure, with explicit sign-off required for high-centre-of-gravity, abnormal, or specialist road transport loads — precluding sole reliance on haulier-provided securing without officer confirmation of adequacy.

Implementation of a dynamic weather-responsive cargo monitoring protocol requiring defined watchkeeping rounds at specified roll or sea state thresholds during adverse passages, with pre-established master's standing orders to reduce speed, alter course, or seek shelter when conditions approach the design limits of the cargo securing arrangement.

Pre-loading risk assessment process that requires calculation or estimation of dynamic lashing forces for each non-standard cargo unit based on vessel-specific GM, anticipated sea state for the passage, and load geometry — with results documented on a cargo securing declaration signed by both the responsible officer and the haulier or cargo owner before the vessel sails.

representative_cases.description: A ro-ro cargo vessel was on passage when the vessel during heavy weather conditions. The vessel took a roll to starboard recorded at fifteen degrees and twelve degrees to port on the bridge inclinometer. During a low loader trailer loaded with a road crane shifted on the trailer parting the hauliers securing arrangements and fell onto an adjacent trailer. This resulted in damage to both vehicles.

Cargo vessel was proceeding on passage when it took a succession of moderate rolls causing the lashing that was holding the concrete blocks to fail. When it was noticed that the blocks spilled onto the deck, the vessel's speed was immediately reduced and the movements were minimised. Once the crew secured the blocks in situ, the vessel proceeded on passage. However, it has been reported that there

An offshore supply vessel took on water resulting in a cargo shift that damaged two windows. Vessel took water over the bow due to weather conditions. Cargo shifted causing a twist lock to come free that subsequently hit two windows causing damage.

A ro-pax vessel was on passage when the vessel took a roll to port recorded at ten degrees on the bridge inclinometer but held this angle of heel for a moment as the autopilot corrected the heading. During this heel a low loader trailer loaded with a tracked stone crusher slid off the trailer parting the hauliers securing arrangements. The vehicle landed against adjacent vehicles resulting in four

A general cargo vessel experienced cargo shift, resulting in approximately 160 cubic meters of untreated forest logs falling overboard from where they were stowed on deck. The vessel sought port of refuge to secure the remaining deck cargo again. Search and rescue planes were unable to locate the logs that had gone over board, but continued to monitor the coast for washed up cargo. There was no da

An empty plastic barrel of approximately 200l has been washed from the deck. Item has been securely stowed and checked by crew before the adverse weather but wave action and wind intensity managed to break it free and to be lost at sea.  The mentioned barrel has not been carrying any contents whatsoever and closed tightly but may contribute to marine litter or become a navigational hazard to small

A general cargo vessel had cargo fall overboard from the deck during heavy weather. The vessel was carrying timber and woodchips and some logs about 3.5m in length fell overboard. There were no injuries or damage, and the logs were monitored by the coastguard because they presented a possible hazard to navigation.
A cargo vessel experienced rough seas and the deck cargo shifted. However, the vessel made it safely to harbour.

A container vessel alongside had two containers fall overboard from on deck into a harbour during loading. The containers were partially submerged, later located and recovered. Both containers were empty.

Conditions were a very rough sea and swell, the ship was rolling moderately within 10 degrees to both sides. The ship suddenly started rolling 13-15 degrees to both side and then the duty officer on bridge heard one abnormal sound on ship's starboard side. They went to the bridgewing to look and found some containers had collapsed overboard. The crew mustered and assessed the damage, also adjustin

title: Tow Line Failures Under Emergency and Dynamic Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by repeated tow line parting events occurring across a range of vessel types and operational contexts, united by a common pattern of under-resourced or procedurally deficient towage arrangements initiated under time pressure or in response to primary propulsion failures. The incidents consistently reveal that emergency towage is being improvised rather than executed against a pre-established framework: informal towing arrangements are made with vessels not equipped or rated for the prevailing conditions, tow lines are deployed without documented assessment of safe working load relative to vessel displacement and sea state, and dynamic load management during manoeuvring or in deteriorating weather is not adequately anticipated. The recurrence of this pattern across both commercial and SAR contexts suggests that the problem is systemic rather than isolated, rooted in an industry-wide absence of standardised emergency towing procedures and equipment readiness requirements for non-specialist vessels undertaking towage roles.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a primary contributory factor, with tow lines showing evidence of wear, fatigue, or inadequate rated strength that is not identified prior to use due to absent or insufficient inspection and maintenance regimes. These hardware vulnerabilities are compounded by adverse environmental conditions — including rough sea states, wind-generated lateral loading, and night-time operations that reduce visibility and impair real-time monitoring of tow line tension and geometry. Human factors, particularly risk acceptance bias under time-critical pressure, further erode decision quality during the critical rigging and setup phase: urgency to prevent grounding or collision compresses the planning window, leading crews to accept improvised configurations that would not withstand scrutiny under non-emergency conditions. The convergence of degraded hardware, environmental loading, and compressed human decision-making under stress creates conditions in which tow line failure is a foreseeable and frequently realised outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 33: Distress situations and obligations to render assistance; Regulation 34: Safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction, Subdivision and Stability (Part G, Regulation 3-4: Emergency towing arrangements on tankers and relevant provisions for shipboard emergency equipment readiness)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for onboard emergency equipment condition, maintenance, and crew preparedness for emergency response operations)
preventable_by: Mandate the development and onboard retention of a formal Emergency Towing Procedure for all commercial vessels and SAR craft, specifying minimum tow line rated strength relative to vessel displacement, approved attachment points, pre-deployment inspection criteria, and dynamic load management protocols for varying sea states — ensuring that emergency towage is executed against a documented standard rather than improvised under operational pressure.

Implement a scheduled inspection and certification regime for all tow lines and associated towing equipment, requiring evidence of periodic load testing, condition assessment, and replacement at defined service intervals; inspection records should be retained onboard and made available during port state control examinations to create accountability for equipment readiness.

Establish minimum competency and equipment standards for any vessel undertaking an emergency towing role, including mandatory pre-tow radio coordination between the towing vessel master and the vessel master or harbour authority to confirm tow line suitability, sea state assessment, and the availability of a certified tug as backup — thereby preventing reliance on informal arrangements with vessels and equipment unsuited to the prevailing conditions.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel had an engine failure, an arrangement with a nearby fishing vessel was made to tow the stricken vessel to shore. Due to deteriorating weather conditions the tow line broke three times on the passage, a tug took over the tow to bring the fishing vessel alongside. No reported crew injuries.

Tow line parted between bulk carrier and tug whilst manoeuvring. No injuries were sustained and the tug crew managed to get another tow line to the bulk carrier which was brought safely into the lock.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was carrying out towage for a pleasure craft that had experienced propulsion failure. The vessel was drifting towards a busy harbour entrance and ferry terminal therefore the crew of the SAR vessel deemed it necessary for safety to tow the stricken vessel clear. During the towage, the towline came under strain and caused the two vessels to shift, causing them to co

A tug was assisting a general cargo vessel into a lock when it parted its towing line. The tug was acting as the stern tug at the time of the incident. No damages, injuries or pollution were reported, beyond the parted towing line.

A disabled fishing vessel was being towed back to harbour by another fishing vessel when the towline parted. The fishing vessel started to drift towards a breakwater where there was a significant risk of grounding. A search and rescue vessel was deployed and towed the vessel back to harbour.

An tug was involved in towing a vessel during approach to the berth when it almost girted, taking some water over the side resulting in damage to some of the equipment. The vessel crew reported it was due to a failure of the emergency release for the towing hook. After the incident, the vessel's crew were able to let go from the vessel and return to the berth.

The aft tug was connected centre lead aft on the line using a 30 metres fixed line. Prior to swinging in the turning circle, the tug peeled off the port quarter. The vessel swung to starboard with the tug keeping only a tight line. The tug then reported that the tow line came off the hook (on the tugs side) as a result of the locking mechanism not being set properly after a release test, cleaning

When preparing to lift a vessel off the berth, a tug boat experienced a loud bang as tension came onto its towing line. The tug crew reported that the line was buried but they were still able to continue with the unberthing manoeuvre. The tug was taken out of service.

A tug was assisting a bulk carrier to a berth. The tug was connected up to the centre lead aft. The pilot asked for 50% dead astern on the tug and this request was adhered to. After about eight minutes the tow parted. The spare tow rope was reconnected to the bulk carrier to complete the berthing operations.

A tug was requested to tow an LPG (liquified petroleum gas) tanker had a propulsion failure shortly after departing port and was at anchor. When the tug connected the aft tow line to the tanker and proceeded to tow, the main tow wire parted in the heavy weather and swell. The tanker anchored with the tug standing by but the tug then proceeded for shelter due to worsening conditions. There was no d

title: Vessel Encroachment on Active Dive Operations at Night
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated failure of third-party vessels — predominantly fishing vessels and sailing yachts — to recognise, respect, or comply with internationally recognised dive operation signals and verbal warnings, resulting in near-miss and fatal incidents involving divers in the water. Across all representative incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: dive support vessels and dive personnel correctly signalled their operational status, yet approaching vessels either failed to identify those signals, chose to disregard them, or lacked the foundational knowledge to interpret their meaning. The recurrence of these incidents is attributable not to isolated individual error, but to a systemic absence of enforceable safety governance at the local harbour and dive site level, an absence of mandatory mariner education regarding dive flag and Alpha flag protocols, and the structural vulnerability of dive operations conducted in shared navigable waters without regulated exclusion zones or escalation mechanisms.

The role of latent factors in perpetuating this risk profile is significant. Hardware and signalling deficiencies — including uncertain deployment of the Alpha flag, Day Shape Ball, and adequate night lighting on dive support vessels — reduce the conspicuity of dive operations to approaching mariners, particularly under night-time conditions where visibility of float markers, diver surface markers, and regulatory signals is materially degraded. Human factors dominate the causal picture: wilful non-compliance, failure to maintain a proper lookout as required under COLREGS Rule 5, and inadequate situational awareness aboard approaching vessels are recurring antecedents. Environmental conditions, including wind speeds between 17 and 28 kph and sea states producing wave heights up to 1.28 metres, compounded manoeuvrability constraints and reduced signal detection probability. Critically, the absence of any authority mechanism or formal traffic management protocol means that even when warnings are issued in real time, there is no effective intervention pathway available to dive support personnel, leaving divers wholly dependent on the voluntary compliance of other vessel operators.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (particularly Regulations 10, 19, and 22 relating to watchkeeping, navigational equipment, and visibility of signals)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (relating to passenger and diver tracking, emergency preparedness, and vessel coding compliance for dive support vessels)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (relating to the use of VHF radio for broadcast warnings, vessel traffic coordination, and failure to establish communication with approaching vessels)
preventable_by: Establish mandatory enforced exclusion zones around active dive sites in harbour approaches and coastal waters, underpinned by port authority or harbour master directives with a defined escalation pathway — including VHF broadcast warnings and coast guard notification — that can be activated by dive support vessel crew when approaching vessels fail to comply with verbal or signal-based warnings.

Require all commercially operated and coded dive support vessels to carry and correctly display a standardised night-dive signalling package, including an illuminated Alpha flag, 360-degree white all-round light, and reflective diver surface marker buoys, with compliance verified at vessel coding inspection; mandate pre-dive VHF safety broadcasts on the working channel to alert all vessels in the vicinity of the commencement and duration of dive operations.

Introduce compulsory dive operation awareness training as a component of commercial fishing vessel certification and recreational coastal skipper qualifications, covering the legal obligations imposed by COLREGS Rule 18 regarding vessels restricted in their ability to manoeuvre, the meaning and required response to the Alpha flag and dive flag, and the criminal and civil liability consequences of non-compliance when divers are known to be in the water.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel had a near miss whilst divers were in the water. A dive flag was up at the harbour entrance to alert vessels that divers were in the water, a fishing vessel came alongside the diving pontoon. Despite being told to move due to the close proximity to the divers, the crew continued with their operation.

Near miss was reported between a dive vessel and a sailing vessel. Sailing vessel manoeuvred dangerously to the dive vessel and endangered the divers which were in the water at the time. No injuries/damages were reported.
An uncoded recreational dive vessels passenger went missing presumed fatal.

A dive support vessel was operating with two divers in the water with a float marker visible. A fishing vessel approached the area and drifted with the engines running to collect fishing nets. The crew of the dive vessel advised the fishing vessel that they were close to the divers, however the fishing vessel drifted over the transect buoys. The divers were not below that part of the transect at t

A sailing yacht with a three metre long keel passed over some divers who were four metres below the surface. No divers were injured and no contact occurred. The crew of the dive vessel tried to call the sailing yacht to tell them what had happened but they were unable to get in contact.

Vessel was waiting to unload there catch at the landing davits, they manoeuvred behind the current boat being unloaded at the Davit, crew decided to unload by hand and then move off the harbour area. Rather than radio port control on diver movements (which were in operation as per Alpha Flag present) the vessel moved along the front of the pontoon about 3-4mtrs away, If a diver was coming out of t

A pleasure vessel narrowly missed contacting commercial divers in a port area. The pleasure vessel passed close enough to cause interaction with the divers. The crew of the recreational craft were warned not to come in close to the divers, but didn't heed the warnings. After the incident, the harbour master intercepted the pleasure vessel and warned them about the incident. There were no injuries,
A racing motorboat passed close to a dive vessel.

A training vessel was entering a cove when they narrowly missed striking a submerged spear fisher. The fisher had a marker buoy but the crew misidentified them as a lobsterpot. Fortunately, the swimmer surfaced and the crew took evasive action in time to prevent hitting them.
Service vessel near miss with diver under water.

title: Door Entrapment Injuries from Uncontrolled Closure in Dynamic Conditions
description: This theme is characterised by finger and hand entrapment injuries occurring when passengers or crew pass through or operate doors aboard vessels, with the door closing unexpectedly and forcefully due to vessel motion, mechanical stiffness, or poor hand placement. The incidents span offshore supply vessels, cruise ships, and fishing vessels, indicating the hazard is not confined to any single vessel type or operational context. A consistent pattern emerges: individuals place their hand on a door frame, door edge, or sliding door mechanism rather than the designated handle, and the door closes under dynamic forces before the hand can be withdrawn. The recurrence of these incidents across varied vessel categories and sea states suggests that the hazard is systemic rather than isolated, rooted in inadequate risk controls at the design, maintenance, and operational safety communication levels.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (requirements for watertight and weathertight doors, including mechanical integrity and operability under dynamic conditions)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for passenger and crew safety briefings, muster drills, and communication of onboard hazards prior to departure)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for hazard identification, risk assessment, preventive maintenance, and safety management system implementation)
preventable_by: Retrofit all internal and external passenger and crew access doors with approved motion-compensating dampers, hydraulic retarders, or hold-open retaining devices rated for the vessel's operational sea state range, and establish a scheduled preventive maintenance regime to verify their correct function before each voyage, with defective doors taken out of service until rectified.

Mandate pre-departure safety briefings for all passengers and crew that specifically address the hazard of uncontrolled door movement in a dynamic sea environment, including explicit instruction to use door handles rather than frames or edges, supplemented by permanent, clearly visible pictogram-based warning signage affixed to all pinch-zone door frames and sliding door mechanisms throughout the vessel.

Integrate door-entrapment risk into the vessel's ISM-compliant Safety Management System by conducting formal risk assessments of all door types aboard, establishing a passenger and crew defect-reporting pathway for stiff or faulty doors, and requiring Masters to log and rectify reported door deficiencies before the vessel sails, with findings reviewed during periodic internal and flag-state audits.

representative_cases.description: An offshore supply vessel passenger was passing through a door when the door closed and trapped the passenger's finger between the door and the frame. As a result of the incident, the passenger sustained a severe laceration to the tip of one of their fingers that required medical treatment at a shore side hospital. The incident occurred because the passenger put their hand on the frame which is no

Cruise ship's passenger was exiting their washroom within their cabin when the ship moved and the door closed on the casualties thumb. They sustained a fractured thumb however, remained on the cruise.

Cruise ship's passenger was attempting to open their balcony sliding door when resistance was noticed in the door. They forced their wrist which resulted in a closed fracture. The area has been inspected and the sliding door was found stiff to open however, the issue has been resolved.

A fishing vessel crewmember trapped their hand in a watertight door. They were opening the door to go onto the working deck when the vessel rolled back and the door swung shut onto their hand resulting in a fractured finger.

A cruise ships passenger caught hand in door frame resulting in closed fracture to a finger. leaving the bathroom in the cabin and when exiting using door frame to hold themself the door suddenly closed by itself.

A ro-ro passenger vessel crewmember hit their head on the upper door frame as they exited a walk-in freezer. The casualty did not report the incident at the time but later experienced headaches and vomiting. They were diagnosed with concussion and signed off work.

A cruise ship passenger was standing in the bathroom of their cabin near the bathroom door when the door moved and struck their left torso, causing a fractured rib. The incident was reportedly due to the movement of the vessel.

A service vessel was on passage, when a crew member was transiting between bathroom and cabin, the vessel took a heavy roll The crew member braced themselves against the door frame, the door came free of its hold back resulting in the door crushing the crew members thumb. The crew member was treated onboard and later re-examined once ashore.

A cruise ship passenger was leaving the restaurant area, they were passing through a set of sliding doors when the passenger stopped between the doors. The doors started to close causing the passenger to fall and fracture their right wrist. The passenger was escorted to the medical centre for treatment. The passenger remained onboard.

A cruise ship passenger was knocked over by an automatic door and sustained a fractured wrist. They were trying to access a disabled toilet, with a powered door that opened outwards. The passenger operated the door activation button while standing close to the door and the door struck them as it opened causing them to fall and break their wrist. A crew member assisted them to the ship's medical ce

title: Slip, Trip and Fall Injuries During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by a high frequency of slip, trip, and fall incidents occurring predominantly during night-time or reduced-visibility conditions across a range of vessel types, including passenger ferries, cargo vessels, hire craft, and ro-ro vessels. The incidents span multiple operational phases — underway deck transit, mooring, disembarkation, and vessel-to-vessel transfer — yet share a consistent injury aetiology: surface hazards, vessel motion, and reduced ambient lighting combining to produce falls resulting in fractures, sprains, and trauma requiring medical disembarkation or helicopter evacuation. The persistence of this pattern across vessel categories and operating environments indicates systemic failures in onboard safety culture, hazard identification programmes, and PPE enforcement rather than isolated or vessel-specific breakdowns. Notably, environmental conditions were benign or moderate in the majority of incidents, confirming that adverse weather is not a prerequisite for serious injury and that procedural and ergonomic deficiencies are the primary causal drivers.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including inadequate deck lighting, absence of anti-slip surface treatments, unmarked fixed deck hazards such as lashing points, and insufficient handrails or physical safety aids at boarding interfaces — create the conditions in which human error becomes consequential. Human factors, particularly reduced situational awareness during night operations, fatigue, unfamiliarity with vessel geometry among passengers and hire craft occupants, and non-compliance with PPE requirements including non-slip footwear, systematically elevate individual exposure to these latent hazards. Organisational factors compound the risk: absent or inadequate pre-departure safety briefings, lack of structured disembarkation protocols, insufficient crew supervision of passenger movement, and failure to implement mandatory wet surface reporting procedures all represent gaps in the safety management layer. Vessel motion, even at low to moderate sea states, acts as a persistent environmental amplifier that transforms otherwise manageable hazards into injury events, underscoring the need for dynamic risk assessment at all phases of operation.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew and passenger safety procedures, drills, and safety briefings)
SOLAS Chapter V — Safety of Navigation (safe manning, risk assessment, and operational safety measures including transfer operations)
SOLAS Chapter IX — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code requirements for hazard identification, procedural controls, and safety culture)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory fleet-wide night operations deck safety audits to identify and remediate latent hardware deficiencies, including installation of adequate task lighting at all crew and passenger transit areas, anti-slip deck surface treatments, high-visibility marking of all fixed deck hazards such as lashing points and raised fittings, and certified handrails at all boarding and disembarkation interfaces.

Establish and enforce structured Safe Movement Protocols for all high-risk operational phases — including disembarkation, vessel-to-vessel transfer, and mooring — requiring full vessel securement before passenger movement, mandatory crew escort and supervision of passengers during night operations, compulsory pre-departure safety briefings for all hire and charter vessel occupants, and documented PPE compliance checks including non-slip footwear verification for crew prior to deck duties.

Integrate a Wet Surface and Deck Hazard Reporting System into the vessel Safety Management System, requiring immediate hazard marking and reporting of all wet, contaminated, or obstructed walking surfaces, with mandatory follow-up housekeeping action and sign-off, supported by regular night-specific safety drills to build crew situational awareness, three-point contact discipline, and dynamic risk assessment behaviours during low-visibility operations.

representative_cases.description: Inland passenger vessel's crew member slipped due to wet floor and sustained multiple sprained injuries.
A cargo vessel crewmember fell on the main deck and was assisted by other crew. The casualty complained of pain in multiple areas and was later evacuated by helicopter for medical assessment.
A hire broads vessel passenger fell, resulting in an open fracture to the ankle.
A passenger ship crew member was walking along a corridor when they slipped, as a result of wet flooring. They sustained a fracture to their right hand and were medically disembarked.
Crew member was stepping from a port tender to a dredger when they missed their footing and landed on the side of their ankle. This resulted in a closed fracture.
A general cargo vessel crewmember broke their leg.
A person from an inland waterway hire cruiser fell from the vessel onto the wooden jetty and injured their leg. The casualty was assessed by paramedics and transferred to an ambulance.
Passenger ferry crew member tripped on a raised lashing point on the main deck resulting in closed fractures to the ankle.
A ro-ro passenger ferry crewmember fell on deck resulting in a knee injury. They were transferred into the care of the ambulance service on arrival into port.
A passenger was stepping off a hire vessel when it was mooring when they fell and landed awkwardly. The passenger was diagnosed with a fractured leg.

title: Unassisted Manoeuvring in Constrained Waters Causing Grounding
description:
This theme is characterised by vessels grounding during departure, transit, or berthing manoeuvres in constrained or shallow waterways — canals, dock approaches, and berth faces — where lateral movement control is critical and margins for error are narrow. Across these incidents, a recurring pattern emerges: vessels attempt complex manoeuvres, often at night and in moderate wind conditions, without adequate tug assistance or with insufficient mooring restraint to counter environmental forces. The incidents keep occurring because risk-based tug-assistance thresholds are either absent from port or company procedures, or present but not enforced. Masters and pilots are afforded discretion in conditions where procedural safeguards should instead mandate escalation, and waiting or approach procedures fail to account for the vessel's vulnerability to even moderate lateral forces when stationary or at slow speed in confined waterways.

Environmental conditions — particularly wind speeds in the 13–30 knot range acting on hull and superstructure — are consistent contributors, generating lateral drift or stern swing that engine power alone cannot adequately counteract at slow manoeuvring speeds. Night-time operations compound the hazard by degrading visual reference quality for assessing proximity to canal edges, pier structures, and shallow water boundaries. From a hardware and procedural perspective, mooring arrangements at waiting dolphins and berth faces are in several cases insufficient to restrain vessel movement under wind loading, and pre-positioned tug support is absent, requiring reactive mobilisation after the incident has already developed. Human factors manifest primarily in failures of risk reassessment — masters and pilots proceeding despite marginal conditions, not revising the approach strategy after initial contact, and not adequately monitoring vessel position while stationary. Together, these latent procedural gaps and compounding environmental and human factors create the conditions in which grounding becomes a foreseeable outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 34-1: safe navigation, passage planning, and operational limitations)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: risk assessment, shipboard procedures, and company safety management obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code Part A – Safe Manning and Master's Discretion vs. Procedural Override Requirements
preventable_by: Establish and enforce risk-based tug-assistance requirements within port and company Safety Management Systems, specifying mandatory tug support thresholds for vessel size, wind speed, time of day, and waterway constraints — removing reliance on master or pilot discretion alone in identified high-risk manoeuvring scenarios.

Require formal pre-manoeuvre risk assessments for all departures, arrivals, and transits in constrained waterways, including documented go/no-go criteria that account for wind speed, tidal state, visibility, and under-keel clearance, with escalation procedures when any threshold is exceeded before or during the manoeuvre.

Implement mandatory waiting and stationary vessel procedures for canals and confined waterways that include pre-positioned tug standby (rather than reactive mobilisation), minimum mooring line configurations verified against prevailing wind and current loads, and continuous positional monitoring with defined action triggers if lateral movement is detected.

representative_cases.description: While general cargo was departing the dock, it ran aground. The vessel was lying against the jetty walkway when it continued its passage without tug assistance. No damage/pollution/injuries reported.

A general cargo vessel was outbound in a canal with a pilot on board when it partially grounded. The vessel was moored at a waiting dolphin, waiting for a bridge to lift in order for it to pass, when the stern began to swing out and touched the canal bottom. A tug was mobilised to assist but was not necessary as the vessel re-floated shortly after and was able to proceed to the berth that it left

A cargo vessel began it's approach to berth, it was positioned approximately 30 meters inside the berth, and it attempted to approach closer to send the mooring ropes ashore. It was noted that the vessel began to drift east, despite efforts to counteract with the engine, the vessel grounded on the east side of the pier. The vessel later refloated on the flood tide. The cargo vessel made three furt

Cargo ship grounded while departing dock for approximately 20 minutes. The vessel did not use forward spring when bending around the bullnose. The vessel was able to free itself and no damage was reported.

A general cargo vessel grounded when on approach to a berth. The vessel was maneuvering under the advice of a pilot and with tug assistance. A tug was made fast using the tugs line on the aft of the general cargo vessel. The tug started to assist the vessel in a swing against the prevailing current, during which the tug's line parted and the vessel started to drift into shallow water. The crew of

A tug was berthing a  ro-ro cargo vessel as the vessel's stern veered away from the berth this forced the tug to run aground on its starboard quarter. Engines were stopped and the ro-ro cargo vessel was also informed, crew engaged the emergency quick release as the tow gear was coming under tension and the tug started to list to port. The crew checked the engine room and steering gear compartment,

A general cargo vessel was preparing to berth, as the vessel turned to starboard off the berth, the combination of wind and tide risked bringing the cargo vessel down hard onto the quayside. The pilot aborted the berthing and was heading back into the channel when the vessel grounded. Tug assistance was arranged, the cargo vessel later floated free and berthed with no further incident. No vessel d

A ro-pax vessel was pushed broad side against a slip way by strong winds and grounded. There was no pollution, injuries or damage reported. The local lifeboat service assisted the vessel off the slipway and it was able to continue the rest of its daily operations without further incident.

Whilst an oil tanker was manoeuvring astern towards its berth, the bow sheered to port resulting in grounding. The vessel remained safely aground with the stern afloat and propeller and rudder clear. Tug assistance was used to get the vessel alongside with no further incident. The vessel was undamaged.
A general cargo vessel was shifting berth when the vessel was blown onto the side of the channel resulting in it grounding. The vessel re-floated and was taken to a layby berth for damage inspection.

title: Fishing Vessel Propulsion Loss Due to Gearbox Failure
description:
This theme is characterised by repeated loss of propulsion events aboard small commercial fishing vessels, uniformly attributable to mechanical failure of the gearbox drivetrain. Across all incidents in this cluster, the gearbox is the singular point of failure, resulting in total loss of motive power and dependence on external tow or lifeboat escort. No incidents report crew injury or vessel damage, suggesting that emergency response protocols are generally adequate; however, the consistency and frequency of this failure mode across the fishing fleet is indicative of a systemic, fleet-wide vulnerability rooted in reactive rather than preventive maintenance culture. Operational pressures inherent to commercial fishing — including extended sea time, trawling loads, and limited downtime for servicing — appear to chronically suppress adherence to structured maintenance regimes, allowing latent mechanical degradation to progress undetected until catastrophic failure occurs. Several incidents involve gearbox lubrication failure specifically, reinforcing that even basic preventive maintenance tasks are being deferred or neglected.

The latent hardware dimension of this theme centres on ageing drivetrain components subjected to sustained mechanical stress without adequate inspection or servicing intervals, rendering gearbox wear and lubrication degradation the dominant failure pathway. Human factors are principally manifest in the failure to implement or comply with Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS), inadequate pre-departure engineering checks, and a probable absence of crew training in early-warning symptom recognition such as anomalous noise, vibration, or oil pressure fluctuations. Environmental conditions — predominantly night-time operations, moderate to heavy sea states, and elevated winds — are not causative but consistently elevate the operational risk profile during the tow recovery phase and may delay crew detection of early failure indicators. The combination of single-gearbox vessel design with no propulsion redundancy, coupled with deficient maintenance culture, creates a highly predictable and preventable failure pattern that warrants targeted regulatory and industry intervention.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E – Additional requirements for machinery, boiler and electrical installations, specifically relating to machinery maintenance and reliability)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (relating to emergency preparedness and response to loss of propulsion requiring external rescue or tow)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring implementation of Planned Maintenance Systems, safety management procedures, and deficiency reporting)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce structured Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS) for all critical drivetrain components, including gearboxes, with documented service intervals, oil pressure checks, and lubrication inspections that must be completed and recorded prior to each voyage departure — with flag state or port state control verification during inspections.

Implement mandatory pre-departure engineering checklists specifically covering gearbox oil pressure, fluid levels, temperature baselines, and audible/tactile indicators of bearing or gear wear, supported by crew competency training to recognise early-warning signs of gearbox degradation before failure becomes irreversible.

Establish fleet-level gearbox failure reporting and trend analysis through the Maritime and Coastguard Agency or relevant flag state authority, enabling identification of high-risk vessel classes, gearbox models, and operational profiles, with targeted advisory notices and mandatory equipment replacement thresholds issued to owners of vessels with ageing or repeatedly failing drivetrain components.

representative_cases.description: Fishing vessel lost propulsion as a result of suspected gear box drive issue and was towed back to port
Beam trawler's gear box failed resulting in a tow to harbour from another fishing vessel. There was no damage or injuries and there was never any imminent danger.

A fishing vessel with six people onboard suffered gear box failure. A search and rescue vessel was deployed and towed the vessel back to harbour. No injuries to crew and no pollution or damage to vessel reported.
A fishing vessel had gearbox failure and was towed by a lifeboat to port.
Fishing vessel lost gearbox oil pressure resulting in being towed back to port.
A fishing vessel had gearbox issues and was towed by another fishing vessel.
A fishing vessel had problems with the propulsion gearbox. After an unsuccessful attempt to repair the gearbox at sea, it was towed to a port and berthed there.
A fishing vessel experienced a gear box failure. A search and rescue vessel was deployed and towed the vessel back to a harbour.
Fishing vessel reported gearbox problems and was escorted back to the harbour by a lifeboat .
Fishing vessel's gearbox failed resulting in a tow from another fishing vessel. The casualty vessel was not in danger at any point.

title: Dynamic Motion Hazards During Shipboard Entertainment Activities
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of musculoskeletal injuries sustained by performers and passengers engaged in physically demanding entertainment and recreational activities — principally dancing and live performance — aboard cruise vessels operating in sea states ranging from slight to moderate. The incidents share a common latent architecture: physical activity optimised for a stable, land-based environment is conducted in a dynamic marine environment without adequate procedural adaptation. The attribution of causation to individual carelessness or pre-existing conditions in several investigation reports represents a systemic investigative deficiency, obscuring the layered organisational and environmental factors that elevate injury risk. Critically, the absence of sea-state-conditional activity protocols, inadequate footwear standards for professional performers, and the failure to integrate vessel motion data into real-time entertainment risk management decisions are recurring systemic failures that make such incidents not merely foreseeable but predictable.

Environmental conditions — specifically wave heights between 1.25m and 2.38m across sea states 3 and 4 — create continuously shifting deck surfaces that meaningfully degrade balance, spatial awareness, and joint stability, even when vessel stabilisers are deployed. Human factors compound this baseline risk: performers wearing high-heeled footwear face an elevated centre of gravity and reduced ankle stability; passengers dancing recreationally may be unaware of biomechanical vulnerabilities amplified by vessel motion; and alcohol consumption in at least one incident further impaired coordination and fall response. Night-time operating conditions introduce additional variables, including reduced visual depth perception and altered spatial orientation. From a hardware perspective, stage and dancefloor surface anti-slip properties under dynamic loading conditions remain largely unassessed in incident reports, representing an unaddressed latent hazard. The convergence of these environmental, human, and procedural deficiencies in the absence of formalised risk management frameworks for entertainment activities constitutes the root cause driving persistent injury recurrence across this cluster.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural integrity and vessel stability as baseline for safe passenger movement)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safety of persons on board, including risk management obligations for passenger and crew activities)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for hazard identification, risk assessment, and procedural controls governing all onboard activities)
preventable_by: Implement a formal Sea-State Activity Risk Matrix that mandates graduated restrictions on high-intensity entertainment and recreational activities — including live dance performances and passenger dancing — when wave heights exceed defined thresholds (e.g., 1.0m), with mandatory crew-communicated advisories to passengers and automatic review of performer footwear and choreography standards as sea conditions deteriorate.

Establish and enforce vessel-specific Performer Footwear and Stage Safety Standards as part of the Entertainment Department's Safety Management System documentation, requiring risk-assessed approval of performance footwear relative to prevailing sea conditions, mandatory anti-fatigue and anti-slip stage surface certification, and pre-show dynamic motion briefings for all performing crew when stabilisers are deployed or sea state is 3 or above.

Introduce mandatory pre-activity safety briefings for passengers in recreational dance and entertainment areas, delivered by crew at the commencement of each event, covering vessel motion awareness, the biomechanical risks of vigorous physical activity at sea, alcohol interaction warnings, and the availability of seating and rest areas — supported by visible static safety signage at all dancefloor and entertainment venue entry points, and post-incident review protocols that require investigation teams to assess environmental and systemic factors rather than defaulting to passenger or crew carelessness as primary causation.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship singer fell whilst on stage resulting in a fracture to their left ankle. They were wearing '2 inch high scene shoes' during a performance and lost their balance, falling on stage onto their left side. Both of the vessel's stabilizers were extended at the time. No contributory factors or defects were identified by the vessel.

A cruise ship passenger was dancing when they felt their knee 'pop' and they over balanced causing them to fall to the floor and bang their head. The passenger was diagnosed with a dislocation of the right patella and was medically disembarked. The cause of the incident was reported to be carelessness on the part of the passenger.

A cruise ship crew member fell and injured their hand. accident occurred on 26 May 2025 at around 21:40 hours while he was performing the show, while conducting a dance move, accidentally the heel of partners right shoe hit two fingers of there right hand, sustaining the injuries.

A cruise ship passenger was dancing on a carpeted area next to the dancefloor when their knee popped out causing them to fall to the floor, resulting in a dislocation to the right knee. The area was inspected post-accident with no defects noted.

A passenger was injured whilst dancing when another passenger stepped on their foot. This resulted in the passenger having a fractured foot. They reported that they had consumed alcohol. The area of the incident was inspected by the crew and no defects were found.

A cruise ship passenger sustained a fractured shoulder when they fell whilst taking part in a dancing activity in the lounge area. There were no defects noted that could have contributed to the fall, vessel motions reported to be a factor.

A cruise ship passenger was dancing when they fell and sustained a fractured femur. It is unclear if it was a medical event, however they reported feeling dizzy before they fell and cannot recall why they fell. The area was inspected and there were no defects found. The passenger was medically disembarked.

A cruise ship dancer was taking part in a performance when they lost their balance and fell. They sustained a sprained ankle as a result of the fall which required them to have 9 days off work. They also dislocated their shoulder however they reported that they had a hypermobility condition, so dislocation was normal for them and they had already popped their shoulder back in before they sought me

A passenger was dancing in the show lounge with their spouse, when they fell, striking their left arm on the floor. The passenger was assisted by crewmembers to the medical centre, where an x-ray was taken. Upon review by a shore based consultant radiologist, the passenger was diagnosed with a comminuted, undisplaced fracture of the left distal radius extending to the radiocarpal articular surface

A cruise ship passenger was dancing when they lost their balance and rolled their ankle resulting in a fracture. The passenger reported that the incident was caused by their footwear and consumption of alcohol. The dance floor was inspected by the crew and there were no defects found. There were no damages or pollution. The injured passenger was disembarked for further medical treatment due to the

title: Passenger Falls From Unmitigated Deck-Level Trip Hazards
description:
This theme characterises a recurring pattern of passenger injuries resulting from trips and falls on low-profile, deck-level hazards — primarily sun lounger legs and fitness equipment structural bases — in high-traffic recreational areas aboard cruise vessels. These incidents share a common latent profile: portable or semi-permanent equipment is positioned in pedestrian zones without adequate hazard assessment, demarcation, or procedural controls, and this risk is compounded during night-time operations and moderate sea states when visibility is reduced and vessel motion impairs passenger balance and stability. The consistent recurrence of these injuries across multiple vessel areas and conditions indicates that the hazard is systemic rather than incidental, and that reactive attribution of causation solely to passenger inattention forecloses the identification and correction of underlying shipboard safety management failures.

The latent conditions driving this theme operate across all three causal domains. From a hardware perspective, sun lounger wheels and protruding structural bases present low-profile, visually inconspicuous hazards that are inherently difficult for passengers to detect, particularly at night or when attention is divided. Environmentally, sea states generating vessel motion — even at moderate levels — materially increase the biomechanical difficulty of maintaining balance while walking, and night-time lighting standards on open decks frequently prove inadequate to illuminate deck-level obstructions. Human factors are present both at the passenger level, where distraction and unfamiliarity with equipment layout reduce situational awareness, and at the organisational level, where the absence of structured safety briefings for fitness equipment users and the lack of formalised procedures for reviewing and adjusting deck furniture arrangements in response to sea conditions and reduced visibility represent failures of safety management. Collectively, these factors create a foreseeable injury pathway that should be addressed through proactive design, procedural, and operational controls rather than post-incident attribution.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural safety and deck design obligations)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety, briefings, and protective measures in recreational and common areas)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — systematic hazard identification, risk assessment, and procedural controls for recurring foreseeable risks)
preventable_by: Implement a formal dynamic deck furniture risk assessment procedure requiring that sun loungers and portable recreational equipment in passenger areas are repositioned, reduced in number, or secured with high-visibility hazard marking during night-time operations and whenever sea state or vessel motion exceeds defined threshold conditions.

Mandate a structured passenger safety orientation and posted instructional signage for all fitness centre equipment, including explicit identification of structural trip hazard features such as rear support bases, minimum safe mounting procedures, and required footwear, supported by periodic staff-supervised familiarisation checks.

Establish minimum lighting standards for open deck recreational areas during night-time operations that specifically address deck-level illumination sufficient to render low-profile obstructions — including lounger legs, wheels, and equipment bases — clearly visible to a person walking at a normal pace, with compliance verified through periodic photometric audits.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was in the fitness centre getting onto a rowing machine, their left foot caught on the rear support base, causing them to fall and strike the adjacent rowing machine with the left side of their chest. The passenger went to the medical centre where they were treated for a fractured rib, the passenger remained onboard. The area was inspected and found in good condition with n

A cruise ship passenger was walking in an open deck area when their foot struck the leg of a sun lounger and they fell to the deck. In order to break their fall, they put their wrists out and sustained one fractured wrist and a laceration to the bridge of their nose. The area was inspected and was found to be dry and without defects. The cause of the fall was attributed to lack of care on the part

A cruise ship passenger was in the pool area when the tripped on the leg of a sun lounder and fell down. They sustained a broken femur. The passenger was talking to someone else and picking up items at the time and it is thought that they did not see where they were going. The deck was reportedly in good condition and was not slippery or wet.
A cruise ship passenger was attempting to adjust the position of the sun lounger they had been using, but they tripped over one of the legs and fell to the deck, resulting in a fracture to their arm.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in the pool area of the vessel near some sun loungers. Their foot came into contact with the wheel of one of the sun loungers causing the passenger to trip and fall. When they were falling they put out their arms to break their fall and sustained a broken humerus. The team on board investigated the area and no defects or deficiencies were identified. The area wa

A cruise ship passenger tripped over a chair in a location between two jacuzzies and fell resulting in an injured shoulder and a cut above the eye. The cut required stitches and an x-ray revealed that the shoulder was fractured. The casualty was landed to a medical facility ashore for further treatment.

A cruise ship passenger got their left foot caught under a sun lounger and the momentum caused the passenger to fall on their right side. This fall resulted in a suspected periprosthetic fracture to the hip. The passenger was medically disembarked to a shoreside hospital.

Passenger tripped and fell, resulting in multiple injuries, including nasal fracture, patellar fracture and lacerations. Suspected neck injury. Casualty taken ashore via tender and passed to ambulance service.

A cruise ship passenger was walking back to a sunbed to get their swimming goggles. The passenger hit their right foot against the front leg of the sunbed lost their balance and fell down between two sunbeds. The right middle tow started to swell and was painful and starting to bruise, a displaced fracture was diagnosed.

A cruise ship passenger stood up and tripped over a leg of a lounger, causing them to fall onto their left side. The passenger was assisted by nearby crew and taken to the medical centre for assessment. They were treated for a fracture to the left wrist which was placed in a cast. The passenger remained onboard.

title: Propeller Fouling Causing Loss of Propulsion in Hazardous Environments
description:
This theme is characterised by the loss of propulsion due to propeller or propulsion unit fouling by debris, lines, nets, or foreign objects, occurring predominantly during night-time operations in confined or environmentally challenging waters. The incidents share a consistent pattern: a single mechanical failure — the fouling of the propeller or pump jet — immediately renders the vessel dependent on external assistance, while prevailing environmental conditions (elevated wind, wave action, confined berths, or proximity to hazards such as rock walls) rapidly escalate the situation from a manageable technical failure to a serious safety event. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types — sailing vessels, fishing vessels, and commercial passenger vessels — indicates that propeller fouling in operationally demanding environments is a systemic risk rather than an isolated occurrence, and that current industry practice does not adequately account for its likelihood or consequences.

The latent causes underpinning this theme span all three major risk domains.
