From a hardware perspective, the absence of propeller protection systems such as rope cutters or shaft seals, combined with possible deferred maintenance and the inherent vulnerability of exposed propeller and pump jet configurations to ingestion, creates a persistent mechanical weak point. Human factors are evident in the prevalence of solo skipper operations, which eliminate any crew redundancy for managing an emergency, and in the consistent absence of documented pre-departure or pre-manoeuvre checks designed to identify debris or fouling hazards. Procedurally, there is a systemic gap in immediate-action contingency planning for loss of propulsion in restricted waters, illustrated by instances where dispatched rescue assets lacked towing capability. Environmental conditions — particularly night operations with reduced visibility, moderate-to-rough seas, and confined harbour or marina environments — do not cause fouling directly but materially reduce a mariner's ability to detect hazards, increase the rate of debris encounter, and compress the time available to respond effectively once propulsion is lost.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E – Additional Requirements for Machinery, Propulsion and Steering)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulations 14 and 34 – Safe Manning and Safe Navigation/Voyage Planning)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Emergency Procedures and Contingency Preparedness)
preventable_by: Mandate the installation and regular inspection of rope cutters or equivalent propeller protection devices on vessels operating routinely in debris-prone, harbour, or net-fishing environments, with compliance verified during annual safety surveys.

Introduce a formal pre-departure and pre-manoeuvre checklist requirement for operations in marina, harbour, and restricted waterway environments, specifically including visual or instrumental assessment of the water around the propulsion system for floating lines, debris, or nets prior to engaging propulsion — with particular emphasis on night-time and low-visibility departures.

Require vessels operating in restricted or hazardous waters at night or in adverse conditions to carry a documented loss-of-propulsion contingency plan, including pre-identified anchor positions, towing attachment points, and verified emergency towing arrangements with SAR authorities ensuring responding assets are capability-matched to the tasking prior to deployment.

representative_cases.description: A sailing vessel fouled its prop within a marina and were being blown against a rock wall. This necessitated assistance from a nearby vessel which recovered the sailing boat to its berth where it was met by CRTs. No persons were injured.

A fishing vessel suffered a fouled propeller and damaged gearbox, they requested assistance as the vessel with four crew onboard was drifting. A pilot vessel launched to provide assistance if needed but were unable to tow. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene to tow the fishing vessel back to shore. No injuries to crew.

A fishing vessel fouled it's propeller, the lone skipper anchored the vessel and requested assistance. A nearby recreational motor vessel offered assistance and took the fishing vessel under tow back to harbour.

Passenger/Ro-Ro vessel was departing harbour when vibration was felt coming from the schottel pump jet unit. Multiple attempts were made to clear obstruction by back flushing with no success. This resulted in loss of propulsion. There was propulsion from the other unit but manoeuvrability is limited. The vessel was able to get alongside a pier without any assistance. No injuries and/or other damag

A fishing vessel suffered a propulsion failure. The crew initially thought they had fouled the propeller, they requested assistance as they thought they had picked up something in the screw and were unable to put the vessel into gear. Due to the depth of the water they were unable to anchor, another fishing vessel in the area responded to the assistance request and towed the vessel back to a harbo

A passenger vessel experienced propulsion failure and was towed into port by two tugs. The vessel experienced a mechanical failure to their propulsion system. The vessel was a historical paddle steamer and the crew suspected that one of the paddle wheels had struck some floating debris, resulting in one of the radius rods on the wheel being broken. The crew reduced the speed to dead slow ahead, be

A service vessel got a rope caught around the propeller and began taking on water.  The seven people on board were all recovered.  The available electric and hand pumps could not cope with water influx. The boat was recovered for repair.

A sailing yacht with two crew onboard fouled its propeller. The yacht was drifting as it was unable to anchor due to the water depth. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel initially took the vessel under tow, the tow was then taken up by a harbour vessel to take into harbour.

The crew of a fishing vessel discovered the vessel wouldn't move as there was something stuck in the propeller of the boat. They tried to move forward in gear to clear the propeller but were unsuccessful. A lifeboat arrived and tried to cut the vessel free but could not do it without diver assistance. The lifeboat returned to port to collect a diver, who cut the vessel free. The vessel was badly d

The motorboat fouled its propeller, due to this they experienced steering failure and collided with a pier. They sustained damage, water ingress & lifeboat crew members assisted the persons onboard to safety, stabilized the water ingress and assisted the vessel safely alongside the pontoon.

title: Recurring Machinery Failures from Unresolved Latent Defects
description:
This theme is characterised by machinery and fire incidents arising from the failure of critical engineering components — including gas system solenoids, engine crankcase assemblies, fuel injectors, and camshaft drive bushings — where the underlying defect was present and symptomatic prior to the casualty event but was not identified, investigated, or resolved before the vessel resumed operations. A consistent pattern across incidents is the application of symptomatic remediation, such as alarm resets or brief anchorage investigations, without achieving definitive fault diagnosis. This reactive posture, combined with apparent schedule pressure and insufficient appreciation of escalating risk, allowed developing hardware defects to progress to failure in service. The recurrence of such incidents across vessel types and machinery systems indicates a systemic latent weakness in Planned Maintenance System implementation and machinery casualty response protocols rather than isolated equipment failures.

Latent hardware degradation — through component wear, fatigue, lubrication failure, or material defect — forms the technical foundation of each incident, but human and procedural factors are the principal enablers of escalation. Decision-making under operational pressure repeatedly resulted in vessels departing or continuing passage without confirmed resolution of identified faults. Pre-departure inspection and testing procedures demonstrated insufficient depth to detect developing defects, and fault-investigation responses at anchorage or in port were inconclusive yet treated as sufficient. Fire detection relied on passive CCTV observation rather than automated systems, reducing the margin for early intervention. Together, these factors reflect a cultural and procedural environment in which warning signals are managed rather than resolved, and in which the threshold for halting operations in response to recurring safety system activations is insufficiently conservative.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E — Additional requirements for periodical surveys and maintenance of machinery)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction — Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — SMS procedures for maintenance, non-conformity reporting, and emergency response)
preventable_by: Mandatory 'no-restart without root cause confirmation' protocol: Vessels must not restart or recommit to passage following any machinery safety shutdown (oil mist detector, gas trip, or fire alarm) until a documented engineering investigation has confirmed the fault cause and either achieved a verified repair or obtained a formal risk assessment approval from a qualified superintendent or classification society surveyor.

Enhanced Planned Maintenance System (PMS) auditing with mandatory escalation triggers: Introduce risk-based maintenance intervals and condition-monitoring requirements for high-criticality components (crankshaft-to-camshaft drives, fuel injectors, gas solenoid actuators) with automated PMS overdue alerts, and require that any repeated alarm activation for the same system within a defined period triggers mandatory shore-side engineering review before the vessel departs port.

Active automated early-warning systems to replace reliance on sensory detection: Require installation and periodic certification of automated engine room fire and smoke detection systems, oil mist detection with automatic engine shutdown and lockout, and gas leak detection with automatic protective lockout that cannot be reset without a confirmed clear-fault condition, thereby eliminating dependency on crew sensory observation or manual CCTV monitoring as the primary fault-detection mechanism.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel was on passage when several gas detectors activated and a main engine tripped. The gas detection system was reset, it was noted there was an abnormal sound on the gas supply line. Whilst in port, the gas system was checked and tested, with the vessel underway a crew member was aware of a venting noise. It was confirmed that the liquid natural gas (LNGP) valves were all shut, both m

A ro-ro cargo vessel was leaving port when a oil mist detector alarm sounded on the main engine panel and the engine shut down. Port authorities were informed via radio, AIS status changed and proper navigational lights displayed. The vessel started adrift with anchors ready to let go if necessary. The engine was restarted and the vessel continued to proceed. The main engine shut down again for th

A service vessel experienced a fire in the engine room resulting in no damage. . Master looked at the engine room CCTV camera and could see smoke. Engineer sent to investigate and could see smoke and the occasional flames from starboard engine. Extinguished flames and shut down engine. Master notified VTS. Engineers boarded the vessel once berthed to investigate the fault. It appears one of the fu

A domestic ro-pax vessel experienced a crank case explosion when departing from a berth with a pilot on board. This caused the explosion relief valves to activate but the crank case door remained intact. The engine room fire alarm activated and the engine was shut down. The vessel maneuvered back to berth using the other main engine and the crew confirmed that there was no fire and the situation w

A cruise ship diesel generator had a fuel leak on the main fuel supply line. The spill was contained within the pipe insulation, all leaked fuel was then directed into the bilge, approximately 200 litres. On investigation it was the small adapter insert in the valve that failed in both cases.

A ro-pax ferry had a pin hole on its main engine's fuel line. This was thought to be caused by weld defect as pipe was not corroded.  The crew were concerned that this hole may lead to split fuel pipe with resulting fuel leakage into vessel bilges.  They stopped the engine in order to isolate the fuel line. The vessel returned to its port of departure for repairs without incident.

A cruise ship experienced a fire in the engine room resulting in damage to the crank case. Catastrophic failure occurred on DG Nr2 A5 & B5 units causing a crank case explosion, initial indication appears the start of the failure was at a piston crown. This has lead to a fire. The fire was extinguished by the Hi-Fog system. All other equipment and systems assessed and found to be operable.

A passenger vessel conducted an emergency stop whilst approaching its berth after an alarm went off for the forward standby fuel pump. A leak was confirmed and the forward engine was shut down. The vessel was kept in a safe position and the forward engine de-clutched. The vessel berthed safely on the aft engine. An estimated 300-400 litres of fuel was captured in the bilges and contained onboard.

A fish carrier crew member noted a mist at the funnel, they called another crew member who went to the engine room and discovered that oil was being pushed out of the crankcase dipstick and was forcing its way up the breather to the funnel.  The engine was shutdown and this coincided with engine room alarm simultaneously activating. It was suspected the breather may have been blocked and after dra

A tanker vessel was alongside completing a refit. The port main engine was running offload in accordance with manufacturers engine running in procedure following a 12k hour major overhaul. The manufacturer’s service engineer and two ship's engineers were in attendance. Everything appeared to be running as expected when suddenly the crank case explosion doors lifted directing smoke and vapour into

title: Recreational Vessel PPE Non-Compliance and Immersion Fatalities
description:
This theme is characterised by a systemic and normalised disregard for personal protective equipment compliance — most critically lifejacket and personal locator beacon wear — among recreational mariners operating in conditions that carry inherent and often underestimated risk. The incidents share a convergent failure pattern: solo or reduced crew operations, night-time or low-visibility conditions, and environmental hazards (cold water, swell, deteriorating weather) that individually may appear manageable but collectively create life-threatening exposure. The recurring fatality mechanism is prolonged undetected immersion: without a lifejacket to maintain buoyancy and a PLB or MOB device to trigger an immediate alert, a survivable entry into the water becomes fatal through cold shock, hypothermia, and the absence of timely rescue. The dismasted sailing vessel, the transom-swamped angling boat, and the impulsive secondary rescue attempt all reflect the same underlying condition — mariners who have normalised the omission of basic safety equipment and who have not internalised the speed at which benign conditions can become lethal.

The latent hardware dimension of this theme centres on vessel design vulnerabilities — low-freeboard craft, exposed transoms, and the absence of integrated emergency alerting systems — that are permitted to operate without mandatory fitment of distress signalling devices. Human factors are dominant: overconfidence in personal swimming ability, underestimation of cold water incapacitation, poor passage planning, failure to assess swell and tidal conditions, and the well-documented compounding error of unsupported bystander rescue. Procedural failures include the absence of enforced pre-departure safety briefings, no watch-keeping protocols for solo night passages, and inadequate risk assessment frameworks accessible to recreational users. Environmental conditions — cold water temperatures, anomalous swell, night darkness, and wind-driven sea states — are not in themselves exceptional, yet they are routinely underweighted in recreational voyage planning. The consistent lesson across all incidents is that survival outcomes are determined not by the severity of the initiating event but by the absence of layered safety measures that should have been in place before departure.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (lifejacket carriage, donning requirements, MOB equipment, distress alerting devices)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (mandatory carriage of distress alerting equipment, EPIRB and PLB requirements, DSC watchkeeping)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (voyage planning obligations, weather assessment, safe manning, and watch-keeping responsibilities)
preventable_by: Mandate automatic-inflation lifejacket wear and personal PLB or AIS-MOB device activation as a legal condition of recreational vessel operation at night, when single-handed, or in sea states exceeding defined thresholds — enforced through harbour authority departure checks and coast guard boarding inspections with proportionate penalties for non-compliance.

Introduce a compulsory pre-departure risk assessment protocol for recreational mariners that explicitly addresses cold water immersion survival time, swell and tidal anomalies beyond wind-sea state, vessel freeboard vulnerability, crew complement adequacy for night operations, and communication redundancy — delivered through a standardised digital or paper checklist aligned with national boating safety certification schemes.

Require all recreational vessels operating beyond sheltered waters after nautical twilight or in forecast sea states above Force 3 to carry and have activated a registered EPIRB or category-appropriate PLB for every person on board, supplemented by a targeted public safety campaign — co-developed with RNLI, coast guard agencies, and angling and sailing associations — that uses incident data to communicate the specific, quantified survival-time consequences of cold water immersion without a lifejacket.

representative_cases.description: Sailing vessel's crew member washed up ashore. The casualty was not wearing a lifejacket and is thought to have been approximately 6 hours in the water prior to recovery. The search and rescue services were tasked to find the vessel which was recovered dismasted a few days later and towed to port.   Water temperature 15.4°C.

A single handed recreational angling vessel was at anchor when a swell came over the transom and engulfed the vessel. The sea was slight and the wind was force 3 but the swells were reported to be 2m. The vessel sank and then crewmember entered the water without a lifejacket. They called for help by their mobile phone and local lifeboat services attended. They were successfully recovered after abo

A row boat crew of two people were launching their vessel when one of them fell into the water. The other crew member jumped in after them in an attempt to save them but also got into difficulty. The first crewmember was washed over a weir and drowned whilst the second crewmember was recovered by search and rescue services alive.

A dinghy crewmember fell over board and was recovered with breathing difficulties. It was suspected that they had inhaled water, dislocated their shoulder and injured their ribs. They were rescued by search and rescue teams and transferred to a shore side ambulance.

A crewmember fell from their tender while returning to their houseboat moored on a river. The alarm was raised by a member of public who reported him holding on to pile. RNLI attended found man submerged between two moored boats. River temp 6-8C, not wearing a PFD.  CPR conducted but subsequently declared deceased at hospital.

DISTRESS PHASE. SAILING VESSEL FOUND AGROUND WITH NO PERSONS ON BOARD AND SIGNS OF A POTENTIAL MAN OVERBOARD. Person has been assessed and confirmed the person has been in the water over 3hours time. Ambulance has been requested Person has been assessed by paramedics. Confirmed to be conscious.

Pleasure vessel started taking on water with 2 people on board. There was no life raft on board, one individual had a lifejacket on and the second individual was going to get a lifejacket while the vessel was going down. The boat sank stern first and that is when one of the members was last seen (owner of boat). The casualty with a lifejacket has been taken ashore while the search for the missing

A recreational fishing vessel's tender capsized with two people on board. They were both wearing life jackets however, one of the occupants did not have a correctly fitted lifejacket and was lost.  Time in water: 45 minutes

A tender with two people onboard were heading to a fishing vessel when the tender capsized and both people entered the water. Emergency services were called, two search and recue (SAR) vessels, helicopter and a rescue team were tasked to the scene. One casualty who was wearing a lifejacket was able to swim back to shore and passed into the care of the ambulance service. The second casualty who was

The owner of a recreational craft and a third party were on board the vessel, which was alongside its berth, watching a firework display. The owner made their way to the transom/swim platform to relieve themselves and, as the boat rocked, they fell in the water. The other person on board saw the owner in the water and It is not clear how but they also ended up in the water.  Members of the public

title: Fuel System Contamination and Maintenance Failures Causing Propulsion Loss
description:
This theme is characterised by recurring propulsion failures attributable to contaminated or degraded fuel systems, encompassing blocked fuel filters, injector pump deficiencies, and the absence of effective onboard fuel treatment processes. Across all incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: contaminated fuel — whether introduced during bunkering, transferred from poorly managed day tanks, or accumulated through degraded system components — reaches critical engine fuel systems without being intercepted by adequate filtration, purification, or pre-departure inspection regimes. These failures are systemic rather than isolated, reflecting organisational shortcomings in preventive maintenance scheduling, bunkering quality assurance, and crew competency in emergency fuel system recovery procedures. The repeated dependency on external towing assistance further underscores the operational impact of these deficiencies, particularly when they manifest in vessels carrying passengers or operating in exposed waters.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including worn injector pump seals, degraded filter media, and inoperative centrifugal separators — form the physical foundation of these incidents, but their occurrence is enabled by deeper human and procedural failures. Maintenance intervals are frequently inadequate or unenforced, pre-departure engineering checks are either absent or insufficiently rigorous, and crew members in several cases lacked the practical competency to execute fuel system recovery procedures such as bleeding after filter replacement. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations and sea states of force 5 or above, consistently compounded the consequences by impairing crew situational awareness, delaying detection of early warning signs, and increasing navigational risk during the loss-of-propulsion period. The convergence of these latent procedural gaps, hardware vulnerabilities, and adverse environmental context transforms what should be detectable, preventable maintenance failures into high-risk casualty events.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (specifically Part E, regulations governing machinery maintenance, propulsion reliability, and engineering systems)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring documented maintenance systems, risk assessment, and crew competency assurance)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction (relevant to fuel leakage into bilges, fire risk from diesel accumulation, and fuel system integrity)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory bunkering fuel quality assurance protocol requiring representative fuel samples to be independently tested against ISO 8217 or applicable standards before acceptance, with a documented chain of custody and clear authority for the Chief Engineer to reject non-compliant fuel deliveries, ensuring contaminated MGO cannot enter the ship's fuel system undetected.

Establish and enforce a structured Planned Maintenance System (PMS) for all fuel system components — including filters, separators, injector pumps, and seals — with defined inspection intervals, condition-based replacement criteria, and mandatory pre-departure engineering checks that verify fuel filter differential pressure, separator operation, and fuel system integrity before each voyage.

Conduct regular shipboard drills and verified competency assessments for engineering crew in fuel system emergency recovery procedures, including fuel system bleeding, filter replacement at sea, and day tank management, ensuring all watchkeeping engineers can execute these procedures independently under adverse conditions and without reliance on external assistance.

representative_cases.description: A Ro/Ro passenger vessel suffered a black out with bot main engines stopped. The cause was a contaminated MGO which clogged up the filters and lost pressure to the engines.. The crew rectified the issue and the vessel continued passage. There was no damage or pollution.

Charter vessel reported an engine failure which resulted in a tow. It was noticed by an engineer that the fuel line and filters were fully blocked. This issue has been resolved and no injuries were sustained to any of the passengers. No further damages reported.

A leisure fishing vessel had engine failure and fuel leaking into the starboard bilge. The fuel leaked from the injector pump and started to fill the starboard bilge with red diesel. The vessel was towed by a lifeboat into port.

A fishing vessel lost engine power in adverse weather conditions.  The casualty vessel was not in any immediate danger but a tow was arranged to shore. It later transpired that the fuel filters were blocked and although the crew had changed the filters, they were unable to bleed the fuel system and restart the engine at sea.

A cruise ship discovered that their fuel filters supplying the boiler were clogged. This was suspected to be due to residue in the system where the day tank had been run close to empty. The filters were cleaned and system reinstated and subsequently the filters re-clogged.  At this point it was identified that the clogging was due to potential fuel contamination. The boilers were immediately shut

The vessel was on passage at the time when it had a main engine cooling fault, tug assistance was requested and was towed to safety into a nearby port where the vessel would also have the issues repaired

A ro-ro passenger vessel lost propulsion power on both main engines during fuel change over from marine gas oil (MGO) to very low sulphur fuel oil (VLSFO). Electrical power was maintained throughout and the bow thruster and windlasses were fully operational if required. The vessel was drifting in 80 metres of water and away from the nearest coast until problem was resolved .

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was being moved when the engine cut out and the vessel started to drift. The vessel was then anchored between moorings and it was found the fuel to be contaminated with water. An investigation is in progress.

A beam trawler suffered propulsion failure after suspected clogging of fuel filters after taking a new batch of bunkers in a previous port. They were assisted into port by the local lifeboat service and changed/cleaned the filters before proceeding on their way. There was no damage, injuries or pollution.

A general cargo vessel experienced propulsion failure due to water contaminated fuel oil which resulted in the vessel being towed to a nearby port. There was no material damage to the vessel and no pollution. The source of the breakdown is contaminated bunkers.

title: Unresolved Machinery Defects Causing Propulsion and Power Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by the persistent operation of vessels with known, symptomatic machinery deficiencies — particularly in propulsion, fuel delivery, governor, and electrical generation systems — that were inadequately diagnosed, misattributed, or deliberately deferred prior to high-risk operational phases such as berthing, towing, and ocean passage. Across all representative incidents, observable warning signs including engine hunting, intermittent faults, and generator irregularities were present before the critical failure event, yet in each case the vessel proceeded into demanding operations without verified resolution of the underlying defect. This recurring pattern indicates a systemic weakness in machinery management culture, go/no-go decision-making frameworks, and engineering accountability, where the threshold for halting or deferring operations in the presence of unresolved symptoms is insufficiently enforced.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including design flaws in alternators, incorrectly specified or calibrated governors, and degraded fuel system integrity — form the technical foundation of this theme, but their consequences are consistently amplified by human and procedural failures. Misdiagnosis, incomplete corrective action, and the absence of structured pre-departure engineering sign-off allow these latent conditions to persist into operational service. Environmental factors, particularly night-time conditions and adverse sea states, further compress available response time and degrade situational awareness once the primary failure occurs, transforming a manageable technical fault into a navigational emergency. The combination of inadequate design validation at procurement, insufficient lifecycle equipment management, and a culture that tolerates symptomatic operation without root-cause confirmation represents the core systemic failure driving incidents within this cluster.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (mandatory standards for propulsion and electrical system reliability, maintenance, and design adequacy)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (relevant to electrical equipment design standards and fault prevention in generation systems)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requires documented procedures for machinery maintenance, defect reporting, go/no-go decision criteria, and safety management system accountability)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory structured go/no-go decision criteria within the vessel's Safety Management System that explicitly prohibit departure or commencement of high-risk operations (towing, berthing approaches, restricted waterway transits) where any propulsion or primary power generation system exhibits unresolved symptomatic defects, requiring written sign-off from both the Master and Chief Engineer with documented root-cause confirmation — not merely symptom suppression — before operations may proceed.

Require independent pre-installation design verification and post-installation calibration testing for all newly fitted or replaced machinery control components (governors, alternators, voltage regulators), with acceptance test records reviewed by a competent third-party surveyor or Class society representative prior to the equipment being placed in operational service, ensuring that latent design or specification deficiencies are identified before in-service failure can occur.

Establish a vessel-wide defect tracking and escalation protocol within the planned maintenance system that flags any machinery symptom recurring across more than one operational day as a Category A defect requiring mandatory rectification and root-cause verification before the next departure, with unresolved Category A defects automatically notified to the company's Designated Person Ashore (DPA) to prevent normalisation of symptomatic operation and ensure shore-based technical oversight is engaged in the decision-making process.

representative_cases.description: Recreational craft's engine was running normally after having issues in the previous days. During a procedure the engine was turned off and with some issues they were able to restart it. However, when the vessel was preparing for berthing the engine suddenly failed. The crew members were instructed to re-hoist and mainsail which maintained the manoeuvrability and provided some propulsion. They req

A tug was departing port towing a offshore platform. The crew connected the tow line and the engines started hunting. The crew believed it was fuel issue due to poor weather and the engineers cleaned the filters. However, this did not stop the issue and then the vessel lost a generator, followed quickly by the second. The vessel lost all power and dropped anchor and slipped two line. The tug made

A cruise ship experienced a partial power outage whilst on passage in open sea. After a few minutes, other diesel generators were brought online and all services restored. The outage occurred due to a problem with a ship's alternator. The incident forced the ship to maintain a limited speed, resulting in a 2 hour delay to the next port. The incident was reported to be due to a poor design of the p

The governor of the auxiliary engine no 3 was replaced, during testing the engine over-sped to 1200 rpm when it should operate at 720 rpm. The engine was stopped by cutting off the fuel supply as multiple safety cut-offs failed to operate. A significant amount of damage has been caused to the equipment but all parts remained inside the entablature and no injuries to personnel.

A general cargo was approaching a wharf to berth when the engine went into overspeed and tripped. The vessel anchored, a pilot was assigned to board the vessel and assist the crew in getting the vessel alongside using lines only.  The vessel was berthed, it was found that there was further damage to the electrical system.

A fishing vessel was on a test run after electrical work was carried out the previous day, the vessel had an engine failure and started to drift. The two crew were able to get the engine started but it failed again and the vessel grounded. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to tow the vessel back to shore. No reported damage, no injuries.

A fishing vessel was towing its fishing gear with a following tide. The speed of the vessel began to increase and the crew attempted to control it by using a variable air valve, which was the established practice to control the speed whilst towing gear. However this was not effective and the vessel continued to pick up speed until the engine overspeed alarm activated and the engine automatically s

A fishing vessel with two crewmembers on board had engine failure. A lifeboat was ready, but the fishing vessel managed to restart the engine. The engine failed again as they entered the harbour and they were assisted by another vessel back to their mooring.

During the final approach to the berth, a cargo ship's main engine stopped. It was then restarted, but stopped again. The situation was brought under control by a tug and there were calm conditions, so the vessel was able to drift safely until the main engine was restarted. The engine failures were potentially attributable to a recently replaced governor, that was not correctly set up.

A pilot vessel experienced an engine shut down unexpectedly whilst outbound to sea. The starboard engine was reset and appeared to be operational. The following morning the pilot vessel was attempting to put a pilot on a vessel when it suffered engine and steering failure. The vessel returned to port under one engine and emergency steering. Whilst heading back the vessel reported the emergency ste

title: RIB Wave-Impact Injuries: Night Operations and Passenger Safety
description:
This theme is characterised by spinal and musculoskeletal injuries sustained by passengers and crew aboard Rigid Inflatable Boats (RIBs) as a direct consequence of wave-induced slamming forces transmitted through the rigid hull to inadequately supported or unbraced occupants. The incidents cluster around a consistent failure pattern: commercial RIB operations conducted at night or in dynamic sea conditions without formal speed management protocols, structured passenger safety briefings, or adequate seat retention systems. Notably, injuries occur even in relatively modest sea states, indicating that vessel speed relative to conditions — rather than absolute wave height — is often the primary injury mechanism. The bow seating position is disproportionately exposed to vertical shock loading, yet passengers are routinely seated there without instruction on bracing posture or forewarning of the physical demands of RIB passage in open water.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a persistent underlying factor, with RIB seating configurations frequently lacking shock-mitigating suspension, grab handles, or restraint systems appropriate for the dynamic loads generated during commercial passenger operations. Human factors compound these shortcomings: operators demonstrate a systemic absence of pre-departure safety briefings tailored to RIB-specific hazards, and there is little evidence of formalised speed-reduction thresholds tied to sea state, visibility, or operational lighting conditions. Environmental conditions — including night-time operation that reduces the operator's ability to anticipate wave strikes, ship wakes generating sudden localised disturbance in otherwise calm water, and wind-driven chop — interact with these latent failures to produce foreseeable and preventable injury events. The recurrence of this pattern across multiple operators and contexts suggests a sector-wide normalisation of risk that requires both regulatory attention and industry-level procedural reform.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19: Emergency Training and Drills; passenger safety briefing obligations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34: Safe Speed and Voyage Planning; operator responsibility to manage speed relative to prevailing conditions)
SOLAS Chapter IV / HSC Code – High-Speed Craft Code (Seating, restraint systems, and structural load requirements for high-speed passenger craft operations)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure passenger safety briefings for all commercial RIB operations, explicitly covering impact-bracing posture, the hazards of bow seating in dynamic conditions, and instructions to hold grab handles at all times underway — with compliance recorded in the vessel's safety management log.

Establish and enforce formalised speed management protocols for RIB operators that define maximum permissible speeds relative to sea state, swell, visibility, and lighting conditions, including mandatory speed reduction during night operations and when transiting ship wakes or areas of localised chop.

Require that all commercial passenger RIBs are fitted with ergonomic shock-mitigating seating incorporating adequate cushioning, passenger grab handles, and where practicable, restraint systems — with particular attention to bow bench seats — as a condition of survey and certification for passenger-carrying operations.

representative_cases.description: RIB's crew member sustained a back injury after the RIB caught a wave. The casualty has been taken for further medical assistance.

A passenger on a sight seeing RIB (rigid inflatable boat) was injured. They sustained a spine or neck injury which caused them to be unable to move. Emergency services extracted the injured passenger ashore to an ambulance.

Lower back injury sustained by a passenger sitting on the front bench seat of the RIB caused by vessel going over a wave, causing the passenger to be lifted out of the seat and landing heavily on seat on impact.
A RIB's passenger injured there back, resulting in being extracted from the vessel and taken to hospital.

A RIB was on passage when a ship went past, as the wake from the ship came closer the skipper altered course to be bow on and reduced speed. Shortly after the vessel went over the wake one of the passengers complained of pain in their back. The crew acted and stopped the vessel to attend to the passenger.
RIB ride passenger reported minor back pain after a high speed ride. The passenger had not followed the skippers instructions to hold on and was using a camera.

A RIB (rigid inflatable boat) with ten passengers onboard was on a trip when the bow hit the trough of a wave and then buried into the back of the wave resulting in two passengers hitting the seats in front of them. One passenger sustained an open wound above the right eye and a head injury. The second passenger sustained a head injury with a swollen lump on the forehead. A patrol vessel nearby as

A commercial rib ride passenger injured their back after the rib went over a wave. The rib skipper stopped to assess the passenger's injuries and returned to port to get the passenger medically assessed. The passenger managed to disembark the vessel unaided and was assessed by paramedics.

A RIB (rigid inflatable boat) skipper was leaving harbour for a cruise when they went over a wave and the subsequent jolt hurt a passenger's back. The skipper returned to harbour and the passenger left the vessel, confirming later by email that after a walk and pain killers they felt better.

A leisure RIB (rigid inflatable boat) with four people on board had a crewmember who sustained a back injury after wave impact. The casualty was evacuated to hospital by helicopter following medical advice.

title: Propulsion Failure During Active Dive Operations at Night
description:
This theme is characterised by the complete loss of propulsion on dive support vessels — including RIBs and RHIBs — during active dive operations conducted in night-time conditions, with divers in the water at the moment of failure. Across all representative incidents, the mechanical breakdown of the engine or drive train transformed a routine operational event into a potential life-safety emergency, as the vessel's primary function as a safety platform for personnel in the water was immediately compromised. The recurrence of this pattern indicates systemic deficiencies in pre-departure engineering verification, planned maintenance scheduling, and operational risk thresholds governing night dive operations. The absence of mandatory standby or buddy-vessel protocols, combined with insufficient procedural clarity on emergency escalation, further compounded outcomes — as evidenced by operators attempting in-company tows in adverse sea states rather than immediately engaging emergency services, resulting in secondary vessel damage.

The latent hardware deficiencies driving these incidents are developing mechanical faults — whether in fuel systems, drive trains, or electrical propulsion components — that are not identified through routine inspection or scheduled maintenance prior to departure. These undetected deficiencies are allowed to persist, in part, due to inadequate pre-operation engineering checks and the absence of formal vessel serviceability certification requirements for dive support operations. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness, moderate wave heights of approximately 1.0–1.3 metres, and winds around 21–22 kph, significantly amplified the consequences of each failure by reducing situational awareness, complicating diver recovery, and increasing the hazard exposure of rescue and towing operations. Human factors, though less frequently cited, manifested in the form of poor escalation decisions and an underestimation of risk when operating at night with divers deployed — reflecting a broader cultural gap in recognising the compound risk profile that night dive operations with a single-vessel configuration present.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (mandatory maintenance standards and propulsion system reliability requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency procedures, rescue boat requirements, and provisions for recovering persons from the water)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning obligations, operational risk assessment, and the requirement to render assistance to persons in distress)
preventable_by: Implementation of mandatory pre-departure engineering inspections and formal vessel serviceability certification for all dive support vessels, including RIBs and RHIBs, with documented sign-off by a qualified engineer prior to any night or diver-in-water operation — ensuring developing mechanical faults are identified before departure rather than manifesting as in-water failures.

Adoption and enforcement of a mandatory buddy-vessel or standby-vessel policy for all dive operations conducted at night or in conditions exceeding defined sea state thresholds, ensuring a dedicated support vessel with operational propulsion is on station whenever divers are in the water, eliminating reliance on passing vessels or improvised in-company towing for diver recovery.

Development and implementation of clear, graduated emergency escalation procedures with defined operational triggers — including immediate SAR notification upon loss of propulsion with divers in the water or passengers on board — to prevent operators from defaulting to informal towing responses in adverse conditions, and to ensure lifeboat and rescue assets are engaged without delay when vessel incapacitation occurs at night.

representative_cases.description: A dive vessel with ten people onboard requested a tow after suffering an engine failure. The vessel anchored until a search and rescue (SAR) vessel took the dive vessel under tow and back to shore. No injuries/other damages were reported.

A dive boat lost propulsion whilst three divers were still in the water, the dive vessel was drifting but in no immediate danger. A search and rescue vessel recovered the divers to shore and the dive vessel was towed to a marina.
Dive RIB suffered an engine failure while 2 divers were in the water. Assistance was provided by a passing by vessel. No injuries/other damages were reported.

A commercial RHIB with nine persons on board broke down. They were towed by a dive vessel from the same company with seven persons on board. It was reported that the dive vessel was taking damage so a lifeboat was tasked. This decision was made quickly due to children being on board. The lifeboat took the RHIB under tow back to the marina where CRT met the casualties. No medical assistance was req

A diver support vessel experienced propulsion failure whilst there were three divers in the water. The local lifeboat service attended and rescued the three divers from the water and took them back ashore. The dive vessel was towed back to shore separately. There were no injuries, damages or pollution.

A dive vessel had a gearbox failure whilst two divers were in the water. The vessel drifted towards one diver and managed to recover them. The other diver was recovered by another vessel and transferred to the dive vessel. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked and then towed the stricken vessel to a harbour.

A dive vessel suffered an outboard engine failure whilst four divers were in the water. The vessel had anchored but were concerned they not be able to recover the divers on the ebb tide. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene, the divers were recovered and the dive vessel taken under tow. The dive vessel managed to get the outboard working and no longer required a tow, however th
Dive vessel lost power resulting in drifting away from two divers in water. Towed to safety by RNLI.

A dive support vessel experienced propulsion failure whilst there were three divers in the water and began to drift. The crew of the dive vessel issued an urgency message by VHF and a sailing vessel and the local lifeboat service rendered assistance, to recover the divers and tow the vessel to a place of safety. There was no pollution, damage or injuries.

Fishing vessel experienced steering failure while two scallop divers were working on the seabed. They drifted away from the diver's position but managed to drop anchor. The fishing vessel informed the coastguard and the divers were recovered by a local lifeboat. In the meantime, back-up steering was restored to the fishing boat so the divers were returned to the vessel to make its own passage back

title: Passenger Stairway Falls Under Night-Time Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by recurring slip, trip, and fall incidents involving passengers on cruise vessel staircases, predominantly occurring during night-time operations across a range of sea states from calm to moderate. The defining injury mechanism is stairway misjudgement — particularly misplacement of the final or penultimate step — resulting in fractures to the foot, hip, leg, wrist, and arm. The consistency of this pattern across multiple vessels and operational contexts indicates a systemic safety deficiency rather than isolated individual error. High-footfall transition points such as theatre exits, entertainment venue stairways, and main passenger thoroughfares represent disproportionate risk locations where passenger attention is frequently divided and environmental hazards are insufficiently mitigated by passive engineering controls.

The latent conditions underpinning this theme involve an interplay of hardware, human factors, and environmental elements. Hardware deficiencies include inadequate step-edge demarcation, insufficient tread contrast markings, substandard localised stairway lighting, and the absence of compliant anti-slip surfaces — all of which degrade step visibility under night-time lighting conditions. Human factor contributions include passenger unfamiliarity with shipboard stairway geometry, a tendency to disregard or not use available handrails, and distraction associated with post-entertainment venue egress. Even modest vessel motion in sea states above Beaufort 3 introduces subtle dynamic loading that compounds misjudgement risk, though the recurrence of incidents in calm and alongside conditions confirms that environmental motion is an aggravating rather than primary factor. The evidence collectively points to an over-reliance on passenger behavioural compliance in lieu of robust passive engineering controls, representing a systemic gap in passenger safety management obligations under the ISM Code.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (as it establishes standards for means of escape, including stairway construction and lighting requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (covering safe mustering and movement of passengers through vessel spaces, including stairway access routes)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring systematic identification of onboard hazards, risk assessment, and preventive maintenance of passenger safety infrastructure)
preventable_by: Conduct a vessel-wide night-time stairway lighting and step-edge visibility audit, implementing high-contrast anti-slip nosing strips and photoluminescent or LED step-edge markings on all passenger stairways, with particular priority given to high-footfall locations adjacent to entertainment venues and public gathering spaces.

Establish a mandatory adverse-conditions passenger safety protocol requiring targeted real-time PA announcements and posted signage at stairway approaches during night-time hours and sea states of Beaufort 3 or above, advising passengers to use handrails and proceed with care, supplemented by crew deployment at high-risk stairway locations during peak passenger movement periods.

Integrate stairway infrastructure safety into the vessel's SMS preventive maintenance schedule, requiring periodic inspection and certification of stair tread condition, handrail integrity, and localised lighting lux levels against defined minimum standards, with findings recorded and reviewed by the Designated Person Ashore as part of routine ISM Code compliance oversight.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was exiting the theatre when they missed a step and fell resulting in a fractured arm.
A cruise ship passenger was descending a staircase when they missed the second to last step. They fell, twisted their foot and landed on their wrists. They sustained a fractured foot.
A cruise ships passenger tripped down stairs resulting in a closed fracture to the hip. The passenger miss placed the last step and slipped causing a fall to the deck.
Cruise ship's passenger was walking down the stairs when they slipped and twisted their foot resulting in a fractured leg.
A guest was descending the stairs on a cruise ship when they missed the last step and fell and sustained suspected hip injury.
Cruise ships passenger tripped down some stairs resulting in internal injuries. Passenger tripped down the last few steps landing on there upper back and left arm, passenger evacuated to hospital.
Cruise ship's passenger tripped on the threshold and in order to avoid hitting their head, they tried to turn which resulted in a fractured rib. No defects found in the area.
A passenger ship's passenger fell down some steps and fractured their ankle.
A cruise passenger fell whilst descending a staircase resulting in a fractured fibula in their leg. The casualty was disembarked for treatment ashore.
A cruise ship crewmember missed the last step on a staircase resulting in a fractured left foot. The vessel was alongside and the casualty was disembarked.

title: SAR and Vessel Machinery Failures from Maintenance Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by propulsion, steering, and fuel system failures occurring across both SAR vessels and recreational or fishing craft, frequently during night operations and in varying sea states. A defining and operationally significant feature is the recurrence of SAR vessels themselves becoming casualties — requiring external assistance from lifeboats or other SAR units — thereby compounding demand on emergency response resources. The incidents cluster around a common root cause: inadequate preventive maintenance regimes, deferred servicing, and insufficient pre-deployment technical inspections. Single-engine configurations without redundant propulsion amplify the consequence of any individual component failure, transforming a manageable mechanical defect into a full SAR activation. The pattern is consistent across vessel types and operators, indicating systemic rather than isolated failure in maintenance culture and operational readiness standards.

Latent hardware vulnerabilities are the primary driver of this theme, manifesting as cooling system degradation, fuel system component wear, steering overload under high-tempo manoeuvring, and outboard engine fatigue — all failures that develop progressively and are detectable through structured inspection. Human factors compound these latent conditions: crews and operators may normalise minor pre-existing faults, omit pre-departure checks under operational pressure, or lack formal procedures for detecting early failure indicators during transit. Environmental conditions, particularly night operations, moderate-to-rough seas, and high-tempo SAR tasking cycles, do not cause these failures but materially delay detection, reduce crew response time, and elevate the consequences of propulsion loss. The absence of mandatory engineering logs, inconsistent inter-deployment servicing intervals, and reliance on single propulsion sources without contingency protocols collectively sustain this incident pattern across the fleet.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part C – Machinery Installations, including propulsion reliability, steering gear, and redundancy requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (applicable to fuel leak incidents and fire risk from machinery spaces)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (obligations relating to vessel seaworthiness, operational readiness, and the duty to ensure vessels are fit for intended service prior to departure)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory structured pre-deployment checklists for all SAR and operational vessels, covering propulsion systems, fuel system integrity, cooling systems, and steering gear, with signed verification by a responsible crew member before every deployment — including exercises and post-tasking returns to station.

Establish a minimum redundant propulsion requirement for all SAR vessels, either through twin-engine configurations or carriage of a certified auxiliary outboard, combined with formal contingency procedures requiring crews to practise propulsion-failure recovery drills at defined training intervals.

Introduce a fleet-level preventive maintenance audit programme with standardised engine-hour-based servicing thresholds, centralised maintenance records, and independent compliance inspections, ensuring that inter-deployment servicing intervals are protected from operational scheduling pressure and that deferred maintenance items are formally risk-assessed before a vessel is returned to service.

representative_cases.description: A search and rescue vessel experienced steering difficulties during recovery that resulted in two of the propellers being damaged.
A search and rescue vessel had been tasked to an incident, whilst returning to station the vessel had an engine failure. The crew were able to self recover by paddling the vessel back to the station.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service when the vessel suffered engine failure. The vessel was towed backed to station by another SAR vessel. Incident reported by phone, waiting for ARF.
Sailing vessel suffered engine failure resulting in being towed to safety by SAR.
A search and rescue vessel had a fuel leak from its port engine and was escorted by a lifeboat to safety.
A search and rescue vessel experienced propulsion failure when its outboard engine's propeller dropped off. The vessel was rescued by another search and rescue vessel.
The vessel lost engine power and propulsion and required assistance as it was in the open sea.
A fishing vessel with two crew onboard suffered engine failure. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene and towed the vessel to port.
SAR craft was on exercise when it experienced engine failure and was towed back to station by another SAR asset
A search and rescue vessel was towed back to safe water after experiencing propulsion failure due to engine overheating. There were no injuries, pollution or damage.

title: Engine Cooling and Turbocharger Failures from Deferred Maintenance
description:
This theme is characterised by catastrophic mechanical failures of high-stress engine components — principally turbochargers and sea water cooling systems — that were not detected or mitigated prior to failure, resulting in engine fires, loss of propulsion, and unplanned towage. Across all incidents in this cluster, the consistent underlying pattern is not an acute operational error but a latent degradation of critical hardware that reached a failure threshold during normal or moderately demanding operations. The recurrence of these incidents reflects systemic weaknesses in planned maintenance systems (PMS), particularly the absence of condition-based monitoring regimes capable of detecting progressive wear in rotating components such as turbocharger bearings and drive shafts before catastrophic shear or seizure occurs. The reliance on reactive detection — crew observation of smoke, flames, or gauge anomalies — rather than engineered automatic shutdown and fire suppression systems further amplifies the consequences of these latent failures.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant causal pathway in this theme, with procedural gaps in maintenance planning and inspection serving as the primary enabling conditions. In the majority of cases, there is no recorded evidence that turbocharger condition monitoring, bearing inspection, or drive shaft fatigue assessment formed part of a structured maintenance schedule. Human factors, while not primary causes, are nonetheless relevant: the absence of proactive maintenance culture and over-reliance on individual crew vigilance for fire and fault detection represent systemic human and organisational failures at the vessel management level. Environmental conditions — night operations, elevated sea states, and wind loading — consistently appear as aggravating factors that increase mechanical stress on already-degraded components and reduce the speed and effectiveness of crew detection and emergency response. Together, these factors create a risk profile in which individually manageable deficiencies combine to produce preventable machinery casualties.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Parts C, D, and E covering machinery reliability, bilge systems, and fire protection in machinery spaces)

SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction (covering fixed fire detection, automatic shutdown systems, and fire extinguishing arrangements in machinery spaces)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring documented planned maintenance systems, deficiency reporting, and corrective action procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate and audit condition-based monitoring programmes for high-wear rotating components (turbocharger bearings, drive shafts, cooling pump impellers), including scheduled vibration analysis, oil-contamination sampling, and boroscope inspection intervals defined in the vessel's Planned Maintenance System, with compliance records subject to Port State Control and flag State inspection.

Require installation and periodic testing of automatic engine protection systems — including high-temperature shutdown interlocks, loss-of-coolant-flow alarms with automatic load shedding, and turbocharger oil pressure low-pressure trips — to eliminate sole reliance on crew observation as the primary safeguard against thermal runaway and machinery fire escalation.

Establish mandatory pre-departure machinery readiness checks that include verification of turbocharger boost pressure baselines, cooling water flow confirmation, and review of any outstanding maintenance deferrals on propulsion-critical components, supported by a formal 'fit-for-voyage' sign-off by the responsible engineer and documented in the vessel's safety management system.

The skipper put the port engine into neutral and continued on the starboard engine alone. What appeared to be white smoke was reported to be coming from the port engine room as well as the smell of fiber glass melting. The fire alarm activated in the port engine room. CCTV of the

Fishing vessel's turbo blown resulting in a tow from another fishing vessel.  The vessel was engaged in fishing, it was noted via a gauge in the wheelhouse that the turbo boost pressure had dropped followed by the main engine accelerating above normal revs. PRT side turbo on main engine had failed. There were no injuries and no imminent danger.

Crew transfer vessel suffered an engine fire as a result of a faulty turbo. A crew member saw small flames on top of the outboard turbo on the outboard engine and switched both port engines off. They then activated the emergency ventilation shut off and the fire extinguishing system for the engine room. There was no indication of fire re-igniting and no injuries/damage was reported.

A fishing vessel had a main engine turbo failure resulting in an oil leak that flowed into the air cooler of the main engine. The crew shut down the engine as a precaution to prevent overheating. A nearby fishing vessel towed the stricken vessel back to shore. No pollution or injuries reported.

A general cargo vessel was in passage to its next port when an alarm from the main engine that it was slowing down & the crew members noticed an unusual noise with the turbocharger surging. The engines RPM was reduced immediately and the Chief Engineer then ordered to stop the main engine, after making an assessment they managed to make temporary repairs and the vessel able to proceed with its voy

A crew transfer vessel's turbo caught fire, resulting in no injuries or damage. Fire alarm sounded at 0728, the crew noticed it was the turbo and the fire was extinguished at 0735 with no reported damaged to the vessel and no injuries.

An inland waterways passenger vessel had a fire in its engine room. The crew operated the vessel's onboard fire suppression system and commenced boundary cooling until the shore-side fire brigade arrived. The fire was thought to have been caused by a suspected failure of a newly installed turbocharger. There were no signs of fire damage, the turbocharger was changed and the vessel was back in serv

A high speed passenger vessel was alongside at a pier when the fire alarm in the starboard engine room activated. The passengers were evacuated and the crew responded by isolating the space and deploying the fixed fire suppressant system. There was reportedly a lot of white smoke but no injuries or damages. The local fire service attended as a precaution. The source of the fire was reportedly rela

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on a routine training exercise. Mid-exercise a fire was detected in the lagging surrounding the port turbocharger. The fire was contained whilst at sea without further spread. The lagging was able to be removed and the vessel continued on passage back to harbour without further incident or the need for assistance. There were no injuries and, other than to the l

A search and rescue (SAR) hovercraft was paged to persons in water. It launched on service as normal, conducted a short search then stood down after approximately thirty minutes. On return to the station, the crew started to follow normal recovery procedures. The hovercraft was flown to the bottom of the trailer, winch attached, elevation and thrust provided and then winch engaged. As the hovercra

title: Hydraulic System Failures Causing Undetected Marine Oil Pollution
description:
This theme is characterised by the failure of hydraulic system components — including hoses, seals, pipe-to-coupling connections, and valves — across a range of vessel types and operational contexts, resulting in oil releases to the marine environment. A consistent pattern emerges in which mechanical degradation (through vibration fatigue, wear, or seal deterioration) goes undetected prior to failure, reflecting systemic weaknesses in planned maintenance regimes and pre-operational inspection protocols. Critically, in the majority of incidents, the volume of oil discharged could not be quantified, undermining both environmental impact assessment and regulatory compliance obligations under MARPOL. The recurrence of these incidents across cable-laying vessels, tugs, cruise ships, dredgers, and ro-pax ferries indicates that this is not an equipment-type-specific problem but rather a sector-wide vulnerability in how pressurised hydraulic systems on deck machinery are maintained, monitored, and responded to when failures occur.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including deferred maintenance, inadequate inspection frequencies, and the use of components operating beyond their effective service life — form the technical foundation of these incidents. These hardware vulnerabilities are compounded by human and procedural factors, most notably the absence of structured overnight or heavy weather deck monitoring, confirmation bias leading crews to attribute sheens to external sources rather than their own vessel, and the lack of established 'vessel-first' pollution response protocols. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness and heavy weather sea states, repeatedly reduced crew detection capability and delayed intervention, allowing discharges to continue unobserved and rendering post-incident quantification impossible due to seawater dilution. Collectively, these factors reveal a latent organisational tolerance for reactive rather than proactive hydraulic system risk management, which must be addressed through both procedural reform and enhanced technical oversight.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance and integrity of machinery systems)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew operational readiness and watchkeeping duties applicable to deck monitoring)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – planned maintenance systems, hazard identification, and emergency response procedures)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory scheduled inspection and pressure-testing protocols for all exposed deck hydraulic fittings, hoses, and couplings at defined intervals and prior to heavy weather passages, with records retained in the vessel's planned maintenance system and subject to flag state or class audit verification.

Establish a 'vessel-first' pollution response standing order requiring all observed oil sheens in the vicinity of the vessel to be treated as originating from the vessel until positively excluded, with immediate cessation of relevant operations, source investigation, and SOPEP activation — eliminating the confirmation bias that has repeatedly delayed intervention.

Install continuous or periodic automated oil leak detection on high-risk hydraulic circuits associated with deck machinery, winches, and ride control systems, supported by real-time flow or pressure loss monitoring to enable rapid quantification and early crew alert, particularly during night-time operations or periods when manual deck rounds are impractical due to weather conditions.

representative_cases.description: A cable laying vessel reported that hydraulic oil had leaked from their plough. The plough was recovered, the quantity of oil that had leaked was unknown at the time.

A tug was operating in a harbour when the crew noticed there was a leak of hydraulic oil under pressure from the aft winch. The pumps were immediately shut down and the crew contained as much of the oil as possible, it was estimated that approximately ten litres of oil was lost into the harbour.

A cruise ship was on passage during heavy weather when a small pipe from hydraulic system on the mooring deck disconnected from the coupling, releasing an unknown amount of hydraulic oil over the mooring deck. The system was not under pressure, the sheen of oil was observed in the morning and reported. Due to the very rough seas, sea water entered the mooring station deck, making it impossible to

A dredger was engaged in dredging operations at near a berth when a sheen of oil became visible on the surface around the stern of the vessel. As the vessel was operating alongside an oil berth this was assumed to be related, the dredger proceeded across the river whilst stopped it was visible that the oil was originating from the vessel. An arrangement was made for the vessel to head to a berth t

A ro-pax cargo vessel had hydraulic oil appear in the water not far from the vessel, a SOPEP response was requested, and which contained the oil successfully with the vessel's mooring rope. The contained area was successfully cleaned up with absorbent pads, booms and the originally source of the leak was identified and isolated in the end. However, approximately five litres of hydraulic oil had ma

A dredger was depositing in a spoil ground using the bottom doors. Once finished the doors were taking longer than normal to close when the low level hydraulic oil alarm sounded, crew proceeded on deck to investigate where it was found that the through deck hydraulic oil pipe had been holed. The hydraulic system was isolated and the pumps stopped.  Estimated loss of hydraulic oil is 1000 litres. R

A replenishment tanker was alongside when an minor oil spill occurred whilst carrying out testing of the ships port RIB rescue boat, a small amount of oil ran out of the davit while the rescue boat was swung out, the oil dropped onto the deck and down the ship's side via a loose scupper plug. Subsequent inspection found a loose hydraulic hose which was tightened up. Port authorities notified.

Potential pollution event occurred during routine discharge of laundry grey water from the Starboard Laundry Tank. Laundry drain tanks are in proximity to a previous Contained Oil Leakage that occurred on the 31st January. Upon inspection the Environmental Officer and Chief Engineer observed that the “Starboard Laundry Tank” appeared to contain a very slight amount of oil within. The dedicated dis

A passenger vessel experienced a loss of hydraulic oil. During the start up procedures, a low level oil alarm activated on the stern thruster. Checks were carried out and it was found that the hydraulic oil level in the tank was low. Checks were carried out internally and externally to find where the oil had gone. It was found that oil was leaking to sea.

A visible sheen was noticed coming from the vessel's stern due to a failure of the stern tube seal while moored alongside, resulting in approximately one litre of lube oil being lost in a twenty-four hour period. An oil Teflon additive was added internally to recoat the seal and the vessel proceeded to a dry dock, where repairs were to be effected. No further visible oil loss was reported during t

title: Environmental Pollution from Inadequate Discharge and Spill Controls
description: This theme is characterised by repeated failures to prevent or contain the uncontrolled release of pollutants — including oil, contaminated bilge water, and untreated sewage — into coastal and harbour environments. The incidents share a common pattern of non-compliance with MARPOL obligations, whether through deliberate circumvention, procedural inadequacy, or operational inattention during routine but high-risk activities such as bunkering, line-blowing, and sewage discharge. Across the cluster, neither adverse weather nor mechanical catastrophe was the primary driver; rather, incidents occurred during manageable or benign conditions, indicating that systemic deficiencies in crew training, safety management system (SMS) enforcement, and environmental compliance culture are the root causes. The recurring nature of these events across vessel types — bulk carriers, passenger ro-ro vessels, cruise ships, and service vessels — underscores a fleet-wide and industry-wide problem rather than isolated lapses.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
SOLAS Chapter XII – Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers
SOLAS Chapter II-2 – Construction: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (fuel handling and containment relevance)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory automated tank level monitoring and high-level alarm systems with independent overflow shutoff interlocks for all bunkering operations, supplemented by enforced procedural checklists requiring documented sounding checks at defined intervals and pre-operation verification of all deck containment systems, including scupper bungs and save-all trays.

Integrate geo-fenced automated discharge control systems on vessels generating sewage or operating in environmentally sensitive coastal zones, ensuring that discharge operations cannot be initiated unless positional compliance with MARPOL restricted zones is electronically confirmed, with override actions requiring senior officer authorisation and mandatory logging.

Conduct regular, unannounced MARPOL compliance audits and targeted crew competency assessments focusing on pollution prevention responsibilities, environmental emergency response procedures, and the consequences of non-compliance; findings should be fed back into SMS reviews and reported at fleet management level to identify and address systemic cultural or procedural deficiencies across the operating fleet.

representative_cases.description: Discharge of contaminated bilge water into the harbour in Dover.

A bulk carrier crew were carrying out bunkering of Low-Sulphur Marine Gas-Oil (LSMGO) whilst alongside. The crew were monitoring soundings as the tank was filling however the tank overflowed. The crew activated the emergency stop button and initiated oil spill response procedures. The save all tray overflowed onto the deck and then some of the LSMGO seeped through a crack in the fish plate and int
A cruise ship discharged approximately ten cubic metres of sea water contaminated with untreated sewage during a flushing operation. This occurred within the twelve nautical mile zone from the shore.
Passenger Ro-Ro, oil from overflow tank caused oil to enter the water resulting in pollution.

A service vessel had a spill of marine gas oil (MGO) on the deck. The approximate amount was less than five litres with a small amount entering the water and a sheen seen on the surface. The pollution was left to disperse naturally.

A container vessel had an oil spill whilst alongside. The crew were blowing through the lines after bunkering however there was some oil in the lines which came out through the overflow onto the deck. A small amount of oil entered the water through a leaky bung. The crew contained and cleaned up the spillage on board and the oil that went overboard was cleaned up with the help of a shoreside compa

A ro-pax vessel was alongside in a port waiting for a bunkering operation to commence. The bunker wagon was on the vehicle deck, the driver removed the cap from the bunker hose. There was fuel in the hose and this spilled onto the deck. The flow was quickly stopped and a clean up on deck commenced. Estimate spillage comprised of 40 litres. Following departure, a sheen was reported indicating that
The vessel was alongside in a harbour when it was required to do an internal transfer and 100 litres of red diesel spilled onto the deck. The diesel had mostly dissipated and booms were also used.
Supply boat reported fluid spraying out of a hose during offloading.  Approximately 50l of chemical was lost to the sea. The failure point was thought to be a quick release coupling.

A trailing suction hopper dredger was taking on fresh water bunkers whilst alongside when an overflow of freshwater mixed with lube oil was observed coming from one of the lube oil overflow vents. The freshwater bunkering was immediately stopped and the crew attempted to contain the spillage but about 50ltrs of lube oil/water mixture went into the harbour.

title: Electrical and Battery System Fires Aboard Marine Vessels
description:
This theme encompasses a recurring pattern of fire and explosion events originating from electrical systems, battery banks, and charging equipment across a diverse range of vessel types — from commercial workboats and narrowboats to passenger cruise ships and cargo vessels. The common characteristic is the failure of an electrical or battery component, most frequently manifesting as thermal runaway, short circuit, overcharging, or gas accumulation, in an environment where early detection and suppression capabilities are absent or inadequate.
