A consistent and critical finding across these incidents is the reliance on third parties — adjacent vessel crews, marina staff, or passing members of the public — to identify fire outbreaks, rather than onboard automated detection systems or vigilant watchkeepers. This delayed detection systematically allows localised and potentially containable electrical faults to escalate into fully developed fires, explosions, and total vessel losses, with associated risk to life including responding emergency personnel.

The latent hardware vulnerabilities underlying these incidents include inadequate battery management systems (BMS), poorly designed or maintained charging infrastructure, insufficient ventilation in battery compartment spaces, and the inherent flammability of vessel construction materials such as timber. These hardware deficiencies are compounded by procedural failures: unattended overnight charging without safeguards, absence of routine inspection regimes for battery installations, lack of vessel-specific emergency response protocols for electric or hybrid propulsion systems, and inadequate crew training on the explosion risks posed by hydrogen off-gassing from batteries during charging or thermal events. Environmental factors, particularly night-time conditions, further reduce the probability of early visual detection and complicate emergency response coordination. Collectively, these latent conditions create a systemic vulnerability in which the convergence of a single hardware fault with inadequate detection, suppression, and procedural safeguards produces disproportionately severe outcomes. The increasing proliferation of electric and hybrid propulsion systems across the maritime sector makes resolution of these latent factors an urgent safety priority.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
preventable_by: Mandate the installation of automatic fire and smoke detection systems in all spaces containing battery banks, charging equipment, or electrical propulsion components, with audible and visual alarms capable of alerting both onboard personnel and shore-based watchkeepers when a vessel is unattended, and require these systems to be surveyed and certified as part of vessel safety inspections.

Introduce and enforce vessel-type-specific battery safety management standards requiring documented charging protocols, routine inspection and testing regimes for battery management systems, mandatory ventilation specifications for battery compartment spaces, and prohibitions on unattended charging without approved automatic isolation and suppression safeguards in place.

Develop and disseminate emergency response guidance specific to electric and battery-powered vessels — to be held onboard, lodged with marina operators, and made available to fire and rescue services — detailing explosion risk from hydrogen off-gassing, safe firefighting approaches, and vessel-specific isolation procedures, supplemented by mandatory crew training on the unique hazard profile of electrical propulsion and energy storage systems.

representative_cases.description: A workboat was moored alongside when crew on another vessel noticed smoke coming from the workboat. The crew closed all the fire flaps and accessible hatches and began boundary cooling with available hose pipes. The fire service attended and made the area safe, they ventilated thick smoke from the wheelhouse and accommodation. Two fire crew then entered the vessel to confirm the fire was out. The

A narrowboat with an electric motor experienced a fire. The vessel was alongside in a marina with no crew on board. The boat started smoking and the fire brigade attended. Once they had arrived the boat exploded causing minor injuries to two fire fighters. The vessel sank whilst it was being extinguished.

A wooden pleasure yacht caught fire, the single crewmember on board abandoned to the dinghy and was then rescued by another pleasure sailing yacht and the vessel burned down to the bilges. The wreck settled on a sandbank and was not considered to be a hazard to navigation and would be covered up by the sand after the tides. The fire was reported to have started due to charging an electrical item.

A cruise ship was alongside in port when one of the passengers called for assistance due to a fire on the battery of their electric mobility scooter. There were two batteries that were found to be overheating and the passenger attempted to assist the situation by carrying one of the batteries to an open deck area, but dropped it. The crew managed to contain the situation without further incident a
A cargo vessel's standby battery exploded during testing of the emergency generator. The explosion was due to a build up of gases and a faulty battery. The batteries were replaced and tested weekly.

A recreational motorboat's battery caught fire , no injuries. Coastguard Response Team were tasked to assist the Fire and Rescue Service and patrol with a battery fire aboard a RIB. The fire was extinguished by the FRS.

A motor yacht had an electrical fire which resulted in significant damage to the vessel and eventually to the vessel sinking. All persons were evacuated successfully. There were some minor injuries sustained and there was some pollution when the vessel sank.

Passenger/Ro-Ro vessel, mobile platform fire on car deck resulting in no damage. 	At 0135 whilst the vessel was arriving at Loch Ryan Port, an Able Seaman reported smoke coming from the ship’s Mobile elevating work platform (MEWP) stored on the car deck: deck 3, forward.  The AB, after raising the alarm, started fighting the fire with a fire extinguisher/rigging hoses for fire teams. Vessel raised
Ro/Ro passenger vessel batteries exploded inside the battery box upon starting the generator. No persons were injured and there was no damage to the vessel.

A research vessel crew heard a bang which was followed by a pre-warning alarm on the fire detection system and the smell of smoke. The alarm panel indicated the fire head in the mezzanine deck had been activated. A crew member reported the cause to be from a stainless steel frame that was used for research in the water. The battery casing on one of the sensors had exploded with signs of scorching

title: High-Pressure System Injuries from Inadequate Hazard Controls
description:
This theme is characterised by serious and potentially life-altering injuries sustained during maintenance operations on pressurised systems — including fuel injectors, hydraulic circuits, and engine enclosures — where personnel were inadequately protected against the specific hazards inherent to those systems. Across all incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: crew members were permitted to work in close proximity to live pressurised systems without formal isolation procedures, without task-specific risk assessments that identified high-pressure injection or chemical exposure as credible injury mechanisms, and without appropriate PPE mandated prior to task commencement. The recurrence of these incidents reflects not individual failure but systemic organisational deficiencies — namely, the absence of robust permit-to-work regimes, LOTO protocols, and hazard-specific training that together would create the procedural barriers necessary to prevent exposure. The underestimation of injuries such as high-pressure fluid injection — which frequently appear superficially minor but cause devastating subcutaneous tissue destruction — further compounds harm by delaying appropriate medical intervention.

Latent hardware factors, including undetected pressurised leaks, degraded engine components permitting toxic residue accumulation, and PPE rated inadequately for the pressures encountered, created the physical conditions for injury. Human factors — including troubleshooting behaviour under live pressure, a lack of hazard awareness regarding injection injuries, and possible normalisation of risk during routine maintenance — removed the behavioural safeguards that might otherwise have interrupted the causal chain. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations in confined machinery spaces, further degraded situational awareness and the ability to detect visible warning signs such as spray mist or discolouration before contact occurred. The involvement of contracted personnel in some incidents introduces an additional layer of risk through potential unfamiliarity with vessel-specific procedures and supervision gaps. Collectively, these latent conditions created environments where injury was a foreseeable consequence of task execution rather than an exceptional event.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Machinery and Electrical Installations (safe design, operation, and maintenance of pressurised machinery systems)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures, including emergency response to injuries sustained during operations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code obligations for risk assessment, safe working procedures, PPE provision, and contractor management)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory pressure isolation and lockout/tagout (LOTO) procedures for all maintenance tasks involving pressurised fuel, hydraulic, and pneumatic systems, with formal permit-to-work documentation required before any work commences — explicitly prohibiting any manual intervention in the spray or discharge path of any system under live pressure.

Introduce task-specific, hazard-identified risk assessments for all pressurised system maintenance operations that explicitly name high-pressure fluid injection and chemical exposure (e.g. hexavalent chromium) as credible injury mechanisms, mandating appropriate PPE — including injection-resistant gloves and respiratory protection — to be donned before any enclosure or system is opened, not after hazards are visually identified.

Deliver targeted crew and contractor training on the specific and serious medical consequences of high-pressure injection injuries and hazardous substance exposure, including the deceptive initial presentation of such injuries, combined with clear protocols requiring immediate medical escalation and communication of injury mechanism to treating clinicians whenever pressurised system contact is suspected.

representative_cases.description: A cable layer crew member was working on some new fuel injector nozzles carrying out a pressure adjustment in line with manufacturer guidelines. When adjusting the opening pressure for the nozzles, the crew member observed that the nozzle was not opening. The crew member put their fingers into the path of the potential spray pattern when the injector nozzle opened releasing the high pressure calib
Vessel engineer and contracted engineer replaced a cylinder head cover that over pressurised and blew out causing injuries to the contracted engineer.

A replenishment tanker crew member was carrying out maintenance on an engine to investigate an exhaust leak. When they removed the covers they found yellow residue that suggested the presence of hexavalent chromium. They donned PPE and carried out a test to confirm the presence of the substance. The area was secured and the substance was cleared up.

A fishing vessel was alongside in harbour with the nets being pulled ashore by the crew. A hydraulic oil leak, under force, cut through the crewmember's glove. The casualty had a cut finger and the oil went into the wound which resulted in the hand becoming swollen. The casualty had two hospital operations to clean the oil out of their hand and was repatriated to a local fisherman's mission after
Standby safety vessel's crew member suffered high pressure injury to the finger. Cas was air medivac'd to hospital

A crewmember was in the process of securing a fuel hose to a helicopter on the flight deck. As they went to make the connection, the hose became pressurised. The hose kicked and jarred the crewmember's shoulder as they restrained it. The crewmember dislocated their shoulder as a result.

An oil products tanker was alongside and the crew were carrying out general ship board maintenance when approximately 5L of gas oil accidentally spilt overboard. The oil created a sheen on the water however it began to naturally disperse soon after after being agitated by passing vessels.

A service vessel's crew member got pressurised oil in the face. When removing the filter itself from the housing, a large quantity of oil, under pressure was ejected from the filter housing and was inadvertently directed towards the crew member while trying to replace the lid, which could not be done because of the seal becoming damaged.

A vehicle carrier crew member was connecting an oxy-acetylene hose to the oxygen cylinder for gas cutting. Upon opening the oxygen cylinder valve, the hose slipped out from the connection and hit the crew member under the chin. As a result of the impact, the crew member sustained a laceration to the underside of their jaw behind the chin. First aid was administered and radio medical advice sought.

Fish factory crew member suffered injuries to his foot, the crew member was using a water jet to clean out a tank when some water sprayed in his face causing him to look away, he believes that when he looked away he accidentally passed the waterjet over his foot. The water tore through his wellington resulting in a laceration between his first and second toe. The crew member was immediately taken

title: Passenger Falls Compounded by Alcohol Impairment and Vessel Motion
description:
This cluster is characterised by passenger fall and injury incidents aboard cruise ships and ferries in which the convergence of voluntary alcohol intoxication, ambient vessel motion, and environmental conditions such as night-time lighting and confined spaces consistently produce serious physical harm. The incidents share a common latent architecture: passengers in a physiologically compromised state — whether through intoxication, pre-existing injury, or fatigue — are exposed to the inherent dynamic instability of a marine environment without adequate procedural safeguards or crew intervention. No hardware defects were identified in any case, indicating that the risk is not infrastructural in origin but rather systemic and behavioural. These incidents recur because the predictable interaction between alcohol-impaired coordination and vessel motion is not being addressed through proactive risk management at the operational level.

While hardware factors are largely absent, the role of human and procedural latent causes is central to this theme. Alcohol consumption — in several cases reaching clinically significant levels — impairs balance, proprioception, and judgment, all of which are essential for safe movement aboard a vessel subject to rolling and pitching. Environmental conditions including low lighting, confined bathroom spaces, stairwells, and elevator thresholds amplify the consequences of even minor losses of balance. The procedural gap is most significant: there is a consistent absence of effective passenger safety briefings targeting alcohol-related mobility risk, no evidence of crew protocols for identifying and assisting visibly intoxicated passengers in high-risk transit areas, and no structured responsible-service-of-alcohol frameworks being applied. The cumulative effect is that a foreseeable and preventable chain of events — intoxication, vessel motion, loss of balance, contact with hard surface — is permitted to complete itself without interruption.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (as it underpins broader passenger safety environment standards and vessel design safety obligations)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (covering passenger muster, safety briefings and onboard passenger safety communication obligations)

SOLAS Chapter IV / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (requiring operators to identify foreseeable risks and implement procedural safeguards through the Safety Management System)
preventable_by: Implement a structured Responsible Service of Alcohol (RSA) policy integrated into the vessel's Safety Management System, requiring bar and hospitality crew to monitor passenger consumption levels, refuse service to visibly intoxicated passengers, and notify designated safety-trained crew to escort or assist high-risk individuals transiting through staircases, elevators, and other fall-risk areas.

Mandate targeted in-voyage passenger safety communications — delivered via cabin cards, digital signage at elevator lobbies and bar exits, and verbal reminders from crew — specifically warning passengers of the compounded risk of alcohol consumption and vessel motion, advising use of handrails, footwear on deck, and requesting crew assistance when needed, particularly during night-time hours or adverse sea conditions.

Conduct systematic risk assessments of high-traffic passenger transit areas (elevator thresholds, stairwells, bathroom layouts, corridor handrail continuity) to confirm that grab-rail provision, non-slip surfacing, and lighting levels meet the needs of a passenger population that may include individuals with impaired mobility or coordination, and establish minimum inspection and audit frequencies for these features under the SMS.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was in their cabin preparing for bed when they lost their balance and fell against a cabinate in the bathroom area. This resulted in a broken shoulder bone that was treated on board. The passenger had consumed 8 'small' glasses of wine and had a previous injury to their wrist, which was in a cast at the time of the incident. They had bare feet, the lighting in the area was

Cruise ship's passenger twisted their ankle while stepping out of the elevator. They sustained a closed fracture. No defects were found in the area and the alcoholic drinks may be a contributing factor.

A cruise ship passenger was walking from a lift to a restaurant when they slipped and as they fell hit their head.  The passenger suffered a contusion to the head was referred ashore for a possible cranial haemorrhage. The area was inspected with no defects found. The vessel was moving slightly and movement was considered to be a contributory factor along with alcohol consumption by the guest prec

A cruise ship passenger was walking down a staircase when their knee buckled and they lost their balance but didn't fall, sustaining a ligament/tendon rupture to their upper right leg. The passenger was able and had no previous injuries or ailments and did not use glasses. They had purchased 11 alcoholic drinks on the day of the accident. The lighting in the area of the incident was artificial and

A passenger of a ferry fell down a staircase on board and sustained a head injury, requiring ambulance assistance and hospital attendance. The passenger sustained bone fractures and injuries to the head, ribs and spine. They were taken to a shoreside hospital for treatment. There were no witnesses to the fall, but the passenger had consumed alcohol before the incident.

A passenger on a cruise ship fell whilst they were asking a crew member for directions. As they were walking, they fell over and landed on their left side, dislocating their shoulder. The passenger had some health problems which may have contributed to the fall. The passenger also reported that they had consumed alcohol before the accident happened.

A cruise ship passenger had been in a bar consuming alcoholic beverages for around two hours when they tried to turn and fell down landing on the left arm face down on the deck. The passenger does not remember the details but there was CCTV footage showing the passenger at the bar. A crew member on duty called the medical team who arrived shortly afterwards and took the passenger to the Medical Ce

A passenger was dancing in a nightclub, they went over on their ankle resulting in a fracture. They attended the onboard medical centre for treatment. The passenger had also consumed a large quantity of alcohol which could have contributed to the fall. No other defects were found in relation to issues with floor tiles that were situated in the disco.

A motor cruiser was alongside when a person onboard fell and sustained an injury to their head that required the assistance of the ambulance service. The person was confirmed to have been under the influence of alcohol at the time of the accident.

A cruise ship passenger was walking back to their cabin assisted by a friend as the passenger was unsteady on their feet, which the vessel assumed was due to alcohol. As they approached the door to their cabin the passenger lost their balance and fell to the floor. A medic attended with the passenger being transferred to the medical centre by stretcher where they were treated for two fractured ank

title: Propulsion Failure in Tidal, Confined Waters at Night
description:
This theme is characterised by mechanical propulsion or hydraulic failures occurring at the most operationally vulnerable moments — immediately upon departure or at critical navigational chokepoints — in environments where tidal currents, confined waterways, and reduced visibility compound the consequences of any loss of control. Across all incidents in this cluster, the common thread is not simply that machinery failed, but that the failure occurred before the vessel had established a safe navigational envelope, leaving crews with insufficient time, sea room, or residual capability to arrest drift or regain control. The recurrence of these incidents points to systemic deficiencies in pre-departure risk governance: departure decisions are routinely made without adequate contingency planning for propulsion loss in tidal or confined areas, and maintenance regimes are failing to detect latent faults before they manifest at critical moments.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including astern gear faults, unresolved engine faults, and hydraulic system failures — represent the proximate mechanical triggers in each case, but their significance is amplified by the absence of robust pre-departure inspection protocols that would surface such faults before vessels enter high-risk environments. Human factors, including underestimation of tidal set, poor departure timing relative to tidal state, and a failure to define abort criteria prior to getting underway, consistently reduce the window available for effective emergency response. Environmental conditions — ebbing tidal currents, night-time darkness, and wind — then act as force multipliers, accelerating drift towards fixed structures, mooring fields, or shallow water and preventing crews from stabilising the situation unaided. The pattern reveals a systemic gap at the intersection of maintenance governance, voyage risk assessment, and contingency planning, none of which individually caused these incidents but whose collective absence made escalation from minor fault to serious casualty almost inevitable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E — Additional requirements for machinery, boiler and electrical installations, including pre-departure operational readiness)

SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34 — Safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including voyage and passage planning accounting for tidal conditions and contingency measures)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requiring documented procedures for critical machinery checks, risk assessment, and emergency contingency planning)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure engine and propulsion system checks in confined or tidal environments, requiring positive confirmation of ahead and astern gear engagement, hydraulic system pressure, and control response before lines are singled up, with a formal 'go/no-go' decision gate recorded in the vessel log prior to departure.

Require voyage and passage plans for all departures from tidal berths or confined waterways to explicitly address tidal state, current risk, and propulsion contingency measures — including predefined abort criteria, emergency anchoring positions, and tug or towage assistance requirements — as a standard element of the SMS risk assessment process.

Implement a condition-based and interval-based maintenance programme for all propulsion-critical systems, including astern gear mechanisms, engine mounts, fuel systems, and hydraulics, with mandatory defect reporting and resolution timelines that prevent known or suspected latent faults from being carried into operational service without documented risk acceptance at a senior management level.

representative_cases.description: As the passenger ship departed a failure occurred on the starboard engine which resulted in manoeuvring difficulties. The vessel was stuck in astern gear where due to the tidal conditions it drifted between two sets of moorings. The vessel caught a mooring line with the starboard propeller and was stuck over the mooring itself. Due to this occurrence the passenger ship made contact with a fishing

A recreational motor vessel had just left a berth when it experienced a brief engine failure. The current of the ebbing tide then caught the vessel and pinned it against the underside of a nearby bridge. The vessel was stuck there for about twenty minutes before another vessel came and towed them to safety. The vessel sustained minor damage to its mast, fiberglass roof and a handrail. The engine w

A sailing yacht had an engine failure and grounded on a mud bank at the entrance to a river. The tide was ebbing and there was concern as the bilge keel yacht had started to list heavily with seven crew onboard. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The yacht had started to float free with the flood tide by the time the SAR vessel had arrived, the crew remained aboard and the yach

Crew transfer vessel was transferring members to a windfarm turbine when it was noticed that the engines were not responding as expected. The vessel then landed onto the push tubes. The vessel has checked for any damage or water ingress. It has been noticed that there was damage on the top part of the fendering system, port bow and signs of delamination of the GRP.

A tanker began to come off its moorings after the hydraulics failed meaning that the vessel could not tension its lines. The crew requested two tugs and a pilot which moved it from the berth to an anchorage to carry out repairs.

During anchorage while waiting for the tide and pilot boarding a small fishing vessel approached from the bow reporting engine failure, fishing dropped there anchor but due to strong current started to drift rapidly. The fishing vessel remained just clear of the tanker resulting in no contact or damage to either vessel.

A trailing hopper suction dredger had a problem with the discharge gear meaning that they needed to leave the berth in a hurry, to avoid grounding. The port mobilized a pilot and departure proceedings as quickly as they could and the vessel was able to depart early and without touching the bottom. There were no damages, injuries or pollution.

A cargo vessel with a pilot onboard was proceeding along a channel when it had a main engine failure and the vessel began to sheer to starboard. The crew turned the helm hard to port and readied the anchors. The port bow thruster was used but the vessel continued to sheer to starboard. The starboard anchor was let go and the shore authorities informed of the situation and other vessels told to kee

An inshore tug was attempting to berth when its starboard engine failed and the vessel struck the quayside. The tide was high which meant that the hull of the vessel struck where there was no fendering and sustained minor damage in the form of dents. There were no injuries or pollution. The classification society of the vessel attended shortly after the incident and confirmed that the damage was m

A bulk carrier was departing a port, the vessel experienced engine failure as it was approaching a turn. The vessel was able to make the turn. An anchor was deployed, it was at the top of a flood tide. The vessel may have briefly touched the sides of the channel but there was no noticeable drop in speed. Two tugs assisted in pushing the vessel back into the channel so it could proceed to anchorage

title: Recreational Vessel Propulsion Failure and Inadequate Emergency Preparedness
description:
This theme is characterised by recreational and small commercial vessels experiencing propulsion system failures — encompassing engine, gearbox, battery, and drivetrain degradation — that render the vessel unable to manoeuvre, typically at night and in coastal waters proximate to navigational hazards. The recurring nature of these incidents reflects a systemic pattern rather than isolated equipment misfortune: vessels are departing without adequate pre-departure mechanical checks, without verified emergency equipment readiness, and without crew possessing the seamanship competency to execute secondary contingencies such as anchor deployment. The consequence in each case is an escalation from a recoverable equipment failure to a Search and Rescue tasking, with SAR resources bearing the operational burden of risk that was generated ashore, before departure.

Latent hardware deficiencies — undetected gearbox wear, battery degradation, and fuel or drivetrain faults — form the proximate mechanical cause across this cluster, but their significance is compounded by the consistent absence of effective maintenance regimes and pre-departure inspection practices capable of identifying developing faults before sea. Human factors, including gaps in seamanship knowledge and failure to deploy anchors as a station-keeping measure while awaiting assistance, indicate that crew preparedness is a critical and undertreated vulnerability. Environmental conditions, specifically night-time operation in moderate wind and swell, systematically elevated the consequence of each mechanical failure by reducing situational awareness, accelerating drift toward hazards, and complicating SAR coordination. The combination of latent hardware degradation, procedural deficiency, and adverse operating environment created conditions in which foreseeable and preventable failures produced serious SAR incidents.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance and reliability of propulsion and electrical systems)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (availability and readiness of emergency equipment including anchors and distress signalling)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (safe manning, voyage planning, and operator competency for vessels operating in coastal and near-shore environments)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure safety checklists for all recreational and small commercial vessels that explicitly require verification of propulsion system condition, battery charge status, anchor deployment readiness, and fuel sufficiency — with targeted enforcement and education campaigns delivered through marina operators, vessel registration authorities, and coastal safety organisations.

Introduce minimum seamanship competency standards for recreational vessel operators that include demonstrated knowledge of emergency anchoring procedures, loss-of-propulsion contingencies, and distress communication protocols, with particular emphasis on the elevated risk profile of night-time and coastal operations near hazards.

Establish and promote formal guidance requiring vessels intending to operate after dark or in coastal waters near navigational hazards to carry a serviceable, rigged, and readily deployable anchor as a mandatory condition of departure, supported by periodic vessel safety inspections and harbour authority enforcement at points of departure.

representative_cases.description: A recreational vessel drifted towards rock, search and rescue towed vessel to safety. Search and rescue were tasked to assist a motor vessel that was drifting towards rocks with 3 POB. SAR took vessel under tow and recovered it back to marina.

A recreational motor cruiser experienced propulsion failure. The vessel dropped anchor and called for assistance. Initially a commercial vessel offered their services but subsequently were unable to render assistance and instead the local lifeboat service launched and towed the vessel back to port after about an hour of waiting. There were no injuries, pollution or damages reported.

A recreational fishing vessel with two crew onboard reported a gearbox failure, the vessel was drifting as the anchor was unable to be deployed. Both crew were wearing lifejackets. A search and rescue (SAR) was tasked to assist, the stricken vessel was towed back to its mooring. No reported injuries to crew or damage to vessel.

A recreational fishing vessel experienced an engine and battery failure. The two crew onboard were not in immediate danger, a PANPAN was sent out. With no vessels in the vicinity, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel towed the stricken vessel to a harbour.

A stern trawler was on passage to port when they experienced propulsion failure. The crew transmitted a VHF call for assistance and there were no other vessels in the vicinity who could set up a tow, so the local search and rescue service launched a lifeboat that towed the vessel back to port. There were no injuries, pollution or damage.

A tug had engine failure. The vessel was tied off to a fish farm cage but due to the deteriorating weather a Pan Pan was issued by the crew. A search and rescue vessel towed the tug to port. There were no injuries to crew and no damage to the vessel was reported.

A sailing yacht experienced propulsion failure and was becalmed, there was no water ingress or damage. The vessel transmitted a request for assistance resulting in a sail training vessel assisting by towing the sailing yacht to a harbour.

A recreational motor boat experienced power failure and anchored in the vicinity of an offshore windfarm. The lone skipper was unable to recover the anchor and requested assistance from the Coastguard, due to concerns that the vessel would drift if the anchor was dropped. A windfarm vessel responded to the calls for assistance and towed the vessel back to port.

A yacht with 1POB experienced engine failure approximately 300m from harbor and was towed back to the marina by the local lifeboat service. The other vessels around did not consent to give towage assistance to the vessel. There were no injuries, pollution or damage reported.

A small motor vessel with 3 POB lost propulsion power and required assistance to safety. The vessels around them were not willing to tow them back to port, the local lifeboat service towed them back to port. There were no injuries, pollution or damage reported.

title: Passenger Falls from Environmental and Procedural Hazards
description:
This theme is characterised by passenger falls and injuries arising from a confluence of physical hazard exposure, inadequate passive safeguarding, and procedural gaps in passenger risk management. Incidents within this cluster share a common pattern: a foreseeable environmental or structural hazard — such as a cabin balcony door threshold, an ergonomically deficient furniture arrangement, an unsecured seating area, or a congested buffet space — interacts with a vulnerable or inattentive passenger to produce a preventable injury. The recurrence of these incidents reflects systemic failures rather than isolated lapses; specifically, the absence of rigorous hazard identification processes targeting passenger-accessible spaces, the lack of targeted safety interventions for passengers with known medical vulnerabilities, and insufficient supervision and enforcement of safe conduct in public areas. Secondary procedural failures — including delays in passengers seeking medical attention and inadequate orientation to onboard facilities and hazards — further compound outcomes by deferring treatment and preventing timely injury assessment.

Latent hardware deficiencies play a foundational role across this theme, manifesting as unguarded threshold step changes, furniture configurations that create uncontrolled transition hazards, and seating or environmental features presenting puncture or impact risks upon contact. These physical conditions are consistently exacerbated by environmental factors, most notably low-light or night-time conditions that impair spatial awareness and hazard perception, and in some cases by vessel motion that introduces an additional destabilising variable. Human factors — including passenger medical conditions such as Encephalopathy that affect balance and coordination, distracted behaviour such as mobile phone use on stairways, and unfamiliarity with cabin or vessel layouts — amplify the probability of an adverse outcome when latent hazards are encountered. The systemic root cause across this cluster is the absence of an integrated safety management approach that links passenger medical risk disclosure to proactive onboard safety interventions, mandates engineering controls at known trip hazard locations, and sustains a visible, enforced safety culture throughout passenger-accessible spaces.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural design standards relevant to passenger spaces, thresholds, and access arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings, muster procedures, and onboard safety orientation obligations)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — SMS requirements for hazard identification, risk assessment, procedural controls, and crew responsibility in passenger safety management)
preventable_by: Implement a structured Passenger Medical Risk Disclosure and Safety Intervention Protocol: at embarkation, medical declarations flagging conditions affecting mobility, balance, or cognition should trigger a documented safety response, including targeted cabin hazard briefings, provision of assistive equipment, and where appropriate, proactive crew monitoring arrangements, ensuring that passenger vulnerability is actively integrated into onboard risk management.

Conduct systematic engineering reviews of all passenger-accessible threshold, stairway, and furniture transition points, mandating anti-trip nosing strips with high-visibility contrast markings, minimum luminance standards for night-time wayfinding lighting in cabins and public spaces, and ergonomic furniture placement guidelines that maintain safe clearance and accessible grab support at predictable movement transition zones.

Establish and enforce a Passenger Area Safety Supervision Standard within the vessel's Safety Management System, incorporating defined crew patrol frequencies in high-risk public spaces such as buffet areas and recreational zones, enforceable safe conduct policies with clear crew authority to intervene, crowd capacity thresholds for congestion-prone areas with active monitoring protocols, and a mandatory passenger medical presentation awareness campaign delivered at embarkation encouraging early reporting of injuries to onboard medical staff.

representative_cases.description: Passenger was found laid down in cabin balcony by spouse  who immediately called for assistance, upon the arrival of the medical team, the guest stated that as they was moving from the cabin to the balcony, then tripped on the balcony door threshold, and landed on the balcony. The injured guest was taken to the onboard medical centre and a fracture of the right hip was diagnosed. As the injured gu

A cruise ship passenger got out of bed and rounded the corner of the desk and lost their balance resulting in a fall and a fractured hand. This diagnosis was later in the day after they had returned from an excursion.

A passenger on board a ferry sustained a puncture wound to their leg. Reportedly, they were injured whilst playing on a chair and jumping, when they fell. After seeking medical advice, the crew arranged for the passenger to be disembarked to a search and rescue vessel which took the passenger ashore where they went to hospital.

A cruise ships passenger fell resulting in a closed fracture and dislocated shoulder. The guest stated that the buffet area was crowded with guests. Doesn't recall if accidentally pushed from behind by an unidentified guest or if they tripped, which caused the fall forward and landed into the floor on there right shoulder.

A cruise ship passenger was descending the stairs whilst looking at their mobile phone when they missed a step, fell forward and landed on the floor twisting their ankle. The passenger later reported to the medical centre for treatment for a fractured ankle but has remained onboard. The area was inspected no obvious defects been observed.

A cruise ship passenger was leaning against the bulkhead when a door close by opened and knocked them over. They fell to the deck and sustained a fractured humerus. They were leaning against the bulkhead because they suffer from vertigo. They had consumed two pints of beer before hand. The cause of the incident was reported to be due to choosing a risky place to lean against the bulkhead because i

A ro-pax vessel was alongside embarking passengers, a passenger walking in a passenger area tripped on the edge of a chair leg, fell and struck their shoulder. The passenger initially confirmed to a crew member they felt ok and was not in need of further assistance, though they then laid down on a seat to rest, the crew member maintained an occasional observation of the passenger during the crossi

A cruise ship passenger fell and fractured their wrist in their cabin. The passenger entered their cabin after being ashore, they stated they had consumed at least 5 pints of beer. As they entered the bathroom, they tripped on the threshold and fell to the floor and sustained a fracture to the left wrist.

A cruise ship passenger tripped on her chair leg in the bar deck and fell on her left side resulting in a fractured femur. The casualty was admitted to hospital at their next port of call. Alcohol may have been a factor.

A passenger was sitting on the floor just outside the toilet and was found by crew. The passenger could not recall if they fell or how they ended up on the floor. They were taken to the medical centre on board.  A fracture of the left neck of the femur was confirmed by an x-ray and they were medically disembarked off the vessel and taken by ambulance to the hospital.

title: Passenger Stairway Falls Under Night and Sea Motion
description:
This theme is characterised by passenger falls on vessel staircases occurring predominantly at night and in mild-to-moderate sea states, resulting in injuries ranging from fractures to, in one case, loss of life. The recurring nature of these incidents suggests that no single discrete failure is responsible; rather, they arise from the convergence of environmental conditions — reduced visibility at night and low-amplitude vessel motion — with individual human factors such as unfamiliarity with shipboard movement, inappropriate footwear, and divided attention. Critically, post-incident inspections consistently found no physical defects, which risks masking the true hazard: the stairway environment as designed may be adequate under static, daytime conditions but inadequate under the dynamic operating conditions that are routinely encountered in service.

The latent hardware dimension centres on stairway design elements — step nosing visibility, handrail continuity, tread grip, and localised step lighting — which may meet baseline standards yet fail to provide sufficient compensation when vessel motion and low-light conditions interact. Human factors include passengers' inherent inability to anticipate or adapt to shipboard dynamics without prior experience or adequate briefing, compounded by the absence of consistent, operationally specific safety communication regarding staircase use in variable conditions. Procedurally, the evidence suggests that passenger safety briefings do not reliably address context-specific risks such as night-time movement or staircase use in sea states above calm. The fatality within this cluster underscores that what is routinely classified as a minor or less serious event category carries a potential severity that existing preventive measures are not reliably mitigating.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction — Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (Regulation 13: Means of Escape — stairway design, lighting, and non-slip surface requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19: Emergency Training and Drills — passenger safety briefing obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XIV / ISM Code (International Safety Management): Safety Management Systems — risk assessment, hazard identification, and procedural controls for routine passenger movement
preventable_by: Mandate vessel-specific dynamic risk assessments for passenger stairway use that account for operational sea state thresholds, triggering enhanced controls — such as temporary handrail rope guides, crew posting at stairway heads, or passenger movement restrictions — when sea state exceeds a defined threshold (e.g., Sea State 2 or above) or during hours of darkness.

Retrofit or upgrade stairway lighting to include illuminated step-edge nosing strips and low-level continuous lighting on all passenger stairways, ensuring visibility of individual step profiles is maintained independently of ambient lighting conditions, and verify compliance through scheduled operational inspections rather than only post-incident inspection.

Revise mandatory passenger safety briefings to include explicit, operationally specific guidance on staircase use during vessel motion and at night — including demonstrations of correct handrail use, advice on appropriate footwear, and awareness of vessel movement effects on balance — and implement a verification mechanism to confirm passenger comprehension prior to departure from the first port.

representative_cases.description: Cruise ship's passenger was descending the stairs while holding the handrail when they tripped and lost balance. This resulted in a fractured ankle. There were no defects or hazards and the guest was disembarked on the next port.

Cruise ship's passenger was descending a flight of stairs when they fell, landing on their arm and sustaining a closed fracture. They were escorted for further medical assistance. No defects found in the area.

A research vessel passenger fell down a set of stairs and sustained minor head and back injuries. The vessel headed to an anchorage where a coastguard team assisted the passenger off the vessel and passed to a relative who took them to hospital for a medical assessment.

A cruise ship passenger was descending the staircase when they tripped and landed on their left side; their left foot twisted as they fell. The passenger was treated for a fracture to the left ankle. The area was inspected and no obvious defects were found, the area was well lit by artificial light.

Cruise ship's passenger was walking up the stairs when they appeared to be unsteady on their feet. As a result they fell backwards down the stairs and sustained a head and neck injury. The casualty was disembarked for further medical attention however, the injuries sustained resulted in loss of life.

A cruise ship passenger was descending a staircase when they stepped down and sustained a fractured ankle. There were no damages or defects in the staircase area. The passenger was not under the influence of alcohol or medication. The passenger was using the handrail whilst descending. The passenger remained on board for the rest of their cruise even though they had a fractured ankle.

A cruise ship passenger was descending a staircase in an indoor passenger area when they slipped and sustained a fractured patella on their left leg. They sought immediate medical assistance on board the vessel and reported to the medical center. They medically disembarked the vessel. All the steps on the stair case were inspected and were found to be in good condition.

A cruise ship passenger was descending a spiral staircase when they fell down the last four or five steps. They were taken to the medical centre, an x-ray taken and treated for a fracture to the left lower leg. The passenger remains onboard.

A passenger on a cruise ship was walking down a staircase when they caught their heel and fell. They landed on their left shoulder on the deck at the base of the staircase. The passenger had a fracture to the left neck of their humerus bone.

A cruise ship guest tripped on a step while ascending the stairs in the theatre while the ship was on passage in moderate seas. The passenger was assessed by the onboard doctor and diagnosed with a fracture to their right proximal humerus with some swelling.  The guest did not wear prescription eye glasses or contact lenses or have any mobility issues. The area was inspected and found to be clean,

title: Passenger Falls in Cabins and Shared Spaces at Sea
description:
This theme is characterised by passenger falls and injuries occurring within enclosed vessel spaces — notably cabins, spa areas, and sleeping quarters — during routine passages in moderate to adverse sea conditions. The incidents share a recurring pattern in which passengers undertake ordinary, low-risk activities such as sitting, sleeping, or handling luggage, yet sustain serious injuries because the combined operational environment renders these activities unexpectedly hazardous. A common thread is that the sea state at the time of each incident, though frequently classified as slight or moderate in formal reporting, was sufficient to introduce meaningful vessel motion that destabilised unbraced passengers. Night-time conditions further reduced spatial awareness and reaction time, narrowing the window for self-correction. The consistency of this pattern across multiple incident types indicates a systemic, foreseeable risk rather than a series of isolated personal misjudgements, and points to an institutional failure to adequately account for predictable onboard motion in the design and management of passenger-accessible spaces.

The latent hardware and design factors are central to understanding why these incidents recur. Furniture placement adjacent to hard structural surfaces such as bulkheads, the absence of bed retention features such as lee boards or anti-roll bolsters, and the lack of luggage restraint fixtures in cabins collectively create an environment in which ordinary vessel motion can amplify the consequences of minor losses of balance into fractures and serious trauma. These design deficiencies are compounded by a persistent gap in human factors management: passengers consistently received no safety briefing, signage, or advisory specific to the risks of movement during sea passage, nor were they guided to modify behaviour — such as avoiding luggage handling or taking precautions when sleeping — during adverse conditions. Pre-existing passenger vulnerabilities, including mobility impairments, were not factored into risk management protocols. The combined effect of inadequate cabin safety design standards, absent passenger communication systems, and environmental conditions that are routine on ocean passages creates a latent risk profile that is both predictable and preventable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Structural and mechanical safety of accommodation spaces, including furniture securing and cabin design standards
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements — Passenger safety briefings, musters, and communication of onboard hazards

SOLAS Chapter XII / ISM Code (Chapter IX): Safe Management — Operator obligations under the International Safety Management Code to identify and mitigate foreseeable onboard risks through documented procedures and safety culture
preventable_by: Mandate the installation of bed retention features (lee boards, anti-roll bolsters, or adjustable bed rails) in all passenger cabins as a design standard, and require a pre-voyage risk assessment of furniture placement relative to hard structural surfaces such as bulkheads, with remedial repositioning or padding applied where impact injury risk is identified.

Implement a formalised, sea-state-triggered passenger advisory protocol whereby ship officers issue cabin-specific safety guidance — via in-cabin displays, PA announcements, or printed notices — when wave heights exceed 1.0 m, advising passengers to avoid standing unsupported, handling heavy luggage, or sleeping without bed restraints during vessel motion.

Require operators to incorporate passenger vulnerability screening into embarkation procedures so that passengers with declared mobility impairments or balance conditions are offered adapted cabin assignments with enhanced physical safeguards, and are individually briefed by medical or safety staff on fall prevention strategies relevant to the voyage conditions anticipated.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger fell and sustained a broken rib. The passenger was in the spa/swimming pool waiting area when they went to sit down on a lounge couch, but accidentally sat on the edge of the couch, causing them to slip and fall to the deck. When they landed on the floor, they struck their back on the bulkhead, sustaining a broken rib. The on board paramedics attended and the passenger was

Cruise ship's passenger was inside their cabin when they slipped from the bed and landed on the carpet. This resulted in a right distal humerus fracture with elbow dislocation. No defects found in the area.

Passenger ship's guest lost their balance while they had their suitcase on the bed. This resulted in them loosing their balance and fracturing their hand. Furthermore, they also stated that the suitcase fell on their body due to the movement of the ship. There was no defect found.

A cruise ship passenger fell out of bed in the early hours of the morning. The casualty mobility issues but unsure of the reasons why they fell out of bed. The casualty sustained a head injury when falling from bed and was taken to hospital for further treatment.

A cruise ship passenger was stepping out of bed when they fell and fractured their leg. They were not entirely sure why they fell but believed they placed their foot incorrectly when stepping down. Their partner helped them to their feet and they both went to the medical centre on board where the fracture was diagnosed and it was recommended that they disembark at the next port. The passenger had

A cruise ships passenger slipped of bed resulting in closed fracture to the spine. While seated on the bed, suddenly slipped off, landing with there buttocks on the floor. They felt a strong pain on his back and dialled the 911.

A cruise ship passenger was sitting on the end of the bed in a cabin when they inadvertently slipped off falling to the deck. The passenger fell on their left side striking the left side of their head on the adjacent bulkhead resulting in an injury to the scalp and signs of confusion. The passenger was admitted to the medical centre for treatment and later medically disembarked. The area was inspe

A cruise ship passenger was seated in their cabin, they stood up from the chair, walked across the room and tripped on bed sheets that were on the floor. The passenger fell forward and onto their left side, causing a bruise on the left arm and a fracture of the left hip. The passenger was escorted to the medical centre on a stretcher for treatment.  The area was inspected with no defects found.

On board a ro-ro/Passenger vessel, an elderly passenger sustained a broken hip caused by falling from their bed. Shore authorities contacted by vessel's crew to arrange ambulance attendance at destination port in the UK. Once alongside, the injured passenger was successfully disembarked and taken by the ambulance crew.

A cruise ship passenger was walking to the bathroom in their cabin, as they stepped into the bathroom the vessel made a sudden movement that caused the passenger to lose balance and fall forwards. They tried to stop the fall by putting out their left hand but sustained a fracture to the left wrist.

title: Shipboard Door Entrapment and Crush Injury Hazards
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of finger, hand, and wrist entrapment injuries caused by self-closing, watertight, and fire doors across a broad range of vessel types, including inland waterway vessels, utility vessels, ro-ro ferries, and cruise ships. The incidents affect both crew and passengers, occurring predominantly during night hours and spanning a wide spectrum of severity, from minor fractures to partial amputations. What unites these events is not equipment malfunction but rather the convergence of predictable mechanical hazards — high door-closing forces, absence of anti-trap guards, and confined passageways — with routine shipboard activity. The frequency and consistency of this pattern across vessel categories and operators indicates a systemic, industry-wide failure to treat door entrapment as a managed engineering risk rather than an acceptable operational inevitability.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a primary enabling condition: the near-universal absence of passive anti-trap devices, finger guards, or slow-close mechanisms on shipboard doors means that the consequences of any momentary lapse in attention are immediately severe. Human factors compound this risk significantly. Night-time operations reduce spatial awareness and depth perception, fatigue from irregular watch schedules impairs reaction time, and the habitual, repetitive nature of door usage fosters complacency, eroding the vigilance that procedural controls depend upon. Non-deck crew, including hotel and service personnel on passenger vessels, are particularly vulnerable due to gaps in safety induction and lower rates of PPE compliance. Environmental conditions, including vessel motion in even moderate sea states, introduce dynamic instability that can cause crew and passengers to reach for or lean against doors at the moment of closure. Collectively, these factors create a high-frequency, high-consequence hazard that is disproportionately under-engineered relative to its documented injury burden.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (watertight door design, operation, and control system requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction (fire door construction standards and self-closing mechanisms)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety training, drills, and familiarisation requirements)
preventable_by: Mandatory retrofitting and new-build specification of passive engineering controls on all self-closing, watertight, and fire doors in crew and passenger areas, including finger guards, anti-trap rubber edging, and where operationally feasible, adjustable hydraulic slow-close mechanisms, with flag state administrations incorporating these requirements into port state control inspection criteria.

Implementation of a structured, role-specific door safety training programme for all crew categories — including hotel, catering, and service personnel — covering pinch-point hazard awareness, correct hand placement techniques, and mandatory PPE (cut-resistant gloves) requirements in designated high-risk door zones, with mandatory refresher sessions at intervals not exceeding 12 months and documented competency records retained onboard.

Conduct of systematic shipboard door hazard audits at each Safety Management System review cycle, mapping all self-closing and power-operated doors against a risk matrix that accounts for traffic frequency, lighting adequacy, sea state exposure, and proximity to accommodation or high-footfall areas, with audit findings used to prioritise engineering upgrades and to update vessel-specific risk assessments under ISM Code requirements.

representative_cases.description: Inland waterway vessel's crew member sustained a fractured wrist after getting his hand caught in a fire door. No defects reported.
Utility vessel's crew member sustained a hand injury after having it trapped between a door. This was due to lack of attention whilst closing and securing watertight doors.
While crew member was passing a door in the engine room they caught there finger between the door and frame, resulting in a broken little finger.
Cruise ship, passenger caught thumb in door resulting in closed fracture. Passenger was closing the toilet door, left-hand thumb got caught in the door frame.
A crew member tried to hold a door open but misjudged how quick the door was closing and trapped their finger. The crew member went ashore to get medical assistance.
A container vessel crewmember severed their finger after getting it trapped in a door.
A crewmember of a ro-ro ferry was shaken but uninjured when a hydraulically operated watertight door was moving to the open position, then rapidly and unexpectedly moved to the closed position.
Passenger cargo vessel stewardess trapped their thumb in a door resulting in a fracture
A cruise ship crew member fractured their finger after getting it crushed between the toilet door and frame. The casualty was disembarked several days later.

title: Steering Gear Failure Causing Grounding During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of vessel groundings attributable to sudden and unexpected steering gear failures — mechanical, hydraulic, electrical, or software-related — occurring predominantly during night-time transits. Across the clustered incidents, the loss of steerage is consistently the proximate cause of grounding, while the inability to enact effective corrective or emergency measures within the available time window converts a recoverable equipment failure into a navigational casualty. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel types and operational contexts indicates a systemic shortfall, not isolated equipment defects, pointing to inadequate pre-departure checks, insufficient preventive maintenance regimes, and the absence of rehearsed emergency steering procedures as the underlying drivers.

Latent hardware and software deficiencies form the technical foundation of this theme. In several incidents, steering systems resumed normal function after grounding — consistent with transient faults such as electrical trips, hydraulic locks, or software resets — suggesting that the root causes were identifiable and potentially addressable through rigorous inspection and testing prior to departure. Human factors are compounded by night-time operating conditions, which materially reduce situational awareness, shorten available response windows, and impair the crew's ability to assess sea room and execute emergency manoeuvres in time to prevent contact. Environmental proximity factors — confined channels, shallow banks, and soft mud substrates — further compress the margin for error once steerage is lost. The convergence of latent equipment vulnerability, undertested emergency procedures, and degraded night-time situational awareness creates the conditions under which these otherwise preventable incidents repeatedly materialise.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part F, Regulation 29 — Steering Gear: testing, trials, and emergency steering requirements)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34 — Safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including voyage planning obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XIV / ISM Code (International Safety Management): SMS requirements for maintenance, pre-departure checks, and emergency response procedures
preventable_by: Mandate documented pre-departure steering gear functional tests for every voyage, including full hydraulic lock-to-lock helm movements, autopilot engagement/disengagement checks, and verification of backup and emergency steering modes, with completed checklists retained as part of the vessel's safety management system records.

Implement a structured preventive maintenance schedule for all steering system components — hydraulic fluid levels, seals, actuators, electrical connections, and software firmware — with independent verification at defined intervals, ensuring that transient or resettable faults are identified and rectified prior to the vessel getting underway rather than discovered during a grounding event.

Require crew to conduct periodic drills in emergency steering procedures, including transfer to manual or alternative steering, engine-only manoeuvrability assessments, and anchoring as a last resort, with specific emphasis on night-time scenarios in confined or shallow waterways to reduce human response time and improve decision-making under degraded situational awareness conditions.

representative_cases.description: A tug vessel grounded after the skipper reported they lost steerage. The vessel re-floated and made its way back alongside with no reported breach of hull or pollution. MCA have requested a dive report and confirmed that repairs to the steering was successful.

A cargo vessel had departed port when it grounded due to a loss of steering. Tug assistance was arranged and assisted the cargo vessel back to it's berth with no further issues once it had floated on the flood tide. outside.
The vessel's autopilot failed and then, as a result, grounded. The vessel managed to make it to a port with local lifeboats escorting the vessel and did not have any damage.

The vessel had swung to starboard, when the vessel's helm malfunctioned. It turned hard to port causing the vessel to swing to port towards a bank. All attempts made to turn the helm to starboard were not successful, the engine was stopped immediately and engaged in full astern power. The vessel's port bow landed ashore on a bank. The crew then attempted the restart the steering equipment and foll

General cargo vessel was departing when it proceeded upstream manoeuvring slowly. There was an attempt to apply the helm order but steering appeared unresponsive and the vessel moved across to rest in soft mud. The main engine astern and the bow thruster which then allowed the vessel to manoeuvre off the mud and proceed to passage. No damage was reported on the vessel.
Fishing vessel suffered loss of steering and resulting in a grounding, no damage reported and no injuries.

A general cargo vessel went aground following a steering failure after it left port. The vessel refloated on the tide and manoeuvred off the bank using emergency steering. An assessment and repairs were carried out before it continued on its passage.

A fishing vessel experienced steering failure as it approached a port. The vessel veered away from the port entrance and grounded on the breakwater. The vessel was towed free by the search and rescue services.

A cargo vessel with a pilot on board was proceeding along a channel. The pilot noticed the vessel was sluggish and had to give the vessel lots of helm for the rudder to be effective. During the operation, control of the vessel was lost due to the rudder issue and the bow thrusters were turned off at the time meaning they could not be used to prevent the grounding. It was a soft grounding, there wa

title: Wire and Lifting Equipment Failure Under Deficient Oversight
description:
This theme is characterised by the repeated failure of wire ropes and associated lifting equipment across a range of operational contexts — lifeboat drills, mooring operations, cargo handling, scientific sampling, and informal maintenance work — where the common thread is not the immediate mechanical failure but the latent conditions that made failure inevitable. Incidents recur because inspection regimes are either absent, insufficiently rigorous, or inadequately documented, allowing wire degradation through corrosion, fatigue, or overloading to progress undetected until the point of catastrophic parting. Compounding this hardware deterioration is a pattern of procedural normalisation: non-standard rigging arrangements are improvised, uncertified equipment is pressed into service for lifting persons, and in at least one case a crew member actively concealed an unauthorised over-the-side operation, indicating that safety culture had deteriorated to the point where risk-taking workarounds were perceived as the only practical means of completing routine tasks.

Latent hardware failure is the proximate mechanism in almost every incident, but the enabling conditions are human and organisational. Night-time operations consistently reduced the effectiveness of pre-use visual inspections and suppressed the likelihood of supervisory intervention. Environmental factors — moderate sea states introducing dynamic shock loading on already-degraded falls during drills, for example — provided the final increment of stress that triggered parting. Critically, the absence of enforced permit-to-work systems, the lack of formal load testing schedules, and insufficient non-destructive examination intervals created an environment where compromised equipment remained in service. The deliberate concealment behaviour observed in the over-the-side painting incident further signals an organisational failure: when crews cannot access legitimate, safe means to perform routine work, they self-organise hazardous alternatives, and the absence of management awareness perpetuates rather than interrupts the cycle of risk.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 20: operational readiness, maintenance, and inspections of lifesaving appliances including davit falls)

SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Structure, Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E, Regulation 35-1: safe access, shipboard working conditions, and maintenance systems)
SOLAS Chapter VI – Carriage of Cargoes and Oil Fuels (Regulation 5: care of cargo and cargo handling equipment, including crane and lifting gear maintenance and certification)
preventable_by: Implement a mandatory, interval-based wire rope inspection and replacement programme across all shipboard lifting equipment — including davit falls, cargo crane wires, winch wires, and scientific equipment wires — specifying maximum service life, load-test cycles, and non-destructive examination requirements, with records maintained in the vessel's Safety Management System and subject to flag State and port State control verification.

Enforce a robust permit-to-work system for all over-the-side, at-height, and non-routine lifting operations, requiring explicit management authorisation, a documented risk assessment, confirmation of certified equipment, and the assignment of a designated safety observer; system effectiveness should be audited periodically by the company's designated person ashore to detect workaround behaviours indicative of underlying safety culture deficiencies.

Mandate pre-operational functional checks and documented load-rating confirmation for any wire or lifting device used in a non-primary or improvised role — including use of mooring winch wires as stoppers during crane lifts — and introduce crew training that explicitly addresses the risks of repurposing equipment outside its certified design intent, reinforcing that improvised rigging arrangements require the same formal approval process as any other safety-critical lifting operation.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel was at anchor testing their starboard lifeboat. During a test launch, lowering without crew on board, one of the davit wires parted causing the lifeboat to partially fall and be suspended only from the other fall. The lifeboat was lowered to the water and recovered. There were no injuries or pollution. The only damage reported was the parted wire fall.

A container vessel crew member was inside a basket that was rigged over the transom of the vessel. The crew member was painting and when noticed by a pilot vessel, hid in the bottom of the basket. The basket was suspended by the ship's provision crane. Although the crew member wore a lifejacket and the presence of tag-lines to the basket, no harness was observed. The man-basket appeared to be self

A mooring buoy was craned up, once in the air the operator paid out the starboard winch hook, to get ready to stop off the mooring. Once paid out and hooked in, the operator went to haul in on the winch, which parted undramatically. The load never came off the main crane wire.

A research vessel was carrying out sediment sampling from the sea bed when the wire for the sediment grab parted and the equipment dropped to the seabed. It was later recovered and there was no pollution. There was no obvious reason as to why the wire parted.

A cargo vessel had damage during hatch cover operations. The wire rope on the gantry crane broke during hoisting of the hatch cover. The hatch cover dropped into the cargo hold and subsequently the crane and hatch cover were damaged. No one was injured.

A bulk carrier was alongside loading scrap. The crane wire parted which caused the grab to fall from height and struck the ship's bulwark and the quay side. The crane hook also detached and fell into the dock and was later recovered by divers. There were no injuries reported.

During operation on the work boat, the crane winch cable experienced a catastrophic failure as it was lifting a load, resulting in the snapping of the winch cable and the load falling back into the water (fish pen). At the time of incident, the skipper was operating the crane, a fish farm worker and the crew member man were on the pen. No injuries were sustained or further damage incurred during t

A bulk carrier experienced equipment damage when alongside rigging the accommodation ladder. The wire for the accommodation ladder parted as shore personnel were pulling the ladder over onto the quay. The distance to the quay was about 2.5m due to large Yokohama fenders. There was no one standing on the accommodation ladder at the time that the wire parted and the ladder fell and ended up hanging

A cruise ship lowered its gangway whilst alongside. As it was lowering, two crew members walked down it. As they approached the end of the gangway, it fell approximately a further 2 metres, unexpectedly. No injuries were sustained. The gangway wire was not properly spooled on to the winch whilst recovering the gangway from a previous port visit.

title: Crew Injuries from Motion, Falls, and Off-Duty Activities
description:
This theme encompasses a range of crew musculoskeletal and traumatic injuries occurring both during operational duties and off-duty or recreational periods, united by a common failure to adequately account for vessel motion as a persistent hazard across all shipboard environments. Incidents span accommodation spaces, weather decks, recreational areas, and small patrol vessels, affecting crew during sleep, leisure, and routine movement. A recurring characteristic is the mismatch between formally reported sea states and measured wave heights, suggesting systemic underreporting of environmental severity that causes both crews and management to underestimate prevailing risk. The breadth of vessel types affected — offshore supply vessels, cruise ships, landing craft, patrol boats, and tankers — indicates that this is not a fleet-specific problem but a cross-sector safety culture gap in which vessel motion risk is treated as an operational concern rather than a continuous, whole-ship hazard requiring structured mitigation.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a primary driver, most notably the inadequate provision or deterioration of bunk lee boards, anti-slip ladder surfaces, and restraint fittings in accommodation spaces, where several of the most serious injuries occurred in relatively benign sea conditions. Human factors including fatigue, reduced night-time alertness, and an informal attitude toward risk during recreational activities compound these hardware shortfalls. Safety Management Systems are frequently not extended to cover non-operational crew activities, leaving organised games, social events, and off-watch movement outside the scope of formal risk assessment. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time operations and elevated sea states — act as force multipliers on these latent vulnerabilities. The consistent discrepancy between reported and actual sea states further erodes the reliability of risk-based decision-making at both vessel and fleet management level, preventing timely intervention and obscuring the true frequency and severity of this injury pattern.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (accommodation and structural safety fittings)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety equipment, PPE, and operational readiness)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – SMS scope, risk assessment, and fleet safety oversight)
preventable_by: Conduct a mandatory fleet-wide audit of all crew accommodation bunk restraint systems, lee boards, and ladder anti-slip fittings, with deficiencies rectified to a defined standard and compliance verified through port state and flag state inspections; establish a recurring inspection regime embedded within the vessel's Planned Maintenance System.

Extend Safety Management System (SMS) risk assessment requirements explicitly to cover all non-operational crew activities aboard vessels underway, including organised recreational events, requiring a documented pre-activity risk assessment that incorporates current and forecast sea state thresholds above which activities must be suspended or relocated to a safe space.

Implement a standardised, instrument-verified sea state reporting protocol across all vessel classes that requires actual measured wave height to be recorded alongside the descriptive sea state classification, with reporting discrepancies flagged for review; use aggregated fleet data to establish sea-state-linked operational restrictions for crew movement, door operations, and recreational activities, supported by targeted crew awareness training.

representative_cases.description: A replenishment tanker's crew member fell out of bed and sustained a fracture to the neck. The casualty was extracted with a stretcher and taken for further medical assistance.
Passenger ship's crew member suffered a sprained toe while playing tug of war (organised game for crew members). No further injuries/incidents reported.
Landing vessel's crew member sustained a body impact injury with a manually operated hinged watertight door which has led to a medical discharge from the vessel.
