A cruise ships crew member injured there knee whilst dancing. Will be medically disembarked due to a rupture of ligament of the left knee.
A person on police boat had a back injury and was air lifted to hospital.
Cruise ship crewmember fractured their collar bone after falling from the ladder of their bunk bead. The casualty was medically disembarked.
Offshore supply ship crewmember broke their toe after striking their foot on their bunk. The casualty went to hospital on arrival and has been signed off work.
An offshore supply crewmember fell from their bunk and fractured their left wrist. The casualty was airlifted from the vessel and was passed into the care of the ambulance service.
RNLI ILB crewmember land awkwardly on a wave and dislocated their ankle whilst on operation.

title: Machinery Failure Cascading to Night-time Grounding
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring causal chain in which a single mechanical failure — most commonly propulsion or steering loss — removes the vessel's ability to maintain navigational control, resulting in grounding. The pattern is consistent across vessel types ranging from leisure motor cruisers and sailing vessels to inland passenger craft and rigid inflatable boats, indicating that the vulnerability is not confined to any one sector. In the majority of cases, the grounding is not caused directly by poor seamanship or adverse weather, but by the removal of the primary means of hazard avoidance at a critical moment. Night-time operations compound the risk significantly by narrowing the crew's situational awareness window and reducing the time available to deploy contingency measures such as anchoring, distress signalling, or sail-only manoeuvring. The recurrence of this pattern across incident categories — spanning navigation, machinery, and flooding — points to systemic deficiencies in pre-departure maintenance culture, emergency preparedness, and redundancy planning rather than isolated operator error.

At the latent level, hardware deficiencies are the primary enabler of these incidents. Engines affected by fuel mismanagement, overheating, flooding, or accumulated mechanical wear represent single points of failure on vessels that carry no effective redundancy. Planned maintenance programmes, where they exist, appear insufficient to surface developing faults before they manifest underway. Human factors compound these latent conditions: solo crewing reduces response capacity, alarm management culture is frequently inadequate, and crews demonstrate limited proficiency in emergency anchoring or manual steering procedures when propulsion is suddenly lost. Environmental conditions, particularly darkness, act as a force multiplier by compressing decision-making time and degrading spatial awareness near shallow or obstructed water. The consistently successful SAR response across these incidents, while commendable, should not obscure the fact that the groundings themselves were largely preventable through upstream interventions in machinery reliability, crew training, and pre-departure readiness.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (mandatory maintenance and machinery reliability standards)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency equipment readiness, distress signalling, and evacuation preparedness)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (navigational watchkeeping, voyage planning, and safe operation in proximity to hazards)
preventable_by: Mandatory structured pre-departure engine and steering system checks for all recreational and commercial small vessels, supported by a simple documented checklist covering fuel levels, cooling systems, engine compartment integrity, and steering gear, with particular emphasis on vessels operating after dark or in tidal or confined waters.

Compulsory emergency drill training for all vessel operators — including solo operators — covering loss-of-propulsion scenarios, specifically the immediate deployment of anchor to arrest drift, transmission of a distress signal, and where applicable, transition to sail or manual steering; this training should be a condition of vessel registration renewal or commercial licence maintenance.

Implementation of minimum redundancy requirements for propulsion-critical systems on passenger-carrying and commercially operated vessels operating in inland or constrained waterways, including requirements for backup steering capability and automatic engine alarm systems that alert the helmsman to overheating or fuel starvation before failure occurs, combined with a mandatory review of planned maintenance records by port state or flag state authorities at defined intervals.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel with two crew onboard grounded following a fuel issue, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The SAR vessel towed the fishing vessel into port to effect repairs.
Sailing vessel ran aground due to engine failure and awaited a tow from the local search and rescue service
A leisure fishing vessel ran aground and was towed by search and rescue services. They ran aground after experiencing engine failure. There was no damage to the vessel or injuries to the crew.
A vessel experienced machinery failure and became stuck in mud. All persons were taken ashore and the vessel was towed to a marina.
A motor cruiser had engine failure with one person on board, as a result of the engine failure the vessel went aground on the mud and was towed free.
An inland waterways passenger vessel lost propulsion and drifted aground as a result. They were assisted by search and rescue craft back to safe haven and all passengers disembarked, uninjured.
A dialling vessel had an overheating engine and ran aground.
A sailing vessel, with five crew onboard, ran aground as a result of steering failure. It was towed to safety by a passing vessel.
A RIB's (rigid inflatable boat) engine flooded resulting in the RIB being washed ashore.

title: Night Recovery Operations: Compounding Visibility and Motion Risks
description:
This theme is characterised by damage-causing contacts between vessels or their propulsion systems and recovery infrastructure — including davit systems, trailers, carriages, A-frames, and slipway hazards — occurring predominantly during night-time launch and recovery operations in mild to moderate sea states. The recurring nature of these incidents reflects a systemic failure to adequately account for the compounding effect of reduced visibility, vessel motion, and the inherent precision demands of recovery manoeuvres. Night conditions degrade operators' spatial awareness and depth perception, while even slight sea states introduce unpredictable craft movement that dramatically narrows the margin for alignment error. The frequency across both davit-based offshore recoveries and shore-side trailer or carriage recoveries indicates that the underlying vulnerability is not isolated to a single vessel type or recovery method, but is endemic to the broader operational context of SAR and rescue craft handling after dark.

The latent hardware dimension of this theme includes inadequate physical safeguards such as insufficient guides, buffers, or fenders on davit systems and carriages, as well as recovery infrastructure that offers insufficient tolerance for misalignment under dynamic conditions. Human factors are equally prominent: operators and ground crews lack standardised spotter protocols, clear role assignments, and night-specific procedural guidance, leaving critical alignment judgements to individual discretion under degraded sensory conditions. Environmental thresholds for safe recovery — defining the maximum permissible sea state, wind speed, or visibility level — are absent or inconsistently applied, meaning that operations proceed in conditions that objectively exceed the safe envelope of the recovery system. Collectively, these latent failures transform routine recoveries into high-probability contact events whenever ambient conditions degrade, demonstrating that existing procedures have been calibrated for daytime or ideal conditions and have not been systematically validated for the night operational environment that SAR vessels routinely face.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 17: requirements for launching and recovery appliances, including davits and associated equipment for rescue craft)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34: safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including operational planning accounting for environmental conditions)

SOLAS Chapter XIV – Safety Measures for Non-SOLAS Vessels Operating in Polar Waters (by analogy: environmental threshold criteria and enhanced procedural controls for operations in degraded conditions)
preventable_by: Establish and formally document environmental operating limits (maximum sea state, wind speed, and minimum effective lighting levels) specifically governing night-time launch and recovery operations for all rescue craft, and mandate that recovery be deferred or delayed when these thresholds are exceeded, with authority to halt operations delegated to a designated Safety Officer independent of mission command.

Introduce mandatory night-specific recovery Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) that prescribe defined spotter roles, standardised hand and radio signal protocols, and step-by-step clearance verification checkpoints during davit, carriage, and trailer recovery evolutions, with compliance verified through periodic audits and competency assessments that gate night recovery certification separately from daytime qualifications.

Conduct engineering reviews of all recovery infrastructure — including davit systems, trailer rollers, and carriages — to retrofit physical alignment aids, sacrificial fender or buffer protection, and improved guide rails that increase the tolerance for minor misalignment, and supplement these hardware controls with enhanced fixed or portable lighting systems at recovery sites and on vessel superstructures to materially improve operator visibility during after-dark evolutions.

representative_cases.description: A services vessels rescue craft sustained damage during recovery. While recovering the Fast Rescue Craft (FRC) from the sea on the STBD side using the designated davit the Craft struck the Ship's superstructure causing the craft to sustain damage.
A search and rescue vessel was being recovered onto its trailer when the propeller struck the trailer and was damaged. The roller of the trailer was also damaged.
A search and rescue vessel was being recovered onto its trailer when the propeller made contact with the trailer. There was no damage to the vessel or the trailer.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was returning to shore for recovery. During the recovery onto the carriage the propellers struck the carriage resulting in both propellers sustaining damage.
A search and rescue vessel was launching when it grounded as it came off the trailer. The two propellers touched the seabed and sustained minor damage.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was returning to shore for a recovery. During the stern recovery onto the carriage a propeller struck rocks resulting in damage to the propeller.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was being recovered onto its carriage when a wave caused the bow to tip and the propeller then struck the carriage. The propeller sustained a chipped blade.
A search and rescue vessel was being recovered to the carriage, as they reversed the vessel hit the port side A frame of the carriage. The recovery was continued with no further issues.
The SAR (search and rescue) vessel was on a practice exercise and was being recovered onto its trailer when the propeller made contact with the steel frame, taking the tips off all three blades.

title: Fire and Explosion from Uncontrolled Ignition Sources Onboard
description:
This theme is characterised by fires and explosions arising from the accumulation of flammable vapours or unregulated ignition sources in spaces that fall outside routine crew monitoring or formal hazard control frameworks. Across the incidents examined, a consistent pattern emerges: flammable conditions develop undetected — whether through fuel system leaks aboard recreational and cargo vessels, or through unregulated guest behaviour on cruise ship balconies — and are subsequently ignited due to absent or inadequate procedural controls. The recurring failure to identify and mitigate ignition risk before it manifests reflects a systemic deficiency in pre-operational checks, hazardous area protocols, and the enforcement of safe work practices. Night-time conditions further compound detection failures, reducing the crew's capacity to identify physical warning signs such as fuel vapour, spills, or nascent fire.

Latent hardware failures — including fuel system integrity deficiencies, absent or non-functional vapour detection equipment, and thermally vulnerable glazing — create the preconditions for these events. Human factors are equally prominent: complacency following incomplete venting periods, use of non-intrinsically safe devices in hazardous atmospheres, and failure to conduct formal atmosphere testing or hazard assessments before re-entering compromised spaces all recur across incidents. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness and open-deck wind exposure, amplify both ignition risk and the speed of fire development. Together, these factors reveal a latent systemic vulnerability in which procedural non-compliance and inadequate safety equipment intersect with poorly supervised or unmonitored spaces, creating conditions that make escalation to a serious casualty highly probable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
preventable_by: Mandate mandatory pre-start vapour purging procedures (bilge blower operation with timed verification) and post-leak atmosphere testing using calibrated gas detection equipment before any re-entry, ignition source introduction, or engine start in fuel-exposed spaces, with compliance recorded in the vessel log.

Require that all vessels carrying flammable fuels maintain readily accessible intrinsically safe (ATEX-rated) lighting and tools designated for use in hazardous areas, supported by crew training on the prohibition of non-intrinsically safe personal devices in any fuel-compromised compartment.

Implement structured guest balcony fire risk controls on passenger vessels, including periodic night-time crew patrols of open-deck areas, clearly communicated restrictions on smoking materials and electrical appliances on balconies, and installation of heat or smoke detection covering balcony spaces to enable early automated alerting.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship was underway when a fire was reported on a guest balcony. Emergency teams were dispatched and the fire was confirmed as extinguished twenty six minutes later. There were no injuries and the cause of is yet to be identified. The glass of the balcony door and the outer glass of the balcony handrail collapsed.

A recreational motor vessel with two crew onboard was approaching a marina for to fuel when an explosion occurred onboard. The vessel later sank with fuel seen on the surface, a boom was placed around fuel to contain the fuel. The two crew were taken to hospital for treatment of their injuries

A fuel leak was reported, compartment left open to vent and fuel shut off to isolate. Next day repair was completed but while cleaning up leaked fuel with a wet vac while using mobile phone as a light a explosion was caused.

A general cargo ship crewmember sustained an injury after a possible explosion in the engine room or from a generator whilst the vessel was alongside. The crewmember was taken by the ambulance service and was conscious.

Two occupants were onboard refuelling the vessel. After the vessel was started an explosion occurred onboard where one occupant received serious injuries and the other occupant sustained minor burn injuries.

A products barge had an explosion when running the diesel generator. The explosion occurred within the battery cabinet, suspected to be caused by a build up of hydrogen gas. The cabinet was damaged but there were no injuries or pollution.

A pleasure sailing vessel was alongside when another crew member from a nearby vessel noticed smoke emanating from the cabin area. The witness took a fire extinguisher from their vessel and gave it to the crew member on the smoking vessel who used it to extinguish the fire. There were no injuries, damages or pollution reported.

A sailing vessel had a fire onboard caused by a paraffin cooker. The fire was extinguished by the crew and with the help of a person from a neighbouring vessel using fire extinguishers. All three were treated for smoke inhalation. The fire service checked the vessel and deemed it safe but repairs. There was no reported pollution.

A fishing vessel was alongside in a port, when a fire started inside of an accommodation section. Firefighters managed to put out the fire. No external damage to the vessel was sustained. One of the crew members experienced smoke inhalation and was taken to hospital.

title: Undetected Structural Defects Leading to Hull Breach
description: This theme is characterised by progressive, latent structural deterioration — manifesting as weld fatigue, corrosion-induced plating fracture, or material degradation in hull appendages — that accumulates over extended periods without detection until an operational trigger precipitates a hull breach, water ingress, or pollution event. Across these incidents, the common thread is not acute damage from a single catastrophic event, but rather the slow development of a critical structural deficiency that existing inspection and maintenance regimes failed to identify. The incidents recur because structural degradation in areas such as engine room shell plating, skeg welds, and underwater hull sections is inherently difficult to detect without targeted, methodical inspection programmes, and because operational and commercial pressures can allow survey intervals to become the ceiling rather than the floor of structural oversight.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural integrity, watertight integrity, and damage control obligations)

SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – requiring operators to establish and maintain hull inspection and maintenance programmes as part of the Safety Management System)
SOLAS Chapter XII: Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers (enhanced survey and structural inspection requirements applicable to certain vessel types with hull fatigue risk)
preventable_by: Implement risk-based, enhanced close-up hull inspection programmes that specifically target fatigue-prone structural areas — including engine room shell plating, skeg weld connections, and fender contact zones — at intervals more frequent than class survey requirements, with findings formally recorded and trended to detect progressive degradation before it reaches a critical threshold.

Require operators to establish and enforce a documented structural anomaly response protocol within the Safety Management System, mandating that any bilge alarm activation, unexplained water ingress, or abnormal hull noise triggers an immediate cessation of passage and independent structural assessment before the vessel proceeds, removing the discretion to continue navigation with an unconfirmed hull defect.

Mandate pre-berthing infrastructure integrity assessments for port authorities and terminal operators, including formal inspection and replacement schedules for fender systems — particularly fender extension pieces — with clear competency requirements for PEC holders regarding known port infrastructure deficiencies, ensuring that structural vulnerabilities in berthing infrastructure are identified and mitigated before vessel contact occurs.

representative_cases.description: A hopper dredger suffered water ingress from a leak in its hull. There was a fracture of the shell plating in the engine room. The water ingress was minor and pumps were managing. The vessel went underway to port for repairs following the boarding of a pilot.

A ro-pax catamaran was berthing under PEC direction when a section of a fender sheared off, leaving an exposed sharp metal corner. The corner then damaged the hull of the vessel resulting in cuts and scrapes to the hull including a penetrating damage into a void space. The incident was reportedly due to the extension pieces on the fenders are not the same strength as the main fender and are prone

A tug suffered a pollution incident when diesel was reported around the vessel. The pollution source was identified as a crack in a weld in the hull of the vessel. The pollution has been contained and clean up operation started. No pollution has got out of the containment.

A vessel was on a routine passage when the local steering gear bilge alarm sounded. The crew investigated and found around 230 litres of water in the steering gear bilge, with salt water emitting from the skeg breather pipe. The vessel continued on passage and successfully berthed, following which it was placed out of service. The suspected cause of the ingress was a crack in a weld on the skeg, b

A research vessel with a pilot onboard reported that there was water ingress, the vessel continued into port where it berthed. Divers were deployed to inspect for damage, the survey discovered a crack around nine centimetres in size. The divers put a temporary patch over the area with a through bolt and tightened it from the inside of the bilge well. The bilge well was blanked off as an extra prec

A passenger ferry crewmember heard an unusual noise from above the bridge. When they went to investigate, they found a horizontal spar from the main mast had broken off due to corrosion. The spar eventually fell down to the monkey island. There were no injuries.

A container ship crew noticed an oil slick on the surface next to the vessel. Upon investigation, it was evident there was a crack in one of the bunkers. The extent of the fuel leak was not known but was described by the ship as a few tons or hundreds of litres. The type of fuel was very low sulphur fuel oils (VLSFO) and transferred to another bunker.

The vessel had suffered water ingress in the bow thruster room and set off the high-level bilge alarm. This was a result of corrosion in the bilge suction area eventually led to a small hole in the ship's hull.

Non-Commercial fishing vessel with 3POB started to take on water through a hole in the hull caused by damaged wooden plank. The vessel was assisted by local lifeboat services who brought the vessel back into port and assisted them in pumping out the water. The vessel made it successfully back to port where it continued to be pumped out. No injuries or pollution reported.

title: Passenger Deck Slip Hazards Under Adverse Environmental Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by passenger slip, trip, and fall incidents occurring on open and semi-exposed deck areas of cruise vessels, where the immediate hazard arises from wet, contaminated, or unstable deck surfaces compounded by adverse environmental conditions including moderate to rough sea states, vessel motion, and reduced night-time visibility. These incidents recur because the risk is fundamentally situational and dynamic rather than structural: no physical defects are typically identified post-incident, meaning standard maintenance and inspection regimes do not capture or mitigate the hazard. The root cause lies in a systemic absence of dynamic risk management protocols that would escalate passenger access controls in response to the compound effect of concurrent risk factors — specifically wet surfaces, vessel motion, and low-light conditions — none of which individually may trigger intervention, but which collectively create a foreseeable and elevated slip risk. Reliance on static administrative controls such as wet floor signage represents an insufficient response to this compound hazard profile, particularly at night when signage visibility is itself degraded.

Latent hardware factors include deck surface coatings that may become disproportionately slippery or adhesive when wet or aged, and lighting infrastructure that is inadequate for hazard identification under operational night-time conditions. Human factors are central to this theme: passengers routinely underestimate the combined effect of vessel motion and surface conditions, and in some instances aggravating factors such as inappropriate footwear or alcohol consumption further impair their ability to manage the risk. Critically, crew situational awareness and proactive hazard intervention appear insufficient — there is little evidence of active passenger management or deck access supervision during periods of elevated environmental risk. Organisationally, the absence of formalized dynamic risk assessment triggers within safety management systems means that the decision to restrict or warn against open deck access is not systematically linked to real-time sea state, wind, or lighting parameters, leaving the management of a foreseeable and recurring hazard dependent on individual crew initiative rather than procedural mandate.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (structural and surface safety of passenger areas)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety management and emergency procedures on deck)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – dynamic risk assessment, safety management systems, and hazard identification obligations)
preventable_by: Implement a formalised Dynamic Deck Access Risk Assessment Protocol within the vessel's Safety Management System, establishing defined environmental thresholds (sea state, wind speed, time of day) that automatically trigger tiered control measures — including mandatory anti-slip matting deployment, enhanced deck lighting activation, crew supervision posting, and where thresholds are exceeded, temporary restriction of passenger access to open promenade and exterior deck areas.

Introduce a structured routine deck inspection and hazard log regime with defined inspection intervals during sea passage, particularly during night hours and following precipitation or spray events, requiring crew to identify, mark, and remediate surface contamination, coating degradation, or moisture accumulation — with findings recorded and reviewed as part of the vessel's continuous safety improvement process.

Develop and deliver targeted passenger safety awareness communications at embarkation and during sea passage, using multiple channels (safety briefings, in-cabin video, public address, and posted notices) to explicitly inform passengers of the compounding risks of vessel motion, wet deck surfaces, and night-time conditions, including specific guidance on appropriate footwear for open deck areas and advice against accessing exterior decks unaccompanied or under the influence of alcohol during adverse conditions.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was walking along the ships promenade deck with their partner, they slipped and fell over landing onto their knee. A crew member was alerted by another passenger to request for help, the injured passenger was then taken by wheelchair to the medical centre where a fracture to the right knee was diagnosed. The passenger was medically disembarked. The area was inspected but no

A cruise ship passenger was walking to the promenade deck when they tripped and fell resulting in a dislocated left shoulder. The passenger was taken to the medical centre for treatment. The floor was wet but appropriate signage was displayed.

Cruise ship's passenger felt their foot stick to the deck resulting in them missing their step and falling forward. They put their hand out to break the fall and then rolled over sustaining fractures in both arms.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in an exterior accommodation area when they slipped on a wet section of deck and fell. They sustained a fractured hip (Left intertrochanteric femur fracture) and were medically disembarked. They were wearing flip flops at the time of the incident. The deck was wet due to rain and humidity and 'wet floor' signs were in place at the time of the incident.

A cruise ship passenger was exiting a toilet area when one of their legs slipped from under them and they fell forward onto the deck. They put out one of their arms to break their fall and subsequently fractured their arm. It was reported that half an hour after the injury, the passenger was alcohol restricted for being intoxicated. The consumption of alcohol may have been a factor in the fall. Ad

A cruise ship passenger slipped resulting in a closed fracture to the left forearm. The passenger slipped and fell on the wet open deck landing on their left arm sustaining a left distal radius fracture. The on board medical team was called and the injured guest reported to the medical centre.  The area was inspected, no defects were found and safety signage was in place.

A ro-pax vessel  passenger slipped and fell to the deck. They were quickly attended to by crew and two off duty paramedics who were travelling as passengers. Upon assessing the casualty it was determined that the they had fractured their right hip. The right leg was shortened with the foot rotated 90 degrees to the outside. The passenger was made comfortable on the deck and the coastguard were con

A cruise ship passenger was walking on the open deck when they slipped, lost balance and fell sideways on the wet deck. They tried to prevent the fall by putting out their right hand, the passenger was taken to the medical centre for treatment for a fractured right wrist. At the time of the incident the passenger said there were no posted wet floor signs. The passenger remained onboard.  The area

A cruise ship passenger was walking on the exterior deck area and slipped on the wet glass floor sustaining a fracture to their knee. The guest was wearing flip flops and the wind was reportedly a force 7. The guest was treated in the cruise ship's medical center and disembarked at the scheduled end of the voyage. There was no CCTV of the incident, witnesses reported the event to medical staff on

title: Hire Vessel Night Operations: Inexperience and Grounding Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by hire and charter vessel incidents occurring predominantly during night-time operations, where operator inexperience, limited local knowledge, and inadequate pre-departure preparation combine to produce groundings, propeller fouling, and vessel sinkings. The incidents recur because the hire sector systematically places inexperienced operators in unfamiliar vessels on waterways with variable depths, tidal influence, and navigational hazards, without adequate competency verification or operational restrictions. Night conditions consistently amplify these underlying deficiencies by reducing situational awareness, limiting hazard identification, and delaying the detection of emergencies such as flooding. The pattern is further reinforced by structural gaps in hire company onboarding processes, where commercial pressures can result in superficial vessel handovers and insufficient emphasis on route-specific risks, tidal planning, and emergency procedures.

The latent conditions sustaining this theme span human factors, organisational practices, and environmental exposure. On the human factors side, operators lack the navigational competency and local knowledge required to safely manage vessels in tidal, low-visibility environments; this is compounded by inadequate briefings that fail to communicate site-specific hazards such as shallow tidal flats, submerged obstructions, and channel constraints. Organisational latent failures include the absence of mandatory competency checks, no enforced curfews for night operation, and insufficient oversight of hire fleets for navigational equipment serviceability. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time darkness, ebb tides, and moderate wind or wave activity, act as active triggers that expose these latent weaknesses. The presence of vulnerable or less-able persons aboard several incidents further elevated the consequence severity and rescue complexity, underscoring that risk assessment at the point of hire is consistently inadequate.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 33, and 34: navigational equipment requirements, safe manning, and voyage planning obligations)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulations 7 and 19: survival craft, rescue procedures, and emergency drills applicable to passenger-carrying hire vessels)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (Regulation 15: maintenance of distress communication capability, relevant where hire vessels lack operable communication equipment during rescue events)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure competency assessment and vessel-specific handover briefings for all hire operators, incorporating tidal passage planning, night navigation restrictions, and site-specific hazard awareness as enforceable conditions of hire, with hire companies held accountable through flag state or port authority licensing requirements.

Introduction of operational curfews or conditional night-use restrictions for hire vessels operated by charterers without verified navigational qualifications, supported by geofenced tracking or check-in protocols to enable timely intervention when vessels deviate from planned routes or cease movement in hazard-prone areas.

Mandatory fitment and verification of functioning electronic chart displays, depth sounders, and VHF radios on all hire vessels prior to each charter, combined with periodic fleet safety audits by competent authorities to ensure hire companies maintain minimum equipment standards and documented pre-departure safety check records.

representative_cases.description: A hire cruiser sank with eight people on board. All persons were rescued without injury. The owner arranged for the vessel to be lifted the following day.
A hire boat ran aground on mud near the shore on an ebb tide. As the two persons on board were less able to evacuate, mud rescue was undertaken to bring them ashore.
A hire cruiser with seven people on board ran aground. All were safely evacuated to shore to await the refloating of the vessel on the high tide the following day.
A hire cruiser experienced a fouled propeller and was provided assistance by the hire company.
A hire motor cruiser ran aground with four people on board. They were rescued from the vessel and the company returned later to pick up the vessel at high tide.
A broads cruiser with six crew onboard ran aground. The rental company waited for high tide before re-floating the vessel and towing it to safety.
A hire cruiser ran aground and were recovered by a boat recovery company. No persons were injured and no damage was reported.
Recreational hire boat ran aground on rocks. It required a tow to get off but, once afloat it was able to continue under its own power

title: Rudder Failure and Inadequate Emergency Steering Preparedness
description:
This theme is characterised by the sudden or progressive failure of rudder assemblies across a range of vessel types, rendering vessels unsteerable and necessitating SAR intervention. The incidents share a common trajectory: an undetected mechanical or structural deficiency culminates in rudder loss underway, and the crew's inability to execute effective self-rescue measures — whether through deploying a temporary rudder, utilising an anchor to hold position, or managing alternative steering — escalates a recoverable equipment failure into a protracted emergency. Contributing factors consistently include single-handed operation, proximity to navigational hazards such as shipping channels, and night-time conditions that compound both the physical and cognitive demands placed on those aboard. The recurrence of this pattern across incident categories strongly suggests that rudder failure is not simply an unpredictable casualty event, but a foreseeable failure mode that vessel operators and crews are systematically underprepared to prevent or manage.

The latent hardware dimension is central to this theme: corrosion, material fatigue, manufacturing defects, and impact damage to rudder assemblies are all slow-developing conditions that routine inspection and maintenance regimes are specifically designed to detect. The evidence across these incidents points to an absence of such regimes, or their inadequate implementation, allowing degradation to progress undetected until failure under operational load. Human factors compound the hardware vulnerability: crews lacking practised emergency steering procedures, vessels not equipped with temporary rudder systems, and solo operators with no crew redundancy to assist damage control all represent latent training and procedural deficiencies. Environmental conditions — principally darkness — did not cause these incidents but materially increased their severity by impairing damage assessment, anchoring attempts, and coordination with rescue services. Collectively, these factors indicate a systemic gap in both preventive maintenance culture and emergency seamanship preparedness that spans vessel types and operator categories.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part F – Alternative Design, and steering gear requirements under Regulation 29)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency equipment, drills, and crew preparedness for casualty situations)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34 – Safe Navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including voyage planning and emergency contingency preparedness)
preventable_by: Mandatory periodic inspection and documented maintenance of rudder assemblies and steering systems, including underwater survey intervals appropriate to vessel age and type, to detect corrosion, fatigue cracking, or bearing wear before operational failure occurs.

Requirement for vessels — particularly those engaged in offshore or coastal passages — to carry a functional emergency or jury-rigged steering system, with crew trained and drilled in its deployment under realistic conditions including reduced visibility and single-handed scenarios.

Integration of rudder failure as a standard scenario within vessel safety management systems and pre-departure briefings, ensuring all crew are familiar with emergency steering procedures, anchor deployment as a position-holding contingency, and early escalation protocols to avoid prolonged uncontrolled drift near navigational hazards.

representative_cases.description: A motor cruiser reported that it had lost or damaged it's rudder, the two crew onboard had not fitted a temporary rudder but they were able to manoeuvre using the bow thrusters. The crew requested assistance as the vessel was not making much headway, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel deployed and towed the vessel to a mooring.

A yacht lost their rudder outside a marina, no ingress of water was reported and the anchor was also functioning. The vessel was towed firstly by another yacht who took the vessel as close to the marina as it could and then a work boat towed the last section of the journey back to its berth.

A sailing yacht broke its rudder. The yacht was anchored but the lone skipper was unable to get back to the vessel's berth. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel deployed and towed the yacht back to its berth. There were no injuries to the skipper.

A sailing vessel with 1 pob broke its rudder and could not put its anchor down. The casualty vessel was towed to safety by a lifeboat due to the fact there was only one person onboard, there was no chance of resolving its own issue and its proximity to a shipping channel.

A sailing vessel with two crew onboard reported a snapped rudder and were drifting. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel towed the sailing vessel back as it was deemed unseaworthy. Both crew were wearing lifejackets.

A sailing yacht had no steerage after losing it's rudder/tiller. One of the crew members onboard also sustained a minor laceration injury to their little finger. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel assisted the yacht by towing it to a place of safety. The injured crew member made their own way to hospital for an assessment and treatment.

A fishing vessel with six crew onboard suffered a rudder failure and were unable to manoeuvre. Due to the deteriorating weather conditions, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel towed the vessel to a safe haven.

A fishing vessel suffered the loss of its rudder, resulting in a grounding on rocks. There was some water ingress in the forward compartment. The fishing vessel was able to get off the rocks, a search and rescue vessel was tasked to the scene. The fishing vessel was escorted back to port as they had power and some steering and the water ingress was minimal. There were no injuries to crew.

title: Autopilot Misuse and Failure in Confined Waters
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring failure mode in which autopilot systems are engaged — whether deliberately, accidentally, or through equipment malfunction — at the most operationally critical phases of a voyage, specifically during harbour departures, channel transits, and confined waterway navigation. Across all incidents in this cluster, the common thread is the removal or degradation of active helm control at precisely the moment when situational awareness and manual responsiveness are most essential. Whether the autopilot is consciously activated in contravention of best practice, inadvertently triggered through inadvertent contact with a control console, or engaged by a faulty system with a hard-over rudder deflection, the outcome is consistent: a loss of directed steering control that the crew is slow to recognise and slower still to safely recover. The persistence of these incidents reflects not merely individual operator error but a systemic absence of embedded procedural culture, explicit operational restrictions, and vessel-specific training that would otherwise prevent autopilot use in restricted or congested waters.

The latent causal architecture of this theme involves the intersection of hardware design deficiencies, human factors vulnerabilities, and adverse environmental conditions. Control console layouts that permit accidental mode switching without tactile or auditory confirmation, autopilot systems lacking adequate off-course alarms or pre-engagement rudder position verification, and the absence of fail-safe logic preventing engagement in high-risk operating phases all represent latent hardware and software failures. These are compounded by human factors including mode complacency, unfamiliarity with control system transition procedures, overconfidence in automation, and the cognitive burden created by simultaneous bridge team movements during active manoeuvres. Night-time and adverse sea conditions further degrade the visual and perceptual cues that would otherwise allow rapid identification of an unintended course deviation. Together, these latent conditions create an environment in which a single triggering event — an accidental button press, a system fault, or a procedural shortcut — is sufficient to produce a serious navigational incident.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 24, 25: navigational equipment carriage, watchkeeping, and steering gear testing requirements)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Nuclear Ships / XI-2 – but principally Chapter V Reg. 26: Manning, Hours of Work, and Bridge Resource Management obligations
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: procedural controls, SMS procedures, and equipment maintenance obligations)
preventable_by: Establish and enforce a formal, vessel-specific Safe Steering Mode Procedure within the Safety Management System that explicitly prohibits autopilot engagement during harbour approaches, departures, pilotage waters, and buoyed channel transits, with mandatory helm mode confirmation logged at defined waypoints and cross-checked by a second bridge watchkeeper.

Require manufacturers and flag administrations to mandate physical and logical safeguards on autopilot control systems, including mode-change confirmation prompts, audible engagement alerts, pre-engagement rudder position verification interlocks, and off-course alarms with defined sensitivity thresholds, ensuring that accidental or fault-driven engagement is immediately signalled to the bridge team.

Implement mandatory crew familiarisation and competency verification programmes for all autopilot and steering control systems aboard a vessel, including simulator or on-board drills that specifically practise mode transition procedures, accidental engagement recovery, and fault identification scenarios, with records maintained under the vessel's SMS and reviewed during internal and external audits.

representative_cases.description: Fishing vessel was leaving the harbour when they placed the vessel into auto-pilot. They made contact with another vessel and immediately put the boat into gear. No damages reported to the fishing vessel however, the pleasure vessel had 2 aluminium oars bent.

As the vessel left port under the command of the pilot and master, the master and pilot moved to the inside centre console on the bridge when the master knocked the vessel into autopilot by accident instead of hand steering. The vessel stopped swinging the vessel was put full astern and tug assistance was requested to control the vessel.

The vessel was inbound with a pilot on board, on passing 3rd buoy they began to use the automatic pilot, applied but remained at hard to starboard with the flat rudder 60 degrees whilst transiting the MAC between 1st & 3rd buoys. This resulted in a rapid swing to starboard as the engine was full ahead. The swing was seen visually and the pilot managed to change to hand steering in good time to app

Replenishment vessel's crew conducted manual helm checks however, control was lost when transitioning back to autopilot. Equipment limitation appeared to affect the control systems ability to regain control of the ship's steering in the call up mode. This was a result of lack of familiarity with the system. No injuries/damage occurred.

A commercial sailing vessel undertaking training with four people on board sustained damage above the waterline after a collision with a channel marker. While sailing up a channel the skipper lent on the coach roof to adjust the mainsheet which caused the autopilot to engage. It was apparent the yacht was on a collision course with a buoy. The crew became aware the autopilot had been engaged and t

A research vessel experienced random unpredicted helm applied by the autopilot, resulting in the necessity to switch to hand steering and take out a full turn in the lower leg of the channel. The vessel was instructed to remain on hand steering until clear of the VTS area.

Motorboat struck rocks resulting in the vessel sinking. The crew abandoned to life raft and were recovered by lifeboat. One casualty sustained head and possible spinal injuries whilst the other had a broken arm. The skipper admitted it was autopilot error.

A general cargo vessel veered to starboard whilst changing from manual to automatic steering and ran aground on a sandy seabed, no report of water ingress. The vessel refloated and proceeded to anchor. There was damage reported to a fixed pile navigational marker that was knocked to the side. There were no injuries.

title: Unguarded Deck Machinery and Dynamic Strike Hazards
description:
This theme is characterised by crew members sustaining serious injuries — including lacerations, amputations, and crush injuries — during routine deck operations involving dynamic energy sources such as rotating capstans, tensioned lines, chain assemblies, and strong magnetic components. The unifying feature across these incidents is the absence of a defined safe system of work that controls either the energy source itself or the positioning of personnel relative to it. Tasks are routinely performed without formal risk assessment, without physical guarding or tool-assisted methods, and without procedural boundaries that enforce standby or abort criteria when conditions deteriorate. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a systemic normalisation of manual interaction with hazardous deck machinery in high-variability environments, where the consequences of foreseeable deviations — vessel movement, gear becoming fast, unexpected component behaviour — are not mitigated at the design or procedural level.

Latent hardware deficiencies, including the absence of capstan nip-point guards and the lack of tool-assisted methods for chain manipulation, directly expose crew to contact injury when vessel motion or component behaviour deviates from expectation. Human factors compound this exposure: instinctive postural corrections, multi-tasking under load, and misjudgement of dynamic line or chain behaviour are predictable responses to an unstable working environment that task design fails to account for. Environmental conditions — night operations, sea states at or above Category 4, and wind-driven vessel movement — systematically degrade situational awareness, reduce reaction time, and increase the unpredictability of machinery and line behaviour. Critically, these environmental factors are known at the time of task commencement yet consistently fail to trigger formal risk review or task modification, indicating that the procedural framework governing these operations does not adequately integrate environmental risk thresholds into go/no-go decision-making.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (safety of machinery and mechanical equipment on deck)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe working practices and crew safety procedures)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (operational risk assessment, watchkeeping, and environmental condition management)
preventable_by: Mandate the development and implementation of task-specific Safe Systems of Work (SSoW) for all deck operations involving dynamic machinery — including capstans, tensioned lines, and chain handling — that define explicit exclusion zones, required tooling, minimum crew configurations, and environmental abort thresholds (e.g., sea state, wind speed, lighting) above which operations must not commence or must be suspended.

Require physical guarding or engineering controls on all rotating deck machinery nip points, and require the use of approved handling tools (e.g., chain hooks, line handlers) in place of bare-hand contact during operations involving tensioned or dynamic components, with compliance verified through pre-operational equipment checks and periodic SMS audits.

Introduce mandatory pre-task dynamic risk assessments for all deck machinery operations conducted at night or in adverse weather, incorporating a formal go/no-go decision gate that accounts for current sea state, vessel motion, visibility conditions, and crew fatigue — with outcomes recorded and requiring sign-off from the officer of the watch or vessel master before work commences.

representative_cases.description: Fishing vessel's crew member was taking out a twist of chain and a hanging flat link hit them above their right eye (near the eyebrow). They were wearing full PPE at the time & hard hat. The crew member was taken for further medical assistance and it was decided that it was a small wound above the eye and that they were fit to work.

Diving vessel's crew sustained a finger injury which resulted in finger amputation. The static gear for diving purposes had become fast on the seabed. The slack rope fed through block on davit, under a hook and round the capstan (approximately 4 turns). Rope was being paid by the crew member using right hand. The wind and the tide caused the vessel to beam-on and the casualty to lose balance. The

A tug crew member was letting go of headlines, when the vessel moved and the weight came back on suddenly. They then got their fingers trapped on their right hand and whilst trying to release the line. The reasons behind the reason.  The crew member lost the tips of two fingers.

A fishing vessel crew member was working on deck when they were hit by a chain whilst hauling nets  and sustained an open wound to their head. First aid was administered onboard and medical advice sought by VHF. The vessel continued it's passage to port and then the crew member was transported to hospital for further assessment and treatment.

Service vessel's crew member caught finger resulting in closed fracture. Whilst carrying out maintenance on ROV thruster assembly, the strong magnets pulled the 2 sections of the thruster (rotor & stator) together. Injured Party (IP) got their right-hand little finger caught between the 2 sections, causing a crushing injury.

A crewmember had abrasions and bruising injuries to their knee whilst the vessel was shooting fishing nets. A chain snapped out of position and caught the crewmember in their knee. There was no requirement to airlift casualty because a doctor confirmed they were happy for them to remain onboard to recover.

A fishing vessel was in process of shooting nets. The net was out, passing chains. One of the chains jammed, the weight swung briefly with the vessel's motion and a crewmember got arm their caught, resulting in a fracture and a laceration. First aid was applied onboard. A helicopter was tasked to evacuate the crewmember and take them to hospital for surgery. There was no damage to vessel.

A sailing yacht was seeking shelter as the wind was due, the crew set about stowing the sails. The furling gear had loosened, a crew member went forward to furl the genoa. As they rotated the furling gear the genoa released catching the crew member’s fingers between the line guard and the drum. The pressure was intense enough to amputate the tips of the index and middle finger to the right hand. T

title: Inadequate Risk Assessment in Small Vessel Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by the recurrent operation of small, inherently vulnerable vessels — hire kayaks, tenders, inland waterway craft, and fishing vessels — in conditions that demonstrably exceed the capability envelope of either the vessel type or the crew operating it, compounded by an absence of enforceable pre-departure risk controls. Across these incidents, the common thread is not a single catastrophic failure but rather the systematic underweighting of foreseeable, often acknowledged hazards: strong tidal currents, adverse sea states, night-time darkness, and turbulent lock conditions. Operators and hire businesses repeatedly substitute informal verbal advisories or assumed competency for structured risk assessment, creating conditions where hazardous voyages proceed without meaningful gatekeeping. These incidents continue to occur because the decision to depart is treated as a personal or informal judgment rather than a process governed by defined operational limits, skills verification, and documented go/no-go criteria.

Latent hardware vulnerabilities — low freeboard on tenders, inherent instability in recreational inland craft, and the physical limitations of hire kayaks in open coastal waters — interact directly with adverse environmental conditions to produce rapid, high-consequence capsize events that leave little margin for recovery. Human factors, including optimism bias, external pressure to complete journeys, and an overreliance on survival equipment such as PFDs as a primary safeguard rather than a last resort, further compress the safety margin. The role of environmental conditions — sea state, tidal current, wind, and darkness — is consistently foreseeable from available forecast data, yet no formal threshold triggers a mandatory halt to operations. The absence of enforceable weather operating limits, mandatory pre-departure skills assessments for hire users, and structured supervision protocols for night or exposed-water routes represents the latent organisational and procedural failure underpinning every incident in this cluster.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 35: safe manning, voyage planning, and meteorological warnings)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (provision, maintenance, and mandatory use of lifejackets and PFDs)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (carriage of distress alerting equipment appropriate to operating area)
preventable_by: Hire operators and vessel operators must implement mandatory pre-departure skills assessment and documented go/no-go checklists that define enforceable weather operating limits (wind speed, sea state, visibility, and tidal conditions) specific to vessel type, with night and exposed-water routes requiring demonstrated competency or compulsory guided supervision — verbal safety briefs alone must not be treated as an adequate risk control.

Competent authorities should require all small vessel hire operations and tender services to maintain a formal voyage risk management framework, including written pre-departure risk assessments, defined abort criteria, and mandatory carriage and use of appropriate safety equipment (buoyancy aids, PLBs, tow lines, and suitable immersion protection) verified at the point of departure rather than assumed from an unsigned briefing.

Lock authorities and inland waterway operators must introduce standardised lock operating procedures that mandate lifejacket wear for all persons aboard vessels entering locks, require lock keepers to assess vessel type, size, and loading prior to operating sluice controls, and define maximum sluice opening rates based on vessel characteristics — supported by periodic competency checks and incident reporting requirements for lock keepers.

representative_cases.description: Six kayakers in four hire kayaks (two single kayaks and two tandem kayaks) set off from a beach on a planned route around a headland. The group had received a safety brief beforehand and were advised to stay further offshore due to strong currents closer to shore. Approximately thirty minutes after setting off the group was in difficulty with one of the tandem kayaks capsizing and both kayakers in

A tender capsized and the two crew onboard ended up in the water. Search and rescue assets were deployed but a nearby sailing club safety boat, that was marshalling a dinghy sailing race arrived on scene a few minutes after hearing the call over VHF. Both crew members were wearing a PFD  Time in the water:  More than 30 minutes

A recreational inland waterways vessel entered a lock, a lock keeper assisted with a bow and stern line and then returned to the controls. The outer lock was closed and the sluice opened a little,  The sluice was about ¼ open, after only a few seconds, a passenger who got off on the pontoon earlier started to shout, the sluice was closed as the vessel had capsized. The three passengers were in the

A fishing vessel was dredging for oysters, another fishing vessel was nearby. The lone skipper had deployed the dredge line and bag, increased speed and started towing. The dredge line went taut and the bow came around to starboard. As the skipper stepped out of the wheelhouse, the starboard side dipped under the water and the boat quickly capsized and almost fully submerged despite the shallow wa

A sail training vessel's crew members were recovering the tender after departing a port, the two crew stepped out onto ladder and the tender capsized and the two crew entering the water. The crew righted the tender and were recovered. No injuries sustained.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel capsized in a rescue operation due to casualties hanging onto the sponsion ropes. During the operation, the rescue boat approached a surfboard that had five members of public clinging to it. The vessel rescued two people and intended to take them to shore and return for the rest. At the point where the vessel went to pull away, it found itself hindered by the remai

Whilst an offshore supply vessel was alongside in a harbour they deployed their fast rescue craft (FRC) for testing. During the test, the FRC grill intake was fouled by a rope which stopped the engine. The supply vessel then launched it's daughter craft to tow the FRC, the crew from the FRC transferred to the daughter craft. As the FRC was towed back, water came over the stern, resulting in a caps

Following maintenance, a jack-up barge was going to be towed and returned to its normal location. After being made fast to the towing vessel, the jack-up was let go from the quay wall and started to list. The tow was released and the jack-up listed to about 45 degrees, before eventually laying on it's side on the seabed. During the initial event, one crew member was lifted off the barge, the secon

title: Alcohol Impairment and Inadequate Oversight Causing Overboard Incidents
description:
This theme is characterised by incidents in which alcohol intoxication — affecting skippers, crew members, and passengers alike — serves as the primary proximate cause of falls overboard, serious injury, and life-threatening situations. Across all representative incidents, intoxicated individuals demonstrate impaired balance, degraded judgement, and reduced capacity to self-preserve or respond to rescue, with outcomes ranging from hypothermia and compound fractures to a broken neck and near-drowning. The recurrence of these incidents is not incidental; it reflects a systemic failure of pre-departure and in-voyage fitness-for-duty checks, ineffective alcohol service controls, and — critically — the absence of meaningful enforcement action by harbour authorities and vessel operators even where patterns of alcohol-related behaviour were already documented. The incidents do not represent isolated lapses; they represent the predictable endpoint of known risks that were not acted upon.

While hardware deficiencies are minor contributors in this cluster, the dominant latent causes are human and procedural in nature. Environmental conditions — including night-time darkness, adverse sea states (in some cases more severe than officially recorded), and moderate wind — significantly amplified the consequences of alcohol-impaired decision-making, reducing the margin for error to near zero. The absence of personal flotation devices, safety watches, physical barriers at vessel peripheries, and passenger welfare monitoring protocols compounded individual vulnerability into collective systemic failure. Where environmental data discrepancies exist between logged and recorded sea states, this also raises concerns about the reliability of condition assessments used to sanction vessel operations, suggesting that procedural controls at the gateway to departure were either absent or ineffective across multiple vessel types and operational contexts.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for lifejackets, personal flotation devices, rescue equipment, and man-overboard recovery procedures)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (watchkeeping standards, situational awareness obligations, and fitness-for-duty requirements for officers on the bridge and in command)

SOLAS Chapter XI-2 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security (port State and harbour authority responsibilities for oversight, access control, and intervention where known risks to vessel or crew safety are identified)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure breathalyser testing for skippers and crew of all commercially operated and charter vessels, with enforceable blood alcohol limits aligned to company and flag State policy, and harbour authority powers to deny departure where limits are exceeded or a pattern of prior alcohol-related incidents is recorded against a licence holder.

Implementation of structured passenger and crew welfare monitoring protocols on vessels serving alcohol, including designated welfare officers, defined intoxication thresholds triggering intervention, cessation of alcohol service, and physical access controls — such as locked deck gates, perimeter netting, and increased night-time patrols — to prevent unsupervised access to vessel peripheries by vulnerable individuals.

Establishment of a mandatory reporting and follow-up mechanism requiring harbour authorities and vessel operators to record, escalate, and act upon alcohol-related incidents involving licence holders or crew, including referral to competent authority review of fitness-for-duty certification, ensuring that documented patterns of behaviour result in enforceable intervention before a serious or fatal incident occurs.

representative_cases.description: A skipper of a pleasure craft was piloting their tender to shore when they fell overboard. They were found on the beach by a member of the public who called the ambulance service as they were suspected to have hypothermia. They were taken to hospital. It was reported that the skipper may have been under the influence of alcohol at the time as there had been previous instances in the harbour involv

An internal waters cruise ship passenger fell overboard whilst intoxicated. The vessel recovered the casualty and then the local lifeboat service transferred the casualty ashore into the care of the land side ambulance service. The casualty was described as 'heavily' intoxicated, but was conscious and not reported to be seriously injured. The vessel crew rescued the casualty who was also reported

A cruise ship crew member was in their cabin and fell, sustaining a broken neck. They reported that they had been drinking at the time and later on before they were medically evacuated, the crew on board carried out a breathalyser test on two occasions. On both occasions the injured crewmember's reading was over the company limit. The injured crewmember was discovered by their cabin mate several h

A sailing vessel crewmember fell down stairs resulting in a compound fracture of the leg. A lifeboat was tasked and the casualty was extracted and taken to hospital. It was suspected that the skipper had taken alcohol and therefore a lifeboat crewmember helped assist the vessel to safety.

A commercial RHIB was on bareboat charter when a crewmember fell overboard whilst trying to retrieve their hat. A sailing vessel initially called a mayday stating that the casualty was unconscious but this was found out to be incorrect. The casualty was recovered and the skipper of the RHIB left the location with the casualty. CRT met the vessel and ensured the casualty was medically fine before t

Charter boat with passengers on board got into difficulty at sea and did not attempt to fix the vessel. The vessel then crashed into the pontoon and collided with a pleasure vessel that was in the neighbouring berth. The pleasure vessel's damages were scratches and gel cracks. The charter vessel sustained a hole in the superstructure. It has been reported multiple times that the skipper may have b

A passenger on board an inland waterway cruiser fell overboard. They hung onto a boat rope and had a life ring around them. The casualty was recovered by a rescue craft after some time in the water and was landed ashore and was met by ambulance staff. It was confirmed that the casualty had consumed alcohol as it was their birthday.

A cruise ship passenger fell and sustained a fracture to the head of the femur. They had been drinking alcohol at the time and dancing before they fell. The passenger was then evacuated ashore for medical treatment by helicopter.

title: Eye Injuries from Inadequate PPE During Deck Maintenance
description: This theme is characterised by a recurring failure to adequately specify, enforce, and re-evaluate personal protective equipment — particularly eye protection — across all phases of routine deck maintenance tasks including descaling, paint chipping, wire brushing, and mechanical repair. A unifying latent cause across all incidents is the treatment of PPE selection as a static, task-initiation decision rather than a dynamic risk control process responsive to changing hazard profiles. In several cases, PPE was worn but proved inadequate due to poor fit, insufficient coverage, or an incorrect protection standard relative to the particle velocity and size generated by the specific tool or task phase in use. Critically, post-task clean-up activities and transitional work phases were not recognised as presenting equal or greater particulate risk than the primary maintenance task, and no compensatory controls such as dust suppression, vacuum collection, or upgraded sealed eyewear were applied. The systemic absence of formal Job Safety Analyses, pre-task toolbox talks, and PPE pre-use inspection protocols allowed these inadequacies to persist across vessel types and operational contexts, indicating a broader cultural normalisation of inadequate eye protection compliance during maintenance operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Safety of Life, covering maintenance of machinery and mechanical components including the obligation to manage associated occupational hazards during repair operations

SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements, relevant where maintenance of lifesaving equipment such as lifeboat muster links involves the use of power tools generating high-velocity projectiles

SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation, insofar as it underpins the operator's duty to ensure crew are fit for duty and that environmental conditions such as wind speed, sea state, and lighting are assessed as risk factors before authorising deck maintenance activities
preventable_by: Mandate a dynamic, phase-specific PPE risk assessment for all maintenance tasks, explicitly requiring re-evaluation of eye protection requirements at each task phase — including clean-up — with a minimum standard of indirect-vent sealed goggles for all grinding, chipping, wire brushing, and particulate-generating operations, and a mandatory secondary redundant layer (sealed goggles beneath a full-face shield) for any power-tool application generating high-velocity metal fragments.

Introduce a mandatory pre-task toolbox talk and written Job Safety Analysis for all scheduled and unscheduled deck maintenance, incorporating an environmental risk modifier that automatically elevates PPE requirements and may suspend operations when wind speeds exceed a defined threshold (e.g., 15 kph), visibility is reduced, or sea state exceeds a vessel-specific operability limit — with a designated officer sign-off required before work commences.

Implement a standardised two-stage medical examination protocol for all eye injury events involving high-velocity or fine-particulate sources, requiring an initial examination followed by a mandatory review at 24 hours using slit-lamp assessment where available or teleconsultation with a qualified ophthalmologist before the crew member is returned to duty, to prevent missed retained foreign bodies and premature return to work as occurred in multiple incidents within this cluster.

representative_cases.description: Passenger ship's deck crew were descaling on the forecastle. After they have completed their work they started sweeping up the dust and debris. The injured crew member was wearing safety glasses however, it was suspected that some of the dust got behind as the particles were too small. After feeling discomfort in their eye, the doctor removed a small flake of metal from the individuals eye. The in

A fisheries patrol vessel crew member was chipping paint and possibly a paint or rust chip went into their eye. The eye was flushed with saline but the crew member could still feel it scratching on the eye. Following medical advice by radio, the vessel headed to the nearest harbour for the crew member to get further treatment.

A cruise ship crew member was carrying out maintenance, they were wearing PPE full face shield, face mask and gloves. Whilst using using a wire brush machine to chip paint from a lifeboat muster link, they felt a sudden discomfort on the right eye. The crew member went to the medical centre where the medical team removed a particle from the eye and the following day returned to duty. There was sti

Whilst a cargo vessel was departing port, the steam boiler fan failed. A crew member had removed the fan and started on the repair. Whilst pulling the bearing, one of the ball bearings broke and its metal filings got into the crew member's eye. The eye was examined but no visible pieces were found but the crew member still felt discomfort in the eye. Once ashore the crew member went to hospital fo

A cruise ship's crewmember injured their eye whilst chipping rust from a guard rail using a chipping gun. A small chip of rust went into the casualty's eye despite them wearing safety glasses. The casualty went to hospital on shore and returned to work eight days later.
A cruise ship crewmember was chipping rust when rust particles entered their eye. They were wearing goggles however the particles were able to get behind them because there was not a proper seal.
A research vessel crewmember was working on deck when they got something in their eye, which was suspected to be rust.

A ro-ro cargo vessel crew member was working in the engine room when an object penetrated their eye. The metal object was 2mm in size and appeared metallic. The crew member was initially given drops for the inflammation and after finding the foreign object they were given further drops for an irritation to the eyelid.

title: Struck-By and Trip Hazards in Confined Workspaces
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries resulting from contact with fixed overhead obstructions, unsecured equipment, and cluttered deck spaces within confined or restricted working environments aboard vessels. The incidents share a common latent failure: the working environment had not been systematically assessed to identify physical hazards — whether low-clearance pipework and sprinkler heads, unsecured pallets in alleyways, or stowed equipment left in fall zones — before crew were required to work within or transit through those spaces. Across all cases, no compensatory controls such as hazard marking, physical guarding, pre-task briefings, or enforced housekeeping standards were in place. The recurring nature of these incidents points to a systemic gap in routine risk assessment and safety culture, whereby the acceptance of cluttered, poorly marked, or physically hazardous working spaces has become normalised aboard affected vessels.

Latent hardware factors are significant throughout this theme: fixed firefighting system components installed at crew head height, pipework at low clearance, and the inherent spatial constraints of shaft tunnels and engine rooms create environments where the margin for safe movement is minimal and unforgiving. Human factors — including reduced situational awareness, inattention to footing, and unfamiliarity with specific spatial hazards — consistently amplify the underlying physical risks, particularly during transitional movements such as stepping up, standing from a crouched position, or walking through a passageway. Night-time working conditions compound these deficiencies across multiple incidents by degrading visual detection of obstructions and overhead hazards. The convergence of unguarded fixed hazards, inadequate housekeeping discipline, absent or unenforced procedural controls, and degraded environmental conditions reveals a systemic failure in how physical workspace risks are identified, communicated, and mitigated as part of routine shipboard safety management.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction (installation and accessibility standards for fixed firefighting system components)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe access, means of escape, and unobstructed passageways)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships – ISM Code (systematic risk assessment, hazard identification, and shipboard safety management procedures)
preventable_by: Conduct systematic confined-space and machinery space hazard mapping to identify all low-clearance obstructions, fixed equipment at head height, and restricted movement areas; mark identified hazards with high-visibility banding or signage and include them in vessel-specific risk assessments and pre-task toolbox talks before work commences.

Implement and actively enforce a housekeeping and clear-passageway procedure — aligned with 5S or equivalent standards — requiring that all portable equipment, pallets, and storage items are returned to designated stowage immediately after use, with compliance verified through structured supervisory walkthrough inspections at regular intervals and following night-shift handovers.

Introduce a mandatory pre-task briefing requirement for all night-time or low-visibility work in confined spaces, engine rooms, and restricted-access areas, incorporating identification of overhead, underfoot, and lateral struck-by hazards; supplement with adequate task lighting provisions and, where low-clearance hazards cannot be eliminated, require the use of appropriate head protection as a minimum compensatory control.

representative_cases.description: A replenishment tanker crew member was working in a machinery space shaft tunnel with low head room. As the crew member went to step up onto the floor plating, their head brushed a fixed firefighting system sprinkler head resulting in a superficial injury to the scalp. The crew member was not aware of the low proximity of the sprinkler head once they were on the step.

A ro-pax ferry crewmember had just exited the lift and was walking down an alleyway, when they tripped over an unused pallet that had been left there. The crewmember put out their right hand to break the fall. Their hand hit the pallet and caused it to shift. They felt immediate pain along their entire right arm and sustained bruising to the left palm and thumb. The crewmember disembarked at the n

An offshore support vessel crewmember was working in the engine room when they overbalanced and fell backwards. They struck a storage box and sustained grazes to their arm and shin. The crewmember was able to walk back to the engine control room without assistance but their ankle began to swell shortly after. They went to a hospital ashore where they were diagnosed with an ankle fracture and signe
