A dredger crewmember sustained a minor head injury in the engine room whilst fixing a dredge pump. The casualty sustained superficial wounds to their scalp after they stood up and caught their head on a pipe. The casualty only required minor first aid.

A fishing vessel crew member and shipyard worker were aboard the vessel clearing the factory deck area of any obstructions around the fish room hatch. This was to allow them, and the crew assisting carry out maintenance work, Part of the safety barrier at the forward side was removed so they could both gain access to the area. A submersible pump was in the bilge wells with the hose running through

On board a general cargo vessel, a crew member was removing the securing wedges from the top of the hatch covers with a sledgehammer. With the hammer swinging towards him, the wedge splintered on contact sending a metal shard into his leg below the knee, causing a serious injury resulting in medical sign off. There were no other damages or injuries.

A general cargo vessel crewmember fell from a height during cargo operations. The crewmember sustained lacerations to their leg and was taken to hospital by ambulance. There were no other injuries reported and no pollution or damages.

Ro/Ro passenger crew member was preparing the main engine for a service. The floor grid plate had been lifted to allow access to the lube oil filters. The casualty stepped forward to open the oil suction V/V to empty the sump and fell down the exposed space, a height of approximately 30cm onto pipework below resulting in a broken ankle. Colleagues extracted the crew member from the vessel and was

title: Propulsion Failure Due to Latent Machinery Maintenance Deficiencies
description: This theme is characterised by the catastrophic failure of main propulsion systems in fishing vessels, arising primarily from undetected or neglected deficiencies in engine cooling systems and associated machinery. Across all incidents in this cluster, the common thread is an absence of structured, documented preventative maintenance regimes that would otherwise identify developing faults — such as degraded sea water pump impellers, cooling system leaks, or blocked sea strainers — before they escalate to total propulsion loss. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a systemic reliance on informal maintenance practices prevalent in small-scale fishing vessel operations, where structured safety management systems are rarely implemented and pre-voyage machinery checks are either cursory or undocumented. The consequential loss of propulsion in open waters, frequently at night and in adverse sea conditions, then triggers a cascade of secondary risks including prolonged drifting, dangerous towing operations, and inadequate communication of the vessel's distress.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part C – Machinery Installations)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 33 – Distress situations, obligations and procedures; Regulation 14 – Ships' manning)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – maintenance, emergency preparedness, and reporting procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce documented preventative maintenance schedules for all propulsion and cooling system components — including sea water pumps, impellers, heat exchangers, and sea strainers — with records subject to inspection by flag state or port state control authorities prior to departure on fishing voyages.

Require all fishing vessels to operate under a formal voyage reporting and check-in protocol, obligating crews to make scheduled radio or satellite contact with a designated shore-based contact at defined intervals, ensuring that loss of communication triggers a timely distress response rather than allowing vessels to drift undetected for extended periods.

Ensure all fishing vessels carry a minimum inventory of critical engine spare parts — including cooling system components such as pump impellers, belts, and seals — and that crew members are trained in basic fault diagnosis and emergency repair procedures sufficient to address common propulsion failure modes at sea, reducing dependency on shore-based engineers who may lack the resources or capability to effect repairs in offshore or adverse-weather conditions.

representative_cases.description: A fishing vessel suffered an engine failure. Two engineers from the harbour attended on request from the vessel but repairs were unsuccessful. Two engineers and two of the crew returned to the shore and the master remained on board. The vessel was towed to harbour for repairs.

A fishing trawler suffered propulsion failure after an overheating incident and was drifting without power for at least two days. The alarm was raised from someone ashore after they had not received contact from the vessel. The vessel was able to be contacted and was under tow by another fishing vessel to port for repairs. There were no injuries or pollution reported.

A twin rig trawler suffered main engine propulsion failure after a suspected issue with the sea water pump and cooling system. The main engine reportedly suffered overheating and there was 'no water' in the system. The vessel was towed by another fishing vessel, however the towing line parted multiple times. A search and rescue vessel was then dispatched to stand by to monitor the situation and th

A fishing vessel with two crew onboard suffered engine failure, the crew attempt to restart the engine but were unable to. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the scene to provided support whilst the fishing vessel was towed by another fishing vessel back to shore. No injuries to crew. No damage to vessel.

A fishing vessel experienced engine failure. They called for assistance and dropped anchor. Another fishing vessel set up a tow and brought them back to port. There were no injuries, damage or pollution.

A stern trawler was towing fishing gear when it experienced propulsion failure and could not recover the gear. The local search and rescue vessel was launched and towed the vessel back to port. There were no damages, injuries or pollution. The cause of the propulsion failure was due to a water pump for the main engine breaking down. Whilst under tow, the fishing vessel crew were able to recover th

A fishing vessel had a fuel issue, broke down and were unable to start their engine. The vessel was in no immediate danger, they had their gear down which effectively acted as an anchor. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked took the fishing vessel under tow and proceeded to harbour.

Fishing vessel lost engine power just after it had taken aboard some prawn pots. The vessel headed towards the harbour but the engine laboured and then stopped. The crew were unable to access the vhf due to where the pots had been stacked so they contacted the coastguard by mobile phone. The local lifeboat were on exercise and provided a tow back to harbour. It was found that that coolant had leak

title: Propulsion Loss Due to Latent Fuel System Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring pattern of propulsion loss events arising from latent deficiencies within vessel fuel and engine systems — specifically fuel injection pumps, replacement fuel pump components, fuel gauging instruments, and broader engine mechanical integrity. Across all incidents, the critical commonality is not the failure event itself but the conditions that allowed the failure to remain undetected until operational demand exposed it. Pre-departure checks were either absent, inadequate, or relied upon single-point instrumentation without independent verification. Post-maintenance procedures failed to include confirmatory sea trials or component specification validation before vessels returned to service, including passenger-carrying and search and rescue operations. The incidents recur because systemic procedural gaps normalise the practice of accepting unverified readiness states as sufficient, creating conditions in which latent mechanical degradation progresses unimpeded to operational failure.

Latent hardware failures are the proximate driver across this cluster, manifesting as worn or contaminated fuel injection components, defective replacement parts, faulty gauging instruments, and piston liner failures — all of which developed over time without detection. Human factors, while not typically the immediate cause, are embedded in the upstream failures: maintenance schedules were deferred or inadequately executed, post-installation verification was omitted, and procedural reliance on single instruments was accepted without challenge. Environmental conditions — predominantly night-time operations, constrained port environments, and moderate sea states — did not cause these incidents but materially elevated the consequences of propulsion loss when it occurred, reducing response time and complicating emergency management. The convergence of latent hardware vulnerability, procedural insufficiency, and elevated operational risk environment is the defining pattern of this theme and the primary lever for preventive intervention.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part E — Additional requirements for machinery, boiler and electrical installations, including maintenance and operational readiness of propulsion systems)

SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (Regulation 34 — Safe navigation and avoidance of dangerous situations, including adequacy of pre-departure checks and bridge resource management during pilotage)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requiring documented maintenance regimes, pre-departure verification procedures, and post-maintenance testing protocols)
preventable_by: Mandate independent dual-verification fuel system checks prior to all departures, requiring cross-validation of fuel levels and engine readiness through at least two independent methods (e.g., electronic gauge confirmed by manual dipstick measurement), with completed verification recorded in the vessel log and signed off by the responsible officer or crew member before departure is authorised.

Implement a mandatory post-maintenance sea trial and component specification verification protocol for all critical propulsion system repairs or replacements, requiring documented confirmation that replacement parts meet original manufacturer specifications and that the repaired or modified system has been tested under operational load conditions before the vessel is returned to service — particularly for passenger-carrying or SAR-tasked vessels.

Establish a proactive fuel system component lifecycle management programme under the vessel's Safety Management System, incorporating scheduled inspection and calibration intervals for fuel injection components, fuel gauging instruments, and associated hardware, with defect trends reviewed at regular intervals to identify developing faults before operational failure — replacing the current reactive, post-incident investigative approach with structured predictive maintenance practice.

representative_cases.description: A general cargo vessel was under pilotage departing from a port when it experienced problems with the engine where the engine did not respond properly to telegraph orders. The bridge team called the engine room team who found that the injection pumps for two of the cylinders on the main engine were stuck. The engineer put the engine in local control and stopped the engine. The pilot and the bridge

A recreational motor vessel with seven people onboard had an engine failure. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel that was on exercise was diverted to assist. The SAR vessel towed the motor vessel to a marina. No reported injuries. The engine failure was due to a new fuel pump that had been recently fitted.

A search and rescue vessel was on a tasking when it unexpectedly ran out of fuel. It was discovered that the fuel gauge was faulty and had been showing that the tank contained enough fuel when it didn't. Another search and rescue vessel was in the vicinity and towed it back to port.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was on service, on return to the station the SAR vessel suffered engine failure. The vessel was towed backed to station by another SAR vessel. Incident reported by phone, waiting for ARF.

A tug had a main propulsion failure to its starboard main engine during sea trials. The tug returned to port on the port engine and was taken out of service to investigate and effect repairs.  The engine had just been repaired when it had a piston liner failure and expulsion from the engine.

A cargo vessel was preparing to leave port with a pilot onboard. During pre-start checks a failure in the main engine starter motor was identified. The departure was then cancelled. The vessel ordered a new part, fitted and tested the engines. The vessel was underway three days later.

A ro-ro cargo vessel had engine failure to the port engine whilst on passage. The vessel made its way into port on the starboard engine with two tugs standing by. An electrical problem was identified as the issue and rectified swiftly on arrival.

Offshore supply vessel sustained a port side main engine failure after a loud shuddering noise. The vessel had had a main engine major overhaul and was on route to port at the time of the incident. The vessel turned around and headed back to port. There were no injuries.

title: Passenger Trip Hazards from Inadequate Deck Safety Management
description:
This theme is characterised by a recurring failure to manage foreseeable deck-level trip hazards in areas accessed by passengers during embarkation, transit, and disembarkation. Across all incidents, a common latent organisational deficiency is evident: the absence of enforced procedural controls — such as post-lashing stowage requirements, pre-passenger-access deck inspections, vulnerability-based passenger assistance protocols, and systematic risk assessment of vessel-to-shore transition interfaces — that would otherwise identify and mitigate hazards before passenger exposure. The hazards themselves, whether unsecured restraint chains, vessel-to-pontoon gaps, or fixed deck transition strips, are either created through routine operational activity or are persistent features that have been normalised through repeated use without formal review. The consequence is that foreseeable, preventable injuries continue to occur because no robust safety management layer exists between the hazard and the passenger.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including inadequate gangway or bridging plate provision, raised deck transition strips, and hazardous pontoon surface materials — create the physical conditions for injury, but these are consistently compounded by procedural and human factor failures. Crew members responsible for lashing operations do not stow equipment clear of pedestrian routes, and no supervisory or inspection mechanism exists to catch this omission before passengers are admitted to the space. The absence of targeted assistance protocols for mobility-impaired or otherwise vulnerable passengers further increases the consequence severity when hazards are encountered. In all cases, night-time environmental conditions reduced passenger visibility of deck-level hazards, demonstrating that existing risk assessments have not adequately accounted for the interaction between lighting conditions, vessel movement, and passenger vulnerability. Collectively, these incidents reflect a systemic gap in the application of the safety management system to routine passenger-facing operations.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction — Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (including safe means of access and escape)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew duties, passenger safety briefings and supervision)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — risk assessment, procedures, and safety management obligations)
preventable_by: Introduce a mandatory post-lashing stowage checklist and a formal pre-passenger-access car deck inspection, requiring a designated officer to certify that all restraint equipment is stowed clear of pedestrian routes and that the deck is safe for passenger access before any passenger movement is permitted.

Implement a systematic risk assessment of all embarkation and disembarkation interfaces — including vessel-to-pontoon transitions and internal deck transition points — that explicitly evaluates hazard severity under night-time lighting conditions, vessel movement, and varying sea states, with bridging plates, non-slip coverings, adequate lighting, and stationed crew supervision specified as controls where gaps or surface hazards cannot be eliminated.

Establish a passenger vulnerability management protocol within the vessel's Safety Management System, requiring crew to identify mobility-impaired or otherwise at-risk passengers at the point of boarding, brief them on deck hazards, and provide a dedicated crew escort for movement through potentially hazardous areas, particularly during night operations or adverse conditions.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel passenger was returning to the car deck at the end of the passage, the passenger tripped and fell over some restraint chains used to secure large vehicles which had been left on the car deck. The chains and hooks were in the path of the passengers as they returned to their cars. The passenger sustained injuries to the head, face and a fractured arm, they were assisted by other pass

Ferry was disembarking passengers when the casualty tripped. They stepped from the vessel to the pontoon and injured their leg on the rubber. This occurred as a result of a small gap between the vessel and pontoon.

A canal boat was returning to its mooring when a crew member was alerted that a passenger had fallen over below deck and was laying on their back on the floor. The passenger was passed into the care of the ambulance service and taken to hospital for treatment for a fractured arm. A cruise ships passenger tripped on an obstacle fixed to the deck, resulting in a fractured jaw. Passenger did not notice the obstruction while walking and caught their foot resulting in falling and fracturing their jaw.

A ro-pax vessel was alongside. A passenger tripped and fell over on a car deck during disembarkation, injuring their arm and face. The first aid team treated the passenger for their injuries, the passenger then made their own way to hospital.

A cruise ship passenger was walking along the embarkation deck. They walked to the ship’s rail to look at some passing ships. As they stepped away from the ship’s rail, they tripped over a bollard light fixed to the deck. They then fell to the deck and had a broken arm. A pilot boat with six passenger onboard had returned to a pontoon and moored alongside a tug ready for disembarkation. One passenger jumped off pilot boat onto tug walked forwards and tripped on mooring wire in the centre of the deck. A cruise ship passenger was taking part in a behind the scenes tour, when they tripped over a bollard on the main working deck and fell to the deck. They were diagnosed with a fractured pelvis as a result of the fall.
